caveats: a: The available occupation mix is NAICS 541200, which also includes CPA, tax-only, and payroll-only establishments.
a: No source directly measures wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task exposure for the frozen firm lens.
a: Vendor-sponsored adoption evidence may overrepresent firms already using modern cloud accounting tools.
rho: Survey use of AI does not establish production-grade implementation or realized labor substitution.
rho: Both surveys cover broader accounting populations; S6 is global and includes large firms.
rho: Five-year capability and regulatory developments are uncertain.
e: Public datasets do not report recurring-revenue share or lens eligibility at the six-digit industry and EBITDA band.
e: NAICS self-classification may mix bookkeeping firms with billing offices and miscellaneous accounting practices.
e: The frozen firm-count estimate may include businesses whose owner dependence makes them operationally nontransferable.

s5: Retirement intentions from 2020 may not predict completed transfers in 2026-2031.
s5: Public deal feeds undercount private transactions and often omit NAICS and EBITDA.
q: The 2021 Intuit billing survey is dated and not limited to NAICS 541219 or the EBITDA band.
q: Pricing-model prevalence is not a direct measurement of benefit retention.
q: The estimate assumes competitive repricing occurs over renewals rather than immediately.
d5: BLS publishes the projection only for NAICS 541200, not 541219.
d5: Chained-dollar output is not a direct constant-quality quantity index for the current service basket.
d5: Self-service software could reduce commodity bookkeeping demand faster than the broader sector projection implies.
o: Occupational employment is not service quantity and includes in-house workers outside the screened industry.
o: The pace of reliable agentic close and exception handling is highly uncertain.
o: Operator need varies sharply with client complexity, software standardization, and willingness to self-serve.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Primarily small and midsize business clients purchasing monthly or otherwise repeat bookkeeping, close, reporting, billing, and controller support under hourly, fixed-fee, subscription, or bundled arrangements; project cleanup and advisory work may supplement recurring fees.
lens.excluded_activities: CPA offices, tax-return-preparation-only firms, payroll-only firms, one-person practices below the EBITDA band, captive accounting departments, software-only vendors, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms whose work is predominantly one-off cleanup or project consulting rather than recurring or repeat outsourced service.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market non-CPA bookkeeping, outsourced accounting, billing, controller, financial reporting, and related repeat services supplied to external customers, including mixed practices that also provide tax return preparation or payroll alongside accounting services.

a.bridge: S2 measures the employment mix of broader NAICS 541200 rather than wage-weighted AI exposure in NAICS 541219: accountants and auditors are 31.73% of employment, bookkeeping/accounting/auditing clerks 8.88%, and tax preparers 7.13%. S3-S5 describe tasks and observed technology use across those broader occupations and accounting professionals. I mapped routine document intake, coding, reconciliation, report drafting, and client communication to high exposure; exception resolution, judgment, control, and accountable review to lower exposure; and weighted the professional task families more heavily because their wages exceed clerical wages. This likely biases exposure upward because the surveys include tech-forward respondents and CPA, tax, and payroll firms, but downward because OEWS omits self-employed owners and current tools are improving quickly.
caveats: The available occupation mix is NAICS 541200, which also includes CPA, tax-only, and payroll-only establishments.
No source directly measures wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task exposure for the frozen firm lens.
Vendor-sponsored adoption evidence may overrepresent firms already using modern cloud accounting tools.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-wage study of employees at US non-CPA bookkeeping and outsourced-accounting firms in the $1-10M EBITDA band, coded to O*NET tasks and separating already-automated from manual minutes, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The service basket is unusually digital and language/data intensive. BLS says software has already automated many clerical bookkeeping tasks, while accountants use AI and process automation to increase productivity and shift toward analytical work. The estimate excludes work already automated and retains substantial human effort for ambiguous coding, reconciliations, controls, client context, and final review.

d5.bridge: S9 projects chained-dollar output for broader NAICS 541200 from $210.0 billion in 2024 to $258.6 billion in 2034, a 2.1% annual rate; mechanically applying that rate for five years gives about 1.11. I haircut the base to 1.08 for the narrower non-CPA bookkeeping/billing mix and for self-service pressure on the current service basket, while retaining a range around the broad-sector path. The broad industry includes CPA, tax-only, and payroll-only services and may embed changing service mix, so it may overstate constant-quality demand for NAICS 541219.
caveats: BLS publishes the projection only for NAICS 541200, not 541219.
Chained-dollar output is not a direct constant-quality quantity index for the current service basket.
Self-service software could reduce commodity bookkeeping demand faster than the broader sector projection implies.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain six-digit real receipts or quantity indexes for NAICS 541219 from Census Service Annual Survey microdata or a custom industry study, decomposed into bookkeeping, billing, controller, and advisory services and held to a constant service basket.
d5.rationale: Underlying demand should be broadly stable to moderately growing as the economy expands and businesses continue to generate transactions, reporting needs, and regulatory obligations. BLS expects real output growth in the broader accounting-services industry even as clerical employment declines, consistent with productivity growth rather than disappearance of service demand.

caveats: Public datasets do not report recurring-revenue share or lens eligibility at the six-digit industry and EBITDA band.
NAICS self-classification may mix bookkeeping firms with billing offices and miscellaneous accounting practices.
The frozen firm-count estimate may include businesses whose owner dependence makes them operationally nontransferable.
e.change_evidence: A stratified Dun & Bradstreet or Data Axle sample of NAICS 541219 firms in the frozen revenue/EBITDA band, verified through websites and owner interviews for recurring-revenue share, external customers, and transferability, would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: The official NAICS definition includes non-CPA accountant, bookkeeper, and billing offices, and monthly accounting/bookkeeping is commonly billed as an ongoing service. A majority of firms in the frozen EBITDA band should therefore fit the recurring external-service lens. The range removes firms dominated by one-off cleanup or consulting, software resale, captive arrangements, weakly transferable owner books, or other nonrecurring activity.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $29,298.0M / sectoral output (T30) $36,927.9M, year 2022

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $116,910 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541219); class <5: 35,754 firms, avg revenue/firm $191,355 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $29,947 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 5,777 firms, avg revenue/firm $656,548 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $102,750 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 2,081 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,497,395 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $234,342 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,247 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,177,727 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $653,814 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 282 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,787,344 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,157,719 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 249 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,583,161 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,847,265 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 531

o.bridge: S3 and S4 project employment and describe task change for bookkeeping clerks and accountants across all industries, not the share of NAICS 541219 service quantity requiring an outsourced accountable operator. I translate the 6% projected decline and automation of routine clerical work, together with 5% accountant growth and the continued prominence of analytical, advisory, compliance, and explanation duties, into an operator-required share. Because non-CPA bookkeeping has more routine content than the accountant occupation, relying on S4 alone would bias the estimate upward; direct-to-customer software progress could also make the proxy optimistic.
caveats: Occupational employment is not service quantity and includes in-house workers outside the screened industry.
The pace of reliable agentic close and exception handling is highly uncertain.
Operator need varies sharply with client complexity, software standardization, and willingness to self-serve.
o.change_evidence: Survey current clients of LMM NAICS 541219 firms and track five-year cohort outcomes by workflow: continued outsourced operator, client self-service, software-only substitution, offshore provider, or eliminated task, with revenue and transaction volume attached.
o.rationale: Transaction ingestion, basic categorization, and standard reporting are increasingly self-served or automated, so some current quantity will no longer require a service firm. Most clients will still need exception handling, reconciliations, close ownership, controls, interpretation, communication, and accountable delivery across fragmented systems. The durable unit is therefore a technology-enabled operator, not manual data entry.

q.bridge: S7 measures billing-method prevalence across accounting practitioners, finding monthly services split between 50% hourly and 49% fixed-fee billing; S10 covers broader tax, audit, and accounting firms and reports hourly billing at less than half of client arrangements with subscriptions growing. I convert the mix into discounted retention: hourly work passes labor savings through quickly through fewer billable hours, while fixed, bundled, and subscription fees retain more benefit until renewals and competition reset prices. Broader CPA/tax samples and pricing-advice respondents may have more pricing power than non-CPA bookkeeping firms, biasing retention upward.
caveats: The 2021 Intuit billing survey is dated and not limited to NAICS 541219 or the EBITDA band.
Pricing-model prevalence is not a direct measurement of benefit retention.
The estimate assumes competitive repricing occurs over renewals rather than immediately.
q.change_evidence: Review anonymized engagement letters, invoices, renewal histories, and gross-margin cohorts from 15-20 LMM non-CPA firms before and after workflow automation, separating hourly, fixed, bundled, and subscription contracts.
q.rationale: The roughly even mix of hourly and fixed monthly pricing prevents either immediate full pass-through or durable full retention. Fixed and subscription engagements can preserve early efficiency gains, but annual repricing, transparent software alternatives, client bargaining, and rivalry should share a meaningful portion over five years. Advisory capacity created by automation can support retention when firms sell outcomes rather than hours.

rho.bridge: S5 measures reported AI and automation use among 700 US accounting professionals, not the five-year share of exposed labor opportunity implemented by LMM NAICS 541219 firms; S6 measures global tax and accounting firms. I treat daily AI use, planned investment, workflow automation, and the move from experimentation to integration as diffusion anchors, then discount for fragmented client systems, data quality, review requirements, change management, privacy, and the smaller-firm lens. The vendor and respondent-selection effects likely bias reported readiness upward.
caveats: Survey use of AI does not establish production-grade implementation or realized labor substitution.
Both surveys cover broader accounting populations; S6 is global and includes large firms.
Five-year capability and regulatory developments are uncertain.
rho.change_evidence: Obtain workflow-level implementation logs from 20-30 US non-CPA LMM firms showing baseline labor hours, go-live dates, exception rates, review time, and sustained headcount or avoided-hiring outcomes for at least 12 months.
rho.rationale: Implementation should advance meaningfully because core workflows are already software-mediated: in the Intuit survey, 46% used AI daily and 64% planned AI investment or upgrades within a year, while Wolters Kluwer reported 41% adoption and 77% planning higher investment. Full capture remains constrained by integrations, inconsistent source documents, client-specific charts of accounts, accuracy checks, confidentiality, and the need to preserve accountable review.

s5.bridge: S8 reports that 26% of US CPA-firm single owners and sole practitioners planned to retire within five years in a 2020 survey; it does not measure non-CPA NAICS 541219 LMM firms or completed qualifying control transfers. I reduce that retirement intention for delayed exits, internal succession without a qualifying external control change, client-book wind-downs, and failed transfers, while allowing non-retirement strategic sales. The CPA population may have stronger succession infrastructure and larger transferable practices, biasing the proxy upward for this non-CPA lens; the older vintage may bias it either way.
caveats: The succession survey covers CPA firms, while NAICS 541219 explicitly excludes CPA offices.
Retirement intentions from 2020 may not predict completed transfers in 2026-2031.
Public deal feeds undercount private transactions and often omit NAICS and EBITDA.
s5.change_evidence: Acquire five years of closed US non-CPA bookkeeping/accounting-practice transactions from BizBuySell, DealStats, Accounting Practice Sales, and Poe Group, matched to an estimated eligible-firm denominator and coded for control transfer versus book sale or internal reorganization.
s5.rationale: Aging owners and the recurring client-book model create a credible transfer channel, but planned retirement is not equivalent to a qualifying control transfer. Some practices will be absorbed through internal succession, client attrition, or asset/book sales that do not preserve a standalone transferable operation.

records.principal_driver: Digitally mediated recurring bookkeeping and close workflows create a large implementable labor opportunity if firms standardize client systems and preserve accountable review.
records.principal_weakness: The same automation that lowers delivery cost can let clients self-serve commodity bookkeeping and force savings into lower prices at renewal.

caveats: The a and rho values rely on broader occupation data and vendor-sponsored surveys of accounting professionals rather than workflow-level evidence from US lower-middle-market non-CPA firms; reported AI use, automation adoption, and investment plans do not directly measure implementable labor substitution.

The s5 bridge starts from 2020 retirement intentions among CPA-firm owners, not completed qualifying transfers among non-CPA NAICS 541219 firms. The discount to a 0.16 base is transparent but remains weakly anchored, and F is the base-case weakest link near the HIGHEST_PRIORITY cutoff.

The q estimate converts billing-method prevalence into five-year benefit retention without direct pass-through, renewal, or realized-margin evidence. Fixed-fee and subscription billing can delay pass-through but do not by themselves establish a 0.60 retained share.

The d5 and o estimates use broader NAICS 541200 output and all-industry occupational projections. Self-service substitution also appears in both the d5 haircut and the o bridge, creating a conservative overlap, although removing that overlap would raise D and would not change the base tier or action.

The e value is an honest estimate based on the industry definition and evidence that monthly services exist, not a measured share of firms in the frozen EBITDA band meeting the recurring, external-customer, and transferable-operation lens.

sources_audited.note: The official 2022 NAICS manual defines 541219 as non-CPA accounting services other than tax-return-preparation-only or payroll-only establishments and explicitly includes non-CPA accountant, bookkeeper, and billing offices.
The May 2023 OEWS page is for broader NAICS 541200 and reports accountants and auditors at 31.73%, bookkeeping/accounting/auditing clerks at 8.88%, and tax preparers at 7.13%, with occupation wages.
BLS supports the cited bookkeeping duties, prior automation of many tasks, 6% projected employment decline for 2024-2034, and shift toward analytical and advisory work.
BLS supports the cited accounting duties, use of AI and robotic process automation, 5% projected employment growth for 2024-2034, and greater emphasis on advisory and analytical duties.

The Intuit release reports an April 2025 survey of 700 US accounting professionals, including 46% daily AI use, 64% planning AI investment or upgrades, 95% firm adoption of automation in the prior year, and the cited workflow areas.
The Wolters Kluwer release reports insights from more than 2,700 professionals worldwide, AI adoption rising from 9% to 41%, 35% daily use, and 77% planning increased AI investment.
The Intuit rate-survey page reports monthly accounting and bookkeeping billed hourly by 50% of respondents and fixed-fee by 49%, with hourly billing favored for uncertain-scope work.
The Journal of Accountancy article reports a 2020 survey of 587 CPA-firm representatives and that 26% of single-owner and sole-practitioner respondents planned to retire within five years.
The BLS 2024-2034 industry table reports broader NAICS 541200 employment growth of 2.4% and real output increasing from $210.0 billion to $258.6 billion at 2.1% annually.
The Thomson Reuters Institute article says hourly billing remains the largest model but covers less than half of client arrangements, while subscription and bundled models are gaining traction.

records.title: Other Accounting Services

caveats: a: No source directly reports a wage-weighted task-exposure share for this six-digit residual industry.
a: The OEWS mix covers management, scientific, and technical consulting broadly and likely differs materially from 541618.

a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio is 1.3991, which is economically implausible as a true compensation share and reflects a 2024-wage/2022-receipts mismatch plus harmonization; this materially weakens the labor-opportunity foundation but is not re-estimated here.
rho: Tool access and individual speed gains do not prove firm-level operational implementation or avoided hiring.
rho: BCG is far larger and better resourced than the firms in the frozen lens.
rho: The frozen labor ratio's value above one means even a well-researched rho cannot cure uncertainty in the downstream labor base.
e: The frozen n of 256 is already a margin-bridged estimate and is not re-estimated here.
e: NAICS 541618 is a residual code dominated by telecommunications and utilities management consulting but may include miscoded general consultancies.
e: Existing-client revenue evidence covers consultants broadly rather than the exact industry and EBITDA band.
s5: Gallup covers all employer businesses, not consulting firms or the LMM EBITDA band.
s5: A stated plan does not necessarily close, and some transfers are gifts or internal succession rather than qualifying control sales.
s5: Professional-services deal counts include accounting, staffing, human-capital services, and consulting specialties outside 541618.
q: The survey population is consultants across niches and likely includes many solo operators below the frozen EBITDA band.
q: Pricing method prevalence is not revenue-weighted and does not show contract-specific pass-through.
q: Demand loss belongs in d5 and o, and implementation friction belongs in rho, not in this estimate.
d5: Management analysts are an occupation across industries and do not measure output of NAICS 541618.
d5: The residual industry has material telecom and utilities exposure not isolated in the occupational projection.
d5: Government procurement, enterprise investment cycles, and AI-driven new-service demand create wide uncertainty.
o: Management consulting has no universal licensure requirement or statutory barrier to client self-service.
o: Operator need varies materially between high-context telecom or utility engagements and generic research or benchmarking.
o: AI capability boundaries may move rapidly, but persuasive errors can preserve the need for accountable expert review.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business, nonprofit, and government clients buy scoped advisory projects, fixed-fee deliverables, time-and-materials work, value-priced engagements, and recurring retainers; repeat revenue arises from continuing client relationships, successive initiatives, and implementation support.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal strategy units, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, administrative and general management consulting, human-resources consulting, marketing consulting, process and logistics consulting, and practices whose client relationships, expertise, or delivery capability cannot transfer beyond an individual founder.
lens.included_activities: US other-management-consulting firms in the $1-10M normalized EBITDA band that repeatedly provide outsourced advice, analysis, recommendations, facilitation, and implementation support to external organizations, including telecommunications and utilities management consulting.

a.bridge: BLS reports an occupation mix for the broader NAICS 541600 consulting sector, and a randomized field experiment measures performance on selected BCG consulting tasks; neither measures the wage-weighted share of current, not-yet-automated tasks in NAICS 541618. I map exposure across research, synthesis, interview and meeting notes, data analysis, benchmarking, first-draft recommendations, proposals, reports, presentations, project administration, and knowledge retrieval, while retaining human time for problem framing, expert diagnosis, stakeholder trust, facilitation, political judgment, field observation, and implementation. The BCG task set can overstate exposure because it preselected AI-suitable exercises, while broader occupation data can understate it by including scientific consulting roles outside this code.
caveats: No source directly reports a wage-weighted task-exposure share for this six-digit residual industry.
The OEWS mix covers management, scientific, and technical consulting broadly and likely differs materially from 541618.

The frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio is 1.3991, which is economically implausible as a true compensation share and reflects a 2024-wage/2022-receipts mismatch plus harmonization; this materially weakens the labor-opportunity foundation but is not re-estimated here.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted time-and-motion study of NAICS 541618 firms reporting hours and compensation by research, modeling, interviews, analysis, deliverable production, sales, facilitation, implementation, and administration before and after AI use would materially change a.
a.rationale: Consulting is concentrated in highly paid knowledge work: in broader NAICS 541600, management analysts were 11.91% of employment, management occupations 16.21%, project-management specialists 3.55%, computer occupations 9.56%, and office support 14.98% in 2023. In a 758-consultant BCG experiment, AI users completed inside-frontier tasks over 25% faster and completed over 12% more tasks. A 48% base recognizes broad exposure in analysis and production without treating the whole advisory relationship as substitutable.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment of management analysts across all industries, whereas d5 is constant-price, constant-quality demand for the current outsourced service basket in NAICS 541618. I convert the 9% 2024-34 employment projection to a roughly half-decade demand anchor, recognizing that consulting demand may grow faster through efficiency, cost-control, telecom, utility, and AI-transformation needs or slower because software and client teams produce more work per person. Employment embeds productivity and in-house work, so it may understate output demand but overstate outsourced-firm demand.
caveats: Management analysts are an occupation across industries and do not measure output of NAICS 541618.
The residual industry has material telecom and utilities exposure not isolated in the occupational projection.
Government procurement, enterprise investment cycles, and AI-driven new-service demand create wide uncertainty.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast of US outsourced telecom, utilities, and other management-consulting spend, deflated by a consulting-services price index and segmented by advisory versus implementation, would materially change d5.
d5.rationale: BLS projects management-analyst employment to grow 9% from 2024 to 2034 and expects consulting demand to increase as organizations seek efficiency and cost control. AI adoption itself creates advisory and implementation work, although public-sector cuts, recessions, and self-service analysis can offset it. The base therefore assumes modest five-year real growth with a broad cyclical and substitution range.

caveats: The frozen n of 256 is already a margin-bridged estimate and is not re-estimated here.
NAICS 541618 is a residual code dominated by telecommunications and utilities management consulting but may include miscoded general consultancies.
Existing-client revenue evidence covers consultants broadly rather than the exact industry and EBITDA band.
e.change_evidence: A representative CRM and financial-data panel for EBITDA-band NAICS 541618 firms reporting external-client revenue, repeat-client revenue, retainers, top-client concentration, founder-originated sales, team delivery, and post-founder retention would materially change e.
e.rationale: The Census definition is inherently an outsourced management-advice service, and consulting-industry evidence indicates high-performing firms can earn 70% of revenue from existing clients. Firms large enough to fall within the frozen EBITDA band should usually have repeat external clients and teams, but eligibility is reduced by founder rainmaking, individual specialist reputation, project-only relationships, customer concentration, and weak codification of intellectual property.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

o.bridge: BLS describes consultant duties and the BCG experiment measures human-plus-AI performance on selected tasks; neither directly measures the year-five share that still requires an accountable consulting-firm operator. I deduct generic research, benchmarking, first-draft analysis, and simple recommendation work that clients can self-serve, while retaining problem framing, access to stakeholders, specialist context, interviews, facilitation, decision accountability, change management, and implementation. Evidence from a top-tier consultancy may overstate the operator role for commodity boutiques, while the outside-frontier failure result supports continued expert oversight.
caveats: Management consulting has no universal licensure requirement or statutory barrier to client self-service.
Operator need varies materially between high-context telecom or utility engagements and generic research or benchmarking.
AI capability boundaries may move rapidly, but persuasive errors can preserve the need for accountable expert review.
o.change_evidence: Procurement and client surveys tracking which consulting scopes move in-house or to software, paired with project outcomes, renewals, and human-escalation rates for AI-delivered advisory services, would materially change o.
o.rationale: Management analysts gather information through interviews and observation, analyze organization-specific problems, develop solutions, present recommendations, and confer with managers. These relational and contextual steps are difficult to supply entirely through software. The BCG study's 19-percentage-point accuracy decline outside the AI frontier demonstrates why expert judgment and verification remain valuable, although generic deck-and-report work is vulnerable to self-service. A 78% base preserves a substantial operator role while allowing meaningful elimination.

q.bridge: A broad consultant survey measures the prevalence of pricing methods, not the discounted five-year share of implemented AI benefit retained by LMM 541618 firms. I treat project and value-based fees as permitting stronger initial capture than hourly billing, retainers as mixed depending on whether hours are capped, and then discount for competitive bids, renewal repricing, client demands for faster delivery, and scope expansion. Solo-consultant pricing evidence may overstate value-pricing flexibility for staffed firms with procurement-led clients, while specialist expertise may support retention above the base.
caveats: The survey population is consultants across niches and likely includes many solo operators below the frozen EBITDA band.
Pricing method prevalence is not revenue-weighted and does not show contract-specific pass-through.
Demand loss belongs in d5 and o, and implementation friction belongs in rho, not in this estimate.
q.change_evidence: Five years of engagement-level proposals, contracts, time records, realized margins, change orders, renewal prices, win/loss data, and client-requested AI discounts from LMM 541618 firms would materially change q.
q.rationale: Consulting Success reports 37% of consultants use project rates, 26% value-based rates, 21% hourly rates, 13% monthly retainers, and 3% performance pricing. That mix lets many firms retain initial productivity gains, but more than 80% had lowered fees to win a client in the 2023 study, indicating bargaining pressure. A 56% base reflects meaningful capture on deliverable- and outcome-priced work followed by five-year sharing through competition and client expectations.

rho.bridge: The BCG experiment establishes technical gains under controlled access, while McKinsey surveys organization-wide AI use rather than implementation of exposed labor opportunity in LMM consulting firms. I translate 88% regular organizational AI use, but only about 6% high-performing scaled adopters, into a five-year implementation share that discounts for client confidentiality, weak proprietary data, workflow redesign, quality assurance, employee training, and the uneven AI frontier. Large-enterprise adoption may overstate LMM consulting capability; simpler boutique workflows and low software costs may bias the estimate in the other direction.
caveats: Tool access and individual speed gains do not prove firm-level operational implementation or avoided hiring.
BCG is far larger and better resourced than the firms in the frozen lens.
The frozen labor ratio's value above one means even a well-researched rho cannot cure uncertainty in the downstream labor base.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of US consulting firms with fewer than 250 employees showing AI-active seats, workflow penetration, billable hours saved, rework, client restrictions, failed deployments, and sustained hiring avoidance would materially move rho.
rho.rationale: AI is easy to deploy for drafts, research, summarization, and internal knowledge access, and controlled consulting tasks show measurable productivity gains. Yet scaled economic impact remains uncommon, and the BCG experiment found that AI users were 19 percentage points less likely to reach the correct solution on an outside-frontier managerial task. A 57% base assumes substantial five-year implementation with persistent human verification and uneven adoption across specialties.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans among all US employer-business owners, and FOCUS counts announced professional-services transactions rather than qualifying completed transfers among eligible 541618 firms. I anchor to the 22% of employer owners planning to sell or transfer within five years, then exclude gifts and internal reorganizations, uncompleted plans, closures, minority investments, and nontransferable founder practices. Professional-services deal activity supports the upper range, but announced transactions and broad owner intentions likely overstate completed control transfers for this code.
caveats: Gallup covers all employer businesses, not consulting firms or the LMM EBITDA band.
A stated plan does not necessarily close, and some transfers are gifts or internal succession rather than qualifying control sales.
Professional-services deal counts include accounting, staffing, human-capital services, and consulting specialties outside 541618.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, or Axial transactions matched to NAICS 541618 and seller EBITDA, plus owner-age, intended-exit, and completed-succession data for consulting boutiques, would materially change s5.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that 13% of employer-business owners plan to sell and 11% plan to transfer ownership within five years, with overlaps producing a 22% combined share. The professional-services market recorded 466 announced US transactions in the first nine months of 2025, with consulting and accounting representing more than half. A 14% base reflects active deal and succession pathways after excluding nonqualifying and unrealized outcomes.

records.principal_driver: Research, synthesis, analysis, reporting, presentation, and administrative work are digitally native and demonstrably accelerated by generative AI.
records.principal_weakness: The supplied labor ratio is economically implausible at 1.3991, while generic consulting output also faces direct client self-service and fee pressure.

caveats: S6 does not support the e rationale's claim that high-performing firms earn 70% of revenue from existing clients; the cited PDF is a pricing study, while that statistic appears on a different Consulting Success webpage. Removing the statement would weaken the narrative basis for e but would not plausibly push e below the roughly 0.31 level needed to move F under 4, and e is already an ESTIMATE, so neither the PRIORITY base tier nor VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action changes.

The evidence for a is a disclosed combination of an occupation mix for broad NAICS 541600 and selected BCG tasks rather than a wage-weighted task inventory for NAICS 541618. The 0.34-0.61 range is judgmental, but the high frozen l makes H remain at or above the PRIORITY threshold throughout the registered range.

The 57% rho base extrapolates from enterprise-wide AI adoption and a top-tier consultancy experiment to five-year avoided-labor implementation in smaller firms. The bridge states the relevant confidentiality, workflow, scaling, and firm-size error directions, but no source measures the target construct directly.

The s5 evidence combines broad employer-owner intentions with announced professional-services deal volume, neither of which measures completed control transfers in the stated NAICS and EBITDA band. The record clearly discounts and labels that transformation as a proxy, and F's registered range remains above 4.

The frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio of 1.3991 is economically anomalous and caps H at 10 in the base case; the packet and memo disclose the vintage mismatch and harmonization, and methodology holds this dataset anchor fixed outside the research-input envelope.

sources_audited.note: The Census page defines 541618 as other management consulting, lists the four excluded consulting categories, and expressly includes telecommunications and utilities management consulting. It supports the industry lens, while the transferability value remains openly classified as an estimate.

The May 2023 OEWS page reports the cited broader-541600 shares: management occupations 16.21%, management analysts 11.91%, project management specialists 3.55%, computer occupations 9.56%, and office and administrative support 14.98%. The packet explicitly discloses that this is a broader-industry proxy rather than a six-digit task measure.

BLS reports 9% projected management-analyst employment growth for 2024-2034, describes the cited interview, observation, analysis, recommendation, presentation, and manager-conference duties, and says consulting demand should grow as organizations pursue efficiency and cost control. The occupation-to-industry and employment-to-output transformations are disclosed as proxy limitations.

The Organization Science article reports a randomized experiment involving 758 BCG consultants, 12.2% more completed inside-frontier tasks, 25.1% faster completion, and a 19% lower probability of a correct solution on the outside-frontier task. The packet appropriately limits this to a proxy for smaller consulting firms and broader workflows.

McKinsey reports 88% regular AI use in at least one function and defines AI high performers, about 6% of respondents, by significant value and at least 5% EBIT impact; it also reports that most organizations remain in pilot or experimentation stages. The packet discloses the survey's mismatch to LMM consulting implementation and uses it only as a proxy.

The cited PDF supports the stated pricing mix (37% project, 26% value-based, 21% hourly, 13% retainer, 3% performance-based) and the statement that over 80% lowered fees to win a client. It does not contain the separate claim used in the e rationale that high-performing firms earn 70% of revenue from existing clients. That statistic appears on a different Consulting Success webpage, not the cited PDF.

Gallup reports a 1,264-owner-worker survey and, for employer owners' five-year plans, 13% planning to sell, 11% to transfer, and a 22% sell/transfer/public summary; it also reports Census data that 52.3% of employer businesses are owned by people 55 or older. The packet openly discounts plans, gifts, and the broad population when estimating completed qualifying transfers.

FOCUS reports 466 announced US professional-services transactions in the first nine months of 2025 and that consulting plus accounting represented more than 50% of sector deal volume. The packet correctly describes these as announced, broad-sector transactions and not completed qualifying 541618 transfers.

a: The injected labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a harmonization factor, so the combined labor opportunity is sensitive to that stated vintage mismatch.

rho: Self-reported productivity can diverge from measured productivity, as the METR experiment demonstrates.
rho: Customer-specific data restrictions and mature legacy codebases may constrain implementation more than greenfield work.

e: Project work can be repeatable at the firm level even when individual customer contracts are not recurring, making classification judgment material.

d5: Constant-quality measurement is difficult when software capability, development speed, and delivered scope change rapidly.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business and government customers purchase custom projects, agile development capacity, dedicated teams, enhancements, maintenance, and support under time-and-materials, labor-hour, fixed-price, milestone, retainer, or managed-capacity arrangements.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal development teams, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, firms outside the approximate $1-10M normalized EBITDA band, packaged-software publishers, pure product or SaaS companies, and businesses primarily classified as systems integration, facilities management, hosting, or temporary staffing rather than custom programming.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced custom software design, development, modification, testing, integration, maintenance, and support to external customers.

a.bridge: The exact-industry occupation mix identifies where wages sit but does not measure task exposure, while controlled AI studies and developer surveys measure selected coding tasks or global developers rather than the wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task share in LMM 541511 firms. The range applies the greatest exposure to coding, testing, debugging, documentation, research, and routine support; moderate exposure to analysis, project administration, sales, and management; and lower exposure to client discovery, architecture, security judgment, deployment accountability, and relationship work. Coding experiments can bias exposure upward when tasks are greenfield and bounded, while mature-repository results and high current adoption can bias the remaining opportunity downward because some easy gains are already captured.
caveats: The occupation shares are employment-weighted; wage weighting is inferred because public exact-industry wage detail is incomplete on the fetched page.
Developer surveys are global and self-selected, and controlled experiments cover narrower tasks than client software delivery.
The injected labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a harmonization factor, so the combined labor opportunity is sensitive to that stated vintage mismatch.
a.change_evidence: A representative time-and-wage study of 541511 firms that maps current workflow steps by occupation, current AI automation, billability, complexity, and verified labor hours avoided would materially change this range.
a.rationale: Computer and mathematical occupations represent 56% of exact-industry employment, including 27% software developers and programmers, 10% computer and information analysts, 8% support specialists, and 7% database and systems administrators or network architects; management, business and financial, office support, and sales add another 36%. O*NET shows that developer work combines code and testing with requirements analysis, design, coordination, support, and consequential judgment. A narrow controlled task was completed 55.8% faster with Copilot, but experienced maintainers on their own repositories were 19% slower with early-2025 tools. The base therefore recognizes broad task exposure without equating code generation with whole-job substitution.

d5.bridge: BLS projects constant-dollar output for the broader computer systems design and related services industry, NAICS 541500, rather than exact six-digit 541511. Converting its 2024-to-2034 output ratio of 1,096.2/799.0 to a five-year ratio yields about 1.17, used as the base. The broader group includes systems design, facilities management, and other computer services; stronger infrastructure and managed-services growth could bias it above custom programming, while new application demand and AI transformation work could make it understate 541511 growth.
caveats: BLS output is available only for broader NAICS 541500, not exact 541511.
Constant-quality measurement is difficult when software capability, development speed, and delivered scope change rapidly.
Demand may diverge sharply across government, healthcare, financial services, industrial, consumer, and small-business customers.
d5.change_evidence: An exact 541511 forecast of real revenue or output by service line, deflated with an industry-appropriate price index and adjusted for changes in software quality and scope, would materially change the range; a panel of bookings, backlog, cancellations, and customer technology budgets would tighten it.
d5.rationale: BLS projects 3.2% annual real output growth and 15.8% employment growth over 2024-2034 for the broader computer systems design and related services industry. Custom programming should benefit from digital modernization, cloud migration, data engineering, cybersecurity, and demand for AI-enabled applications, while weak technology budgets, offshoring, customer insourcing, reusable software, and AI self-service could reduce demand for the current service basket. The range therefore includes slight contraction and substantial expansion around the broader-industry projection.

caveats: No public source directly measures eligibility under the full outsourced-repeat-service and LMM lens.
The injected firm count is an estimate derived from revenue size classes and a broad services margin, so the denominator may include firms outside the actual EBITDA band.
Project work can be repeatable at the firm level even when individual customer contracts are not recurring, making classification judgment material.
e.change_evidence: A stratified audit of the injected firm population using private-company databases and firm websites, followed by interviews or quality-of-earnings data on recurring or repeat customers, revenue by service, owner dependence, normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, and contract assignability, would materially change this estimate.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition itself is customer-specific outsourced programming, and the industry product mix spans custom application, website, database, software customization, technical support, and related development services. Most genuine LMM firms should therefore fit the external-service lens. The range excludes one-off or owner-dependent shops without repeat delivery systems, staffing-heavy misclassifications, captive entities, product businesses classified imperfectly, firms with nontransferable customer relationships, and firms placed in the EBITDA band incorrectly by the dataset's margin bridge.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541511 ($192,866,921,104) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541511 ($305,260,086,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: No public study directly measures the five-year share of custom-programming demand that still requires an external operating firm.
AI capability may advance nonlinearly, particularly for bounded greenfield applications, while legacy integration and accountability may remain stubborn.
This construct distinguishes operator displacement from labor efficiency; the same AI tools can raise productivity without eliminating the service provider.
o.change_evidence: A representative customer cohort measuring custom-development spend displaced by internal AI teams, low-code platforms, packaged software, or autonomous agents, together with loss reasons from 541511 proposals and renewals, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: A substantial majority of year-five custom-programming quantity should still require an accountable operator to elicit requirements, design architecture, integrate systems, protect data, test behavior, deploy safely, maintain production code, and coordinate with customer teams. O*NET emphasizes analysis, decisions, design, collaboration, support, accuracy, and standards compliance alongside programming. Stack Overflow respondents remain especially resistant to AI-led project planning and deployment or monitoring and widely require human verification. Basic websites, prototypes, routine integrations, simple applications, documentation, and some maintenance can nevertheless be eliminated, self-served with AI or low-code tools, or supplied as packaged software.

caveats: The cited contracting guidance concerns US federal agile procurement and does not measure private-market revenue mix.
No reliable public source quantifies exact-industry contract shares or multi-year repricing behavior.
Offshore competition, customer insourcing, and vendor-specific bargaining power can move retention widely.
q.change_evidence: Revenue-weighted contract data from representative 541511 firms, segmented by fixed-price, milestone, time-and-materials, dedicated-team, retainer, and outcome-based work, paired with post-AI renewal pricing and realized project margins, would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: Custom software is sold under both fixed-price and time-and-materials or labor-hour structures. Government guidance notes that fixed price creates a strong contractor incentive to control cost when requirements are identifiable, whereas uncertain agile work can use time-and-materials or labor-hour contracts. Automation savings are retained most directly during a fixed-price term, but fewer hours reduce revenue under labor-based billing; renewals, competitive bids, rate-card transparency, scope renegotiation, and customer bargaining share gains over five years. A mixed contract portfolio supports retention below one-half in the base case.

rho.bridge: DORA and Stack Overflow measure adoption, reliance, sentiment, and self-reported productivity among global technology professionals, not the five-year implemented share of the exposed opportunity in US LMM custom-programming firms. The estimate treats widespread current use as evidence of technical readiness, then discounts for code review, security, customer confidentiality, tool integration, legacy context, testing, governance, and organizational change. Digitally sophisticated survey respondents may bias readiness upward relative to smaller firms, while rapid tool improvement over five years may make present implementation constraints too pessimistic.
caveats: Adoption and time spent with AI are not the same as realized labor substitution or avoided hiring.
Self-reported productivity can diverge from measured productivity, as the METR experiment demonstrates.
Customer-specific data restrictions and mature legacy codebases may constrain implementation more than greenfield work.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal panel of US 541511 firms reporting production deployment by workflow, accepted AI-generated changes, cycle time, rework, defects, security incidents, utilization, and net labor hours avoided would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: DORA's 2025 survey of nearly 5,000 technology professionals reported 90% AI adoption, 65% moderate-to-heavy reliance, and more than 80% self-reported productivity improvement. Stack Overflow found only 52% reported a positive productivity effect, 46% distrusted AI accuracy, and resistance was greatest for project planning and deployment or monitoring. Since AI is already embedded in core development workflows but dependable production use requires verification and systems change, a majority rather than all of the exposed opportunity is expected to be implemented within five years.

s5.bridge: Capstone's AppDev series covers global software and application-development services targets of all sizes, while Hyde Park Capital covers broader US IT services; neither isolates qualifying control transfers of eligible LMM 541511 firms. The estimate uses their sustained transaction activity and services-target share as evidence of liquidity, then removes software-product targets, managed services, systems integrators, non-US deals, growth financings, large sellers, non-control transactions, and ineligible firms. Broader category inclusion biases the raw incidence upward, while private small-company deals missing from announced databases bias it downward.
caveats: The transaction taxonomies are materially broader than six-digit NAICS 541511 and include firms outside the target EBITDA range.
Published deal databases undercount confidential small transactions but include financings or targets that do not meet the control-transfer definition.
The injected firm denominator is itself an EBITDA-band estimate rather than an observed eligible-firm census.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year census of announced and private US 541511 transactions matched to unique sellers, with seller EBITDA, service-versus-product revenue, transaction structure, control status, and repeat ownership transfers, would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Capstone counted 238 AppDev transactions year-to-date in 2025, with services representing 59.2% of targets and private strategic or sponsor-backed buyers prominent. Hyde Park Capital reported 734 US IT-services deals in 2024, still above pre-pandemic 2019 volume, and identified software development capabilities as an active acquisition theme. Relative to the injected LMM population, these broad deal counts support a meaningful but minority five-year probability of a qualifying transfer.

records.principal_driver: A majority-computer occupation mix and rapidly improving coding agents create broad scope for faster delivery and avoided technical hiring.
records.principal_weakness: Labor-based billing and intense global competition can pass automation gains to customers before the operator retains them.

caveats: S2 displays the cited exact-industry occupation and service-mix figures, but the accessible commercial page does not disclose the underlying data vintage or estimation methodology; it cannot validate wage-weighted task exposure.

The a and rho bases extrapolate from global developer surveys and bounded or selected-repository experiments to five-year labor opportunity in US LMM delivery firms; the PROXY labels, bridges, and wide ranges disclose this limitation.

S8 and S9 substantiate broad transaction activity but not the 16% five-year probability of an eligible 541511 control transfer; s5 remains a disclosed broad-category proxy whose exact mapping is unobserved.
S10 establishes federal agile contract mechanics, not private-market revenue mix or multi-year repricing, so q is an honest but weakly anchored estimate.
S11 supports the d5 arithmetic only for broader NAICS 541500; exact-industry growth could diverge because the broader category includes systems design and other computer services.

Eligibility e and operator-required share o have no direct representative measurement. Their ESTIMATE treatment and change-evidence statements are appropriate, but the record remains model-conditioned.

sources_audited.note: The Census page directly defines 541511 as writing, modifying, testing, and supporting software for a particular customer and distinguishes packaged software and systems design.
The page directly displays the cited exact-industry occupation shares and the listed custom-application, website, database, integration, and support service lines.

O*NET directly documents requirements analysis, testing, programming, documentation, design, coordination, customer consultation, decision-making, accuracy, and standards compliance for software developers.
The Microsoft Research page directly reports the controlled JavaScript HTTP-server experiment and a 55.8% faster completion time for the Copilot treatment group.
The 2025 survey directly reports a 52% positive productivity effect, 46% distrust of AI accuracy, and the strongest resistance for project planning and deployment or monitoring.
METR directly reports the early-2025 19% longer task time in its raw results and explains later-study uncertainty, participation selection, and changing tool adoption.

Google's DORA summary directly reports nearly 5,000 global respondents, 90% adoption, 65% moderate-to-heavy reliance, over 80% self-reported productivity improvement, and organizational prerequisites.

Capstone directly supports 238 YTD 2025 AppDev deals, the 59.2% services share, and the buyer mix, but its global all-size AppDev taxonomy does not directly measure eligible US LMM 541511 control transfers or five-year demand.

The report directly shows 734 US IT-services deals in 2024 and software-development acquisition themes, but the category is much broader than exact-industry eligible control transfers and much of its demand discussion concerns managed IT, cloud, and systems integration.

TechFAR directly supports the stated incentives and applicability of firm-fixed-price, time-and-materials, and labor-hour structures for federal agile contracts, but it does not establish private-market contract mix, repricing, or five-year gain retention.
BLS directly reports 2024-2034 employment growth of 15.8% and constant-dollar output rising from 799.0 to 1,096.2 at 3.2% annually for the explicitly disclosed broader NAICS 541500 population.

caveats: a: OEWS is for NAICS 541200, includes establishments outside CPA offices, excludes self-employed owners, and is May 2023 vintage.
a: The exposure paper measures technical time-saving potential on occupation tasks, not wage-weighted firm labor, deployment feasibility, or labor removal.
a: Small firms may already automate portions of bookkeeping and tax workflow, reducing the remaining opportunity relative to gross task exposure.
rho: The Thomson Reuters samples cover broader tax, audit, and accounting populations, multiple countries, and firms outside the EBITDA lens.
rho: Reported adoption does not establish sustained production use or monetizable labor avoidance.
rho: The five-year horizon includes substantial uncertainty in model reliability, regulation, professional standards, and software integration.
e: No source measures the eligible share of the frozen LMM firm population directly.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firms near the EBITDA boundaries.
e: State ownership, independence, and firm-licensure rules vary, and attest work may require a split alternative-practice structure.
s5: The succession survey is from 2016, covers firms of various sizes, and reports discussions rather than closings.
s5: Published deal counts are incomplete, may include non-control deals, and lack a consistent eligible-firm denominator.
s5: High transaction activity among larger firms and PE platforms may not represent the frozen LMM population.
q: Survey respondents can offer several pricing models, so the percentages are not revenue shares and cannot be summed.
q: The pricing survey covers tax and accounting firms beyond NAICS 541211 and the LMM lens.
q: Observed fee growth includes inflation, demand, scope, and labor scarcity rather than isolated retention of AI benefits.
d5: BLS occupation employment is not six-digit industry service demand and covers a ten-year horizon.
d5: AICPA revenue growth is nominal and mixes price, scope, acquisitions, and quantity.
d5: IRS filing statistics cover individual returns only and professional preparers include non-CPA firms.
o: Legal responsibility varies by service: tax preparation, bookkeeping, advisory, review, and audit have different operator requirements.
o: The GAO statistic covers all paid individual-return preparers, not CPA firms or business clients.
o: Future standards may permit greater machine execution while retaining only a thin layer of licensed sign-off.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring annual tax and assurance engagements and repeat advisory projects for businesses, nonprofits, and individuals, with a mix of hourly or time-based, flat-fee, project, value-based, and retainer billing.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive accounting departments, non-CPA bookkeeping or stand-alone tax-preparation establishments classified outside NAICS 541211, shell firms, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the specified EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US CPA firms in the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band that provide recurring or repeat audit and assurance, tax compliance and advisory, financial-statement preparation, client accounting, and related accounting advice to external customers.

a.bridge: BLS OEWS measures the adjacent four-digit NAICS 541200 workforce rather than six-digit CPA offices: accountants and auditors are 31.73% of employment, tax preparers 7.13%, office and administrative support 28.76%, and management 10.54%. The Eloundou task study rates accountants and auditors at 100% exposure under its most expansive GPT-powered-software rubric, where exposure means at least a 50% task-time reduction, not job replacement. CPA.com identifies concrete exposure in bookkeeping, tax preparation, audit analysis, advisory modeling, and back-office workflows. I translate those occupation and task signals into a much lower wage-weighted share of current, not-yet-automated work because professional judgment, client interaction, final review, and already-automated work are not substitutable opportunity. The four-digit mix likely has more clerical and payroll work than CPA offices, which may overstate low-complexity exposure, while the technical exposure rubric likely overstates feasible substitution.
caveats: OEWS is for NAICS 541200, includes establishments outside CPA offices, excludes self-employed owners, and is May 2023 vintage.
The exposure paper measures technical time-saving potential on occupation tasks, not wage-weighted firm labor, deployment feasibility, or labor removal.
Small firms may already automate portions of bookkeeping and tax workflow, reducing the remaining opportunity relative to gross task exposure.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit NAICS 541211 establishment-level occupation-and-payroll extract combined with time studies from 20-30 LMM CPA firms that tag hours by workflow, current automation status, reviewer level, and AI substitutability would materially narrow the range.
a.rationale: The occupation mix places most payroll dollars in professional, managerial, tax, and administrative knowledge work. Document intake, data extraction, classification, reconciliation, research, first-draft returns, sampling, summarization, correspondence, and workflow coordination are materially exposed. Partner judgment, attest conclusions, complex tax positions, client development, supervision, and accountability remain less substitutable, so the estimate is far below the headline technical-exposure measure.

d5.bridge: BLS projects accountants-and-auditors employment across all industries to grow 5% from 2024 to 2034, while saying complex tax and regulation support demand and automation should shift duties rather than reduce overall demand. AICPA's mostly small-firm MAP sample reported 6.7% nominal net-client-fee growth in 2024, and IRS data show professionally filed individual returns rose 1.7% between comparable 2024 and 2025 filing-season dates. I use these as directional indicators, not a direct constant-price, constant-quality quantity measure, and set five-year demand near modest growth after excluding price and quality expansion. Employment and nominal revenue can overstate service quantity when productivity or prices rise; tax-return counts omit assurance and advisory.
caveats: BLS occupation employment is not six-digit industry service demand and covers a ten-year horizon.
AICPA revenue growth is nominal and mixes price, scope, acquisitions, and quantity.
IRS filing statistics cover individual returns only and professional preparers include non-CPA firms.
d5.change_evidence: BLS or BEA five-year real-output projections for six-digit NAICS 541211, supplemented by service-line unit volumes for audit reports, business and individual returns, and recurring CAS engagements from an LMM firm panel, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: The current basket is supported by recurring filing, assurance, reporting, and regulatory obligations, business formation and economic activity, and rising complexity. Basic compliance faces self-service and software substitution, but assurance, complex tax, and client accounting demand should offset much of it. The midpoint therefore represents modest real quantity growth rather than extrapolating recent nominal fee gains.

caveats: No source measures the eligible share of the frozen LMM firm population directly.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firms near the EBITDA boundaries.
State ownership, independence, and firm-licensure rules vary, and attest work may require a split alternative-practice structure.
e.change_evidence: A representative broker or transaction database for $1-10M EBITDA CPA firms showing screened opportunities, rejection reasons, revenue recurrence, client concentration, quality-control findings, partner dependence, and state ownership constraints would materially change the range.
e.rationale: Most firms counted in this NAICS and EBITDA band serve external clients through repeat tax, assurance, or accounting relationships, and the sector is described as fragmented with stable, recurring revenue. Eligibility is reduced for owner-dependent books, weakly documented practices, unacceptable client concentration or quality risk, independence conflicts, and firms whose licensed attest ownership cannot be transferred in a compliant control structure. Alternative practice structures make many otherwise constrained firms transferable, but not frictionlessly so.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $68,045.5M / sectoral output (T30) $134,726.2M, year 2022

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $263,807 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541211); class <5: 35,096 firms, avg revenue/firm $300,013 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $46,952 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 9,158 firms, avg revenue/firm $944,405 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $147,799 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 4,257 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,000,227 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $313,036 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,015 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,602,856 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,033,347 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 271 firms, avg revenue/firm $37,129,554 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,810,775 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 88 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,137,349,114 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $177,995,136 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 2,286

o.bridge: BLS expects routine-task automation to raise efficiency and make advisory and analytical duties more prominent without reducing overall accountant demand, while NASBA documents that attest reports remain under licensed-CPA control and GAO reports that more than half of individual taxpayers used a paid preparer in fiscal 2024. These sources measure employment expectations, legal accountability, and current tax-preparer use rather than the exact year-five share of CPA-firm service quantity requiring an accountable operator. I reduce the share for straightforward returns, bookkeeping, data entry, and reporting that can move to self-service or software. Occupational demand can overstate operator-required quantity if software enables fewer professionals to serve more clients; tax self-service can understate the operator requirement for complex business work.
caveats: Legal responsibility varies by service: tax preparation, bookkeeping, advisory, review, and audit have different operator requirements.
The GAO statistic covers all paid individual-return preparers, not CPA firms or business clients.
Future standards may permit greater machine execution while retaining only a thin layer of licensed sign-off.
o.change_evidence: Service-line client surveys and engagement records tracking migrations from CPA firms to self-preparation or software, together with state-board and AICPA changes to attest-signature, supervision, and AI-accountability standards, would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: A high share of the current basket should still require a CPA-firm operator because attest opinions, independence, professional judgment, signed tax positions, quality control, exception handling, and client accountability cannot be supplied entirely by unaccountable software. Operator need is weaker for simple individual tax, routine bookkeeping, document processing, and standardized financial reporting, so the estimate is below full retention.

q.bridge: The 2024 Thomson Reuters tax-professional survey measures offered pricing models, not discounted benefit retention: 66% of respondents' firms use hourly or time-based pricing, 62% offer flat fees, 48% offer value-based pricing, and 29% offer retainers, with overlapping responses. AICPA's 2025 MAP survey shows that mostly small firms were able to grow net client fees, but it does not isolate AI savings. I infer that hourly work initially leaks efficiency through fewer billable hours unless capacity is redeployed, whereas fixed, project, retainer, and value pricing retain more until renewal repricing and competition. Overlap among pricing modes and client pressure make the direction uncertain; using model availability as revenue weights would overstate precision.
caveats: Survey respondents can offer several pricing models, so the percentages are not revenue shares and cannot be summed.
The pricing survey covers tax and accounting firms beyond NAICS 541211 and the LMM lens.
Observed fee growth includes inflation, demand, scope, and labor scarcity rather than isolated retention of AI benefits.
q.change_evidence: Cohort-level engagement data from LMM CPA firms comparing pre- and post-AI hours, realized fees, renewal repricing, win-loss rates, client churn, and contribution margin by billing model over at least three renewal cycles would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: The mix of billing models gives operators a real but partial path to retain implemented savings. Recurring fixed fees and value pricing can preserve margin, and constrained labor capacity can be redeployed into more clients or advisory work. Retention is eroded as clients expect faster delivery, hourly engagements generate fewer billable hours, annual renewals reset fees, commoditized tax work faces software and peer competition, and sellers or employees may capture part of the benefit.

rho.bridge: Thomson Reuters surveys tax, audit, and accounting firms rather than measuring the fraction of exposed wage opportunity implemented. Enterprise GenAI use rose from 8% in 2024 to 21% in 2025, but only about one in seven firms reported a comprehensive AI strategy; CPA.com reports rapid deployment in bookkeeping and tax but methodical audit adoption because of regulatory and liability complexity. I map current adoption, strategic readiness, and workflow-specific maturity to five-year implementation of exposed work. Survey optimism may overstate implementation, while current enterprise-use measures may understate five-year diffusion.
caveats: The Thomson Reuters samples cover broader tax, audit, and accounting populations, multiple countries, and firms outside the EBITDA lens.
Reported adoption does not establish sustained production use or monetizable labor avoidance.
The five-year horizon includes substantial uncertainty in model reliability, regulation, professional standards, and software integration.
rho.change_evidence: Annual AICPA PCPS MAP or CPA.com panel data linking firm size to production hours, AI-enabled workflow penetration, realized time savings, error or rework rates, and headcount avoided by service line would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: Five years is enough for embedded tax, document, research, bookkeeping, and workflow tools to diffuse across a meaningful share of firms, especially under staffing pressure. Implementation will remain incomplete because client-data security, fragmented practice systems, review controls, hallucination risk, audit evidence requirements, change management, and partner accountability slow conversion from technical exposure to avoided labor or avoided hiring.

s5.bridge: An AICPA PCPS survey of roughly 850 responding CPA firms found 49% of multiowner-firm leaders had been in active merger discussions during the prior 24 months, but discussions are not completed qualifying control transfers and the 2016 sample is old and not limited to the LMM band. More recent market sources document 115 CPA-firm deals in 2024 and record sector equity-financing volume, confirming active consolidation without providing a denominator. I discount the discussion rate for noncompletion, internal reorganizations, minority financings, repeat add-ons, and non-LMM firms, then allow five years for retirement and succession events. The old survey may understate today's transaction appetite, while deal lists may overstate unique qualifying transfers.
caveats: The succession survey is from 2016, covers firms of various sizes, and reports discussions rather than closings.
Published deal counts are incomplete, may include non-control deals, and lack a consistent eligible-firm denominator.
High transaction activity among larger firms and PE platforms may not represent the frozen LMM population.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed US CPA-firm control transactions matched to a census of eligible $1-10M EBITDA firms, with seller age, transaction type, ownership percentage, and internal-versus-external succession flags, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Aging owners, weak internal succession benches, and active strategic and sponsor-backed consolidation support a meaningful five-year transfer probability. However, many firms will execute internal partner buyouts, delay retirement, take minority capital, remain too owner-dependent to sell, or enter discussions that never close.

records.principal_driver: A labor-intensive base of repeat compliance and advisory work contains substantial document, research, preparation, and workflow effort that can be standardized and AI-assisted.
records.principal_weakness: Mixed hourly pricing and licensed-quality constraints create a wide gap between technical task exposure and durable owner-retained economics.

caveats: The a base is an honest but judgment-heavy translation from four-digit employment shares, a technical task-exposure rubric, and workflow examples; no source measures the wage-weighted share of current, not-yet-automated work in six-digit LMM CPA firms.

The rho range maps current adoption and strategy indicators to a five-year implemented share without longitudinal evidence on production penetration, realized hours saved, rework, or avoided headcount in the target firm band.

The e estimate is not directly measured: the cited sources establish ownership constraints, alternative practice structures, fragmentation, and recurring revenue, but not the share of $1-10 million EBITDA firms that pass target-specific eligibility screens.

The s5 proxy combines an old survey of merger discussions with broad recent deal compilations that lack a qualifying-control definition and eligible-firm denominator; the resulting five-year transfer probability remains weakly identified.
The q estimate is inferred from overlapping pricing-model offerings and nominal fee growth, neither of which measures discounted owner retention of AI benefits by engagement or revenue mix.

The d5 estimate relies on occupation employment, nominal firm revenue, and individual-return filing counts rather than a constant-price, constant-quality quantity measure for the full CPA-firm service basket.

The o estimate extrapolates from attest-control rules, accountant employment expectations, and all-paid-preparer usage to the year-five operator-required share across heterogeneous CPA services; none of the sources directly measures that share.

sources_audited.note: BLS May 2023 NAICS 541200 table reports accountants and auditors at 31.73%, tax preparers at 7.13%, management at 10.54%, and office and administrative support at 28.76% of employment; it is a disclosed four-digit, employment-mix proxy rather than six-digit wage-weighted task evidence.

The paper's expansive model-zeta measure lists accountants and auditors at 100.0 and defines exposure as at least a 50% task-time reduction with LLM-powered software, while explicitly warning that this does not imply full task automation.

The report describes agentic bookkeeping, near-full tax-preparation automation subject to final review, methodical audit adoption due to regulatory and liability complexity, advisory modeling, and back-office workflow tools; it is directional industry evidence rather than a representative LMM outcome study.
The page states that enterprise-level GenAI use among tax, audit, and accounting firms increased from 8% in 2024 to 21% in 2025.
The article reports that one in seven respondents had a comprehensive AI strategy and identifies 462-plus responses across 25 countries, about half from US firms.

BLS projects 5% accountant-and-auditor employment growth from 2024 to 2034, cites economic and regulatory demand drivers, and says routine-task automation should raise efficiency and shift duties toward advisory and analytical work rather than reduce overall demand.

The NASBA paper states that most states require majority licensed-CPA equity and voting rights, at least 44 jurisdictions require active individual non-CPA owners, and alternative practice structures keep attest services under licensed-CPA control and oversight.

S&P describes a fragmented accounting, auditing, and taxation sector with stable customer bases and predictable recurring revenues and reports 24 global PE/VC-backed deals in the first 11 months of 2024; the deal evidence is broader than US LMM CPA firms.

The 2016 article reports roughly 850 survey respondents, 49% of multiowner-firm leaders in active merger discussions during the prior 24 months, one-fourth with current succession challenges, and another 48% expecting them within five years.
The compiled market article reports upwards of 115 CPA-firm deals in 2024; it does not provide a qualifying-control definition or an eligible-firm denominator.

The 2024 report states that 66% of respondents' firms offer hourly or time-based pricing, 62% flat fees, 48% value-based pricing, and 29% retainers; these are overlapping firm-level offerings, not revenue shares or measured AI-benefit retention.

AICPA reports that 81% of responses came from firms with revenue of $5 million or below, median net client fees grew 6.7%, and revenue growth occurred in audit and assurance, tax, and client accounting advisory services.
The IRS/National Taxpayer Advocate table shows professionally filed individual returns rising from 71.3 million in 2024 to 72.5 million in 2025, a 1.7% increase.

GAO states that more than half of individual taxpayers used a paid preparer in fiscal 2024 and discusses credentials, errors, and oversight; this covers all paid preparers rather than CPA firms or business-client services.

sources.supports: Adjacent-industry occupation mix and wages: accountants and auditors 31.73% of employment, tax preparers 7.13%, management 10.54%, and office and administrative support 28.76%.
