records.title: Research and Development in Biotechnology (except Nanobiotechnology)

caveats: a: The available occupation profile is for NAICS 5415 and includes custom programming, facilities management, and other computer services.
a: Employment shares are not wage shares; published occupation wages only approximate the weighting required by this primitive.
a: Observed AI conversation patterns show use, not the share of all paid work that is technically substitutable.
a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts input is six-digit and rated medium evidence, but its wage and receipts vintages differ and its harmonization factor adds measurement error.
rho: No source measures five-year implementation of exposed labor specifically for NAICS 541512.
rho: Product telemetry is selected toward willing users and does not establish net productivity or headcount effects.
rho: Firm-level AI adoption can remain confined to marketing or internal research rather than billable delivery.
rho: Implementation will vary with customer regulation, data sensitivity, platform maturity, codebase quality, and contract terms.
e: NAICS classification does not reveal repeat revenue, owner dependence, or whether a firm's delivery organization is transferable.
e: The Census survey lists products associated with the broader industry form and does not publish the six-digit revenue mix on that page.
e: The frozen LMM firm count is margin-bridged from size classes rather than directly observed, so the eligible-firm count inherits margin, firm-size, and classification error.
s5: Broad owner age does not measure intent, timing, transferable value, or a qualifying control transfer.
s5: The M&A evidence is global, spans IT services beyond NAICS 541512, and emphasizes large transactions.
s5: Many small technology-services acquisitions are private and unannounced, while headline activity lacks an eligible-firm denominator.
q: A single large consultancy may not represent LMM systems integrators or their service mix.
q: Revenue-recognition categories do not measure customer sharing or the discounted share of savings ultimately retained.
q: Hardware and software resale margins, vendor rebates, and managed support can change retention but are not isolated here.
q: This primitive addresses price and benefit sharing only; demand growth and self-service are handled separately.
d5: The BLS projections are four-digit and cannot isolate computer systems design from adjacent computer services.
d5: Real industry output is not a direct constant-quality quantity measure and may reflect mix shifts toward higher-value services.
d5: Demand is cyclical with enterprise technology budgets and can be delayed by financing conditions, platform transitions, or customer uncertainty.
o: No source directly forecasts the operator-required share for NAICS 541512.
o: Operator need varies sharply between standardized cloud deployments and complex legacy, security-sensitive, or regulated integrations.
o: Some demand may shift to software vendors, hyperscalers, offshore providers, or customer employees rather than remain with lens firms.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers are businesses, nonprofits, and government agencies buying expertise to assess needs and integrate hardware, software, networks, security, and data. Revenue commonly combines time-and-materials professional services, fixed-fee projects, milestone or unit pricing, hardware and software pass-through, and smaller support or maintenance streams; repeat work follows platform upgrades, integrations, migrations, security changes, and continuing customer roadmaps.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal IT units, pure hardware or packaged-software resellers, software publishers, firms principally classified as custom programmers or facilities managers, temporary staffing businesses, shells, one-off practices without repeat outsourced service, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms principally providing outsourced computer systems planning, architecture, configuration, network design, systems integration, implementation, installation, user training, and related support to external customers through recurring engagements or repeat project work.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations and wages for four-digit NAICS 5415 rather than six-digit NAICS 541512, and Anthropic measures selected interactions with one AI vendor rather than wage-weighted task exposure. The bridge uses the BLS occupation mix and published occupation wages to emphasize costly computer, management, and business work, then maps documented duties to current AI-suitable work such as code generation, testing, documentation, research, support triage, data transformation, and project administration. Inclusion of custom programming in NAICS 5415 likely overstates coding exposure for systems design, while omission of firm-specific administrative workflows may understate exposure.
caveats: The available occupation profile is for NAICS 5415 and includes custom programming, facilities management, and other computer services.
Employment shares are not wage shares; published occupation wages only approximate the weighting required by this primitive.
Observed AI conversation patterns show use, not the share of all paid work that is technically substitutable.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input is six-digit and rated medium evidence, but its wage and receipts vintages differ and its harmonization factor adds measurement error.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time study across independent systems integrators should map current hours by occupation and task, distinguish already automated work, and test representative requirements, configuration, migration, coding, testing, documentation, support, and project-management tasks with production AI tools.
a.rationale: Computer and mathematical occupations account for 50.57 percent of employment in the broader industry, including software developers at 21.62 percent, computer support at 7.51 percent, and systems analysts at 4.62 percent; management and business occupations add substantial high-wage labor (S3). BLS duties show automatable artifacts in requirements analysis, diagrams, configuration, code, tests, defect reports, documentation, research, and user materials, alongside harder customer consultation, architecture, security judgment, coordination, and deployment accountability (S4, S5). Anthropic found coding use highly concentrated and classified 79 percent of sampled coding-agent interactions as automation, but its population is self-selected (S7). The range excludes work already automated and preserves a large human share for customer context, architecture tradeoffs, stakeholder alignment, physical installation, and production responsibility.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output and employment for four-digit NAICS 5415, not constant-quality demand for the current six-digit NAICS 541512 service basket. Applying the published 3.2 percent annual real-output growth rate for five years yields approximately 1.17, while the range allows systems design to lag or exceed its broader industry. Broader NAICS coverage can overstate growth if custom programming and other computer services grow faster, while rapid integration demand from AI, cloud, and cybersecurity can make the four-digit average conservative for systems design.
caveats: The BLS projections are four-digit and cannot isolate computer systems design from adjacent computer services.
Real industry output is not a direct constant-quality quantity measure and may reflect mix shifts toward higher-value services.
Demand is cyclical with enterprise technology budgets and can be delayed by financing conditions, platform transitions, or customer uncertainty.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted receipts and project-volume data for NAICS 541512, split by integration, advisory, implementation, security, network, and resale work, together with backlog, bookings, customer IT budgets, and outsourcing rates, would replace the four-digit proxy.
d5.rationale: BLS projects NAICS 5415 output to rise at a 3.2 percent compound annual rate in chained dollars and places the industry among the fastest-growing output categories (S10). It separately projects 15.8 percent employment growth for computer systems design and related services and attributes demand to IT infrastructure, custom solutions, cybersecurity, cloud, and AI implementation (S9). Systems analysts are also expected to be hired as organizations expand IT, and BLS notes that small firms may contract with outside providers rather than hire directly (S4). These signals support strong real demand growth despite technology cycles and periodic project deferrals.

caveats: NAICS classification does not reveal repeat revenue, owner dependence, or whether a firm's delivery organization is transferable.
The Census survey lists products associated with the broader industry form and does not publish the six-digit revenue mix on that page.
The frozen LMM firm count is margin-bridged from size classes rather than directly observed, so the eligible-firm count inherits margin, firm-size, and classification error.
e.change_evidence: A six-digit firm roster enriched with independent ownership, service-line revenue, repeat-customer revenue, hardware and software pass-through, staffing exposure, customer concentration, and normalized EBITDA would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: The six-digit definition is inherently an external expertise and integration service, and Census distinguishes it from custom programming, facilities management, software publishing, and pure resale (S1, S2). The survey form identifies business and government customer classes and service outputs spanning packaged-software integration, databases, networks, security, system design, development, and integration (S2). Most scaled independent firms should therefore satisfy the outsourced-service lens, with exclusions for hardware-led resellers, captive support units, staffing-heavy firms, nonrepeat project shops, and businesses whose economics belong in adjacent codes.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541512 ($168,217,056,007) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541512 ($278,318,454,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $341,141 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541512); class <5: 37,887 firms, avg revenue/firm $394,508 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $61,740 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 4,632 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,847,390 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $289,117 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 2,780 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,880,864 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $607,355 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,795 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,906,109 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,863,306 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 729 firms, avg revenue/firm $51,131,923 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,002,146 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 429 firms, avg revenue/firm $404,366,175 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $63,283,306 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 3,524

caveats: No source directly forecasts the operator-required share for NAICS 541512.
Operator need varies sharply between standardized cloud deployments and complex legacy, security-sensitive, or regulated integrations.
Some demand may shift to software vendors, hyperscalers, offshore providers, or customer employees rather than remain with lens firms.
o.change_evidence: Interviews with CIOs, IT directors, procurement leaders, platform vendors, and systems integrators should quantify which current deliverables will be self-served, purchased as software, moved to hyperscalers, retained in-house, or still require an independent accountable integrator at year five.
o.rationale: Customer-specific systems still require an accountable party to elicit requirements, choose architecture, configure and connect platforms, migrate data, validate security and performance, manage cutover, train users, and resolve failures. Census defines the industry around integrated systems and notes that providers often install systems and train or support users, while BLS describes systems analysts as configuring, overseeing installation, testing, and translating between managers and technical teams (S1, S4). AI, low-code tools, packaged connectors, vendor professional services, and in-house technical teams can eliminate simpler engagements, but they are less likely to remove accountability for complex legacy, regulated, or mission-critical integrations.

q.bridge: Perficient is a large public U.S. IT consultancy rather than a lower-middle-market NAICS 541512 firm, but its contract disclosures directly illustrate adjacent professional-services billing. Its 2023 time-and-materials revenue and fixed-fee revenue are used to anchor a mixed billing model, then lower retention is assigned to hourly work and higher initial retention to fixed-fee work before allowing for renewals and competition. The proxy may understate retention if smaller integrators use more fixed-scope or value pricing and hardware margins, or overstate it if they function mainly as hourly subcontractors with concentrated customers.
caveats: A single large consultancy may not represent LMM systems integrators or their service mix.
Revenue-recognition categories do not measure customer sharing or the discounted share of savings ultimately retained.
Hardware and software resale margins, vendor rebates, and managed support can change retention but are not isolated here.
This primitive addresses price and benefit sharing only; demand growth and self-service are handled separately.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract sample should measure revenue and gross profit by time-and-materials, fixed-fee, milestone, unit, managed-service, and resale terms, then compare realized hours, scope expansion, utilization, renewal pricing, win rates, and customer concessions after automation.
q.rationale: Perficient disclosed that most projects were time-and-materials and reported 668.7 million dollars of that revenue against 896.7 million dollars of professional-services fees in 2023; fixed-fee and fixed-fee-percent-complete work made up most of the remainder (S13). Hourly contracts pass faster completion to the client through fewer billable hours unless the provider redeploys capacity or expands scope. Fixed-fee projects can retain near-term savings, but rebids, rate negotiation, vendor comparisons, offshore competition, and customers' own AI capabilities share the benefit over time. The base case therefore retains well under half of implemented gross benefit.

rho.bridge: The Census adoption study covers U.S. firms across sectors, Anthropic covers users of its own coding products, and METR studies experienced open-source developers rather than lower-middle-market systems-design firms. Current use, interaction-level automation, and developer speed are translated into the share of exposed labor that can be embedded in reliable client delivery within five years. The technically skilled workforce and digital work product imply faster implementation than the all-firm Census average, while vendor selection, security, legacy-system context, quality assurance, and client approval pull the estimate below coding-agent automation rates. Vendor usage data may overstate adoption among ordinary firms; broad Census data may understate this technology-native industry.
caveats: No source measures five-year implementation of exposed labor specifically for NAICS 541512.
Product telemetry is selected toward willing users and does not establish net productivity or headcount effects.
Firm-level AI adoption can remain confined to marketing or internal research rather than billable delivery.
Implementation will vary with customer regulation, data sensitivity, platform maturity, codebase quality, and contract terms.
rho.change_evidence: A matched sample of systems integrators should report deployed tools, seat utilization, task-level hours before and after adoption, rework and defect escape rates, client restrictions, review intensity, training cost, and realized hiring avoidance across completed customer engagements.
rho.rationale: Census found AI use in 18 percent of firms and 32 percent on an employment-weighted basis, but 66 percent of users reported augmentation only and employment decreases were rare, showing that adoption does not equal labor realization (S6). Anthropic coding-agent interactions demonstrate substantial task delegation but much lower enterprise representation than startup representation (S7). METR's early study found a developer slowdown, while its later data suggested improvement but was too selection-biased for a reliable effect estimate (S8). Systems-design firms possess the technical talent to integrate tools quickly, yet client confidentiality, legacy dependencies, testing, cybersecurity, and accountable release processes prevent full implementation.

s5.bridge: Census owner-age evidence covers U.S. employer-business owners broadly, while PwC and LSEG deal evidence covers global IT services transactions and is tilted toward much larger deals. Older ownership is treated as succession pressure, and adjacent IT-services deal activity as evidence that control transfers can clear. Both can overstate qualifying LMM transfers because retirement may lead to closure or internal succession and large firms are more marketable; the broad deal data can understate unannounced small private transactions.
caveats: Broad owner age does not measure intent, timing, transferable value, or a qualifying control transfer.
The M&A evidence is global, spans IT services beyond NAICS 541512, and emphasizes large transactions.
Many small technology-services acquisitions are private and unannounced, while headline activity lacks an eligible-firm denominator.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year database of U.S. systems-design control transactions, failed sale processes, closures, internal successions, and recapitalizations linked to eligible-firm counts, EBITDA, owner age, customer concentration, and recurring revenue would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Census reports that more than half of responding owners of employer businesses were at least age 55, establishing broad succession pressure but not sale intent (S11). PwC reports that IT services increased its share of technology deal volume to 18 percent in 2024 and expected continued sector activity, demonstrating buyer interest in the adjacent transaction market (S12). The base case allows for active consolidation and a large potential firm universe but applies a material haircut for internal transfers, closures, owner-dependent delivery, weak documentation, customer concentration, and firms that never enter a sale process.

records.principal_driver: Strong growth in customer-specific cloud, cybersecurity, AI, and legacy-integration demand supports repeat outsourced work while AI can compress several expensive digital delivery tasks.
records.principal_weakness: Time-and-materials pricing and rapid diffusion of the same tools to competitors, customers, software vendors, and offshore providers can pass through much of the productivity benefit.

caveats: The labor-exposure and demand evidence is primarily four-digit NAICS 5415 evidence rather than six-digit 541512 evidence; the record discloses this scope mismatch and gives plausible error directions.

The numeric bases for a and rho remain judgment-heavy transformations of occupation mix, task descriptions, adoption telemetry, and developer studies rather than measured wage-weighted exposure or five-year implementation in systems-design firms.

The s5 base is not observed from an eligible-firm transaction denominator: broad owner ages and global, large-deal IT-services activity establish succession pressure and buyer interest but not a 24% qualifying-transfer probability.
The q base extrapolates from one large public consultancy's billing mix; the cited filing does not measure LMM contract mix or the discounted share of automation benefit ultimately retained.

The e and o inputs are explicit estimates without a six-digit firm roster or customer forecast; their uncertainty is appropriately reflected in MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

sources_audited.note: The archived Census NAICS page gives the cited 541512 definition, including integrated hardware, software, and communications systems and the installation, training, and support language.

The 2022 Economic Census form distinguishes systems integration and systems design from adjacent activities and lists the cited integration, database, network, security, design, and support products; it is a broader 54150 form, as disclosed.

The BLS 5415 table reports the cited occupation shares and wages, including 50.57% computer and mathematical employment, 21.62% software developers, 7.51% support specialists, and 4.62% systems analysts.
BLS documents consultation, technology research, cost-benefit work, configuration, installation, testing, manuals, training, and outside contracting by small firms.
BLS documents requirements analysis, system design, diagrams, documentation, testing, risk identification, defect reporting, security requirements, and collaborative work.
Census reports 18% firm adoption, 32% employment-weighted adoption, augmentation-only use by 66% of adopters, and AI-related employment decreases at only 2% of firms.

Anthropic reports 79% automation-classified Claude Code conversations, heavier startup than enterprise use, and explicit limitations from product coverage, inference, early-adopter selection, and unmeasured output quality.
METR reports the earlier roughly 20% developer slowdown and explains why later apparent speedups are unreliable because of developer and task selection and time-measurement problems.
BLS projects 15.8% employment growth for NAICS 5415 and ties computer-systems-design growth to strong demand for software, cybersecurity, cloud computing, and AI-based systems.
The BLS output table reports NAICS 5415 chained-dollar output rising from 799.0 to 1,096.2 with a 3.2% compound annual growth rate.
The Census page and graphic report that over half of U.S. business owners were age 55 or older and identify the 2019 Annual Business Survey, data year 2018, as the source.

PwC reports IT services' share of technology deal volume increasing from 14% to 18% in 2024 and anticipates increased activity, while also showing that the evidence is global and oriented toward large deals.
Perficient's 2023 10-K reports $668.7 million of time-and-materials revenue and $896.7 million of professional-services fees, with the remainder largely fixed-fee categories.

caveats: a: OEWS is available at NAICS 541600 rather than 541614 and covers employers of all sizes.
a: The experimental tasks were performed by consultants at one large global firm, not LMM logistics boutiques.

a: The frozen labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and therefore may overstate current labor intensity if nominal receipts grew faster; it is also harmonized by a cross-industry median adjustment.
rho: BTOS measures use in any business function, not operational implementation of exposed labor opportunity.
rho: The SBA size cutoff is below 250 employees and is broader than the $1–10M EBITDA lens.
rho: Census found employment decreases in only 2% of AI-using firms, so current adoption evidence does not yet demonstrate broad labor substitution.
e: No source measures the share of 541614 LMM firms meeting every lens condition.
e: The frozen firm count is an EBITDA-margin bridge with ESTIMATE quality; its size-band membership is not observed firm by firm.
s5: Gallup covers all US employer businesses and reports intentions rather than completed transfers.
s5: BizBuySell is a voluntary marketplace concentrated below the full LMM band and does not expose the number of firms at risk.
s5: Professional-services control can change economically without a publicly observable legal transaction.
q: BLS describes compensation modes but not their revenue shares or price elasticity.
q: The five-year discounted retention rate depends on procurement behavior and competitor adoption that are not directly observed.
d5: Employment projections are not forecasts of constant-price, constant-quality outsourced service demand.
d5: Logisticians include in-house workers across manufacturing, government, wholesale, and other sectors.
d5: Trade policy, recession, reshoring, and freight cycles can materially shift consulting projects within five years.

o: The boundary between consulting, implementation, and software subscription may shift as firms productize their methods.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business and public-sector clients buy scoped consulting projects, implementation bundles, and repeat improvement engagements; fees are commonly hourly or project-based and may be competitively bid.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal supply-chain teams, software-only vendors, freight carriers and warehouse operators, stand-alone IT implementation, shell firms, non-control financing vehicles, and practices without a repeat outsourced service model.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms that repeatedly provide outsourced operations and supply-chain advice or implementation covering manufacturing-process improvement, productivity, production planning, quality, inventory, warehousing, distribution, transportation, and materials handling.

a.bridge: Source population: BLS May 2023 employment and wages for the broader NAICS 541600 consulting industry, plus a field experiment with 758 consultants. Transformation: weight the major occupational groups most relevant to 541614—management, business/financial operations, computer/mathematical, and office support—toward exposure in information gathering, data analysis, scenario modeling, drafting, presentations, proposals, and administration, while assigning lower exposure to executive sales, onsite observation, relationship work, and implementation. Direction of error: the broader 541600 mix includes scientific and specialty consultancies unlike 541614 and may understate 541614's share of management analysts and logisticians; the experiment tests selected consulting tasks and may overstate exposure across messy client data and physical-site work.
caveats: OEWS is available at NAICS 541600 rather than 541614 and covers employers of all sizes.
The experimental tasks were performed by consultants at one large global firm, not LMM logistics boutiques.

The frozen labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and therefore may overstate current labor intensity if nominal receipts grew faster; it is also harmonized by a cross-industry median adjustment.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task diary from 541614 firms separating research, modeling, drafting, implementation, onsite observation, sales, and client governance—and measuring current automation—would materially narrow the range.
a.rationale: In broader consulting, management occupations are 16.21% of employment, business and financial operations 33.12%, computer and mathematical 10.92%, and office support 14.98%; management analysts alone are 11.91%. Core analyst duties include gathering information, analyzing financial and operational data, developing alternatives, and producing reports, all substantially exposed to AI assistance. The HBS/BCG experiment found more than 25% faster work and more than 12% more task completion on in-frontier consulting tasks, but performance fell on an outside-frontier task, so exposure is material rather than universal.

d5.bridge: Source population: US employment projections for management analysts, logisticians, and the broader 541600 consulting industry, not constant-price demand for 541614's current service basket. Transformation: convert 2024–34 growth signals—9% for management analysts, 17% for logisticians, and 9.4% for 541600 employment—into a conservative five-year demand ratio, then discount because occupational employment includes in-house roles and because productivity can meet some demand without proportional labor. Direction of error: e-commerce and supply-chain complexity may make 541614 grow faster than broad consulting, while cyclical capital spending, client insourcing, and AI self-service may make service demand grow slower than occupation counts.
caveats: Employment projections are not forecasts of constant-price, constant-quality outsourced service demand.
Logisticians include in-house workers across manufacturing, government, wholesale, and other sectors.
Trade policy, recession, reshoring, and freight cycles can materially shift consulting projects within five years.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted 541614 revenue and backlog histories, procurement-spend forecasts for supply-chain consulting, and segment-level bookings from public logistics advisory firms would directly test the demand path.
d5.rationale: BLS expects consulting demand to rise as organizations pursue efficiency and cost control and projects 9% management-analyst employment growth from 2024 to 2034. It projects 17% logistician growth as e-commerce, inventory volume, multiple supply chains, and delivery timeliness increase complexity. These signals support modest real five-year expansion, tempered by cyclical project budgets and the distinction between labor demand and service quantity.

caveats: No source measures the share of 541614 LMM firms meeting every lens condition.
The frozen firm count is an EBITDA-margin bridge with ESTIMATE quality; its size-band membership is not observed firm by firm.
e.change_evidence: A stratified review of 541614 LMM company websites, revenue ledgers, customer concentration, repeat-client share, owner billability, second-layer management, and contract assignability would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is inherently external advisory work, and Census product definitions explicitly include advice-plus-implementation bundles for operations and supply-chain management. Eligibility is reduced for owner-centric expert practices, heavily project-only firms without repeat client work, captive or misclassified operations, customer concentration, and firms whose client relationships or delivery knowledge would not survive an owner exit.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541614 ($14,727,537,868) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541614 ($25,011,973,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $253,468 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541614); class <5: 5,724 firms, avg revenue/firm $515,407 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $80,661 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 693 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,238,423 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $350,313 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 419 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,800,974 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $751,352 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 481 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,617,393 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,661,622 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 162 firms, avg revenue/firm $30,714,025 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,806,745 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 151 firms, avg revenue/firm $55,737,146 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,722,863 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 794

o.bridge: Source population: BLS duty descriptions for management analysts and logisticians and an HBS/BCG experiment on general consulting tasks, not a measured operator-required share of 541614 demand. Transformation: classify information search, routine analysis, modeling, and drafting as increasingly self-serviceable, but retain operator need for interviews, onsite observation, stakeholder alignment, software and process implementation, supplier/customer liaison, exception resolution, and accountable recommendations. Direction of error: rapid improvement in agentic analytics and client platforms could lower operator need more than assumed; fragmented operational data, physical constraints, and organizational change could preserve more human-led work.
caveats: Duty descriptions establish human work today, not the exact year-five operator-required quantity.
The boundary between consulting, implementation, and software subscription may shift as firms productize their methods.
o.change_evidence: Buyer interviews and lost-deal analysis tracking which 541614 projects were eliminated, brought in-house, or replaced by software, plus autonomous-system benchmarks on real warehouse and network-design implementations, would materially change this share.
o.rationale: BLS describes management analysts interviewing personnel, observing onsite, developing solutions, presenting recommendations, and checking whether changes work. Logisticians select and implement systems, liaise with suppliers and customers, and resolve unforeseen delivery problems. The HBS experiment shows strong gains on AI-suited tasks but worse results outside the capability frontier, supporting a durable human-accountability layer even as clients self-serve more analysis and drafting.

q.bridge: Source population: management analysts nationally, including self-employed practitioners and consulting-firm employees, rather than 541614 LMM firms. Transformation: use BLS evidence that independent analysts are typically paid hourly or by project and that contracts are competitively proposed to infer mixed pass-through: hourly work reprices more directly with fewer hours, while fixed-fee projects retain more near-term productivity benefit until renewal and competition. Direction of error: logistics implementation may support value-based pricing and raise retention, while transparent staffing models, procurement scrutiny, and rebidding may lower it.
caveats: BLS describes compensation modes but not their revenue shares or price elasticity.
The five-year discounted retention rate depends on procurement behavior and competitor adoption that are not directly observed.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level data from 541614 firms showing revenue by time-and-materials, fixed fee, retainer, and performance fee, together with renewal repricing and win/loss outcomes after AI-enabled delivery changes, would materially move this estimate.
q.rationale: BLS says management analysts work contractually, bidders specify schedule and cost, and self-employed analysts are typically paid by the hour or project. AI-driven hour compression therefore cannot be fully retained: hourly billing transmits savings quickly, project fees preserve some benefit during a contract, and renewal competition shares gains with clients. Specialized domain reputation and measurable supply-chain savings provide partial pricing insulation.

rho.bridge: Source population: nationally representative US employer businesses in the 2026 Census BTOS AI supplement and small businesses under 250 employees in SBA's 2025 analysis, not 541614 LMM firms. Transformation: start from observed current AI use and limited functional depth, then raise the five-year implementation share for a digitally intensive consulting population while discounting for client confidentiality, fragmented data, workflow redesign, validation, and change management. Direction of error: professional-services firms are more AI-ready than the all-business average, which pushes this proxy upward, but small LMM boutiques may lag large knowledge firms, which pushes it downward.
caveats: BTOS measures use in any business function, not operational implementation of exposed labor opportunity.
The SBA size cutoff is below 250 employees and is broader than the $1–10M EBITDA lens.
Census found employment decreases in only 2% of AI-using firms, so current adoption evidence does not yet demonstrate broad labor substitution.
rho.change_evidence: A 541614-specific survey of tool deployment, billable-hour reduction, data readiness, client AI restrictions, error rates, and realized hiring avoidance by workflow would materially change this estimate.
rho.rationale: Census reports 18% of firms used AI in a business function in late 2025/early 2026, 23% had workers using AI for tasks, and 57% of adopters used it in three or fewer functions; 66% used AI solely to augment tasks. SBA reported small-business use rising from 6.3% to 8.8% in six months and roughly a one-year lag behind large firms. Over five years, standardized research, document, modeling, and administrative workflows should diffuse meaningfully, but accountable review and client-specific implementation keep realized substitution below exposure.

s5.bridge: Source population: US employer-business owners across industries in Gallup's fall 2024 survey, supplemented by BizBuySell's broad small-business transaction market. Transformation: begin with the 22% of employer owners reporting a five-year plan to sell or transfer, then discount planned events for nonexecution, gifts or internal transitions that do not constitute a qualifying external control transfer, and nontransferable owner dependence. Direction of error: LMM consulting owners may be older and financially motivated to sell, raising transfers, but human-capital dependence and client portability may cause more closures or failed deals, lowering them.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses and reports intentions rather than completed transfers.
BizBuySell is a voluntary marketplace concentrated below the full LMM band and does not expose the number of firms at risk.
Professional-services control can change economically without a publicly observable legal transaction.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year cohort of 541614 ownership changes from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, GF Data, broker records, and Secretary of State filings—matched to eligible LMM firms and distinguishing internal succession—would materially improve this probability.
s5.rationale: Gallup found 52.3% of employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older and 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer within five years. Actual qualifying transactions should be a subset of intentions. BizBuySell reported service-sector transaction volume rose 4% in 2025, confirming an active but selective market rather than providing a denominator-based transfer probability.

records.principal_driver: A high concentration of analytical consulting work that AI can accelerate without removing the need for implementation and accountable operational judgment.
records.principal_weakness: Firm-specific evidence on transferable ownership, repeat revenue, AI implementation depth, and post-AI price retention is largely unavailable, leaving several primitives dependent on broad proxies.

caveats: S1's exact 541614 definition did not render in the reopened page, although S2 independently supports the same industry scope and implementation-bundle claims; this does not alter the ESTIMATE treatment of e or the base tier/action.

S6 supports the experiment and jagged-frontier conclusion but not the precise statement that performance fell on an outside-frontier task; narrowing that wording would not materially change a or o, their factors, the base tier, or the action.

S8 was inaccessible with HTTP 403, leaving its small-business adoption-growth and one-year-lag statistics unaudited. S7 still directly supports the adoption baseline and limited substitution evidence, while rho is transparently a broad PROXY; removing S8 would not require rho to become MISSING or change the base tier/action.

The numeric bases for a, rho, q, d5, and o remain judgmental transformations of broad occupational, experimental, adoption, pricing-mode, and employment-projection evidence rather than direct 541614 LMM measurements. The packet labels these PROXY, states transformation and error direction, supplies wide ranges, and discloses the limitation, so the appropriate consequence is caveated publication rather than rejection.

Firm availability is especially model-conditioned: e and the injected firm count n are ESTIMATE-quality, while s5 extrapolates broad owner intentions and marketplace activity to qualifying external control transfers. The resulting VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action and CONDITIONAL-to-HIGHEST_PRIORITY interval appropriately restrict use of the high base tier.

sources_audited.note: The cited Census NAICS page is reachable and identifies itself as the official 2022 NAICS search, but its fetched content did not render the 541614 definition; the claimed industry scope is independently corroborated by S2.

The Census NAPCS product list expressly assigns operations and supply-chain advice and advice-plus-implementation bundles covering inventory, warehousing, distribution, transportation, productivity, quality, and production planning to 541614.
The BLS 541600 OEWS table reports 1,848,710 jobs and the cited 16.21%, 33.12%, 10.92%, 14.98%, and 11.91% occupational shares.

BLS supports the stated analyst duties, contractual proposal process, hourly-or-project compensation for self-employed analysts, efficiency-driven consulting demand, and 9% 2024-34 employment projection.

BLS supports the cited logistician duties, including forecasting, system selection and implementation, supplier/customer liaison, problem resolution, and the 17% 2024-34 projection tied to e-commerce and supply-chain complexity.

The HBS page verifies the 758-consultant experiment and the cited in-frontier speed, quality, and task-completion gains, and describes a jagged frontier; it does not itself report the packet's more specific claim that performance fell on an outside-frontier task.

The Census working-paper abstract reports the cited 18% firm use, 23% worker-task use, 57% limited functional breadth, 66% augmentation-only use, and 2% incidence of employment decreases for the Nov. 2025-Jan. 2026 reference period.
The exact cited SBA URL returned HTTP 403, so the 6.3%-to-8.8% increase and one-year adoption-lag claims could not be verified from this source.

Gallup reports that 52.3% of employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older and that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, based on its fall 2024 survey.

BizBuySell reports flat overall 2025 transactions and 4% service-sector transaction growth; the packet correctly treats this only as evidence of an active market, not a denominator-based transfer probability.
BLS reports 9.4% projected 2024-34 employment growth for NAICS 541600 and identifies management analysts as that industry's top-employed occupation.

caveats: a: BLS publishes the occupational mix at broader NAICS 541500, not exact NAICS 541519, and employment shares are not payroll shares.
a: The software-developer experiment and Claude usage data cover task populations that overlap only part of this residual service category.
a: The estimate explicitly discounts work already automated, but no exact-industry baseline separates conventional automation from remaining tasks.
a: The injected labor-intensity input combines 2024 QCEW wages with 2022 SUSB receipts and the specified IPS harmonization; that vintage mismatch is not direct evidence about task exposure.
rho: Both implementation sources survey MSPs globally and may not represent disaster-recovery, installation, or residual consulting firms classified in 541519.
rho: Self-reported use and workload automation do not independently verify quality, durability, or labor-hour savings.
rho: The respondents are reached through industry and vendor channels that may select for more technology-active firms.
e: The official definition and survey form identify included and neighboring activities but do not measure the eligible share of firms.
e: The residual category is unusually heterogeneous and classification practices may vary among similar providers.
e: The injected count of 705 firms is margin-bridged from SUSB size classes using a 15.65% EBITDA margin, so its band assignment is uncertain even though it is not re-estimated here.
s5: The succession survey is cross-industry and measures plans rather than completed qualifying control transfers.
s5: The transaction report covers the global MSP market, a narrower business model that only partially overlaps NAICS 541519.
s5: Deal-count definitions and small private transaction disclosure are not standardized.
s5: The estimated 705-firm denominator inherits uncertainty from size-class receipts and the 15.65% margin bridge.
q: The billing survey is vendor-sponsored, global, and specific to MSPs rather than exact NAICS 541519.
q: Predominant billing model does not reveal revenue weights, contract duration, or post-automation repricing behavior.
q: Recent MSP evidence reports shrinking deal sizes and strong competition, which may erode retention faster than historical pricing data imply.
d5: BLS output is projected only at broader NAICS 541500 and includes custom programming, systems design, and facilities management excluded from 541519.
d5: Vendor-survey revenue growth is self-reported, global, and reflects MSP service lines rather than constant-quality demand for the exact current basket.
d5: Cybersecurity and AI can create new services while simultaneously automating legacy work, making category-level net demand unusually uncertain.
o: No source directly measures the year-five operator-required share for NAICS 541519.
o: The category mixes physically grounded installation and recovery with remote services that may be more readily self-served or software-supplied.
o: A major security failure by an autonomous tool could raise operator demand, while reliable vendor-native agents could lower it sharply.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include small and midsize businesses, larger enterprises, and public-sector organizations. Revenue can combine monthly managed-service subscriptions, per-user or per-device charges, fixed-fee bundles, disaster-recovery and backup retainers, incident-response fees, hourly consulting, installation projects, and software or hardware pass-throughs.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal IT units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, one-person practices without transferable operations, and firms whose primary activity belongs in custom programming, systems integration or design, computer facilities management, data processing or hosting, software publishing, training, repair, telecommunications, or hardware and software wholesaling. Product-resale-only operations and nonrecurring installation practices without repeat outsourced service are outside the lens.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market firms in NAICS 541519 that deliver recurring or repeat outsourced computer-related services to external customers, including computer disaster recovery, software or hardware installation, technical support, monitoring, backup, cybersecurity, domain-related services, and residual computer consulting that does not belong in programming, systems design, facilities management, hosting, publishing, training, repair, or distribution codes.

a.bridge: The source population is the broader NAICS 541500 occupational workforce, where computer and mathematical occupations represent 50.57% of employment and management represents 14.77%, combined with adjacent computer-support duties and measured AI effects in software-development and customer-support-like work. I translate those employment shares and task studies into a wage-weighted share for residual 541519, raising weight for relatively well-paid technical roles and discounting physical installation, high-stakes security response, customer communication, novel diagnosis, and work already automated through conventional IT tools. This may overstate exposure if 541519 contains more field installation and support than the broader group, but may understate it if the residual category concentrates remote support and automation-ready technical work.
caveats: BLS publishes the occupational mix at broader NAICS 541500, not exact NAICS 541519, and employment shares are not payroll shares.
The software-developer experiment and Claude usage data cover task populations that overlap only part of this residual service category.
The estimate explicitly discounts work already automated, but no exact-industry baseline separates conventional automation from remaining tasks.
The injected labor-intensity input combines 2024 QCEW wages with 2022 SUSB receipts and the specified IPS harmonization; that vintage mismatch is not direct evidence about task exposure.
a.change_evidence: Exact NAICS 541519 payroll by detailed occupation, firm-level time studies by ticket category and installation workflow, and production measurements of technician hours eliminated rather than merely accelerated would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Ticket classification, documentation, knowledge retrieval, routine troubleshooting, scripting, monitoring review, patch workflows, reporting, and portions of coding and analysis are strongly exposed to generative AI and agents. The broader computer-services workforce is heavily technical, and randomized software-developer trials found higher completed-task output with an AI assistant. Exposure remains bounded because support specialists also diagnose ambiguous failures, repair or install equipment, train users, handle incidents, communicate with frustrated customers, and remain accountable for security and recovery outcomes.

d5.bridge: The source quantity is BLS projected real output for broader NAICS 541500, growing 3.2% annually from 2024 to 2034; five years of that rate compound to approximately 1.17, used as the base for the current 541519 service basket. I widen the range because exact 541519 mixes defensive cybersecurity and disaster recovery with vulnerable routine support, installation, consulting, and resale-related activity. The broader category may overstate exact-category growth if programming and systems design capture more AI demand, but may understate it if security, recovery, compliance, and small-business outsourcing expand faster.
caveats: BLS output is projected only at broader NAICS 541500 and includes custom programming, systems design, and facilities management excluded from 541519.
Vendor-survey revenue growth is self-reported, global, and reflects MSP service lines rather than constant-quality demand for the exact current basket.
Cybersecurity and AI can create new services while simultaneously automating legacy work, making category-level net demand unusually uncertain.
d5.change_evidence: Exact NAICS 541519 constant-price revenue history and forecasts segmented into cybersecurity, backup and recovery, support, installation, consulting, and resale, plus provider backlog and churn data, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: BLS expects strong output growth for computer systems design and related services, while the 2026 MSP survey reports revenue growth in cybersecurity and business continuity and disaster recovery. Digital complexity, security threats, compliance needs, cloud and AI adoption, and customer skill gaps support continued service demand. Routine support automation, client self-service, vendor-direct offerings, procurement pressure, and macroeconomic sensitivity create a nearly flat downside case.

caveats: The official definition and survey form identify included and neighboring activities but do not measure the eligible share of firms.
The residual category is unusually heterogeneous and classification practices may vary among similar providers.
The injected count of 705 firms is margin-bridged from SUSB size classes using a 15.65% EBITDA margin, so its band assignment is uncertain even though it is not re-estimated here.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census or commercial database classifying the estimated lower-middle-market population by recurring-service revenue, customer concentration, owner dependence, primary NAICS accuracy, and share of product resale would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The exact code is service-oriented and explicitly includes disaster recovery and software installation, while the screen's repeat-service requirement admits managed support, backup, monitoring, cybersecurity, installation follow-on, and recurring technical consulting. Eligibility is reduced by project-only installers, product-pass-through-heavy firms, one-person consulting practices with key-person dependence, and establishments whose reported activities drift toward neighboring programming, design, hosting, repair, publishing, training, or distribution categories.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541519 ($19,543,540,823) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541519 ($42,029,844,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $279,051 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541519); class <5: 8,483 firms, avg revenue/firm $429,327 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $67,190 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 841 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,781,555 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $278,813 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 505 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,678,558 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $575,694 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 526 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,815,287 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,692,592 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 179 firms, avg revenue/firm $40,627,151 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,358,149 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 116 firms, avg revenue/firm $190,265,517 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $29,776,553 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 705

caveats: No source directly measures the year-five operator-required share for NAICS 541519.
The category mixes physically grounded installation and recovery with remote services that may be more readily self-served or software-supplied.
A major security failure by an autonomous tool could raise operator demand, while reliable vendor-native agents could lower it sharply.
o.change_evidence: Longitudinal data on fully self-resolved ticket share, vendor-direct displacement, client insourcing, autonomous remediation failure rates, cyber-insurance and compliance requirements, and revenue retained by human-accountable providers would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Automated chatbots, remote-management platforms, vendor self-service, and AI agents can eliminate some routine troubleshooting, monitoring review, documentation, patching, and installation guidance. BLS nevertheless describes complex diagnosis, physical setup and repair, user training, disaster-recovery maintenance, communication, and escalation, and it expects some small organizations to outsource network support rather than staff it internally. Security, recovery, compliance, and cross-vendor accountability preserve a substantial operator role even as the operator uses much more software.

q.bridge: The source population is a global MSP benchmark rather than all U.S. 541519 firms. The reported predominant billing mix includes 26% combining per-user and per-device pricing, 21% per-user, 14% fixed-fee value subscriptions, 13% per-device, 10% tiered bundles, 12% a la carte, and 3% incident response. I translate the large contracted and unit-priced share into above-half retention of implemented labor benefits, discounted for renewal repricing, competitive bids, service-level commitments, vendor-cost pass-through, falling deal sizes, and hourly or project work. This may overstate retention for government contractors and project installers, but may understate it for mature fixed-fee providers that keep productivity gains until repricing.
caveats: The billing survey is vendor-sponsored, global, and specific to MSPs rather than exact NAICS 541519.
Predominant billing model does not reveal revenue weights, contract duration, or post-automation repricing behavior.
Recent MSP evidence reports shrinking deal sizes and strong competition, which may erode retention faster than historical pricing data imply.
q.change_evidence: Exact-industry contract data linking billing model, renewal cadence, price changes, churn, service-level penalties, pass-through costs, and gross-margin changes after automation would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Per-user, per-device, subscription, and bundled contracts decouple revenue from technician hours in the short run, allowing automation to improve service capacity and margin. Over five years, customers can demand better service levels, benchmark prices, reduce supported seats or devices, switch providers, or negotiate at renewal; product and license costs also limit gross-benefit retention. The result is meaningful but incomplete commercial capture.

rho.bridge: The source populations are global managed service providers rather than the complete U.S. NAICS 541519 lower-middle-market population. Kaseya reports that 53% already use AI for ticketing, patching, and monitoring, while more than half have automated only about one-quarter of workload; MSP Global separately reports planned expansion and material integration, quality, staffing, and vendor-dependency challenges. I use current use as a readiness signal, not as realized coverage, and project deeper but incomplete workflow penetration over five years. Vendor-sponsored and technology-forward respondents may overstate implementation, while the category's technical workforce may implement faster than ordinary small businesses.
caveats: Both implementation sources survey MSPs globally and may not represent disaster-recovery, installation, or residual consulting firms classified in 541519.
Self-reported use and workload automation do not independently verify quality, durability, or labor-hour savings.
The respondents are reached through industry and vendor channels that may select for more technology-active firms.
rho.change_evidence: A representative U.S. NAICS 541519 survey measuring production deployment, percentage of tickets or technician hours automated, exception rates, security incidents, and realized headcount avoidance by firm size would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Providers already use AI in core operational workflows and face hiring pressure that strengthens the incentive to scale without proportional headcount. Realization will still require standardized service catalogs, clean ticket and asset data, security controls, human approval paths, integration across PSA and RMM tools, customer consent, and reliable handling of edge cases. These constraints support a central case where most, but not all, exposed opportunity becomes operational.

s5.bridge: The source populations are U.S. employer-business owners across industries and global managed service provider transactions, not eligible NAICS 541519 firms. Gallup reports that 22% of employer owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years; I haircut intentions for noncompletion, family or internal transfers, and firms lacking transferable operations, then offset part of the reduction for the active MSP acquisition market, where an advisory report counted 466 deals in 2025. The Gallup intention measure likely overstates qualifying closes, while global deal flow may overstate U.S. 541519 activity and also omits undisclosed small transactions.
caveats: The succession survey is cross-industry and measures plans rather than completed qualifying control transfers.
The transaction report covers the global MSP market, a narrower business model that only partially overlaps NAICS 541519.
Deal-count definitions and small private transaction disclosure are not standardized.
The estimated 705-firm denominator inherits uncertainty from size-class receipts and the 15.65% margin bridge.
s5.change_evidence: Closed U.S. transaction counts mapped to NAICS 541519, a verified denominator of eligible independent firms, owner-age and succession-plan data, and broker-level outcomes for listed MSP and disaster-recovery firms would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: General employer-owner succession intentions put a meaningful transfer pool into the relevant horizon, and concentrated MSP deal activity shows that strategic and financial buyers are actively completing transactions. Not every planned transition becomes a qualifying control sale, and recurring revenue quality, customer retention, cybersecurity liability, owner dependence, and clean operating data determine whether a computer-services firm is actually transferable.

records.principal_driver: A large technical work share, contracted recurring pricing, and already-deployed automation in ticketing, patching, and monitoring create credible labor leverage.
records.principal_weakness: Residual-category heterogeneity and fast vendor-led automation make it difficult to know which firms retain differentiated, operator-required demand rather than commoditized support or pass-through revenue.

caveats: The labor-opportunity bridge combines broader NAICS 541500 employment shares with software-developer evidence and then converts them into a wage-weighted 541519 task share without exact-industry payroll or workflow data; the PROXY label and wide H range disclose, but do not resolve, that uncertainty.

The rho base projects 65% five-year realization from current adoption and implementation signals in global, vendor- or industry-channel samples. Those sources do not directly measure durable technician-hour elimination, exception rates, or U.S. lower-middle-market deployment.
S7's survey population is materially broader than pure MSPs and is heavily EMEA-weighted, so it is only partial support for a U.S. 541519 implementation bridge.

The q bridge observes predominant billing models, not revenue weights, renewal cadence, competitive pass-through, or retained automation economics. Because every registered q scenario exceeds the factor cap, C remains 10 throughout the envelope despite this unmeasured retention uncertainty.
The s5 bridge combines cross-industry five-year owner intentions with global MSP deal counts; neither source measures completed qualifying control transfers among eligible U.S. 541519 firms.

The demand factor combines a broader 541500 output forecast, vendor-reported MSP service-line growth, and an explicit operator-required-share estimate. Programming and systems-design activities excluded from the lens may account for a different share of broader-category growth than residual 541519 services.

The eligibility and operator-required shares are honest ESTIMATE inputs, but the residual code's broad classification and unknown mix of recurring managed services, project installation, consulting, and pass-through revenue make the base thesis strongly model-conditioned.

sources_audited.note: The Census NAICS page defines 541519 as other computer-related services, explicitly includes disaster recovery and software installation, and excludes programming, systems-design, and facilities-management services. It supports the boundary but does not measure eligibility or recurring-revenue prevalence.

The 2022 Economic Census form distinguishes 541519 computer-related consulting and installation from neighboring activities and enumerates technical support, recovery, security, domain, and consulting product lines, supporting the claimed heterogeneity and classification boundary.

The BLS NAICS 541500 table reports computer and mathematical occupations at 50.57% of employment and management occupations at 14.77%, exactly supporting the broader-industry inputs described in the bridge.

BLS describes diagnosis, documentation, equipment setup or repair, hardware and software installation, user training, automation pressure from chatbots, complex-case work, and outsourcing by smaller organizations.

Anthropic reports high automation-mode use in Claude Code and frequent human feedback or validation, supporting the directional software-work exposure claim while remaining limited to observed Claude interactions rather than 541519 production work.
The Management Science abstract reports three randomized field experiments involving 4,867 developers and a combined 26.08% increase in completed tasks with an AI coding assistant.

The report supports AI expansion plans, service customization, mixed delivery tooling, and integration, quality, staffing, and vendor-dependency challenges. Its 290-person worldwide member sample is broader than pure MSPs (only 45% identify their business type as MSP) and is 70% EMEA-focused, weakening representativeness for U.S. 541519.

Kaseya states that its 2026 report surveyed more than 1,000 MSPs worldwide, that 53% use AI for ticketing, patching, and monitoring, that more than half have automated only about one-quarter of workload, and that cybersecurity and disaster-recovery revenue grew for many respondents.

The Kaseya pricing page reports the cited predominant billing mix: 26% combined per-user/per-device, 21% per-user, 14% fixed-fee value subscription, 13% per-device, 12% a la carte, 10% tiered bundles, and 3% incident response.

Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or take public within five years, and clearly identifies the measure as cross-industry owner intentions rather than completed qualifying sales.
The advisory PDF reports 466 global MSP deals in 2025, up about 20%, with $4.3 billion in disclosed value, supporting active MSP consolidation but not a U.S. 541519 transfer rate.
The BLS projection table reports a 3.2% annual real-output growth rate for broader NAICS 541500, supporting the stated five-year compounding proxy and its disclosed population mismatch.

records.title: Other Computer Related Services

caveats: a: The occupational distribution is for broader NAICS 541700 and includes biotechnology and social-science R&D outside this code.
a: Employment shares are not wage shares, although the estimate accounts directionally for different occupational wages.
a: Published AI research examples may select unusually data-rich or automation-friendly scientific problems.
a: The injected labor-intensity input combines 2024 QCEW wages with 2022 SUSB receipts and the specified IPS harmonization, so its vintage mismatch is not evidence of task exposure.
rho: The researcher survey spans countries, institutions, and scientific fields and measures use and perceptions rather than implemented labor savings.
rho: Autonomous-lab evidence demonstrates technical feasibility in specific materials workflows, not scalable economics across the industry.
rho: Regulated medical, defense, and safety-critical projects may require slower validation and approval than the central case.
e: No source measures the share of exact-industry firms that earn recurring or repeat external service revenue.
e: The code spans radically different scientific fields, capital requirements, customer types, and intellectual-property models.
e: The injected count of 1,716 firms is margin-bridged from SUSB size classes using a 15.65% EBITDA margin and may misclassify research firms with unusually volatile or nonstandard margins.
s5: The succession source measures intentions across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers.
s5: The M&A source combines CROs, CDMOs, and suppliers globally and overlaps only part of exact NAICS 541715.
s5: Asset purchases of patents, programs, or laboratories may not constitute qualifying transfers of an eligible operating firm.
s5: The estimated 1,716-firm denominator inherits uncertainty from SUSB size classes and the 15.65% margin bridge.
q: Federal contracting rules establish permissible and typical structures, not revenue weights for all 541715 firms.
q: Fortrea is a large global clinical CRO whose customer, scale, and regulatory profile differs from much of the exact industry.
q: No source directly measures commercial sharing of AI benefits in outsourced scientific R&D.
d5: BLS output is available at broader NAICS 541700 and employment at 541710, both including nanotechnology and biotechnology or adjacent R&D.
d5: NSF total R&D spending includes captive business, government, higher-education, and nonprofit performance rather than outsourced services only.
d5: Current-dollar and modeled spending estimates require care when inferring constant-price service quantity.
d5: Government funding, commercial research programs, and venture-backed customer demand can change abruptly.
o: No direct measure exists for the year-five operator-required share of exact-industry demand.
o: Computational R&D may become software-supplied faster than wet-lab, field, hardware, agricultural, or safety-critical research.
o: The accountable entity could shift from a lower-middle-market provider to a platform, equipment vendor, hyperscaler, or customer-owned laboratory.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include manufacturers, pharmaceutical and medical-device companies, aerospace and defense organizations, technology companies, agriculture and food businesses, and federal or other public agencies. Revenue may come from cost-reimbursement and cost-plus contracts, fixed-price or milestone projects, fee-for-service and per-unit work, time and materials, full-time-equivalent arrangements, multi-year programs, and reimbursed pass-through costs.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive manufacturer or corporate R&D units, firms principally developing their own intellectual property or products rather than serving external customers, shells, non-control financing vehicles, nontransferable owner-only practices, nanotechnology and biotechnology R&D, routine analytical testing, engineering design, prototype production, and social-science or humanities research.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market firms in NAICS 541715 that provide recurring or repeat outsourced research and experimental-development services to external commercial or government customers in physical, engineering, agricultural, environmental, computer, chemical, food, geological, health, medical, pharmaceutical, robotics, aerospace, and other covered sciences.

a.bridge: The source population is broader NAICS 541700, where management is 20.28% of employment, business and financial operations 12.14%, computer and mathematical work 14.29%, architecture and engineering 12.29%, and life, physical, and social science work 23.16%. I map those employment shares and their wage levels to task exposure in 541715, assigning high exposure to literature review, search, coding, modeling, data analysis, documentation, project administration, and candidate selection, while discounting laboratory setup, specimen and equipment handling, fieldwork, safety, physical prototyping, validation, and work already automated. This may overstate exact-industry exposure because the broader source includes less experimental social-science R&D, but may understate it because 541715 includes AI, computer, engineering, and data-intensive research and the estimate is wage-weighted rather than headcount-weighted.
caveats: The occupational distribution is for broader NAICS 541700 and includes biotechnology and social-science R&D outside this code.
Employment shares are not wage shares, although the estimate accounts directionally for different occupational wages.
Published AI research examples may select unusually data-rich or automation-friendly scientific problems.
The injected labor-intensity input combines 2024 QCEW wages with 2022 SUSB receipts and the specified IPS harmonization, so its vintage mismatch is not evidence of task exposure.
a.change_evidence: Exact NAICS 541715 payroll by detailed occupation, time-use studies separating computational and documentation work from physical experiments, and audited before-and-after labor hours for deployed AI and laboratory automation would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The industry concentrates highly paid computational, engineering, scientific, managerial, and project work, much of which contains information-intensive subtasks accessible to AI. Researcher surveys show broad use, bibliometric evidence associates AI-augmented work with materially higher output, and autonomous-lab demonstrations extend exposure into experiment selection and execution. The remaining share reflects the irreducibly physical, safety-critical, tacit, novel, and validation-heavy work that makes experimental R&D different from pure desk research.

d5.bridge: The source quantities are BLS projections for broader NAICS 541700 and 541710 and NSF measures of total U.S. R&D, not constant-quality outsourced demand for 541715. BLS projects broader scientific R&D real output at 2.0% annually and physical, engineering, and life-science R&D employment growth of 8.7% over the decade; compounding the output rate for five years gives approximately 1.10. I use that as the base and widen for business-funded R&D expansion, flat or declining agency budgets, field mix, customer capital cycles, and possible AI-driven self-performance. Broader measures may overstate outsourced growth if captive R&D captures spending, but may understate specialized external demand when sponsors outsource scarce capabilities.
caveats: BLS output is available at broader NAICS 541700 and employment at 541710, both including nanotechnology and biotechnology or adjacent R&D.
NSF total R&D spending includes captive business, government, higher-education, and nonprofit performance rather than outsourced services only.
Current-dollar and modeled spending estimates require care when inferring constant-price service quantity.
Government funding, commercial research programs, and venture-backed customer demand can change abruptly.
d5.change_evidence: Exact NAICS 541715 constant-price revenue history and forecast, external-versus-captive R&D spending, government contract backlog, sponsor cancellations, and field-level outsourcing budgets would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: U.S. R&D spending remains large and growing, with business funding leading the recent increase, while BLS expects continued scientific R&D expansion. Demand is supported by defense, aerospace, electronics, medicine, energy, agriculture, materials, robotics, and environmental innovation. A downside remains plausible because federal obligations are uneven, sponsor programs can be canceled, capital markets affect research customers, and AI may let customers conduct more analysis internally.

caveats: No source measures the share of exact-industry firms that earn recurring or repeat external service revenue.
The code spans radically different scientific fields, capital requirements, customer types, and intellectual-property models.
The injected count of 1,716 firms is margin-bridged from SUSB size classes using a 15.65% EBITDA margin and may misclassify research firms with unusually volatile or nonstandard margins.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level Census or commercial data identifying external-customer revenue, sponsor concentration, contract backlog, captive ownership, proprietary-product R&D, nonprofit status, and transferable operating assets for the estimated lower-middle-market population would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition covers establishments conducting R&D, not only external contract-research providers. The Census survey form explicitly includes manufacturer R&D laboratories supporting their own manufacturing establishments, and the industry also contains firms developing proprietary intellectual property or products for themselves. The eligible subset consists of independent organizations with repeat external sponsor work, transferable teams and facilities, and an outsourced service model rather than captive or speculative proprietary R&D.
