Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $344,036 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541715); class <5: 5,421 firms, avg revenue/firm $586,174 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $91,736 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,423 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,821,285 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $285,031 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 933 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,064,385 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $636,076 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,197 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,825,023 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,007,116 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 519 firms, avg revenue/firm $58,594,915 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,170,104 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 549 firms, avg revenue/firm $257,196,235 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $40,251,211 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,716

caveats: No direct measure exists for the year-five operator-required share of exact-industry demand.
Computational R&D may become software-supplied faster than wet-lab, field, hardware, agricultural, or safety-critical research.
The accountable entity could shift from a lower-middle-market provider to a platform, equipment vendor, hyperscaler, or customer-owned laboratory.
o.change_evidence: Longitudinal data on sponsor insourcing, fully autonomous project share, software-only substitution, autonomous-lab reliability, regulatory acceptance, and revenue retained by human-accountable contract-research organizations would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: AI can self-serve literature search, computational screening, code, analysis, writing, and some experiment selection, and robotics can automate bounded laboratory loops. Most outsourced R&D still requires an accountable organization to own scientific protocols, instruments, facilities, safety, quality systems, physical samples, regulatory or government compliance, reproducibility, sponsor communication, intellectual-property controls, and expert validation. Even advanced autonomous laboratories combine specialized hardware, curated data, domain knowledge, maintenance, and human-designed objectives rather than eliminating the operator.

q.bridge: The source quantities are federal R&D contracting rules and a global clinical CRO's disclosed contract structures rather than an exact-industry billing distribution. Federal rules say uncertain R&D specifications usually make cost reimbursement appropriate, while the CRO reports fixed-price, fee-for-service, volume, time-and-materials, and scope-adjusted arrangements. I map cost-reimbursable and time-based work to low retention, fixed-price and per-unit work to higher short-run retention, and then discount all structures for renewal repricing, competitive bidding, customer ownership of results, scope changes, and pass-through costs. Government-heavy providers may retain less than the estimate, while differentiated fixed-price labs with scarce facilities may retain more.
caveats: Federal contracting rules establish permissible and typical structures, not revenue weights for all 541715 firms.
Fortrea is a large global clinical CRO whose customer, scale, and regulatory profile differs from much of the exact industry.
No source directly measures commercial sharing of AI benefits in outsourced scientific R&D.
q.change_evidence: Exact-industry contract-level revenue weights by cost reimbursement, cost plus, fixed price, milestone, per-unit, full-time-equivalent, and time and materials, linked to renewal repricing and post-automation gross margin, would materially change the range.
q.rationale: R&D uncertainty often ties customer payment to cost, effort, or scope, directly sharing labor savings with sponsors. Fixed-price, milestone, and per-unit contracts can retain automation benefits through lower delivery cost or greater capacity, but customers can renegotiate at renewal and sophisticated sponsors compare bids. The mixed contract architecture therefore supports retention below one-half in the central case, with meaningful variation by field and customer.

rho.bridge: The source population is a global survey of academic and corporate researchers plus published autonomous-materials laboratories, not representative U.S. lower-middle-market 541715 firms. The survey reports 84% overall AI use but only 62% use for research or publication tasks and 25% use of specialized research assistants; corporate researchers have better organizational access, while researchers report hallucination, privacy, guidance, and training concerns. I translate broad current use into substantial five-year implementation of exposed tasks, but discount for shallow general-purpose usage, validation requirements, integration with scientific instruments, reproducibility, safety, data rights, and capital needs. Survey respondents may overstate readiness, whereas technology-native contract labs may move faster than the average researcher.
caveats: The researcher survey spans countries, institutions, and scientific fields and measures use and perceptions rather than implemented labor savings.
Autonomous-lab evidence demonstrates technical feasibility in specific materials workflows, not scalable economics across the industry.
Regulated medical, defense, and safety-critical projects may require slower validation and approval than the central case.
rho.change_evidence: A representative U.S. 541715 survey measuring production AI workflow coverage, autonomous-instrument utilization, validation failure rates, capital expenditure, approved-use policies, and verified labor-hour avoidance by scientific field and firm size would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Research organizations have strong incentives to shorten search, analysis, reporting, and experiment cycles, and corporate researchers have comparatively good access to approved tools. Implementation will remain uneven because specialized scientific models and autonomous equipment are far less common than general chat tools, and robust laboratory automation must manage stochastic and epistemic errors, interoperability, reproducibility, and physical failure modes.

s5.bridge: The source populations are U.S. employer-business owners across all industries and life-sciences CRO, CDMO, and supplier transactions, not eligible NAICS 541715 service firms. Gallup reports that 22% of employer owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years; I reduce that for noncompletion, family or internal transfers, nonprofits, institution-owned labs, and research businesses whose value is not transferable without founders or sponsors. Active life-sciences services dealmaking partly offsets the haircut, although its concentration in CRO and CDMO assets may overstate activity in engineering, physical-science, agricultural, and environmental R&D.
caveats: The succession source measures intentions across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers.
The M&A source combines CROs, CDMOs, and suppliers globally and overlaps only part of exact NAICS 541715.
Asset purchases of patents, programs, or laboratories may not constitute qualifying transfers of an eligible operating firm.
The estimated 1,716-firm denominator inherits uncertainty from SUSB size classes and the 15.65% margin bridge.
s5.change_evidence: Closed U.S. transactions mapped to NAICS 541715, owner-age and succession data, a verified denominator of eligible independent firms, and broker or database outcomes segmented by contract-research field would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Employer-owner succession creates a general transfer pool, and outsourced life-sciences services continue to attract strategic and financial buyers despite a slower 2025 market. Qualifying transfers are constrained by founder dependence, customer and grant concentration, intellectual-property ownership, security clearances, specialized facilities, scientific key-person risk, and institutional or nonprofit control structures.

records.principal_driver: AI addresses high-cost computational, analytical, documentation, and experiment-selection work while specialized facilities and expertise preserve outsourced delivery needs.
records.principal_weakness: A large share of the NAICS universe may be captive or proprietary R&D, and cost-based contracting can pass labor savings to sophisticated sponsors.

caveats: S1's cited URL is reachable but its rendered page does not expose the claimed 541715 definition; S2 supplies the relevant exact-industry scope, making this a non-material citation defect.

The a bridge starts from correctly quoted broader-industry occupation shares but does not show reproducible occupation-level task-exposure weights or a full wage-weight calculation; the broad-code and directional nature of the mapping are disclosed.

The rho sources establish researcher adoption, perceptions, and bounded autonomous-lab feasibility rather than verified production labor-hour implementation in U.S. lower-middle-market contract-research firms; the five-year base remains a wide, honestly labeled proxy.

No cited source measures the eligible external-service share e. The 0.48 base is an explicit estimate, and the resulting MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action correctly prevent it from being presented as observed target availability.

The s5 bridge reduces a 22% cross-industry five-year sale-or-transfer intention only modestly to a 20% qualifying-transfer base despite noncompletion and internal-transfer exclusions, while S11 reports sharply weaker CRO/CDMO dealmaking in 2025. This likely biases s5 upward, but a correction large enough to be plausible from these sources does not establish a change in the base PRIORITY tier or the already restricted action.

The q sources demonstrate available contract forms but provide neither exact-industry revenue weights nor observed sharing of AI savings, so the 0.47 retention base is judgment-heavy despite its appropriate PROXY label and broad range.

The d5 bridge uses broader-industry output and total national R&D, while o is an unsupported bounded estimate; possible AI-driven customer self-performance also appears in both discussions, creating some conservative overlap in downside framing without changing the base conclusion.

sources_audited.note: The cited parameterized Census URL reopened to a generic NAICS search page whose rendered text did not contain 541715 or its definition. Exact-industry scope is independently supported by S2, so this source-level defect does not change any primitive's already honest evidence state, the base tier, or the action.

The Census survey form expressly lists 541715 physical/engineering and life-science research, including manufacturer R&D laboratories supporting their own manufacturing establishments, and distinguishes adjacent nanotechnology, biotechnology, social-science, engineering-design, prototype, and IP activities.

The BLS NAICS 541700 table reports the quoted employment shares and wages for management, business/financial, computer/mathematical, architecture/engineering, and life/physical/social-science occupations.

The Wiley release reports worldwide researcher AI use of 84%, research/publication-task use of 62%, research-assistant use of 25%, stronger corporate access, and the stated accuracy, privacy, guidance, and training barriers.

The Nature paper describes an autonomous materials laboratory integrating robotics, databases, machine learning, text-mined literature heuristics, active learning, synthesis, characterization, and explicit failure modes.

The Nature Reviews Materials article directly states that automation will not easily remove humans and identifies robust operation, epistemic and stochastic error handling, reproducibility, reconfigurability, and interoperability as requirements.

The Nature article reports observational associations between identified AI-augmented natural-science research and higher paper and citation output, along with a narrowing of collective scientific focus; the packet appropriately describes these as associations.
FAR 35.006 says uncertain R&D specifications and costs usually make cost-reimbursement appropriate, while permitting fixed-price arrangements for sufficiently defined or limited work.

Fortrea's filing documents an outsourced clinical-development customer model, long-term work, fixed-price and fee-for-service arrangements, transaction-volume and time-and-materials pricing, scope adjustments, pass-through costs, cancellations, and customer funding risk.

Gallup reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years; it measures intentions rather than completed qualifying transfers, as disclosed in the packet.

The Deloitte report supports a broad life-sciences transaction market and buyer selectivity, but it also says the CRO/CDMO subsector stalled in 2025 and its deal value fell by more than 70%; it is therefore only a directional, partial signal for eligible 541715 service-firm availability.

The BLS projection table reports 2.0% annual real-output growth for broader NAICS 541700 and 8.7% decade employment growth for 541710, matching the demand bridge and its disclosed broader-code limitations.

NCSES reports $937 billion of U.S. R&D in 2023, an estimated $993 billion in 2024, inflation-adjusted totals, and that most of the 2023-to-2024 increase came from business funding; it also labels recent values preliminary or estimated.

caveats: a: The only public industry occupation table is for broader management, scientific, and technical consulting and includes work unlike administrative management consulting.
a: The BCG experiments involve a large, highly selective global consultancy and bounded tasks, not end-to-end lower-middle-market engagements.
a: The supplied labor ratio combines more recent payroll with older receipts and may overstate or understate the current labor intensity paired with this task share.
rho: Reported tool use is not the same as implemented labor substitution or avoided hiring.
rho: Experimental productivity on bounded tasks may not persist across discovery, diagnosis, recommendation, and implementation.
rho: Client data restrictions and errors that look persuasive can constrain implementation more than raw model capability.
e: No source measures eligibility within the frozen EBITDA band.
e: Repeat projects are not contractually recurring and may disappear when a principal leaves.
e: The supplied firm count is margin-bridged from older Census size classes and may include firms unlike today's normalized EBITDA population.
s5: Gallup measures owner intentions across industries, not completed consulting-firm transfers.
s5: Equiteq covers specialty consulting globally and does not provide the denominator required for a probability.
s5: A sale can destroy client and employee value if relationships are concentrated in the departing founder.
q: No public source measures the national contract mix or retained AI benefit for NAICS 541611.
q: Public-sector contract structures may differ materially from private consulting engagements.
q: Released capacity creates value only when firms can sell additional work or avoid hiring without impairing quality.
d5: Occupational employment includes internal analysts and adjacent consulting industries and is not service quantity.
d5: The outlook predates much of the latest AI-enabled client self-service and productivity change.
d5: General strategy and transformation spending is cyclical, while regulated and implementation-heavy work is more durable.
o: Management consulting has no universal licensing or statutory sign-off requirement that guarantees operator demand.
o: Routine generalist work is more exposed to client self-service than specialized, political, or implementation-heavy engagements.
o: The operator share depends on client trust, proprietary knowledge, and willingness to accept AI-generated advice, all of which can change quickly.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External clients buy expert capacity, independent judgment, analysis, recommendations, and change support through projects, task orders, retainers, or recurring managed advisory. Pricing may be hourly or time-and-materials, fixed price, cost reimbursement, performance based, or a hybrid; repeat revenue usually depends on relationships, specialization, and continuing transformation needs rather than mandatory subscriptions.
lens.excluded_activities: Internal corporate strategy teams, captive shared-service units, software-only products, staffing without substantive management advice, one-person practices without a transferable operating organization, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing repeat outsourced advice and implementation assistance on strategy, organization, financial planning and budgeting, records and office management, site selection, new-business formation, operational improvement, and related administrative or general management issues.

a.bridge: BLS reports the occupation mix for broader NAICS 541600, not wage-weighted tasks for NAICS 541611, and the consulting experiment studies large-firm BCG consultants rather than lower-middle-market firms. I mapped research, data analysis, modeling, drafting, presentation production, project administration, knowledge retrieval, and first-pass recommendations into exposure; applied lower exposure to interviews, stakeholder persuasion, executive judgment, sales, and implementation; and excluded automation already embedded in templates and conventional analytics. The broader industry includes scientific and environmental field roles, which likely understates exposure for administrative management consulting, while selected experimental tasks and elite-firm workflows likely overstate exposure across the full current service basket.
caveats: The only public industry occupation table is for broader management, scientific, and technical consulting and includes work unlike administrative management consulting.
The BCG experiments involve a large, highly selective global consultancy and bounded tasks, not end-to-end lower-middle-market engagements.
The supplied labor ratio combines more recent payroll with older receipts and may overstate or understate the current labor intensity paired with this task share.
a.change_evidence: A representative time-and-motion study of NAICS 541611 firms that joins payroll by role to task frequencies, current automation, client-facing time, and validated AI feasibility would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Broader consulting employment is concentrated in management, business and financial operations, computer, market research, project management, sales, and administrative occupations. Management analysts gather information, analyze financial and operational data, develop alternatives, and present recommendations, all of which contain substantial digital work. In a randomized experiment, BCG consultants using GPT completed suitable knowledge tasks faster and completed more of them, but AI also reduced correctness on a task beyond its capability frontier. The task mix therefore supports high but not comprehensive current exposure. [S2, S3, S4, S5]

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment for the management-analyst occupation across all industries, not constant-price service quantity for NAICS 541611, while Equiteq describes specialty-consulting market conditions and buyer preferences. I translate the positive occupational outlook and stated consulting demand into five-year service quantity, allow productivity to separate output from employment, and widen for economic cycles and discretionary project deferral. Employment growth can understate quantity when AI raises output per worker, while BLS's cross-industry occupation and Equiteq's transaction-market lens can overstate demand for the frozen general-consulting service basket.
caveats: Occupational employment includes internal analysts and adjacent consulting industries and is not service quantity.
The outlook predates much of the latest AI-enabled client self-service and productivity change.
General strategy and transformation spending is cyclical, while regulated and implementation-heavy work is more durable.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price Census revenue for NAICS 541611, combined with representative firm backlogs, proposal conversion, utilization, client budgets, and service-line mix, would materially change the year-five quantity ratio.
d5.rationale: BLS projects management-analyst employment growth of 9 percent from 2024 to 2034 and expects organizations to seek consulting to improve efficiency and control costs, with especially strong growth at smaller specialist firms. Demand also benefits from transformation and operational complexity. The downside is meaningful because generalist consulting is discretionary, clients can defer projects in weak economies, and Equiteq reports a more selective market with better resilience in regulated and specialized work. [S3, S10]

caveats: No source measures eligibility within the frozen EBITDA band.
Repeat projects are not contractually recurring and may disappear when a principal leaves.
The supplied firm count is margin-bridged from older Census size classes and may include firms unlike today's normalized EBITDA population.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level diligence on the supplied population showing repeat-client revenue, customer concentration, contract duration, delivery-team depth, founder dependence, and transferability would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is inherently an external advisory business and the supplied firm count is already sized to established lower-middle-market operations, so most firms should satisfy the outsourced-service condition. Management analysts commonly work for clients on contract, and consulting relationships often generate repeat projects or task orders. I exclude practices whose revenue is isolated, founder-personal, nonrepeat, captive, or inseparable from an individual rainmaker, as well as software-only models. [S1, S3, S11]

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541611 ($114,205,171,465) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541611 ($205,640,681,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $260,722 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541611); class <5: 79,966 firms, avg revenue/firm $344,991 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $53,991 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 5,880 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,598,754 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $250,205 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 3,308 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,199,427 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $500,710 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,992 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,214,358 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,442,047 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 861 firms, avg revenue/firm $32,354,620 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,063,498 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 507 firms, avg revenue/firm $202,449,895 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $31,683,409 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 3,853

o.bridge: BLS describes management-analyst duties, the experiments measure AI-assisted consultant performance, and Anthropic surveys AI users rather than measuring the share of consulting demand requiring an external lower-middle-market operator. I map interviews, contextual diagnosis, independent challenge, stakeholder alignment, accountable recommendation, sales, and change implementation to operator-required quantity, and map generic research, synthesis, drafting, and routine analysis toward client self-service or software supply. BLS may overstate operator need because it observes the existing occupation, while AI-user and elite-consultant evidence may overstate self-service because users are selected and experiments omit full client engagements.
caveats: Management consulting has no universal licensing or statutory sign-off requirement that guarantees operator demand.
Routine generalist work is more exposed to client self-service than specialized, political, or implementation-heavy engagements.
The operator share depends on client trust, proprietary knowledge, and willingness to accept AI-generated advice, all of which can change quickly.
o.change_evidence: A revenue-weighted client study that tracks which current consulting scopes were renewed with human firms, internalized with AI, bought as software, or eliminated, split by task and client size, would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Clients can increasingly self-serve generic research, summaries, benchmarks, presentations, and preliminary analysis. However, BLS defines the work around gathering organization-specific evidence, interviewing personnel, developing alternatives, presenting recommendations, and checking whether changes work. The experiments show both strong assistance and persuasive errors, while Anthropic's management respondents identify judgment and management as capabilities AI lacks. This leaves a substantial but reduced role for accountable consulting operators. [S3, S4, S5, S8]

q.bridge: Equiteq describes time-and-materials as the consulting industry's pricing foundation, BLS notes that self-employed analysts are commonly paid by the hour or project, and GSA documents multiple contract types for a federal vehicle that includes NAICS 541611. I assign low retention to hourly, labor-hour, and cost-reimbursement work; higher initial retention to fixed-price, retainer, and performance-based work; and then discount for rebids, renewal repricing, client scrutiny, scope expansion, and competitor adoption. The sources do not measure revenue weights: federal contracting may overstate formal pass-through, while the specialty-consulting report may overstate industry movement toward platforms and recurring services.
caveats: No public source measures the national contract mix or retained AI benefit for NAICS 541611.
Public-sector contract structures may differ materially from private consulting engagements.
Released capacity creates value only when firms can sell additional work or avoid hiring without impairing quality.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract audit showing revenue by pricing model, renewal cadence, utilization treatment, post-AI scope and rate changes, realized project margins, and client concentration would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Hourly and time-and-materials engagements directly expose fewer delivery hours, and federal rules explicitly note that such contracts provide no positive profit incentive for labor efficiency. Fixed-price projects can preserve savings through the contract term, while recurring managed advisory and outcome pricing can retain more value. Equiteq also reports that AI-compressed timelines are making hourly billing harder to defend, so competition and client expectations should dissipate a substantial share of benefits within five years. [S3, S10, S11, S12]

rho.bridge: Gallup measures AI use across US employees and organizations, while OECD surveys mostly medium and large AI-adopting firms in manufacturing and information technology and the experiments cover BCG consultants. I translate current use, demonstrated task gains, and the industry's digital work into five-year implementation of the exposed opportunity, then discount for client confidentiality, hallucination risk, weak workflow redesign, inconsistent proprietary data, review requirements, and smaller-firm change capacity. General workforce adoption likely understates consulting uptake, while large-enterprise and BCG evidence likely overstates lower-middle-market governance and integration capacity.
caveats: Reported tool use is not the same as implemented labor substitution or avoided hiring.
Experimental productivity on bounded tasks may not persist across discovery, diagnosis, recommendation, and implementation.
Client data restrictions and errors that look persuasive can constrain implementation more than raw model capability.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of lower-middle-market NAICS 541611 firms measuring production deployment, task coverage, accepted-output rates, labor hours, rework, client restrictions, and workflow redesign would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: Consulting work is already digital and the experiment shows that trained consultants can realize immediate gains on suitable tasks. US workplace AI use is widespread, but Gallup finds that workflow transformation lags individual productivity, and OECD reports lower adoption by smaller firms plus persistent skills and return-on-investment barriers. The five-year range assumes copilots become routine in research, analysis, drafting, and administration while high-stakes recommendations retain structured human review. [S4, S5, S6, S7]

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year ownership plans among US employer-business owners across industries, and Equiteq counts broader global specialty-consulting deals without an eligible-firm denominator. I use the employer-owner transfer intention as an anchor, check that consulting has an active transaction market, and discount gifts, internal succession, non-control recapitalizations, plans that never close, firms outside the frozen size band, and owner-dependent practices that wind down. Gallup's inclusion of all employer sizes and planned rather than completed transfers likely biases the anchor upward; unannounced private consulting transactions in Equiteq's data may bias visible deal activity downward.
caveats: Gallup measures owner intentions across industries, not completed consulting-firm transfers.
Equiteq covers specialty consulting globally and does not provide the denominator required for a probability.
A sale can destroy client and employee value if relationships are concentrated in the departing founder.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year NAICS 541611 transaction census linking announced and private control changes to the eligible firm denominator, with seller EBITDA, buyer type, ownership percentage, and internal versus external succession, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that 22 percent of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while specialty-consulting deal activity remained substantial but became more selective. Eligible firms in this EBITDA band should be more transferable than microbusinesses, yet management consulting is unusually dependent on senior relationships and expertise. The base is below the stated general employer transfer intention because only completed qualifying control transfers count. [S9, S10]

records.principal_driver: Digitally produced analysis and deliverables account for a large share of high-cost work, and controlled experiments show that suitable consulting tasks can be completed materially faster with AI.
records.principal_weakness: Hourly economics and easy client self-service can pass productivity gains away, while the relationship-heavy remainder is difficult to automate and fragile in a control transfer.

caveats: S1's cited Census URL was reachable but did not render the claimed NAICS 541611 definition, leaving that citation unsupported as reopened; e is already an ESTIMATE and the correction does not change the base tier or MODEL_CONDITIONED action.

S11 was unreachable during repeated audit attempts, so the claimed GSA contract-pool and contract-type details remain unverified; S3, S10, and S12 still support the pricing mechanism, and removing S11 does not change the base tier or action.

The 0.52 exposure share and 0.62 implementation share are judgmental mappings from broader occupation, large-firm experiment, general-workforce, and mostly medium/large adopting-firm evidence rather than measurements of lower-middle-market NAICS 541611 firms.

The 0.42 retention share is not based on a revenue-weighted national contract-mix study; federal contract structures and an M&A advisor's global specialty-consulting observations are broad proxies for private US LMM consulting economics.

The 1.06 demand ratio and 0.59 operator-required share translate occupational growth, current duties, experimental results, and selected AI-user perceptions into service-quantity constructs that none of the sources measures directly.

The attainable tier interval holds the observed labor ratio and estimated firm-count anchor fixed, so it does not capture uncertainty from the wage/receipts vintage mismatch or the margin-bridged firm population.

sources_audited.note: The cited Census page loaded, but its rendered search results did not display the 541611 title or definition, so the claimed definition was not verifiable from the cited page during this audit.

The BLS NAICS 541600 table reports the cited broader-industry occupation shares, including 16.21% management, 33.12% business and financial operations, 10.92% computer and mathematical, and 14.98% office support.

BLS supports the cited management-analyst duties, contractual consulting context, hourly or project payment for self-employed analysts, 9% 2024-2034 outlook, and stronger expected growth for smaller specialist consultancies.

The randomized BCG experiment reports 12.2% more tasks completed, 25.1% faster completion on in-frontier tasks, improved quality, and a 19-percentage-point reduction in correct solutions on an out-of-frontier task.

BCG describes a controlled study completed by 480 consultants on coding, statistical understanding, and predictive modeling, documents strong task gains, and reports goal-misunderstanding and over-reliance risks; it also explicitly excludes a full end-to-end workflow.
Gallup reports the 23,717-employee survey, 50% occasional workplace AI use, 28% frequent use, reported productivity gains, and limited evidence of fundamental organizational work redesign.

The OECD report supports lower adoption by small firms, low use in core processes among small enterprises, and skills, readiness, use-case, cost, and ROI barriers; the packet appropriately discloses the survey's medium/large adopter emphasis.

Anthropic reports that respondents, especially experienced respondents, frequently identify judgment, management, contextual reasoning, trust, and interpersonal work as AI limitations and explicitly says the survey is not population-representative.
Gallup reports a fall 2024 survey based on 1,264 owners and shows that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or go public within five years, while 5% planned closure.

The Equiteq report supports 166 H2 2025 transactions, more selective buyer demand, uneven AI adoption, time-and-materials as the industry's pricing foundation, and pressure on hourly billing as AI compresses delivery timelines.
The cited GSA PDF returned an internal retrieval error on repeated attempts, so its claims about NAICS 541611 pool inclusion and contract-type coverage could not be verified.
FAR Subpart 16.6 defines time-and-materials and labor-hour contracts and states that a time-and-materials contract provides no positive profit incentive for cost control or labor efficiency.

records.title: Administrative Management and General Management Consulting Services

caveats: a: The occupation mix is for NAICS 541800 and includes advertising and related services with different workflows.
a: O*NET task importance does not measure time, wage weight, feasibility, or existing automation.
a: The Muck Rack survey reports individual tool use rather than the share of agency labor technically substitutable.
a: The injected labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and may be distorted where agency receipts include third-party campaign or event pass-through.
rho: Tool use and self-reported speed or quality are not audited implementation or labor savings.
rho: Muck Rack mixes agency and in-house professionals and does not isolate LMM firms.
rho: IPRN covers member agencies across more than 22 countries and does not state a respondent count on the fetched page.
rho: Current high adoption reduces the remaining not-yet-implemented opportunity even as it supports future workflow acceptance.
e: No source measures eligibility under the frozen recurring-or-repeat outsourced LMM lens.
e: The injected firm count uses a 15.65% broad Business & Consumer Services margin and may misclassify high-margin counsel boutiques or lower-margin execution-heavy agencies into the EBITDA band.
e: The code includes lobbying and political consulting as well as conventional PR, creating customer and cyclicality variation.
s5: Gallup covers all US employer businesses rather than PR agencies or the LMM band.
s5: PRovoke's transaction universe is not restricted to the United States, control deals, or the frozen EBITDA band.
s5: Many observed targets below $6 million in fees are likely below the LMM EBITDA threshold.
s5: Founder and rainmaker dependence can make nominal agencies difficult to transfer without long earn-outs.
q: The PRSA guide establishes fee structures but does not measure their prevalence or commercial retention.
q: The guide predates current AI adoption and modern procurement practices.
q: Retention varies sharply between hourly media-relations execution, fixed projects, crisis availability retainers, lobbying, and strategic counsel.
d5: The BLS output forecast is for NAICS 541800, not public relations agencies alone.
d5: Occupation growth includes in-house PR staff and workers outside agencies.
d5: IPRN is a global member network rather than a representative US LMM sample.
d5: The estimate holds quality and the current service basket constant, excluding wholly new AI-visibility services.
o: No source directly measures the future operator-required share of PR service demand.
o: Agency survey statements about human judgment and strategic trust may reflect provider optimism.
o: Rapid improvement in autonomous monitoring, content generation, journalist targeting, sentiment analysis, and crisis simulation could push the value below the range.
o: Lobbying and political consulting are more operator-dependent than routine publicity, widening intra-code variation.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Businesses, institutions, nonprofits, public bodies, political interests, and individuals buy ongoing counsel and campaign execution through monthly or annual retainers, hourly fees against budgets, fixed project fees, and hybrids. Repeatability is strongest in corporate reputation, media relations, public affairs, crisis preparedness, executive positioning, and integrated communications.
lens.excluded_activities: Advertising agencies, media buying and representation, direct-mail and display advertising, standalone market-research firms, software vendors, captive in-house communications teams, shells, non-control financing vehicles, solo practitioners below the LMM band, and firms limited to isolated nonrepeat publicity projects.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market public relations agencies providing recurring or repeat outsourced communications campaigns, media relations, reputation and crisis counsel, public affairs, lobbying, political consulting, executive communications, stakeholder engagement, and related PR consulting to external clients.

a.bridge: BLS measures the occupation mix for four-digit NAICS 541800, not six-digit 541820, and O*NET describes representative PR occupations rather than every agency role. I map high exposure to brainstorming, research, media monitoring, first drafts, editing, releases, speeches, social posts, reporting, lists, and routine administration; I map lower exposure to relationship building, executive coaching, live media response, crisis judgment, lobbying, negotiation, and final reputational accountability. The broader industry contains more advertising, design, media-buying, and distribution work, so it may overstate visual-production exposure while understating 541820's concentration in PR specialists and senior counsel.
caveats: The occupation mix is for NAICS 541800 and includes advertising and related services with different workflows.
O*NET task importance does not measure time, wage weight, feasibility, or existing automation.
The Muck Rack survey reports individual tool use rather than the share of agency labor technically substitutable.
The injected labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and may be distorted where agency receipts include third-party campaign or event pass-through.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit 541820 payroll occupation table combined with agency time-and-motion data by seniority, workflow, wage, and current AI use would materially change the wage-weighted estimate.
a.rationale: PR agencies perform large volumes of language, research, monitoring, and document work, and the reported use cases show AI already fits those workflows. Public relations specialists also spend substantial time maintaining external relationships, advising management, coaching spokespeople, and responding under time pressure, while managers own crisis plans and trusted relationships. Those human-intensive duties hold the current not-yet-automated exposed share near half at base.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for four-digit NAICS 541800 to grow 2.7% annually from 2024 to 2034, implying about 1.14 over five years, while PR-specialist employment grows 5% over the decade. I center below the broad industry output implication because 541800 includes advertising, media buying, and related services, while retaining modest growth due to continuing reputation, outreach, social-media, crisis, and stakeholder needs. IPRN's global member-agency optimism supports direction but likely overstates typical US LMM conditions.
caveats: The BLS output forecast is for NAICS 541800, not public relations agencies alone.
Occupation growth includes in-house PR staff and workers outside agencies.
IPRN is a global member network rather than a representative US LMM sample.
The estimate holds quality and the current service basket constant, excluding wholly new AI-visibility services.
d5.change_evidence: A six-digit BLS or Census real-output forecast for 541820, supported by inflation-adjusted US PR-agency billings, client retention, backlog, and budget surveys by end market, would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Fast-moving public opinion, fragmented media, social platforms, crises, political and regulatory complexity, and reputation management sustain demand for the current service basket. PR budgets remain discretionary and cyclical, traditional media access is harder, and clients may reduce or postpone programs in downturns. AI self-service is reserved for the operator-required share rather than deducted again from demand quantity.

caveats: No source measures eligibility under the frozen recurring-or-repeat outsourced LMM lens.
The injected firm count uses a 15.65% broad Business & Consumer Services margin and may misclassify high-margin counsel boutiques or lower-margin execution-heavy agencies into the EBITDA band.
The code includes lobbying and political consulting as well as conventional PR, creating customer and cyclicality variation.
e.change_evidence: A six-digit establishment census or commercial database segmented by independence, normalized EBITDA, contract duration, retainer share, repeat-client revenue, and principal service would directly establish the eligible share.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is intrinsically an external campaign and consulting service, and LMM-scale employer agencies commonly maintain ongoing client relationships through retainers and repeat programs. Ineligibility remains for project-only publicity shops, election-cycle entities without repeat commercial service, misclassified advertising or event businesses, captive units, and founder-personal practices whose work is not organizationally repeatable.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541820 ($9,020,215,177) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541820 ($16,903,608,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $271,021 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541820); class <5: 6,209 firms, avg revenue/firm $468,889 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $73,381 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 927 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,829,861 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $286,373 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 551 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,315,470 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $518,871 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 348 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,294,121 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,611,030 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 73 firms, avg revenue/firm $38,745,397 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,063,655 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 49 firms, avg revenue/firm $82,824,510 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,962,036 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 421

caveats: No source directly measures the future operator-required share of PR service demand.
Agency survey statements about human judgment and strategic trust may reflect provider optimism.
Rapid improvement in autonomous monitoring, content generation, journalist targeting, sentiment analysis, and crisis simulation could push the value below the range.
Lobbying and political consulting are more operator-dependent than routine publicity, widening intra-code variation.
o.change_evidence: Client cohort data showing five-year agency-spend retention, insourcing, AI self-service, software substitution, and service unbundling by PR engagement type would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Clients can self-serve or buy software for basic releases, social copy, monitoring summaries, research, contact lists, and routine reporting. Most complex LMM-agency quantity should still require an accountable operator because media and government relationships, crisis response, political judgment, executive coaching, stakeholder negotiation, factual validation, brand risk, and trusted counsel depend on context and responsibility. The operator-required share is highest in crisis, public affairs, lobbying, regulated sectors, and reputation strategy and lowest in commodity content and distribution.

q.bridge: PRSA documents hourly fees against monthly or annual budgets, fixed project fees, and flat monthly retainers, but not the five-year retained share of AI benefit. I map fixed projects and flat retainers to higher initial retention, hourly budget work to lower retention because fewer hours reduce billings, and annual review or renewal to gradual customer sharing across all models. The guide is US and industry-specific but old and nonquantitative, so it may miss current performance pricing and stronger procurement pressure; the direction of error is uncertain.
caveats: The PRSA guide establishes fee structures but does not measure their prevalence or commercial retention.
The guide predates current AI adoption and modern procurement practices.
Retention varies sharply between hourly media-relations execution, fixed projects, crisis availability retainers, lobbying, and strategic counsel.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of current US 541820 contracts and account P&Ls showing fee model, utilization, realization, renewal pricing, scope expansion, client concessions, and margin before and after AI deployment would materially move retention.
q.rationale: Flat retainers and fixed projects allow agencies to retain near-term productivity through higher throughput or margin, especially when clients buy access, judgment, or outcomes rather than hours. Hourly-budget arrangements pass benefits through mechanically, and periodic retainer reviews, competitive pitches, AI-native entrants, and client transparency demands should share additional gains over five years. Differentiated crisis, public-affairs, and reputation counsel can retain more than commodity content work.

rho.bridge: Muck Rack surveyed 1,013 PR professionals across agency and in-house settings in late 2024, while IPRN surveyed a self-selected global network of independent agencies in 2026. I translate widespread individual use and regular member-agency deployment into the share of remaining exposed work operationalized within five years, discounting for incomplete policies, limited disclosure, privacy, plagiarism, output quality, client transparency demands, training, and workflow integration. IPRN members are likely more internationally connected and innovation-oriented than typical US LMM agencies, biasing adoption upward; smaller agencies may nevertheless implement faster than large governed organizations.
caveats: Tool use and self-reported speed or quality are not audited implementation or labor savings.
Muck Rack mixes agency and in-house professionals and does not isolate LMM firms.
IPRN covers member agencies across more than 22 countries and does not state a respondent count on the fetched page.
Current high adoption reduces the remaining not-yet-implemented opportunity even as it supports future workflow acceptance.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal US PR-agency survey reporting production hours, utilization, rework, client approvals, policy coverage, and headcount avoided by workflow before and after AI deployment would materially move this value.
rho.rationale: Three-quarters of surveyed PR professionals already used GenAI in 2025, with common use in brainstorming, drafting, editing, and research, and IPRN reported regular use across most member agencies in 2026. The behavioral barrier is therefore low. Implementation will deepen, but confidential client information, factual error, brand voice, disclosure, legal and political sensitivity, and the need for senior review prevent full conversion of exposure into dependable labor avoidance.

s5.bridge: Gallup found that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while PRovoke Media identified 99 PR-firm transactions in 2024, including many small targets. I use the owner-plan rate as a broad anchor and the observed sector activity as evidence that agency transfers clear, then discount gifts, internal transfers, minority investments, non-US transactions, sub-LMM micro deals, unexecuted intentions, and founder-dependent firms that cannot complete a qualifying control sale. The deal count has no eligible-firm denominator and may bias upward because it is global and includes firms with only a minor PR component.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses rather than PR agencies or the LMM band.
PRovoke's transaction universe is not restricted to the United States, control deals, or the frozen EBITDA band.
Many observed targets below $6 million in fees are likely below the LMM EBITDA threshold.
Founder and rainmaker dependence can make nominal agencies difficult to transfer without long earn-outs.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, PRovoke, or Davis+Gilbert data isolating completed US 541820 control transactions and failed sale processes by eligible-firm denominator over five years would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: PR agency M&A is demonstrably active, and the concentration of 2024 deals among smaller firms shows a functioning buyer market below the large holding companies. Employer agencies can transfer client rosters, sector expertise, teams, and methods, but founder relationships, change-of-control churn, earn-outs, political affiliations, and key-person reputational capital reduce the qualifying five-year probability.

records.principal_driver: Large volumes of writing, research, monitoring, editing, and reporting create implementable AI leverage inside recurring agency relationships.
records.principal_weakness: Commodity PR production is easy for clients and AI-native competitors to self-serve, constraining both operator-required demand and retained pricing benefit.

caveats: The labor-opportunity primitives are directionally grounded but not quantitatively identified: the four-digit occupation mix and O*NET tasks do not establish a wage-weighted six-digit exposure share, and adoption surveys do not measure dependable labor avoidance.

The rho bridge uses current tool adoption as a proxy for five-year operationalization of remaining exposed work; the researcher discloses the gap, but audited implementation, rework, utilization, and headcount evidence is absent.

The q base is an assumption-heavy proxy. The PRSA guide verifies fee structures and renewal mechanics but provides no current fee-model prevalence or empirical pass-through, so the base case's capped C factor should not be read as substantiated retention.

The availability factor rests on an honest estimate of eligible firms and a broad succession proxy: Gallup includes gifts and transfers across all employer businesses, while PRovoke's global transaction universe includes sub-LMM, non-control, and partly PR-adjacent deals without an eligible denominator.

Demand evidence is broader than the lens. The BLS output series is four-digit 541800, the occupation outlook includes in-house roles, and the IPRN survey is a self-selected global member network rather than representative US LMM evidence.

The code definition spans conventional PR, lobbying, political consulting, and PR consulting. Marking the lens non-heterogeneous understates meaningful differences in cyclicality, human relationship intensity, fee models, and AI self-service risk, although the packet acknowledges this variation and provides wide ranges.

The central e and o values remain explicitly labeled estimates with no direct population measurement, and the margin-bridged firm count is also an estimate; the resulting MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action are therefore appropriate.

sources_audited.note: The Census manual supports the 541820 definition and expressly includes lobbying, political consulting, and PR consulting, but it does not measure the scored eligibility or operator-required shares.

The May 2023 BLS table supports the stated occupation and wage mix for NAICS 541800, including PR managers and specialists, writers and editors, market-research analysts, project managers, and office support; it does not isolate six-digit 541820 or measure AI exposure.

O*NET supports the cited specialist tasks, including media response, releases, social content, relationship maintenance, management advice, coaching, and strategy, but task importance is not time, wage weight, substitutability, or operator-required demand.

O*NET supports the cited manager duties, including crisis plans, executive speeches and interviews, client-government-media relationships, budgets, supervision, and public response, but it does not quantify automation exposure or future operator-required demand.

Axios reports Muck Rack's late-2024 survey of 1,013 PR professionals, 75% GenAI use, and the cited use cases and policy/training concerns. The mixed agency/in-house survey measures use and perceptions, not labor avoidance or five-year implementation.

The IPRN page reports 82% regular AI use, 67% forecasting growth, conditional client acceptance and transparency, and human judgment and strategic trust as differentiators. It is a global member-agency survey across more than 22 countries and does not disclose a respondent count on the page.

The PRSA guide supports ongoing and project relationships, annual renewal, hourly fees against monthly or yearly budgets, project fees, and flat monthly retainers. It is old and supplies neither fee-model prevalence nor a measured retained share of AI benefits.

PRovoke supports 99 transactions in 2024, 62% involving firms below $6 million in revenue or fees, and the prominence of independent and private-equity buyers. Its global, partly PR-adjacent transaction count has no eligible-firm denominator and is not limited to qualifying US control sales.

Gallup supports that 22% of US employer-business owners planned within five years to sell or transfer ownership. The measure spans all employer businesses and includes transfers rather than completed qualifying sales.
BLS supports the cited 2024-2034 real-output projection for NAICS 541800 from $197.3 billion to $257.3 billion in chained 2017 dollars, or 2.7% annually, but not six-digit 541820 demand.

BLS supports 5% projected PR-specialist employment growth from 2024 to 2034 and the cited reputation, online-news, community/customer-relations, and social-media drivers. The occupation includes in-house roles and workers outside PR agencies.

records.title: Public Relations Agencies

caveats: a: No six-digit occupation mix for the narrowed 541990 lens was found; the published 541900 occupation table also contains photographic and veterinary services.
a: The DOE examples are site-specific demonstrations and do not establish average labor savings across commercial firms.
a: The injected compensation ratio uses 2024 wages and 2022 receipts, so its level has a stated vintage mismatch outside this primitive.
rho: Research pilots and large global testing companies have more technical resources than typical firms in the lens.
rho: Implementation rates will differ sharply by asset accessibility, inspection modality, and customer regulation.
e: The Census industry definition lists activities but does not measure their establishment or revenue shares.
e: The injected count of 863 is estimated using a broad Business and Consumer Services margin bridge and may not represent the margin profile of inspection firms.
e: Firms may use 541990 for government contracting even when their underlying activity fits another industry, increasing classification noise.
s5: Gallup covers all US employer businesses rather than inspection firms and measures plans rather than completed transactions.
s5: EY covers the global testing, inspection, and certification sector, which includes larger laboratories and certification businesses outside the lens.
s5: The eligible population is only an estimated subset of the injected industry count.
q: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-derived benefits for independent US inspection providers.
q: Pricing power varies between emergency or specialized work and competitively bid routine patrols.
q: The global large-company cost pass-through evidence may overstate lower-middle-market bargaining power.
d5: Pipeline mileage is an installed-base measure, not purchased outsourced inspection quantity.
d5: The EY outlook is global, broader than the lens, and nominal rather than constant-price demand.
d5: The narrowed lens spans end markets with different construction and maintenance cycles.
o: Construction and building inspectors are not the same population as 541990 infrastructure and commodity inspectors.
o: Some routine visual patrol quantity may become fully automated or self-served by asset owners.
o: Operator need is highest in safety-critical regulated work and lower in low-consequence repeat observations.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Infrastructure owners, industrial operators, utilities, commodity owners, insurers, and other businesses purchase documented inspections or verification under recurring service agreements or repeat work orders, commonly priced per visit, asset, route, project, or labor period.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 541990 is a residual code combining physical inspection, appraisal, dispute resolution, consumer counseling, brokerage, forecasting, and other unrelated services. The lens is narrowed to recurring business-to-business physical-asset inspection and technical verification because these activities share a coherent field-data, expert-review, and accountable-report workflow; the narrowing is for analytical coherence rather than attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Appraisal, arbitration, conciliation, consumer credit counseling, credit repair, business and patent brokerage, weather forecasting, handwriting analysis, one-off expert assignments, captive internal inspection units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced physical-asset inspection and technical verification, including visual pipeline and power-line inspection, commodity inspection, and fire-suppression equipment testing or inspection without repair or installation.

a.bridge: BLS describes the tasks of construction and building inspectors rather than the narrowed 541990 population, and DOE and PHMSA document drone and machine-learning applications at selected infrastructure sites. I map remote data collection, image triage, planning, scheduling, log preparation, report drafting, and anomaly classification to the inspection workflow while leaving physical access, sensor setup, nuanced diagnosis, validation, and customer accountability largely unexposed. The BLS occupation is more building-focused and may overstate code review work, while demonstration projects may overstate feasible automation; the net direction of error is uncertain.
caveats: No six-digit occupation mix for the narrowed 541990 lens was found; the published 541900 occupation table also contains photographic and veterinary services.
The DOE examples are site-specific demonstrations and do not establish average labor savings across commercial firms.
The injected compensation ratio uses 2024 wages and 2022 receipts, so its level has a stated vintage mismatch outside this primitive.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time study across independent pipeline, utility, commodity, and fire-system inspection firms that separates current manual work from already-deployed drones, computer vision, and workflow software would materially change this value.
a.rationale: The exposed opportunity is concentrated in labor-intensive access and observation that drones can reduce, plus digital-image review, document comparison, scheduling, and report production that AI can accelerate. It is limited by field presence, testing-device operation, mechanical judgment, exception handling, and certification-grade review. The estimate excludes remote and sensor workflows already deployed today.

d5.bridge: EY forecasts the broader global testing, inspection, and certification market, while PHMSA documents a large, stable US pipeline installed base and stronger integrity-management inspection requirements. I convert that evidence to modest real growth in the current US physical-asset inspection basket, reducing the global nominal outlook for inflation, differences in geography and service scope, and the maturity of domestic pipeline mileage. The proxy may overstate growth if customers internalize inspections or infrastructure spending slows, and may understate it if regulation, extreme weather, grid expansion, and aging assets accelerate inspection frequency.
caveats: Pipeline mileage is an installed-base measure, not purchased outsourced inspection quantity.
The EY outlook is global, broader than the lens, and nominal rather than constant-price demand.
The narrowed lens spans end markets with different construction and maintenance cycles.
d5.change_evidence: Segment-level US revenue and inspection-unit histories for pipeline, power-line, commodity, and fire-system providers, deflated by service prices and paired with customer maintenance budgets and regulatory inspection calendars, would materially change this value.
d5.rationale: A persistent installed base and safety regulation support repeat inspection demand even without rapid construction growth. PHMSA requires post-extreme-weather inspections and stronger integrity assessments, while broader testing and inspection demand benefits from regulation, infrastructure investment, and quality requirements. Cyclicality in energy, construction, and commodity volumes keeps the downside close to current real demand.

caveats: The Census industry definition lists activities but does not measure their establishment or revenue shares.
The injected count of 863 is estimated using a broad Business and Consumer Services margin bridge and may not represent the margin profile of inspection firms.
Firms may use 541990 for government contracting even when their underlying activity fits another industry, increasing classification noise.
e.change_evidence: A commercial establishment roster matched to 541990 tax records and coded by primary activity, recurring revenue, customer type, ownership independence, normalized EBITDA, and contract transferability would directly establish the eligible share.
e.rationale: The Census definition confirms that visual pipeline and power-line inspection, commodity inspection, and fire-suppression testing belong in 541990, but the same code also contains many unrelated and often episodic services. A material minority of the estimated lower-middle-market population is therefore expected to fit the narrowed recurring inspection lens, with the wide range reflecting the absence of a firm-level activity mix.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541990 ($27,219,781,409) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541990 ($35,548,824,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $261,287 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541990); class <5: 18,002 firms, avg revenue/firm $408,515 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $63,933 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,791 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,600,634 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $250,499 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 911 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,055,095 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $478,122 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 717 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,186,007 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,437,610 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 146 firms, avg revenue/firm $36,320,979 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,684,233 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 123 firms, avg revenue/firm $86,630,772 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $13,557,716 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 863

o.bridge: BLS describes a related inspector occupation in which remote inspection reduces headcount but certification, worksite examination, testing-device use, mechanical knowledge, detailed findings, and written accountability remain. PHMSA and DOE show drones and AI changing how evidence is collected and reviewed rather than eliminating the inspection obligation. I translate this to the share of year-five service quantity still requiring an accountable inspection provider. Public-sector licensing evidence may overstate formal accountability in some private 541990 niches, while safety-critical utility work may require even more operator oversight.
caveats: Construction and building inspectors are not the same population as 541990 infrastructure and commodity inspectors.
Some routine visual patrol quantity may become fully automated or self-served by asset owners.
Operator need is highest in safety-critical regulated work and lower in low-consequence repeat observations.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement policies, regulator guidance, insurer requirements, and service contracts specifying whether automated outputs may be accepted without human review or third-party responsibility would materially change the operator-required share.
o.rationale: Sensors, drones, and computer vision can replace travel, access labor, and first-pass image review, but customers still need a party to plan the inspection, validate sensor coverage and model output, investigate anomalies, apply standards, maintain records, and stand behind the conclusion. BLS expects remote inspection to reduce inspector demand rather than eliminate the occupation.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-derived benefits for independent US inspection providers.
Pricing power varies between emergency or specialized work and competitively bid routine patrols.
The global large-company cost pass-through evidence may overstate lower-middle-market bargaining power.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract and invoice sample showing pricing unit, renewal timing, escalation and benchmarking clauses, customer concentration, technician compensation, rebid outcomes, and gross-margin changes after technology deployment would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: Per-visit, per-asset, route, and fixed-work-order pricing can let an operator retain near-term savings from faster data capture, image triage, and report production. Retention decays as customers rebid contracts, demand productivity concessions, compare technology-enabled providers, or bring remote monitoring in-house. High labor intensity and technician incentive needs also share part of the benefit internally.

rho.bridge: DOE and PHMSA demonstrate remote inspection, multisensor data capture, and deep-learning image processing in public infrastructure settings, while EY discusses AI adoption in the broader global testing, inspection, and certification sector. I translate demonstrated technical feasibility into a five-year implementation share for smaller US inspection firms, then haircut for equipment integration, data quality, aviation and site access, false-positive risk, customer approval, and workforce change. Demonstration and large-company evidence likely overstates near-term implementation for lower-middle-market firms.
caveats: Research pilots and large global testing companies have more technical resources than typical firms in the lens.
Implementation rates will differ sharply by asset accessibility, inspection modality, and customer regulation.
rho.change_evidence: A cohort study of lower-middle-market inspection firms reporting workflow-level deployment, capital cost, model validation, customer acceptance, realized hours saved, and exception rates after a full inspection cycle would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: Remote capture and automated image analysis are operational rather than purely speculative, and inspection providers face strong safety and productivity incentives. Implementation remains constrained by fragmented asset data, heterogeneous field conditions, sensor capital, proof-of-accuracy requirements, and the need to integrate outputs into regulated customer workflows.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale or transfer intentions for US employer-business owners across industries, while EY documents sustained acquisition activity in the broader global testing, inspection, and certification market. I haircut the employer-owner intention rate because intentions may not close, transfers may be internal or non-control, and small specialist firms can depend on owner credentials and relationships. Active sector consolidation partly offsets that haircut. The proxy likely overstates completed qualifying transfers if the narrowed firms are especially owner-dependent.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses rather than inspection firms and measures plans rather than completed transactions.
EY covers the global testing, inspection, and certification sector, which includes larger laboratories and certification businesses outside the lens.
The eligible population is only an estimated subset of the injected industry count.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, and specialist brokers, matched to the eligible US firm roster and distinguishing control acquisitions from internal transfers, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Employer-owner succession creates a plausible supply of firms, and inspection markets have strategic and sponsor acquisition interest. Actual control availability is lower than stated intent because credentials, customer approvals, contract assignment, and key-person knowledge can prevent a qualifying close.

records.principal_driver: Remote sensing and AI can remove substantial access, review, and reporting effort while safety and compliance obligations keep inspection demand recurring.
records.principal_weakness: The residual NAICS code is so heterogeneous that the eligible inspection population and the relevance of the injected industry-wide inputs are unusually uncertain.

caveats: The cited Census URL did not expose the claimed 541990 activity detail in its current rendered page, so the factual membership basis for the narrowed lens was not independently verifiable from S1 during this audit; e is already labeled ESTIMATE, and no alternative value supported a tier or action change.

The a, rho, and o bases translate one federal-site drone example, one pipeline R&D project, and a different inspector occupation into the narrowed commercial population. Those bridges and likely overstatement direction are disclosed, but the sources do not quantify representative workflow exposure, adoption, or operator-required share.
S8 is broad global TIC evidence dominated by larger players and supports direction rather than the quantitative rho, s5, q, or d5 values for US lower-middle-market physical-inspection firms.
The q base is an honest estimate: major-company pass-through of inflationary cost increases is not direct evidence that independent inspection firms retain 53% of AI-derived benefits over five years.

Pipeline mileage and integrity rules support recurring need but not outsourced purchased quantity across the full lens; the PHMSA gathering series also has a visible reporting break beginning in 2022.
Gallup measures intended sales or transfers across all employer businesses, not completed external control acquisitions of qualifying inspection firms; the haircut and this limitation are disclosed.

sources_audited.note: The cited Census NAICS page was reachable, but its current rendered content exposed only the generic NAICS search interface and 2022 manual link, not the asserted 541990 activity list. This weakens source verification for lens membership but does not independently establish a different eligible-share estimate.
The BLS table is explicitly for NAICS 541900 and lists photographic services and veterinary services among the industries within that broader aggregate, supporting the stated limitation.

BLS supports the cited inspection, testing-device, logging, reporting, physical, and judgment tasks and says remote inspections reduce inspector need while safety and quality sustain demand. It describes construction and building inspectors, not the narrowed 541990 population.

DOE documents Savannah River Site drone inspections across tanks, piping, power distribution, and other assets, with reduced labor, access risk, time, and cost and continued personnel review. It is one federal-site example and does not quantify typical commercial workflow exposure or five-year adoption.

The PHMSA project supports development and field validation of multisensor UAS collection, image processing, condition classification, and deep-learning video review for routine pipeline inspection. It is a completed research project, not evidence of representative lower-middle-market deployment or realized labor savings.
PHMSA directly states that the rule requires post-extreme-weather pipeline inspections and strengthens integrity-management assessment requirements.

PHMSA reports a large transmission and gathering mileage base through 2025 and a roughly stable transmission total. Mileage is an installed-base measure and does not establish outsourced inspection volume; gathering-series comparability also changes sharply beginning in 2022.

EY supports global TIC growth of 4-5% annually, major-player cost pass-through, AI as a strategic imperative, and nearly 1,000 global acquisitions over twelve years. It does not directly measure US lower-middle-market physical-inspection implementation, AI-benefit retention, completed qualifying transfers, or real demand growth.

Gallup directly reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years in its fall 2024 survey. The measure is cross-industry intent and includes transfers rather than completed qualifying control sales.
