records.title: All Other Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services

caveats: a: The available occupation table is four-digit NAICS 541600 and includes several consulting businesses with very different staffing.
a: O*NET importance scores do not measure time, wage weight, feasibility, or current automation.
a: McKinsey's marketing-function value estimate is not a task-exposure measure and includes client-side marketing rather than marketing consultants.
a: Because AI is already widely used in marketing, some technically exposed work is no longer part of the current not-yet-automated opportunity.
rho: The surveys measure marketing organizations and decision-makers, not the frozen US LMM consulting-firm population.
rho: Adoption and self-reported efficiency are not the same as audited operational labor avoidance.
rho: Gartner's campaign-oriented sample may overweight creative execution that NAICS 541613 excludes.
rho: McKinsey's maturity survey is European and includes very large companies.
e: No source measures eligibility under the frozen recurring-or-repeat outsourced LMM lens.
e: The injected firm count uses a 15.65% broad Business & Consumer Services margin and may misclassify high-margin boutiques or lower-margin implementation-heavy firms into the EBITDA band.
e: Boundary errors with advertising, public relations, market research, custom software, and general management consulting are plausible.
s5: Gallup covers all US employer businesses, not marketing consulting or the LMM band.
s5: The Ciesco population is global media and marketing, including large media, agency, technology, and minority transactions.
s5: Founder dependence may require long transition periods or make apparent firms nontransferable.
q: No source directly measures the discounted five-year retained share of implemented AI benefit.
q: Monadnock confirms negotiated time-based consulting rates but does not report their prevalence versus fixed fees or retainers in current US LMM marketing consulting.
q: Value capture varies sharply between strategic advice, recurring fractional leadership, analytics, and implementation-heavy work.
d5: BLS real-output projections are for all management, scientific, and technical consulting services, not marketing consulting.
d5: Occupation growth includes in-house market researchers and workers in other industries.
d5: The estimate holds service quality and the current basket constant, excluding wholly new AI-transformation offerings.
o: No source directly measures the future share of marketing-consulting quantity requiring an accountable external operator.
o: The boundary between advice and execution is fluid, and AI may move work to software vendors, agencies, or in-house teams rather than eliminate it.
o: Rapid progress in autonomous research, first-party-data analysis, scenario modeling, and campaign planning could push the result below the range.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External businesses and organizations buy project-based strategy engagements, ongoing retainers, time-based advisory work, and sometimes implementation support. Revenue is primarily professional fees under fixed-scope, hourly, daily, weekly, team-rate, retainer, or hybrid arrangements; repeatability comes from continuing planning, measurement, product, pricing, customer, and go-to-market needs.
lens.excluded_activities: Advertising campaign creation and media placement, public-relations agencies, direct-mail agencies, standalone market-research and polling firms, software vendors, captive in-house marketing teams, shells, non-control financing vehicles, solo practitioners below the LMM band, and firms limited to nonrepeat one-off advice.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced marketing operating advice and assistance, including marketing objectives and policy, market and sales forecasting, product and pricing strategy, customer-service and sales-management consulting, licensing or franchise planning, and marketing planning and strategy.

a.bridge: BLS measures the occupation mix for four-digit NAICS 541600 rather than six-digit 541613, and O*NET describes representative marketing occupations rather than all firm employees. I map high exposure to information gathering, competitive analysis, forecasting support, survey design, data processing, report and presentation drafting, campaign evaluation, and routine administration; I map lower exposure to executive discovery, pricing judgment, stakeholder alignment, relationship development, negotiation, and final accountable recommendations. The broader 541600 mix includes environmental, scientific, and operations consultants plus more technical staff, so it likely understates 541613's concentration in marketing knowledge work but may overstate exposure if marketing consultants spend more time in senior client interaction.
caveats: The available occupation table is four-digit NAICS 541600 and includes several consulting businesses with very different staffing.
O*NET importance scores do not measure time, wage weight, feasibility, or current automation.
McKinsey's marketing-function value estimate is not a task-exposure measure and includes client-side marketing rather than marketing consultants.
Because AI is already widely used in marketing, some technically exposed work is no longer part of the current not-yet-automated opportunity.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit 541613 payroll occupation table combined with representative time-and-motion studies by consultant level and workflow, including current AI use, would materially change the wage-weighted exposed share.
a.rationale: Marketing consulting is dominated by digital information, analysis, language, and presentation workflows. Market-research specialists prepare reports, collect and analyze customer and competitor data, forecast trends, and assess program effectiveness, while marketing managers develop strategies, pricing, and plans. Generative and analytical AI can substitute or avoid hiring across much of the production layer, but human judgment and client-facing work keep the base below two-thirds.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for four-digit NAICS 541600 to grow 2.6% annually from 2024 to 2034, implying about 1.14 over five years, while employment of market research analysts and marketing specialists grows 7% over the decade. I center modestly below the broad output implication because 541600 includes faster-growing technical and environmental specialties, while retaining a positive base due to expanding data, measurement, pricing, customer, and strategy complexity. The occupation series spans the whole economy and the industry series is broader than 541613, so error is two-sided.
caveats: BLS real-output projections are for all management, scientific, and technical consulting services, not marketing consulting.
Occupation growth includes in-house market researchers and workers in other industries.
The estimate holds service quality and the current basket constant, excluding wholly new AI-transformation offerings.
d5.change_evidence: A six-digit BLS or Census real-output forecast for 541613, supported by inflation-adjusted US consulting bookings, backlog, and marketing-budget surveys by client size, would materially change the year-five ratio.
d5.rationale: Organizations face continuing pressure to understand customers, measure marketing effectiveness, set prices, launch products, and adapt channels, supporting modest real demand growth for the current advisory basket. Demand is cyclical and marketing budgets are readily cut in downturns, while AI may create new advisory needs even as it changes delivery. AI self-service displacement is reserved for the operator-required share rather than deducted again here.

caveats: No source measures eligibility under the frozen recurring-or-repeat outsourced LMM lens.
The injected firm count uses a 15.65% broad Business & Consumer Services margin and may misclassify high-margin boutiques or lower-margin implementation-heavy firms into the EBITDA band.
Boundary errors with advertising, public relations, market research, custom software, and general management consulting are plausible.
e.change_evidence: A six-digit firm census or commercial database segmented by independence, normalized EBITDA, revenue model, repeat-client share, and principal service would directly establish the eligible share.
e.rationale: The six-digit definition is inherently an outsourced B2B advisory service, and firms large enough to reach the LMM EBITDA band generally need multiple employees and repeat clients. Ineligibility remains for misclassified agencies or software-led businesses, captive units, founder-only practices, and consultancies whose revenue is dominated by isolated one-off engagements without a repeat service relationship.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541613 ($34,007,872,680) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541613 ($79,535,987,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $282,178 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541613); class <5: 41,556 firms, avg revenue/firm $395,427 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $61,884 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,444 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,727,383 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $270,335 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,925 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,595,461 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $562,690 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,662 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,903,738 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,393,435 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 274 firms, avg revenue/firm $47,364,223 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,412,501 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 186 firms, avg revenue/firm $120,739,011 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $18,895,655 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,936

caveats: No source directly measures the future share of marketing-consulting quantity requiring an accountable external operator.
The boundary between advice and execution is fluid, and AI may move work to software vendors, agencies, or in-house teams rather than eliminate it.
Rapid progress in autonomous research, first-party-data analysis, scenario modeling, and campaign planning could push the result below the range.
o.change_evidence: Client cohort data comparing five-year consulting spend, project insourcing, AI self-service, and vendor replacement by engagement type and client size would materially change the operator-required share.
o.rationale: Clients can increasingly self-serve routine competitive scans, draft plans, summaries, forecasts, content ideas, and baseline analysis using AI and integrated marketing platforms. A substantial share should still require an accountable external operator for proprietary-data interpretation, market and pricing judgment, independent challenge, executive alignment, cross-functional facilitation, implementation design, and ownership of consequential recommendations. Operator requirement is higher in complex product, pricing, channel, and organizational engagements than in templated small-business marketing plans.

caveats: No source directly measures the discounted five-year retained share of implemented AI benefit.
Monadnock confirms negotiated time-based consulting rates but does not report their prevalence versus fixed fees or retainers in current US LMM marketing consulting.
Value capture varies sharply between strategic advice, recurring fractional leadership, analytics, and implementation-heavy work.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of US 541613 contracts and project P&Ls showing fee structure, utilization, realization, renewal pricing, gross margin, and client concessions before and after AI deployment would materially move retention.
q.rationale: Fixed-scope projects and retainers can initially retain AI productivity through higher throughput or margin, while hourly, daily, weekly, and team-rate work shares gains mechanically through fewer billable units. Over five years, sophisticated client procurement, rate negotiations, new AI-native competitors, lower entry barriers, and renewal repricing should transfer a meaningful portion of gross benefit to customers. Proprietary benchmarks, trusted advice, and outcome-oriented scopes preserve some capture.

rho.bridge: Gartner surveyed 418 marketing leaders about client-side campaign use, and McKinsey surveyed European marketing decision-makers rather than US LMM marketing consultancies. I translate broad adoption, use in creative and strategy development, and reported mature-user efficiency into the share of the remaining exposed consulting opportunity implemented within five years, discounting for immature operating models, weak data foundations, confidentiality, quality review, change management, and client acceptance. Client-side leaders may have larger technology budgets but slower governance than LMM consultancies, making the net direction of error uncertain.
caveats: The surveys measure marketing organizations and decision-makers, not the frozen US LMM consulting-firm population.
Adoption and self-reported efficiency are not the same as audited operational labor avoidance.
Gartner's campaign-oriented sample may overweight creative execution that NAICS 541613 excludes.
McKinsey's maturity survey is European and includes very large companies.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of US marketing consultancies reporting workflow-level production hours, utilization, rework, client acceptance, and headcount avoided before and after AI deployment would materially move this value.
rho.rationale: Marketing organizations already use GenAI for creative work, evaluation, reporting, campaign planning, and strategy, so adoption barriers are lower than in many services. However, only a small minority in McKinsey's European sample described their use as mature, and both sources identify ROI, data, technology, and operating-model constraints. Five years should enable broad bounded deployment without converting every exposed task into dependable labor avoidance.

s5.bridge: Gallup found that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while Ciesco observed active global media-and-marketing M&A and rising Agency Services & Strategy activity. I use Gallup's 22% as an upper anchor and discount gifts, internal transfers, unexecuted plans, non-US and non-consulting deals, minority investments, and founder-dependent firms that cannot complete a qualifying control sale. Active sector deal flow supports a nontrivial probability but does not supply a usable denominator.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses, not marketing consulting or the LMM band.
The Ciesco population is global media and marketing, including large media, agency, technology, and minority transactions.
Founder dependence may require long transition periods or make apparent firms nontransferable.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, or broker data isolating completed US marketing-consulting control transactions and failed sale processes by eligible-firm denominator over five years would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: LMM employer consultancies can be transferable through client contracts, recurring retainers, data assets, methods, and multi-person teams, and broad marketing M&A demonstrates buyer interest. Still, stated intentions do not equal completed control transactions, and founder relationships, key-person selling, earn-outs, and client-change-of-control risk can prevent or delay qualification.

records.principal_driver: High-wage research, analysis, forecasting, writing, and presentation workflows create broad AI leverage in a labor-intensive advisory model.
records.principal_weakness: The same tools that improve consultant productivity enable client self-service and AI-native competition, limiting durable demand and commercial retention.

caveats: The labor-exposure base is a judgmental translation from a four-digit consulting occupation table and representative O*NET occupations; no source measures wage-weighted task exposure for six-digit 541613, but the PROXY label, bridge, and 0.42-0.71 range disclose this limitation.

The implementation base translates client-side campaign adoption and self-reported European marketing efficiency into five-year labor avoidance for US LMM consultancies; this is directionally informative but population- and construct-distant, and is honestly labeled PROXY.

The transferability estimate combines broad employer-owner intentions with global media-and-marketing deal counts that include agencies, technology, large transactions, and non-control deals; neither source supplies a qualifying completed-sale denominator.

The retention primitive is an ESTIMATE supported only by the existence of negotiated time-based rate structures; the source does not establish contract-mix prevalence, repricing, or realized post-AI value capture.

The demand projection bridges from four-digit consulting output and an economy-wide occupation outlook, so faster-growing scientific or technical consulting and in-house marketing employment may bias the 541613 base upward.

The operator-required share is an ESTIMATE without direct client-cohort evidence, and continuing improvement in self-service research, analysis, and planning tools could move it below the registered range.
The injected LMM firm count is itself margin-bridged and estimate-classified; its uncertainty is excluded from the published scenario envelope because dataset anchors are held fixed.

The lens is coherent with the stated recurring-or-repeat outsourced LMM objective, but boundary leakage among marketing consulting, advertising, public relations, market research, software, and broader management consulting remains substantial.

sources_audited.note: The Census 2022 NAICS manual is reachable and is the authoritative source for the 541613 industry definition and adjacent-industry boundaries used by the lens.

The BLS table supports the cited May 2023 occupation mix for four-digit NAICS 541600, including management analysts, market research specialists, marketing managers, project managers, and office support, but it does not establish the six-digit 541613 mix.

O*NET lists report preparation, customer and competitor data collection, forecasting, survey design, effectiveness measurement, analysis, and client-facing communication tasks for market research analysts and marketing specialists.
O*NET lists marketing strategy, policy, pricing, financial evaluation, forecasting, research, coordination, and advisory tasks for marketing managers.

Gartner reports the cited 418-leader survey, 27% limited or no campaign GenAI adoption, 77% creative-development use and 48% strategy-development use among adopters, plus ROI concerns; the population is client-side marketing organizations and campaign work rather than US LMM marketing consultancies.

McKinsey supports the cited 5%-15% of marketing-spend value estimate and general knowledge-work use cases, but that functional value estimate does not measure wage-weighted task exposure in marketing consulting.

McKinsey reports a survey of 500 European senior marketing decision-makers, 6% mature GenAI use, 22% average efficiency gains among leaders, and data, technology, strategy, and operating-model barriers; the mixed European population is not US LMM marketing consultancies and the gains are self-reported.

Gallup reports that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, but intentions across all employer businesses do not establish completed qualifying control transfers for LMM marketing consultancies.

Ciesco reports 2,306 global media-and-marketing transactions in 2024 and 32% year-over-year growth in Agency Services & Strategy activity, supporting active broad deal flow but not an eligible-firm denominator or completed US marketing-consulting control-sale probability.

BLS reports NAICS 541600 real output rising from $493.3 billion in 2024 to $639.7 billion in 2034, a 2.6% compound annual increase, but the projection covers all management, scientific, and technical consulting rather than six-digit 541613.

Monadnock documents negotiated US consulting master-service-agreement data and hourly, daily, and weekly rates, with marketing consulting coverage, but the page does not establish the present prevalence of fee structures or the five-year retained share of AI benefit.

BLS projects 7% employment growth for market research analysts and marketing specialists from 2024 to 2034 and attributes demand to data use, customer understanding, strategy measurement, and product-demand analysis; the occupation spans the whole economy and is not a 541613 output forecast.

records.title: Marketing Consulting Services

caveats: a: The occupational sources include in-house IT workers and adjacent computer-services industries.
a: O*NET importance scores are not hours or wage weights.
a: The frozen compensation ratio mixes 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and then applies an IPS harmonization factor.
a: The boundary between conventional automation and incremental AI is especially difficult in this software-intensive industry.
rho: The field experiment studied customer-service chats at one software company, not managed IT operations.
rho: The MSP survey is global, vendor-sponsored, and does not identify respondents by NAICS.
rho: Existing users may report time savings without reducing staffing or avoiding future hiring.
e: The supplied LMM count is margin-bridged from firm-size data using a broad services margin and is not a verified acquisition universe.
e: Modern MSP activity crosses hosting, systems design, consulting, security, and support classifications, making firm-level NAICS accuracy uncertain.
e: The UK MSP study confirms broad service mixing but is not evidence about U.S. 541513 eligibility.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and includes transfers that may not qualify as outside control events.
s5: The MSP deal count is global, uses a broader sector definition, and has no directly comparable denominator.
s5: Platform recapitalizations and add-on purchases may cause deal counts to overstate unique eligible sellers.
q: The pricing survey is from 2023, global, vendor-sponsored, and broader than the NAICS lens.
q: Security and cloud vendor costs can rise with supported users or endpoints even when technician labor falls.
q: Customers remain responsible for their own cyber risk, which can intensify contract scrutiny and benefit sharing.
d5: Employment is not service quantity and automation can reduce labor while demand rises.
d5: The strongest market-growth evidence covers global MSPs rather than U.S. computer facilities management.
d5: Recurring security, cloud, and consulting revenue may be classified outside 541513.
d5: A shift from client facilities to provider-owned cloud infrastructure can lower this NAICS service basket even if total outsourced IT spending grows.
o: Accountability requirements differ by customer sector, contract, and system criticality.
o: Provider responsibility and customer legal liability are distinct, so formal contracts do not prove every task requires a human.
o: The evolution of trusted agent permissions is unusually uncertain over the five-year horizon.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers are businesses and institutions that outsource some or all day-to-day IT operations. Revenue commonly recurs under managed-services agreements priced per user, per device, as tiered bundles, or as fixed monthly subscriptions, with project, incident, and resale revenue as supplements. Service levels, security duties, supported assets, user counts, and response obligations shape price and delivery cost.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal IT departments, temporary staffing, custom software development, systems-integration design, pure consulting, operation of the provider's own hosting or data-processing infrastructure, hardware resale without managed operations, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms primarily providing recurring outsourced management, operation, monitoring, administration, maintenance, and user or network support for external customers' computer systems or data-processing facilities, including on-site and hybrid delivery consistent with NAICS 541513.

a.bridge: BLS and O*NET describe computer support specialists and network and systems administrators across all employers, not the wage-weighted occupation mix of private NAICS 541513 firms. I map ticket intake, knowledge retrieval, user guidance, documentation, monitoring interpretation, routine diagnostics, access administration, scripting, and change preparation to current AI exposure, while discounting physical installation, novel incidents, security judgment, architecture, client governance, and work already automated by remote-monitoring tools and chatbots. The proxy can overstate exposure because task importance is not time and existing automation is substantial, but it can understate exposure in sales, administration, and management roles omitted from the occupation mapping.
caveats: The occupational sources include in-house IT workers and adjacent computer-services industries.
O*NET importance scores are not hours or wage weights.
The frozen compensation ratio mixes 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and then applies an IPS harmonization factor.
The boundary between conventional automation and incremental AI is especially difficult in this software-intensive industry.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted time study from representative facilities-management providers showing hours by occupation and task, current RMM, PSA, scripting and chatbot automation, ticket complexity, and pre-AI versus AI-assisted handle time.
a.rationale: Core support and administration work is highly digital and information-intensive. O*NET emphasizes computer use, problem solving, information processing, documentation, monitoring, and user communication, all of which AI can assist. BLS also identifies ongoing chatbot and routine-task automation, so the estimate removes a meaningful portion already automated and keeps the base below three-fifths.

d5.bridge: BLS occupational projections, NIST outsourcing guidance, MSP revenue surveys, and global market commentary measure labor, customer behavior, or broader managed-services revenue rather than constant-price U.S. 541513 service quantity. I infer moderate year-five volume growth from continued network complexity, cybersecurity demand, limited small-business expertise, and outsourcing economics, while discounting headline market growth for price, cloud resale, security products, geographic scope, and services classified outside 541513. Cloud migration and automation can shrink client-site facilities work, so broad MSP forecasts likely overstate growth for this specific code.
caveats: Employment is not service quantity and automation can reduce labor while demand rises.
The strongest market-growth evidence covers global MSPs rather than U.S. computer facilities management.
Recurring security, cloud, and consulting revenue may be classified outside 541513.
A shift from client facilities to provider-owned cloud infrastructure can lower this NAICS service basket even if total outsourced IT spending grows.
d5.change_evidence: A U.S. forecast combining 541513 real revenue, managed endpoint and user counts, contract starts and churn, cloud migration by customer size, in-house IT hiring, cyber-services attach rates, and price deflators, with hosting and resale removed.
d5.rationale: Organizations still need networks maintained, users supported, systems secured, and incidents resolved, while smaller customers often lack the budget or expertise to staff these functions internally. BLS explicitly notes that outsourcing supports external network services. Against that, chatbots, automated administration, direct cloud platforms, and migration away from client-operated facilities can reduce the current service basket, justifying a broad downside.

caveats: The supplied LMM count is margin-bridged from firm-size data using a broad services margin and is not a verified acquisition universe.
Modern MSP activity crosses hosting, systems design, consulting, security, and support classifications, making firm-level NAICS accuracy uncertain.
The UK MSP study confirms broad service mixing but is not evidence about U.S. 541513 eligibility.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level review of the supplied 583 candidates using service descriptions, recurring-revenue share, customer concentration, contract assignability, ownership, normalized EBITDA, and primary NAICS verification.
e.rationale: The official industry definition is centered on external management and operation of customer computer systems and support services, which generally fits the recurring outsourced-service lens. The discount covers mixed consulting or resale businesses, project-led contractors, concentrated single-client operations, captive-like arrangements, and firms whose normalized economics or control rights do not fit the lens.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541513 ($11,535,095,371) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541513 ($30,832,208,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $266,793 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541513); class <5: 3,040 firms, avg revenue/firm $354,248 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $55,440 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 692 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,447,064 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $226,465 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 484 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,262,322 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $510,553 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 429 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,791,140 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,375,813 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 154 firms, avg revenue/firm $37,324,773 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,841,327 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 151 firms, avg revenue/firm $116,924,159 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $18,298,631 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 583

o.bridge: BLS, O*NET, NIST, and CISA describe IT tasks, outsourcing choices, and provider accountability rather than the share of year-five 541513 quantity requiring an operator. I classify routine answers, simple remediation, monitoring triage, documentation, and some access changes as potentially self-served or software-only, but retain an accountable operator for heterogeneous-system judgment, physical work, security governance, privileged access, incident response, vendor coordination, SLA ownership, and customer communication. This may overstate operator need if autonomous agents gain trusted execution rights, or understate it because escalating cyber risk and regulation can increase demand for named accountable providers.
caveats: Accountability requirements differ by customer sector, contract, and system criticality.
Provider responsibility and customer legal liability are distinct, so formal contracts do not prove every task requires a human.
The evolution of trusted agent permissions is unusually uncertain over the five-year horizon.
o.change_evidence: Measured shares of incidents and administration changes resolved autonomously without technician approval, customer acceptance of software-only SLAs, cyber-insurer and regulator requirements for human oversight, and provider-versus-software churn from PSA and RMM platforms.
o.rationale: Software and AI can remove a meaningful slice of low-complexity support, yet the remaining service involves privileged access and consequential operational and security decisions. NIST advises formal managed-services agreements and emphasizes that outsourcing does not transfer the customer's liability; this supports continued demand for a responsible operating organization rather than an unattended tool alone.

q.bridge: Kaseya measures billing-model prevalence among a global MSP survey sample rather than the discounted share of implemented benefits retained by U.S. NAICS 541513 operators. I translate the dominance of per-user, per-device, bundled, and fixed-subscription billing into relatively strong short-run retention because price is tied to supported units or outcomes rather than technician hours, then discount for renewal repricing, competitive bidding, customer benchmarking, service-level reinvestment, vendor-license costs, and incident risk. The vendor sample may overrepresent mature recurring-revenue MSPs and therefore overstate retention for project-heavy facilities managers.
caveats: The pricing survey is from 2023, global, vendor-sponsored, and broader than the NAICS lens.
Security and cloud vendor costs can rise with supported users or endpoints even when technician labor falls.
Customers remain responsible for their own cyber risk, which can intensify contract scrutiny and benefit sharing.
q.change_evidence: Customer contract and invoice data from U.S. facilities-management providers showing revenue by billing model, renewal cadence, indexation, cost-plus clauses, SLA credits, repricing after automation, churn, and gross-margin movement.
q.rationale: Most surveyed MSP billing models decouple monthly price from actual labor hours, allowing productivity gains to remain with the provider during a contract term. Over five years, customers can renegotiate, competitors can lower prices, and operators may spend savings on coverage, security, or service quality, leaving the provider with a substantial but incomplete share.

rho.bridge: A controlled customer-support study and a global MSP vendor survey measure realized productivity and reported AI use in populations adjacent to, but broader than, NAICS 541513. I translate demonstrated conversational assistance and MSP ticket automation into the share of remaining exposed work likely to reach routine production within five years, then discount for fragmented PSA, RMM and documentation systems, customer-data controls, hallucination risk, cybersecurity approval, exception handling, and change management. Vendor survey self-selection and promotional framing likely bias implementation upward, while rapid integration into incumbent tools could make the estimate conservative.
caveats: The field experiment studied customer-service chats at one software company, not managed IT operations.
The MSP survey is global, vendor-sponsored, and does not identify respondents by NAICS.
Existing users may report time savings without reducing staffing or avoiding future hiring.
rho.change_evidence: Longitudinal production metrics from PSA and RMM vendors covering AI feature activation, technician adoption, autonomous-resolution share, escalation and rollback rates, security incidents, labor hours per managed endpoint, and customer SLA outcomes.
rho.rationale: The field evidence establishes that AI assistance can raise issue-resolution throughput, and the MSP survey reports that AI is already eliminating repetitive work for a material minority. Digital workflows and existing ticket data ease deployment. Implementation will remain incomplete because service providers hold privileged access, support heterogeneous customer estates, and must validate security-sensitive actions.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans among U.S. employer-business owners across industries, while Drake Star counts global MSP transactions without a matching eligible-firm denominator. I use the broad employer intention rate as a starting anchor and the documented MSP consolidation market as evidence that qualified buyers and transaction infrastructure exist, then discount for internal transfers, gifts, unrealized intentions, add-on deals outside NAICS 541513, and repeat acquisitions by a small set of consolidators. Intentions likely overstate completed qualifying transfers; active sector consolidation and LMM transferability push in the opposite direction.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and includes transfers that may not qualify as outside control events.
The MSP deal count is global, uses a broader sector definition, and has no directly comparable denominator.
Platform recapitalizations and add-on purchases may cause deal counts to overstate unique eligible sellers.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year U.S. transaction database filtered to verified NAICS 541513 targets, linked to a stable eligible-firm denominator and coded for control status, seller type, recurring revenue, EBITDA band, and internal versus external succession.
s5.rationale: Employer firms show materially greater transfer intent than nonemployers, and managed-services deal activity is active and sponsor-backed. Recurring contracts and standardized tool stacks aid transferability, but not every transaction is a control sale and not every owner intention closes, so the five-year probability remains well below one-half.

records.principal_driver: Recurring unit-based contracts and information-heavy support workflows create a credible path to AI-enabled technician leverage and avoidable hiring.
records.principal_weakness: The modern MSP evidence base is broader than NAICS 541513, while cloud migration and existing automation can erode the specific client-facility service basket being screened.

caveats: The a primitive maps occupational task descriptions and importance scores across all employers into a 541513 wage-weighted exposed-work share without industry time-use evidence. The PROXY label, bridge, and broad downside disclose this limitation, and an evidence-state downgrade would not change the model-conditioned action.

The rho base of 0.66 extrapolates from a single-company customer-support experiment and vendor-reported MSP AI use to five-year production implementation. The direction is supported, but the magnitude remains judgmental and is appropriately disclosed as a broad-sector proxy.

The q base of 0.70 is inferred from billing-model prevalence rather than observed benefit retention. Its entire registered range exceeds the 0.50 factor cap, making C insensitive across the envelope even though renewal repricing, competition, and reinvestment are acknowledged; the available evidence does not establish that an honest base correction would cross the threshold needed to change the PRIORITY tier.

The d5 and o primitives rely on occupational projections, cybersecurity guidance, and broad global MSP evidence rather than constant-price U.S. 541513 volume or autonomous-resolution data. Their high base D reflects a plausible but weakly measured judgment, with cloud migration and code-boundary downside explicitly disclosed.

The firm-opportunity factor rests on a margin-bridged estimate of 583 firms and an estimated 0.83 eligibility share. The UK source confirms that MSP offerings and classifications mix substantially but cannot validate U.S. firm-level eligibility; MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS correctly carry this limitation into the action.

The s5 base of 0.24 is slightly above Gallup's 22 percent five-year employer intention rate even though the bridge also describes discounts for nonqualifying and unrealized transfers. Global deal activity supplies directional counterevidence but no comparable denominator; moving the base to the Gallup anchor would not change the base tier or action.

sources_audited.note: The 2022 NAICS manual defines 541513 as on-site management and operation of clients' computer systems or data-processing facilities, includes related support services, and cross-references provider-facility data processing to 518210.

BLS supports the cited user and network support duties, 24-hour service needs, remote and on-site delivery, chatbot-related user-support decline, and outsourcing support for external network services; it is an occupation-wide source rather than a 541513 work mix.

BLS supports the cited administration duties and explicitly discusses DevOps substitution, Networks-as-a-Service outsourcing, and increasing automation of routine tasks; the population spans all employers.

The O*NET profile is updated in 2026 and supports the cited mix of oversight, setup, diagnostics, user assistance, documentation, information processing, monitoring, and physical inspection or repair tasks; importance scores are not time shares.

The BLS summary reports a 14 percent increase in successfully resolved customer issues, larger gains for newer agents, and continuing human responsibility, while clearly describing a customer-support setting at one software company.

Kaseya reports that about 30 percent of MSPs say AI helps eliminate repetitive tasks, names ticket handling and threat detection, and documents tool-integration bottlenecks; it is vendor-sponsored and broader than U.S. 541513.

The 2023 Kaseya survey reports 1,091 respondents and the cited prevalence of per-user, per-device, combined, fixed-fee, bundled, a-la-carte, and incident billing; it does not directly observe five-year benefit retention.

Gallup's fall-2024 survey reports that 22 percent of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years versus 9 percent of nonemployers; these are intentions and include transfers that need not become qualifying control sales.

NIST supports the cited small-business outsourcing rationale, use of formal managed-services agreements, and retained customer liability; it does not quantify retention, service-volume growth, or operator-required share.

The UK government-commissioned 2025 study defines ongoing external IT administration and monitoring, documents diversified offerings and firm-size patterns, and explains classification limitations; it is an adjacent UK market rather than U.S. NAICS evidence.

The Q4 2025 Drake Star report states 466 global MSP transactions in 2025, active strategic consolidation, and appetite for scaled providers with recurring revenue and professionalized operations; it provides no eligible U.S. 541513 denominator.
CISA describes RMM as continuous endpoint monitoring with unattended remote administration and warns that compromised MSP servers can expose thousands of customer networks.

sources.supports: The official definition states that 541513 covers on-site management and operation of clients' computer systems or data-processing facilities and related support services, while provider-owned data processing is classified in 518210.
sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual

sources.supports: BLS describes user and network support duties, round-the-clock service needs, on-site and remote work, projected user-support decline from chatbots, and outsourcing as a demand support for external network services.
sources.title: Computer Support Specialists, Occupational Outlook Handbook

sources.supports: BLS details installation, maintenance, security, performance, access, training, troubleshooting, and monitoring duties and notes increasing routine-task automation, DevOps substitution, and Networks-as-a-Service outsourcing.
sources.title: Network and Computer Systems Administrators, Occupational Outlook Handbook

sources.supports: The 2026 profile details task importance across system oversight, equipment setup, diagnostics, user answers, recordkeeping, training, information processing, monitoring, and physical repair.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Computer User Support Specialists

sources.supports: BLS summarizes a field study in which AI suggestions increased successfully resolved customer issues by 14 percent, with larger gains for less-experienced agents and human responsibility retained.
sources.title: AI improved the productivity of a Fortune 500 software company

sources.supports: Kaseya reports MSP workflow integration barriers and survey findings that 30 percent use AI to eliminate repetitive tasks, while ticket handling and threat detection are current applications.
sources.title: How MSPs Are Tackling Pressure, Burnout and Growth in 2025

sources.supports: A 2023 global survey with 1,091 respondents reports billing across combined per-user and per-device, per-user, fixed-subscription, per-device, bundled, a la carte, and incident models, with recurring fixed-unit structures predominant.
sources.title: MSP Pricing: A Guide to Managed IT Services Pricing

sources.supports: Gallup reports five-year sale or transfer intentions among U.S. employer-business owners, contrasts employer and nonemployer transferability, and documents its fall 2024 survey methods.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: NIST explains why small businesses outsource cyber work to MSPs, recommends formal agreements that define responsibilities and service levels, and states that customers retain liability for protecting systems and data.
sources.title: Building Your Small Business' Cybersecurity Team: From In-House to Outsourcing

sources.supports: The UK government-commissioned study defines ongoing external IT administration and monitoring, documents broad mixed service offerings, firm-size fragmentation, and classification limitations in an adjacent national market.
sources.title: Research on the Managed Service Providers Market 2025

sources.supports: Drake Star records 466 global MSP transactions during 2025, active strategic consolidation, and buyer preference for scaled providers with recurring revenue and professionalized operations.
sources.title: Drake Star Global MSP Report, Q4 2025

sources.supports: CISA describes RMM software as continuously monitoring endpoints and enabling unattended remote administration, while warning that compromise of provider platforms can extend into many customer networks.
sources.title: JCDC Remote Monitoring and Management Cyber Defense Plan

records.title: Computer Facilities Management Services

caveats: a: No six-digit occupation staffing table or task-time study was found for NAICS 541612.
a: SHRM surveys internal HR professionals and functions, not the screened consulting firms.

a: The frozen compensation intensity uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, a March 2026 benefits multiplier, and a harmonization adjustment; that vintage and construction uncertainty is separate from this task-share estimate.
rho: Adoption of any AI tool is not the same as operational implementation of exposed labor opportunity.
rho: Survey respondents work inside client organizations, whose incentives and technology stacks differ from consultancies.
rho: Employment-related AI rules and client policies are changing rapidly and can either slow deployment or create advisory implementation work.
e: No source measures eligibility under the specified earnings and recurring-service lens.
e: The industry includes actuarial, benefits, compensation, assessment, policy, and general personnel consulting with different recurrence and owner-dependence profiles.
e: The frozen firm count is an estimate bridged from SUSB size classes using a 15.65% margin assumption, so actual membership in the earnings band may differ materially.
s5: Gallup measures intentions rather than completed qualifying transfers and covers all employer businesses.
s5: Capstone combines HR services with staffing and does not provide the eligible-firm denominator on the fetched page.
s5: Owner dependence can turn an apparent transaction into an earnout-backed talent acquisition rather than a durable transfer.
q: No source directly measures retained AI benefit in lower-middle-market HR consulting.
q: Korn Ferry is a large global firm with digital, search, interim, and recruitment-process outsourcing businesses beyond NAICS 541612.
q: Efficiency may create additional capacity rather than cash savings when consultants remain salaried or demand is insufficient to fill released hours.
d5: The cited real-output series is four-digit and includes scientific and technical consulting outside HR.
d5: Occupation growth is not a direct measure of outsourced consulting quantity.
d5: Korn Ferry's fiscal 2025 consulting fee revenue fell 5%, underscoring sensitivity to client budgets and the economic environment.
o: SHRM reports respondent beliefs about internal HR functions, not observed substitution of external consulting purchases.
o: Operator need varies sharply between routine document production and high-stakes compensation, employee-relations, actuarial, or transformation advice.
o: Rapid improvement in enterprise HR platforms and legal-information tools could accelerate self-service beyond the base case.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Businesses and other organizations buy specialist advice through hourly or daily billing, fixed-fee projects, recurring retainers, repeat policy and compensation reviews, and implementation engagements; delivery is primarily expert labor supported by data, benchmarks, documents, and client workshops.
lens.excluded_activities: Recruiting and placement firms, professional employer organizations, temporary staffing, standalone training providers, captive internal HR teams, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms without recurring or repeat outsourced client service.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms repeatedly advising external clients on human-resource policies and procedures, employee benefits, compensation systems, wage and salary administration, personnel management, assessments, and related implementation support.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupation shares and wages for the broader NAICS 541600 consulting group, O*NET reports tasks for management analysts and HR specialists across employers, and SHRM surveys HR functions rather than independent NAICS 541612 consultants. I mapped research, data analysis, benchmarking, policy comparison, record preparation, drafting, reporting, scheduling, and proposal work to exposed tasks, while retaining interviews, diagnosis, facilitation, relationship work, negotiation, sensitive employee matters, and accountable recommendations as less exposed; the range is then qualitatively wage weighted using the broader-industry mix. NAICS 541600's scientific and technical consultants may bias the exposure estimate downward, while treating document-heavy professional work as substitutable rather than merely assisted may bias it upward.
caveats: No six-digit occupation staffing table or task-time study was found for NAICS 541612.
SHRM surveys internal HR professionals and functions, not the screened consulting firms.

The frozen compensation intensity uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, a March 2026 benefits multiplier, and a harmonization adjustment; that vintage and construction uncertainty is separate from this task-share estimate.
a.change_evidence: A representative time-and-motion study of independent US HR consultancies, assigning consultant and support-staff hours and compensation to research, analysis, drafting, administration, client interaction, facilitation, sales, and accountable judgment while identifying already-automated hours, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: The broader consulting group is dominated by management, business and financial, computer, and office occupations, which together represent about three-quarters of employment, and management analysts alone represent 11.91%. O*NET and BLS describe core work as gathering and organizing information, analyzing employment and financial data, developing alternatives, preparing policies and reports, and presenting recommendations. SHRM documents active AI use in writing, screening, search, communication, and other process-driven HR tasks. Much of the production layer is exposed, but client context, trust, empathy, interviews, facilitation, and judgment keep the wage-weighted share below the full knowledge-work envelope.

d5.bridge: BLS publishes constant-dollar output only for the broader NAICS 541600 consulting group and employment projections for management analysts and HR specialists, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for NAICS 541612. I use the broader group's 2.6% annual real-output projection as the main anchor, cross-check with 9% management-analyst and 6% HR-specialist employment growth over ten years, and haircut for the HR specialty's economic cyclicality and software self-service. Broader scientific and technical consulting growth may bias the estimate either upward or downward; employment growth can understate demand when productivity rises, while Korn Ferry's recent consulting decline shows the downside can be cyclical and sharp.
caveats: The cited real-output series is four-digit and includes scientific and technical consulting outside HR.
Occupation growth is not a direct measure of outsourced consulting quantity.
Korn Ferry's fiscal 2025 consulting fee revenue fell 5%, underscoring sensitivity to client budgets and the economic environment.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year Census or BEA revenue indexes for NAICS 541612 separated into price, hours, project mix, and quality, plus recurring-versus-discretionary revenue by service line, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: BLS projects real output for management, scientific, and technical consulting to grow 2.6% annually from 2024 to 2034. It projects management-analyst employment up 9% and specifically expects strong growth at smaller firms specializing in functions such as HR; HR-specialist employment is projected up about 6%. Demand for cost control, organizational change, compensation, benefits, workforce planning, and AI governance supports growth, while discretionary projects remain exposed to downturns and client budget cuts.

caveats: No source measures eligibility under the specified earnings and recurring-service lens.
The industry includes actuarial, benefits, compensation, assessment, policy, and general personnel consulting with different recurrence and owner-dependence profiles.
The frozen firm count is an estimate bridged from SUSB size classes using a 15.65% margin assumption, so actual membership in the earnings band may differ materially.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census in the screened earnings band coding repeat-client revenue, retainer revenue, client concentration, principal-originated sales, delivery-team depth, service classification, and assignability of contracts and data rights would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The official industry definition is inherently external advice and assistance, so the main eligibility haircut is not captive status but whether a firm has recurring or repeat outsourced revenue and a transferable delivery organization. Exclusions include one-off project shops, owner-personality practices with weak institutional client relationships, misclassified recruiting or outsourcing operations, unacceptable client concentration, nontransferable intellectual property or data rights, and firms too dependent on one principal's reputation or credentials.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541612 ($10,678,955,284) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541612 ($26,539,675,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $173,623 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541612); class <5: 5,881 firms, avg revenue/firm $394,584 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $61,752 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 651 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,244,538 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $194,770 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 412 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,382,619 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $372,880 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 431 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,933,174 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,241,542 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 144 firms, avg revenue/firm $29,228,493 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,574,259 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 122 firms, avg revenue/firm $121,304,852 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $18,984,209 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 575

o.bridge: SHRM measures beliefs and adoption inside HR functions, BLS describes management-analyst client work across specialties, and NIST addresses AI risk generally rather than the share of HR-consulting demand that retains an external accountable operator. I map services involving diagnosis, client-specific policy choices, sensitive data, negotiation, facilitation, change management, and accountable advice to operator-required demand, while allowing templates, basic benchmarking, routine analytics, and policy drafting to shift to client self-service or software. Internal HR respondents may overstate preference for human work and thus operator need; the independent-adviser value of external expertise may make the estimate conservative.
caveats: SHRM reports respondent beliefs about internal HR functions, not observed substitution of external consulting purchases.
Operator need varies sharply between routine document production and high-stakes compensation, employee-relations, actuarial, or transformation advice.
Rapid improvement in enterprise HR platforms and legal-information tools could accelerate self-service beyond the base case.
o.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of HR-consulting buyers that tracks spend eliminated, brought in-house, replaced by software, and retained with external advisers by service line, together with contract loss reasons and AI self-service usage, would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Software can supply information and draft common artifacts, so some current work will disappear or be self-served. Yet HR advice affects pay, benefits, employee relations, compliance, and organizational change. SHRM reports that 72% of HR professionals believe nontechnical barriers would prevent complete automation even with technical barriers removed; among that group, customer preference is cited by 87% and legal or regulatory barriers by 57%. NIST emphasizes context loss, bias, differentiated human responsibility, and oversight in human-AI decision systems. Most year-five quantity therefore still needs an accountable adviser, although less routinely than today.

caveats: No source directly measures retained AI benefit in lower-middle-market HR consulting.
Korn Ferry is a large global firm with digital, search, interim, and recruitment-process outsourcing businesses beyond NAICS 541612.
Efficiency may create additional capacity rather than cash savings when consultants remain salaried or demand is insufficient to fill released hours.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level data from independent HR consultancies showing revenue by hourly, day-rate, fixed-fee, retainer, and outcome-based billing, plus renewal repricing and realized margin changes after automation, would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Fixed-fee projects and retainers let a consulting firm retain productivity gains until repricing, but hourly and day-rate work directly links revenue to labor input and can convert faster delivery into fewer billable hours. BLS describes self-employed management analysts as paid by the hour or project and competitive proposals as specifying method, schedule, and cost. Korn Ferry reports 23% of total revenue from fixed-fee engagements and recognizes consulting revenue using hours incurred relative to estimated completion hours, illustrating a mixed, labor-referenced model. Competitive bidding, procurement, renewals, client demands for shared savings, and reinvestment in proprietary data and governance reduce retained benefit.

rho.bridge: SHRM measures adoption, use cases, and barriers inside US HR functions of organizations of all sizes, not five-year implementation of exposed labor in lower-middle-market HR consulting firms. I treat current small- and midsize-organization adoption as a starting point, add five years for diffusion into research, drafting, analytics, knowledge management, and administration, and haircut for data privacy, weak integrations, governance, client confidentiality, human review, and employment-law risk. Consultants may adopt faster than client HR teams because productivity is commercially valuable, biasing the proxy downward; client-imposed security and explainability requirements and the survey's measurement of any adoption rather than realized labor savings may bias it upward.
caveats: Adoption of any AI tool is not the same as operational implementation of exposed labor opportunity.
Survey respondents work inside client organizations, whose incentives and technology stacks differ from consultancies.
Employment-related AI rules and client policies are changing rapidly and can either slow deployment or create advisory implementation work.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year cohort of independent US HR consultancies measuring baseline and realized hours by workflow, tool deployment, client approval, security review, rework, error rates, and headcount avoided would materially change the value.
rho.rationale: SHRM reports AI implemented in 33% of small and 35% of midsize HR functions, with adoption concentrated in transactional, process-driven work. The same research finds weak ROI measurement, privacy and security concerns, outdated systems, integration gaps, and legal-governance uncertainty. Five years permits substantial implementation of exposed workflow, but sensitive personal data, quality control, bespoke client environments, and required human oversight make full conversion unlikely.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sell-or-transfer intentions among all US employer-business owners, while Capstone measures completed HR and staffing transactions across a broader sector rather than qualifying control transfers among eligible HR consulting firms. I anchor near Gallup's 22% intention rate, allow for active strategic and sponsor demand in compensation consulting and talent management, and offset that uplift for failed processes, internal transfers, minority investments, staffing deals, and owner-dependent firms that cannot transfer. Intentions likely bias completed transfer probability upward; broader small-business coverage and omission of sector buyer demand may bias it downward.
caveats: Gallup measures intentions rather than completed qualifying transfers and covers all employer businesses.
Capstone combines HR services with staffing and does not provide the eligible-firm denominator on the fetched page.
Owner dependence can turn an apparent transaction into an earnout-backed talent acquisition rather than a durable transfer.
s5.change_evidence: A PitchBook, GF Data, or DealStats cohort matched to a complete denominator of eligible US HR consulting firms, identifying control status, size, service mix, failed sale processes, internal transfers, and owner age, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Gallup finds that 22% of employer-business owners plan to sell or transfer ownership within five years. Capstone reports HR and staffing transaction activity rose 9.4% in 2024 and a further 9.3% year over year through the first quarter of 2025, with private strategic buyers and private-equity add-ons active and compensation consulting and talent-management providers attracting interest. A qualifying control transfer remains a minority event because plans do not always complete and not every reported transaction fits the lens.

records.principal_driver: High-value consultant time is spent producing research, analysis, and documents that AI can compress while clients continue paying for context-specific advice and accountability.
records.principal_weakness: Hourly billing and relationship-led delivery can prevent faster work from becoming retained profit, while routine offerings are unusually vulnerable to client self-service.

caveats: The 0.63 exposure base is a qualitative wage-weighted mapping from four-digit occupational shares and cross-employer task descriptions; no task-time or compensation-weighted study supports the numeric point for NAICS 541612.

The 0.58 implementation base extrapolates current internal-HR adoption and barriers into five-year realized labor conversion at consultancies; adoption of any tool is not evidence of avoided labor hours, and no consulting cohort is observed.

S7 is unweighted and internally inconsistent about respondent count (1,908 in the report narrative versus 1,722 in the methodology), which further limits precision of the adoption and barrier anchors.

Eligibility is an honest estimate without firm-level evidence, and the included actuarial, benefits, compensation, assessment, policy, and general personnel practices can differ substantially in repeat revenue, principal dependence, and transferability.

The five-year transfer estimate combines broad owner intentions with transaction growth in a sector that includes staffing; neither source supplies completed qualifying transfers divided by an eligible 541612 denominator.

Value retention is an honest estimate anchored partly to Korn Ferry: its 23% fixed-fee statistic is company-wide and its hours-based revenue recognition comes from a large global multi-segment firm, not the screened lower-middle-market population.

Demand durability uses four-digit real output and occupation growth as disclosed proxies for 541612 quantity; neither isolates HR consulting, and Korn Ferry's recent 5% consulting decline demonstrates meaningful cyclicality.

Operator-required demand is inferred from beliefs about complete automation in internal HR and a sector-agnostic NIST framework, not observed buyer spend retained with external advisers after self-service adoption.

The record marks the industry as non-heterogeneous even though its own caveat identifies materially different service lines; this should temper use of one set of primitives across the whole lens, though changing that flag alone would not alter the tier or action.

The tier interval holds the frozen compensation and firm-count anchors fixed even though compensation uses mixed vintages and harmonization and the firm count uses size-class averages plus a margin assumption; these limitations are disclosed and readiness remains MODEL_CONDITIONED.

sources_audited.note: The exact Census manual URL was indexed but timed out on direct PDF fetch; the official 2022 Census NAICS page independently confirms the 541612 definition, examples, and cross-references used for the lens.

The BLS table supports the stated NAICS 541600 occupational mix, including management analysts at 11.91%, but it is a four-digit consulting population and does not measure task time or wage-weighted AI exposure for 541612.
O*NET supports the cited management-analyst tasks in research, analysis, documentation, interviewing, and implementation, but it does not quantify task shares or isolate HR consultants.

O*NET supports policy interpretation, record preparation, employment-data analysis, reporting, employee-relations work, and management advice, but covers HR specialists across employers and is materially recruiting-heavy rather than specific to external 541612 consultants.

The 2025 SHRM survey supports AI use in writing job descriptions, screening, search, applicant communication, reported efficiency, and retention of relationship work; its unweighted U.S. HR-professional sample measures internal functions, not consulting-firm task exposure or realized labor conversion.

SHRM reports current automation and GenAI-use thresholds and widespread nontechnical barriers across HR occupations, but the results do not measure independent consultancies or five-year implementation of exposed labor.

The report supports 33% small-organization and 35% midsize-organization HR AI implementation, process-task concentration, weak ROI measurement, technical barriers, and the 72%/87%/57% human-preference and legal-barrier statistics. It is an unweighted internal-HR survey, and the page inconsistently states 1,908 respondents in the narrative and 1,722 participants in its methodology.

NIST supports the cited general points on context loss, bias, differentiated human responsibility, and documented human oversight, but is sector-agnostic and does not estimate HR-consulting adoption or operator-required demand.

BLS supports a 2.6% annual real-output projection for NAICS 541600 from 2024 to 2034, but this broader industry includes scientific and technical consulting and does not directly measure five-year 541612 demand.

BLS supports management-analyst duties, contractual proposals, hour-or-project payment for self-employed analysts, 9% ten-year employment growth, and expected strength at smaller HR-specialist consultancies. It is occupation-level evidence and does not establish the billing mix, retained AI benefit, or demand quantity for screened firms.

Korn Ferry's filing supports company-wide fixed-fee engagements at 23% of revenue, hours-incurred consulting revenue recognition, and a 5% fiscal-2025 consulting fee-revenue decline. Korn Ferry is a large global multi-segment firm, and the 23% figure is company-wide rather than a billing-mix observation for lower-middle-market 541612 firms.

Gallup supports the 22% five-year sell-or-transfer intention among U.S. employer-business owners and documents owner-dependence concerns, but measures broad intentions rather than completed qualifying control transfers in HR consulting.

Capstone supports 9.4% transaction growth in 2024, 9.3% year-to-date growth through first-quarter 2025, the buyer mix, and interest in compensation consulting and talent management. The promotional update combines HR with staffing and supplies no eligible-firm denominator or qualifying-control-transfer rate.
