o: Operator need varies sharply between simple consumer-product visualization and regulated or mechanically complex products.
o: Future AI-CAD, simulation, and automated prototyping capabilities are unusually uncertain.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Manufacturers, product companies, and entrepreneurs purchase scoped product-development projects, milestone phases, hourly design capacity, or retainers; repeat revenue comes from successive products, variants, redesigns, and movement from concept through production rather than a continuously delivered monthly service.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive in-house design departments, engineering-services firms whose primary work is application of engineering principles, industrial research-and-development firms, software-only design tools, independent noncommercial artists, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms without recurring or repeat external-customer engagements.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms supplying repeat outsourced industrial design and product-development work to external customers, including research, concept development, sketching and rendering, CAD and virtual modeling, material and manufacturing analysis, physical prototyping, usability and safety evaluation, and design-for-manufacture coordination.

a.bridge: S2 describes the industrial-designer occupation across all employing industries, while S3 measures the broader NAICS 541400 occupation mix, not wage-weighted exposure in six-digit 541420. I map client and user research synthesis, concept generation, sketching, rendering, virtual-model iteration, presentation preparation, documentation, and administrative work to higher AI exposure; physical prototyping, material and manufacturing judgment, safety evaluation, hands-on testing, and accountable client decisions to lower exposure. S3 shows design occupations at 42.55%, management at about 10%, engineering occupations at 5.74%, and office support at 10.15% in broader specialized design, which I use only as a rough wage-weighting frame. The broad industry is dominated by graphic and interior designers, likely biasing exposure upward relative to industrial design's physical constraints.
caveats: No public source provides a six-digit occupation mix for NAICS 541420; NAICS 541400 includes interior, graphic, and other design services.
Tool usage is not equivalent to task substitution, and professional CAD generation remains less mature than text or image generation.
The frozen labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a harmonization adjustment; the vintage mismatch may overstate labor intensity if receipts grew after 2022.
a.change_evidence: A wage-and-time study of employees at 20-30 US NAICS 541420 firms in the $1-10M EBITDA band, coded by project phase and separating already-automated minutes from manual work, would materially revise exposure.
a.rationale: Industrial design is digitally intensive but not purely digital. BLS identifies sketching, renderings, virtual models, CAD, research, collaboration, prototypes, production-cost analysis, safety, and manufacturability as core work. Current AI in design programs, CAD, and modeling directly affects a substantial portion of ideation and digital production, while physical validation and cross-functional accountability remain less substitutable.

d5.bridge: S8 projects chained-dollar output for broader NAICS 541400 from $57.5 billion in 2024 to $70.3 billion in 2034, a 2.0% annual rate, implying about 1.10 over five years. S2 separately projects industrial-designer employment growth of 3% over 2024-2034 and cites innovative high-tech products as a demand support. I haircut the five-year output path to 1.07 because 541400 is dominated by other design industries and because constant-quality demand for the current 541420 service basket may face manufacturing cycles and client self-service. The broad design-output proxy could bias either direction depending on the relative growth of interior and graphic design.
caveats: BLS publishes output projections at NAICS 541400, not 541420.
Chained-dollar output does not directly hold the industrial-design service basket and quality constant.
Demand is sensitive to manufacturing capital cycles, tariffs, product-launch budgets, and customer insourcing.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain six-digit constant-dollar receipts and project-volume data for NAICS 541420 from Census Service Annual Survey microdata or a custom IDSA member survey, holding project scope and quality constant and separating new-product, redesign, and production-support work.
d5.rationale: Product refreshes, new high-tech products, usability, sustainability, and manufacturing adaptation support continued industrial-design demand. The service is cyclical and exposed to customers delaying product launches, while better internal tools may let some manufacturers insource front-end work. The central case is modest real growth rather than rapid expansion.

caveats: No public six-digit dataset reports repeat-customer revenue or frozen-lens eligibility.
Project revenue can be repeat without being contracted recurring revenue, making eligibility sensitive to customer-history evidence.
Owner-led studios may meet the EBITDA band but remain dependent on the founder's reputation and relationships.
e.change_evidence: Use Data Axle or Dun & Bradstreet to sample NAICS 541420 firms in the frozen EBITDA band, then verify through CRM exports and owner interviews the share of trailing-three-year revenue from repeat customers, external customers, and transferable contracts.
e.rationale: Industrial-design establishments inherently provide external design services, and LMM firms usually need multiple projects and customers to support their scale. Repeat work can come through successive products, variants, and phased development. Eligibility is reduced because BLS describes designers competing for new project contracts, and some firms may depend on one-off startup engagements, a single anchor customer, founder relationships, or work that is effectively captive or nontransferable.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541420 ($2,501,498,861) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541420 ($2,906,073,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $233,495 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541420); class <5: 1,208 firms, avg revenue/firm $356,066 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $55,724 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 177 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,730,514 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $270,825 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 94 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,101,245 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $485,345 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 85 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,402,424 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,627,979 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 16 firms, avg revenue/firm $45,169,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,068,948 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 30 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,040,567 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,414,849 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 131

o.bridge: S2 measures duties and employment prospects for industrial designers across all industries, not the share of outsourced 541420 demand still requiring an accountable service operator. I classify early ideation, basic rendering, simple model generation, and presentation production as increasingly self-serviceable, but retain operator need for requirements discovery, user testing, physical prototypes, materials, production cost, safety, feasibility, manufacturer coordination, and approval. Because in-house industrial designers dominate the occupation and may face more complex products than outsourced firms, the proxy may bias operator need upward; rapid professional text-to-CAD improvement would also make it optimistic.
caveats: Occupational duties are not a direct measure of outsourced service quantity.
Operator need varies sharply between simple consumer-product visualization and regulated or mechanically complex products.
Future AI-CAD, simulation, and automated prototyping capabilities are unusually uncertain.
o.change_evidence: Track client cohorts at industrial-design firms by service phase and outcome, recording work retained with an outsourced operator, insourced, completed with software alone, sent to freelancers or offshore providers, or eliminated; supplement with procurement-leader interviews at product manufacturers.
o.rationale: AI can let clients generate concepts and visualizations without a consultancy, eliminating some low-complexity front-end quantity. The official task description nevertheless spans physical and accountable work that software alone cannot deliver: prototypes, safety and function evaluation, production economics, and coordination with engineers and manufacturers. Most complex year-five projects should still need an operator, though with less manual digital production.

q.bridge: S6 observes transparent hourly pricing in worldwide freelance industrial-design contracts, while S2 states that independent and outsourced designers look for projects and compete for contracts; neither measures LMM US firms' billing mix or five-year benefit retention. I infer immediate pass-through for hourly work, partial near-term retention on fixed-price or milestone phases, and stronger retention on differentiated retainers and outcome-based work. Freelance marketplaces are more price-transparent and commoditized than LMM consultancies, likely biasing the inferred retention downward; competitive rebidding of project work biases it upward if ignored.
caveats: Upwork data concern freelancers worldwide, not established US LMM firms.
No public source quantifies the industrial-design billing-method mix.
Retention depends on whether saved time expands iterations and throughput or simply lowers billed hours.
q.change_evidence: Review engagement letters, bid sheets, time records, project margins, change orders, and renewal or rebid outcomes from 15-20 LMM industrial-design firms, segmented into hourly, fixed-fee, milestone, retainer, and value-based work before and after AI adoption.
q.rationale: Industrial design is commonly bought as hourly capacity or a scoped project. Labor savings under hourly billing reduce invoiced hours directly, while fixed phases can retain savings until clients rebid, renegotiate scope, or observe faster delivery. Repeat competition and customer procurement should share a meaningful part of gains over five years, but firms can retain more by increasing concept breadth, iteration speed, and throughput rather than cutting price.

rho.bridge: S4 and S5 survey global leaders across architecture, engineering, construction, design and manufacturing, and media rather than US LMM industrial-design firms, and they measure AI use, plans, and perceived productivity rather than implemented labor opportunity. I anchor five-year diffusion to current use of AI-enabled design, CAD, and modeling tools and planned system integration, then discount for client confidentiality, fragmented CAD and product-lifecycle systems, training, verification, physical prototyping, and smaller-firm implementation capacity. Autodesk customers and leaders are likely more digitally mature than the target population, biasing the proxy upward.
caveats: Autodesk surveys are global, vendor-sponsored, and span much larger industries and organizations.
Reported productivity gains do not isolate labor substitution or sustained production deployment.
Physical-product liability, intellectual-property protection, and client approval can slow adoption beyond technical capability.
rho.change_evidence: Collect project-system telemetry and staffing records from LMM industrial-design firms for AI-assisted concept, rendering, CAD, documentation, and research workflows, including adoption dates, review time, rework, error escape, utilization, and avoided hires over at least 12 months.
rho.rationale: Implementation has a credible base: Autodesk reported AI in design programs at 43%, CAD at 27%, and modeling tools at 27% across Design and Make respondents, while 67% expected machine-learning models in systems within five years. Yet only 40% in the 2025 survey said their companies were approaching or had achieved AI goals. Production use therefore should expand materially without capturing all exposed work.

caveats: The BizBuySell evidence covers broad small service businesses and architecture/engineering, not NAICS 541420 or the EBITDA band.
No defensible public denominator exists for calculating a five-year industry transfer rate.
Undisclosed acqui-hires and private transactions may be missed, while internal partner buyouts are excluded from the primitive.
s5.change_evidence: Acquire five years of closed US industrial-design and product-development consultancy deals from PitchBook, GF Data, DealStats, BizBuySell, and specialist brokers; match them to eligible firms and code control transfers separately from talent hires, asset sales, and internal reorganizations.
s5.rationale: A functioning US small-business transfer market exists, and BizBuySell reported growing 2025 service-sector and architectural/engineering transaction activity. However, it does not publish an industrial-design-firm denominator or qualifying-transfer hazard. Industrial design firms can transfer when they have teams, process, portfolios, and repeat accounts, but founder-centered creative reputation, customer concentration, and project volatility reduce completed-transfer probability relative to more recurring service businesses.

records.principal_driver: AI can materially compress the digitally intensive concept-to-CAD workflow while industrial-design firms preserve value through physical validation and manufacturability expertise.
records.principal_weakness: Project billing, founder dependence, and client self-service make efficiency gains vulnerable to rebidding, pass-through, and demand elimination.

caveats: S1 could not be reopened because the cited Census PDF timed out repeatedly. This does not change the base tier or action because the source is primarily definitional and S2 independently supports the relevant duty mix.

The rho rationale mischaracterizes an Autodesk digital-transformation maturity statistic as 40% approaching or achieving AI goals. Correcting or removing this decorative statistic does not change the base tier because F remains the weakest link, and it cannot change the MODEL_CONDITIONED action.

S4 and S5 are global, vendor-sponsored, cross-industry adoption surveys rather than production telemetry from US LMM industrial-design firms; they support tool use and plans, not the magnitudes of a or rho. The packet labels both transformations PROXY and states the likely upward bias.

The base F score of 4.046 is only marginally above the PRIORITY weakest-link cutoff and rests on the estimated n, e, and s5 inputs. These are honestly labeled ESTIMATE with ranges that produce a CONDITIONAL low case, so the fragility is disclosed rather than a research defect.
S6 establishes a worldwide freelance hourly-pricing channel but does not establish the US LMM billing-method mix or five-year retention q. The bridge and caveats disclose this population gap.

S3 and S8 measure broader NAICS 541400, while S2 is occupational rather than an outsourced-firm quantity measure. The a, d5, and o bridges disclose those construct and population gaps and provide plausible error directions.

sources_audited.note: The cited Census PDF timed out on repeated direct reopen attempts, so its NAICS-definition claims could not be independently verified in this review. Its role is principally definitional, and the score-driving duty claims are substantially overlapped by S2.
BLS supports the cited industrial-designer duties, physical and digital workflow components, contract competition, 2024-2034 employment growth, and high-tech-product demand statement.
BLS reports the stated 2023 occupation shares for broader NAICS 541400; the packet correctly discloses that this is not six-digit NAICS 541420.

The report supports 43% use in design programs, 27% in CAD, 27% in modeling tools, 13% current machine-learning incorporation, and 67% expected incorporation within five years. It does not support the rationale's characterization that only 40% were approaching or had achieved AI goals; the report discusses digital-transformation maturity instead.
Autodesk states the 2,500-leader global survey population and the cited 98% AI-use, 84% productivity, and 59% agentic-AI figures.
Upwork documents hourly industrial-designer pricing and identifies the data as historical worldwide contracts, supporting the stated time-based contracting channel but not a US LMM billing mix.

BizBuySell reports 2025 service transactions up 4%, architecture and engineering transactions up 17%, and an approximately 50,000-business listing-and-sale data base; it does not provide an industrial-design transfer denominator, as disclosed.
BLS reports broader NAICS 541400 employment growth of 3.8% and chained-dollar output rising from $57.5 billion in 2024 to $70.3 billion in 2034 at a 2.0% annual rate.

records.title: Industrial Design Services

caveats: a: No public source observed a wage-weighted AI-exposed task share specifically for NAICS 541199.
a: The code mixes screen-based knowledge work with physical or identity-bearing services, so firm-level exposure varies widely.
a: The injected compensation-to-receipts ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis; that vintage mismatch is separate from this task-share estimate.
rho: Survey populations span countries, organization sizes, and professional-service sectors.
rho: Individual use may be shallow or unauthorized and therefore overstates durable operational implementation.
rho: Professional duties involving confidentiality, accuracy, supervision, and reasonable fees slow implementation and preserve review work.
e: No census or transaction source classifies firms by the screen's recurring/repeat outsourced-service and transferability criteria.
e: BizBuySell aggregates law firms with the much smaller legal-support subset and is not representative of LMM firms.
e: The injected firm count of 174 is itself an ESTIMATE bridged from SUSB size classes using a 2026 sector margin; eligibility is applied to that uncertain denominator.
s5: Gallup measures intentions for all employer businesses, not completed deals or this industry.
s5: BizBuySell skews toward smaller owner-operated businesses and combines law firms with legal-support businesses.
s5: The probability excludes closures, deaths without transferable operations, gifts without a qualifying control opportunity, and internal reorganizations.
q: Public pricing evidence is for law firms and in-house legal buyers, not the legal-support subsector.
q: The code's billing mix is not observed; patent-agent, paralegal, process-serving, and notary economics differ.
q: This primitive excludes demand or volume loss and implementation difficulty, which are represented elsewhere.
d5: BLS output is for four-digit legal services, not six-digit 541199.
d5: Constant-quality quantity is inferred from a constant-dollar output forecast rather than directly observed service units.
d5: The base extrapolates a ten-year compound rate over the five-year screen horizon.
o: State rules for notaries, process servers, document preparers, and unauthorized practice vary materially.
o: Legal requirements can preserve a human signature or accountable party without preserving today's labor hours or provider margins.
o: The service-line revenue mix of EBITDA-band firms is not publicly observed.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers are principally law firms, corporate legal departments, financial or government institutions, and repeat referral channels. Revenue commonly comes from per-document or per-service fees, project or matter fees, retainers, hourly work, and contracted high-volume workflows.
lens.excluded_activities: Offices of lawyers and attorneys; title abstract and real-estate settlement offices; arbitration and conciliation providers; captive legal-support units; shell or non-control financing vehicles; and walk-in, owner-only, or episodic consumer practices without transferable recurring or repeat operations.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced paralegal and legal-document preparation, legal research and filing, patent-agent filing and search support, process serving, and networked notary services to external law firms, businesses, institutions, or repeat consumer channels.

a.bridge: Clio measures potentially automatable hourly billable tasks across law firms, not a wage-weighted task inventory for NAICS 541199. BLS and O*NET descriptions show that paralegal work centers on research, document drafting, organization, and filing, closely matching the exposed workflows. I haircut Clio's 74% task ceiling for physical service, client interaction, quality review, and current partial automation. The broader-law-firm source likely overstates exposure for process serving and notarization but may understate exposure in document-preparation specialists.
caveats: No public source observed a wage-weighted AI-exposed task share specifically for NAICS 541199.
The code mixes screen-based knowledge work with physical or identity-bearing services, so firm-level exposure varies widely.
The injected compensation-to-receipts ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis; that vintage mismatch is separate from this task-share estimate.
a.change_evidence: Obtain a 2026 OEWS or payroll-vendor occupation-by-wage extract specifically for NAICS 541199, then administer time-and-motion surveys to LMM paralegal-outsourcing, process-serving, notary-platform, and patent-agent firms that classify paid hours by workflow and current automation status.
a.rationale: The industry's repeat legal-support work is unusually document- and information-intensive. Core paralegal duties include research, drafting, maintaining records, and filing; Clio estimates that as much as 74% of hourly billable law-firm tasks such as information gathering and data analysis could be automated with AI. The estimate covers the wage-weighted share of current, not-yet-automated work, with a substantial deduction for physical delivery, identity verification, client coordination, exception handling, and professional review.

d5.bridge: BLS projects constant-dollar output for the broader NAICS 541100 Legal Services industry from $348.5 billion in 2024 to $408.9 billion in 2034, a 1.6% annual rate. Compounding that rate for five years gives about 1.08, used as the base for current-basket demand in 541199. The broader category is dominated by offices of lawyers; legal-support demand may grow faster if work is unbundled and moved downstream, or slower if routine tasks are self-served, so the proxy's error direction is ambiguous.
caveats: BLS output is for four-digit legal services, not six-digit 541199.
Constant-quality quantity is inferred from a constant-dollar output forecast rather than directly observed service units.
The base extrapolates a ten-year compound rate over the five-year screen horizon.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain Census Service Annual Survey product-line revenue and volume series, court-filing and service-of-process counts, USPTO application volumes, state notarization or RON transaction counts, and five-year customer procurement forecasts specifically attributable to NAICS 541199 providers.
d5.rationale: Legal activity remains tied to litigation, regulation, intellectual property, filings, identity verification, and transactions. BLS's real-output projection implies moderate five-year expansion even as it projects essentially flat paralegal employment and explicitly attributes some labor restraint to AI productivity. A near-flat low case reflects substitution and self-service; the high case reflects continued legal-output growth plus outsourced volume.

caveats: No census or transaction source classifies firms by the screen's recurring/repeat outsourced-service and transferability criteria.
BizBuySell aggregates law firms with the much smaller legal-support subset and is not representative of LMM firms.
The injected firm count of 174 is itself an ESTIMATE bridged from SUSB size classes using a 2026 sector margin; eligibility is applied to that uncertain denominator.
e.change_evidence: Purchase a firm-level NAICS 541199 database with revenue, customer concentration, end-market, employee count, owner involvement, and recurring/repeat revenue fields; validate eligibility through a stratified sample of 50 firms in the injected EBITDA band.
e.rationale: NAICS 541199 is defined around specialized legal or paralegal services supplied by non-lawyer establishments, and its illustrative activities are external-service businesses. At LMM scale, outsourced paralegal, filing, process-serving, patent-support, and notary networks are more likely than micropractices to have repeat institutional customers and transferable staff and systems. The discount removes consumer walk-in, highly episodic, captive, and owner-personal practices.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: State rules for notaries, process servers, document preparers, and unauthorized practice vary materially.
Legal requirements can preserve a human signature or accountable party without preserving today's labor hours or provider margins.
The service-line revenue mix of EBITDA-band firms is not publicly observed.
o.change_evidence: Measure actual 2026 service-line revenue and transaction counts across LMM targets, then obtain customer make-versus-buy plans and regulatory counsel opinions for each service line on whether fully automated or self-service delivery would be legally valid by 2031.
o.rationale: A large share of routine research, drafting, intake, and document preparation can move to customers or software, and federal process can sometimes be waived. Yet important workflows still require an accountable person or service organization: federal summons service ordinarily uses an adult nonparty; remote notarization retains identity verification and a notary in 47 states and DC; and USPTO representation is restricted to recognized patent practitioners. Human review, chain of custody, exception resolution, and liability-bearing delivery preserve an operator role even when execution is heavily automated.

caveats: Public pricing evidence is for law firms and in-house legal buyers, not the legal-support subsector.
The code's billing mix is not observed; patent-agent, paralegal, process-serving, and notary economics differ.
This primitive excludes demand or volume loss and implementation difficulty, which are represented elsewhere.
q.change_evidence: Collect three years of customer contracts, invoices, rate cards, renewal histories, bid outcomes, and gross-margin bridges from target firms, tagged by hourly, fixed-fee, per-document, subscription, and cost-plus revenue, and interview the top 20 customers about AI-related repricing expectations.
q.rationale: Retention is split by billing form. Per-service, flat-fee, retainer, and contracted-volume firms can initially keep more labor savings, but hourly work transmits fewer billable hours directly to customers. Competitive renewal and procurement pressure then share savings: 71% of in-house legal professionals expect firms to change commercial models as AI use rises, while only 28% of law firms had changed pricing. Clio also reports hourly billing remains predominant and clients increasingly prefer flat fees. The discounted five-year base therefore assumes roughly half of implemented gross benefit remains with the operator.

rho.bridge: Thomson Reuters surveys legal and other professional-services organizations globally rather than US LMM NAICS 541199 firms, and reports adoption or planned adoption rather than the fraction of exposed opportunity operationally implemented. I map 40% organization-wide use in 2026, only 19% with no plan, and widespread weekly individual use into a five-year workflow-depth estimate, while discounting for quality assurance, confidentiality, unauthorized-tool use, fragmented systems, and change management. Inclusion of large firms likely raises measured governance capacity, while LMM firms may deploy simpler tools faster.
caveats: Survey populations span countries, organization sizes, and professional-service sectors.
Individual use may be shallow or unauthorized and therefore overstates durable operational implementation.
Professional duties involving confidentiality, accuracy, supervision, and reasonable fees slow implementation and preserve review work.
rho.change_evidence: Interview operations leaders at at least 20 US LMM firms across the four main 541199 activity types and collect workflow-level baseline hours, production deployment dates, exception rates, rework, approved-tool coverage, and measured labor-hour savings for 12 months.
rho.rationale: AI use has moved beyond experimentation, but tool use is not the same as redesigned production. Thomson Reuters reports 40% organization-wide GenAI use in 2026, only 19% not planning use, 15% agentic-AI adoption and another 53% planning or considering it; separately, 74% of professionals reported weekly AI use. Five years should permit broad implementation of research, drafting, intake, extraction, scheduling, and quality-control workflows, but accountable review and weak governance keep implementation below full exposure.

s5.bridge: Gallup observed that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, but not specifically legal-support firms or completed qualifying control transfers. I use 22% as the upper bound and haircut the base for plans that do not close, gifts or internal transfers that may not create a purchasable control opportunity, and owner-dependent operations. BizBuySell's completed 2021-2025 legal-business comps and live legal-support listings confirm that external transfers occur but do not provide a population rate. Owner age in this industry is not observed, so the direction of industry bias is uncertain.
caveats: Gallup measures intentions for all employer businesses, not completed deals or this industry.
BizBuySell skews toward smaller owner-operated businesses and combines law firms with legal-support businesses.
The probability excludes closures, deaths without transferable operations, gifts without a qualifying control opportunity, and internal reorganizations.
s5.change_evidence: Match 2021-2026 PitchBook, Axial, BizBuySell, broker, SBA 7(a), and state-entity ownership-change records to a cleaned universe of EBITDA-band NAICS 541199 firms, then interview owners on age, desired exit date, planned buyer type, and transferable management depth.
s5.rationale: A general employer-business survey supplies the closest five-year ownership-transition measure, while observed sales and listings establish an active market for legal and legal-support businesses. The qualifying-transfer probability is set below stated plans because intentions fail, some transfers are internal or non-control, and relationship-dependent or licensed practices may not transfer cleanly.

records.principal_driver: Repeat document- and information-heavy workflows create substantial implementable labor opportunity across scaled legal-support providers.
records.principal_weakness: Commercial retention and durable operator need are uncertain because the code mixes automatable knowledge work with regulated or physical services under varied billing models.

caveats: The rho bridge and rationale state that only 19% had no adoption plan and that 74% of professionals used AI weekly, but neither exact statistic was traceable on S4, S5, or S12. Supported current-adoption and agentic-planning figures preserve the direction of the already disclosed proxy, so removing those two figures does not establish a base-tier or action change.

The rho base of 0.65 converts broad, global organization adoption and planning measures into five-year workflow implementation depth for US LMM legal-support firms. The bridge and wide range disclose this substantial population and construct transformation, but target-level operational evidence is absent.

S7 confirms that legal-support businesses are sold, but says they are far fewer than the mostly small law-firm observations in its aggregate. It therefore supports market existence, not the eligibility share or five-year transfer probability for EBITDA-band 541199 firms; both inputs remain appropriately non-observed.

The regulatory and ethics sources establish that accountable people remain in selected workflows, but they do not quantify the 72% operator-required quantity estimate across the heterogeneous service mix. The packet labels o as ESTIMATE and explicitly discloses that legal requirements may preserve a signature without preserving provider labor or margins.

sources_audited.note: The Census NAICS page defines 541199 as specialized legal or paralegal services outside lawyer, settlement, and title offices and lists notary, paralegal, patent-agent, and process-serving services.
BLS supports the cited paralegal duties, 2024 employment and legal-services share, flat 2024-2034 outlook, and AI-efficiency restraint.

Clio reports up to 74% exposure among hourly billable law-firm tasks, continued predominance of hourly billing, and the cited 71% and 51% flat-fee preferences; the packet discloses the population bridge.

The page supports the survey population, 40% organization-wide use, 15% agentic-AI adoption, 53% planning or considering agentic AI, and 18% ROI tracking, but not the packet's exact 19% no-plan or 74% weekly-use figures.
The legal report supports the 71% commercial-model expectation, 28% pricing-change figure, client pressure, unauthorized use, and governance constraints.
Gallup supports the fall-2024 US survey population and the five-year 22% employer-owner versus 9% nonemployer transition intentions, as well as the 14% all-owner summary in the article text.

BizBuySell supports the existence of completed 2021-2025 legal-business sales and says a much smaller included subset consists mainly of legal-document-preparation and legal-support businesses; it does not provide a subsector transfer rate, as disclosed.
BLS Table 2.11 reports NAICS 541100 output of 348.5 in 2024 and 408.9 in 2034 with a 1.6% annual rate and 1.2% decade employment growth.
NASS supports identity verification and notary involvement in remote notarization and reports authorization in 47 states and the District of Columbia.
USPTO supports registration and qualification requirements for patent attorneys and agents and the prohibition on representation by unrecognized persons.
Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 4 supports service by an adult nonparty and the availability of requested waiver of service.

The ABA page supports lawyer duties concerning competence, confidentiality, client communication, and reasonable fees when using generative AI, but those lawyer-specific duties do not directly establish implementation or operator-need fractions across all nonlawyer 541199 providers.

sources.supports: Global survey of more than 1,500 professionals: organization-wide AI use reached 40% in 2026; 15% had adopted agentic AI and 53% were planning or considering it; only 18% tracked ROI.

sources.supports: Reports that 71% of in-house legal professionals expect outside firms to change commercial models as AI increases, while only 28% of law firms had changed pricing; documents client, financial, governance, and insourcing pressure.

sources.supports: Fall 2024 US survey of 1,264 owners: 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, versus 9% of nonemployers; 14% of all primary owners planned a sale, public offering, or transfer.

sources.supports: Aggregates reported completed sales from 2021-2025, notes that legal-support businesses in the sample are mainly document-preparation and support services, and provides observed sale prices, revenues, earnings, and multiples.

sources.supports: Projects NAICS 541100 Legal Services real output from $348.5 billion in 2024 to $408.9 billion in 2034, a 1.6% compound annual rate, while employment grows 1.2% over the decade.

sources.supports: States that the USPTO registers patent attorneys and non-attorney patent agents, prohibits unrecognized persons from representing inventors, and regulates practitioner qualifications and conduct.

a: The SLB case may reflect unusually suitable data, tooling, and expert support.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include oil and gas, mining and minerals, utilities, infrastructure and civil engineering, environmental users, and government. Revenue is earned through project or fixed-fee work, time-and-materials or day rates, per-unit surveys, monitoring contracts, data licenses, and consulting.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive operator teams; core sampling, drilling, and mine-development services; software-only publishers; equipment manufacturers, resellers, or rental businesses without a service operation; and one-off owner-only practices without repeat outsourced demand.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced geophysical acquisition, processing, interpretation, mapping, monitoring, data management, and related consulting, including seismic, magnetic, gravity, electrical, and electromagnetic methods.

a.bridge: The source population is the exact-industry occupational mix reported by Pell Research, combined with task descriptions for adjacent BLS occupations and seismic-interpretation time savings from one SLB customer workflow. I translate occupational employment shares into a task-level, wage-oriented automation exposure, discounting for field acquisition, client interaction, judgment, and current partial deployment. This may understate exposure because engineering and analytical roles can carry above-average wages, but may overstate it because the occupation shares are employment rather than payroll shares and the seismic case is a favorable single workflow.
caveats: Pell's exact-industry occupation shares are commercial estimates and are not wage-weighted.
The SLB case may reflect unusually suitable data, tooling, and expert support.

The injected labor-intensity input uses 2024 QCEW wages over 2022 SUSB receipts with the specified harmonization, so its vintage mismatch should not be confused with direct evidence for task exposure.
a.change_evidence: Exact-industry payroll by occupation, representative time-and-motion studies separating acquisition from processing and interpretation, and multi-firm measurements of deployed AI hours saved would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: About half of industry employment is reported in architecture and engineering occupations, while computer, mathematical, scientific, and office roles add further data-intensive work. Machine learning has materially shortened horizon interpretation in a real seismic case, but site work, instrument operation, data-quality control, geological judgment, and client-facing interpretation remain human-intensive. The range therefore places a substantial, but not majority-to-dominant, share of current compensation within automatable or strongly augmentable tasks.

d5.bridge: The source quantity is BLS projected real output for the broader four-digit architectural, engineering, and related services industry, rising at about 1.7% annually from 2024 to 2034. Compounding that rate for five years gives approximately 1.09, which I use as the exact-industry base while widening for geophysical exposure to commodity exploration cycles and growth in minerals, infrastructure, environmental, and energy-transition work. The broader category may overstate stability because geophysics can be more cyclical, but may understate upside from exploration and subsurface-data demand.
caveats: The BLS output projection is for four-digit NAICS 5413, not six-digit NAICS 541360.
Customer digital-spending growth is not equivalent to demand growth for independent geophysical service providers.
Commodity prices and exploration budgets can create outcomes outside the stated range.
d5.change_evidence: Exact NAICS 541360 real revenue history and forecasts by end market, backlog, exploration-capex scenarios, and recurring monitoring revenue would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: The broader services category supports modest real growth, and survey and mapping technician employment is also projected to grow. Geophysical demand nevertheless depends materially on oil, gas, and mineral exploration budgets, producing a downside case below today's level and an upside case where subsurface needs across minerals, energy, infrastructure, and environmental markets broaden.

caveats: The product taxonomy establishes what can be sold but does not disclose the industry's revenue weights.
The injected firm-count estimate of 78 depends on SUSB size classes and a 15.65% margin bridge; uncertainty in that denominator does not provide direct evidence for this primitive.
e.change_evidence: Exact-industry revenue split between externally delivered labor services, data licensing, software, equipment, and captive or pass-through activity would materially change this estimate.
e.rationale: The default lens centers on externally sold surveying, acquisition, processing, interpretation, and consulting, so most in-scope activity is outsourced service delivery. The discount reflects mixed firms with proprietary data sales, software, training, equipment resale or rental, and episodic projects that do not behave like recurring outsourced labor services.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541360 ($1,461,125,114) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541360 ($1,992,974,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: The BLS surveying-technician evidence is adjacent to, rather than identical with, geophysical surveying.
Remote sensing, autonomous instruments, and standardized processing could lower operator dependence faster than assumed.
o.change_evidence: Exact-industry revenue by field acquisition, processing, interpretation, licensing, and software, plus evidence on remotely operated acquisition and customer insourcing, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Field acquisition requires site access, instrument deployment, operating judgment, safety management, and quality assurance, while BLS notes that technicians remain necessary to review and interpret increasingly automated outputs for accuracy and completeness. Data-only processing and some interpretation can be software-led or performed by customers, preventing a near-universal operator requirement. The central case therefore treats a human-led operating organization as necessary for most, but not all, in-scope revenue.

caveats: No observed exact-industry billing-model or post-automation margin distribution was found.
The official product list identifies offerings, not their revenue shares or contractual pricing terms.
q.change_evidence: Representative contract-level data on billing model, repricing frequency, gross-margin change after automation, bid intensity, and ownership of AI tools and resulting intellectual property would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Fixed-fee projects, recurring monitoring, proprietary data licenses, and differentiated interpretation can allow a provider to retain early productivity gains through higher throughput, faster delivery, or improved margins. Time-and-materials and day-rate work, competitive rebids, customer procurement power, and customer ownership of digital tools pass a meaningful share of savings through to buyers. The mixed revenue architecture supports moderate retention with a wide range.

rho.bridge: The source populations are global mineral-exploration professionals and global upstream oil-and-gas digital buyers, supplemented by an implemented seismic case, rather than U.S. lower-middle-market NAICS 541360 firms. I map reported AI use, expected digital-spending growth, implementation barriers, and demonstrated technical feasibility into the share of addressable task potential realized within five years. This can overstate implementation if large operators and well-funded explorers lead smaller service firms, while occasional-use responses may understate deeper adoption among specialist processors.
caveats: Adoption surveys cover customer verticals and global populations, not the exact U.S. service industry or lower-middle-market subset.
Reported use does not measure depth, production reliability, or realized labor substitution.
rho.change_evidence: A representative U.S. survey of NAICS 541360 firms measuring production deployment, workflow coverage, spend, staff training, and realized labor-hour savings by firm size would materially change the range.
rho.rationale: AI is already used at least occasionally by most surveyed mineral-exploration professionals, digital spending is expected to rise among upstream buyers, and seismic interpretation has demonstrated large time reductions. Realization remains constrained by budgets, uncertain return on investment, legacy integration, data quality, trust, and change management, making partial rather than complete implementation the central case.

s5.bridge: The source population is a national Gallup survey of U.S. small-business owners, where 22% of employer owners planned a sale or transfer within five years, not verified transactions in NAICS 541360. I haircut planned transitions for noncompletion, internal or family transfers, and firms outside an acquirable lower-middle-market profile, then offset part of that haircut for demonstrated platform acquisition activity in geophysical consulting. This may overstate supply because intentions often do not close, but may understate it if specialist owners approach retirement or strategic consolidation accelerates.
caveats: Succession intentions are general small-business evidence and are not completed transactions.
One consolidator's activity demonstrates buyer appetite but cannot establish an industry-wide transfer rate.
The injected estimate of 78 firms is itself model-derived from SUSB size classes and a 15.65% margin assumption, so both the count and the available-firm implication are uncertain.
s5.change_evidence: Exact-industry owner-age distribution, broker listings, closed-deal counts, internal succession plans, and a verified census of independent firms within the lower-middle-market revenue band would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: General employer-owner succession intentions indicate a meaningful but minority five-year transfer pool. An active geotechnical platform's acquisition of a geophysical consultancy and its broader acquisition program show strategic buyer interest, while transaction readiness, customer concentration, key-person dependence, and internal succession limit the realistically acquirable share.

records.principal_driver: A large analytical and engineering work share, paired with demonstrated machine-learning reductions in seismic interpretation time, creates material productivity potential.
records.principal_weakness: Commodity-linked cyclicality and the industry's mixture of field-intensive services and software-like data products make both automation exposure and value capture highly heterogeneous.

caveats: a: No six-digit occupation-by-wage matrix was located for NAICS 541910.
a: O*NET covers market research analysts across many industries and does not describe all polling, fieldwork, panel, or agency-support jobs.
a: MRII reports tool use and perceived benefits, not the share of wages technically substitutable.
rho: Vendor capability does not establish reliable deployment across heterogeneous client data and methods.
rho: Global professional survey results may not match smaller U.S. firms.
rho: The range excludes pricing, customer self-service, and demand effects.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firms with margins unlike the supplied benchmark.
e: Establishment classification does not reveal firm-level repeat revenue or founder dependence.
e: Ipsos is far larger than the lens and is used only to show that project revenue can recur through retained clients.
s5: Owner intentions are not completed qualifying transfers.
s5: The M&A source covers the broader marketing-services sector and does not provide a denominator of eligible firms.
s5: Undisclosed small transactions and family transfers are difficult to observe.
q: Public-company contract structures may not represent small custom-research agencies.
q: No source directly measures five-year sharing of AI-derived gross benefit.
q: Customer self-service and lost volume are reserved for demand primitives rather than included here.
d5: No six-digit constant-price demand forecast was found.
d5: Employment is not service quantity and can diverge as productivity changes.
d5: The forecast concerns a broader industry and a broader occupation population.
o: Synthetic-response expectations are opinions rather than observed replacement.
o: Human oversight can be performed by a client-side team rather than a lens firm.
o: Vendor product descriptions may emphasize capability and speed more than failure rates or applicability limits.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business, nonprofit, media, campaign, and government customers buy recurring tracking programs, annual or multi-period subscriptions, retainers, and repeat fixed-fee custom projects. Revenue can combine design, fieldwork or panels, analysis, reporting, and advisory interpretation.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive corporate insights teams, internal support establishments, shells, non-control financing vehicles, pure software vendors, report publishers without a service operation, advertising agencies whose primary activity is not research, and firms without repeat outsourced-service customers.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms repeatedly supplying external customers with custom market research, brand or customer tracking, survey and polling programs, statistical sampling, qualitative research, data collection, analysis, and research reporting.

a.bridge: O*NET describes the occupation rather than the wage-weighted staffing of NAICS 541910, while MRII surveys global research professionals rather than U.S. lower-middle-market agencies. I mapped the core tasks of data collection and analysis, survey design, literature review, coding, visualization, and report drafting to exposed work, then reduced exposure for respondent recruitment, client discovery, methodological judgment, quality assurance, facilitation, and senior interpretation. This may overstate exposure where small firms are senior-heavy and understate it where survey operations and open-end coding dominate payroll.
caveats: No six-digit occupation-by-wage matrix was located for NAICS 541910.
O*NET covers market research analysts across many industries and does not describe all polling, fieldwork, panel, or agency-support jobs.
MRII reports tool use and perceived benefits, not the share of wages technically substitutable.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted time-and-motion study of U.S. independent research agencies, segmented by survey operations, qualitative research, analysts, project managers, sales, and principals, with task-level current automation status.
a.rationale: O*NET identifies reporting, data collection and analysis, survey-method design, forecasting, and trend monitoring as core work. MRII finds AI already used in literature reviews and questionnaire development and widely credited with faster data processing, analysis, and report generation. Those activities cover a large share of analyst and production work, but not all judgment, relationship, fieldwork, or quality-control labor.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for NAICS 541900, which combines market research with materially different services, and projects employment for market research analysts across all industries rather than outsourced NAICS 541910 demand. I use the broader industry's 2.4% annual real-output growth and the occupation's 7% decade employment growth as upper and lower directional anchors, then temper them for the current outsourced service basket. The broad 541900 output may overstate demand because veterinary services are a large component, while analyst employment may understate research quantity if AI raises output per worker.
caveats: No six-digit constant-price demand forecast was found.
Employment is not service quantity and can diverge as productivity changes.
The forecast concerns a broader industry and a broader occupation population.
d5.change_evidence: BEA or Census real revenue indexes for six-digit NAICS 541910, combined with agency backlog, client research-budget surveys, project counts, and constant-scope pricing from a representative U.S. panel through 2031.
d5.rationale: BLS projects real output of broader other professional, scientific, and technical services to rise from 200.2 billion chained dollars in 2024 to 253.7 billion in 2034, while market-research analyst employment is projected to grow 7% over the decade because organizations increasingly use data and market research. A five-year base above current demand is defensible, but budget cyclicality and substitution away from traditional studies warrant a low case slightly below one.

caveats: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firms with margins unlike the supplied benchmark.
Establishment classification does not reveal firm-level repeat revenue or founder dependence.
Ipsos is far larger than the lens and is used only to show that project revenue can recur through retained clients.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level sample from Data Axle or PitchBook matched to websites and customer contracts, coding ownership, external-service revenue share, recurring or repeat revenue, founder dependence, and software-only business models for firms in the frozen EBITDA band.
e.rationale: The Census industry form identifies market research, public opinion polling, statistical sampling, and survey design as core services, so most employer firms in the EBITDA band should sell external research repeatedly even when individual assignments are project-based. The deduction allows for captive or controlled establishments, software-led businesses, report publishing, non-repeat project shops, and firms whose transferable customer relationships are too dependent on one principal.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $318,207 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541910); class <5: 2,509 firms, avg revenue/firm $530,196 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $82,976 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 435 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,835,349 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $287,232 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 296 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,275,365 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $512,595 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 309 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,129,165 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,585,214 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 120 firms, avg revenue/firm $46,203,783 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,230,892 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 78 firms, avg revenue/firm $191,354,487 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $29,946,977 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 429

o.bridge: Qualtrics surveys market researchers about synthetic responses, SurveyMonkey documents self-service analysis capability, and ESOMAR sets professional oversight expectations; none measures the year-five share of U.S. outsourced research quantity requiring an independent accountable operator. I reduce operator-required quantity for self-service survey design, automated analysis, synthetic sampling, and client-side AI adoption, while preserving quantity requiring method selection, primary-data provenance, privacy and consent controls, bias review, complex qualitative work, validation, and accountable interpretation. Vendor claims may overstate elimination and thus bias the estimate downward; professional standards may preserve oversight without requiring an external agency, biasing it upward.
caveats: Synthetic-response expectations are opinions rather than observed replacement.
Human oversight can be performed by a client-side team rather than a lens firm.
Vendor product descriptions may emphasize capability and speed more than failure rates or applicability limits.
o.change_evidence: A longitudinal client procurement survey linking 2026 research spend and study counts to 2031 outcomes, classified as outsourced operator-led, client self-service, software-only, eliminated, or synthetic, with separate results for regulated and complex qualitative studies.
o.rationale: SurveyMonkey can already provide conversational analysis, segmentation, and open-text coding without spreadsheets. Qualtrics reports that 71% of researchers expected synthetic responses to comprise most data collection within three years. Yet ESOMAR guidance expects suppliers to explain AI use, stress-test outputs, maintain data governance, and integrate human oversight, preserving a substantial role for an accountable operator even as routine execution self-serves.

q.bridge: Forrester is a large research and advisory provider outside the exact custom market-research lens, and Fitch describes large global Ipsos. I map their annual subscriptions and fixed-fee project structures to lower-middle-market research contracts: cost savings are initially retained within fixed scopes and prepaid subscriptions, then partially shared through renewals, competitive bids, scope expansion, and price pressure. Large firms may have more pricing power than the lens, biasing retention upward; founder-led specialists with scarce expertise may retain more, while commoditized survey shops may retain less.
caveats: Public-company contract structures may not represent small custom-research agencies.
No source directly measures five-year sharing of AI-derived gross benefit.
Customer self-service and lost volume are reserved for demand primitives rather than included here.
q.change_evidence: Anonymized contracts and renewal cohorts from at least twenty U.S. independent agencies, showing fee basis, gross-margin change after automation, realized price changes, rebids, scope additions, win rates, and customer savings clauses over multiple renewals.
q.rationale: Forrester discloses ratable annual research subscriptions and fixed-fee consulting projects, structures that do not mechanically pass through each labor saving. Ipsos operates in a fragmented, competitive market with limited contractually recurring revenue despite high client return, so discounted retention should reflect renewal repricing and customer bargaining rather than full permanent capture.

rho.bridge: MRII measures current global team adoption and attitudes, SurveyMonkey demonstrates commercially available capabilities, and ESOMAR describes governance expectations; none measures five-year implementation of the remaining exposed wage opportunity in U.S. lower-middle-market firms. I translated high and rising adoption plus available workflow tools into implementation potential, then discounted for data privacy, bias, client restrictions, validation, training, integration, and human-oversight requirements. The estimate may be high for regulated or bespoke qualitative work and low for standardized online-survey production.
caveats: Vendor capability does not establish reliable deployment across heterogeneous client data and methods.
Global professional survey results may not match smaller U.S. firms.
The range excludes pricing, customer self-service, and demand effects.
rho.change_evidence: A cohort study of independent U.S. agencies documenting baseline labor hours, deployed AI workflows, client approval rules, error and rework rates, and realized labor avoidance after at least twelve months.
rho.rationale: MRII reports team AI use of 62%, strong expectations of job improvement, and material gains in routine research tasks. Production tools can already draft and analyze surveys and classify open-ended responses. Implementation is constrained by MRII's reported bias, privacy, ethics, and human-judgment concerns and by ESOMAR's emphasis on transparency, governance, and human oversight.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans for U.S. employer-business owners across industries, while Houlihan Lokey tracks completed and announced marketing-services deals across sizes and subsectors. I use Gallup's 22% employer-owner plan rate as the center and retain a wide range because plans may not close, some transfers are internal or non-controlling, and active marketing-services bolt-ons may lift research-agency transfers above the all-industry rate. This can overstate qualifying outcomes when founder dependence blocks a sale and understate them where consolidators target data and research capabilities.
caveats: Owner intentions are not completed qualifying transfers.
The M&A source covers the broader marketing-services sector and does not provide a denominator of eligible firms.
Undisclosed small transactions and family transfers are difficult to observe.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year U.S. transaction census from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, and business brokers for NAICS 541910 firms, separating control sales from minority investments, internal reorganizations, closures, and uncompleted processes, with EBITDA-band matching.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned a sale or ownership transfer within five years. Marketing-services M&A remained active in the first half of 2025, with smaller transactions and strategic add-ons dominating, and the report explicitly includes market-research deals.

records.principal_driver: A large share of research production consists of repeatable text, survey, and data workflows that current AI tools can accelerate under human review.
records.principal_weakness: The same tools are available to clients and software platforms, creating material self-service and renewal-pricing leakage.

caveats: The a and rho sources establish exposed task types, current adoption, perceived benefits, available product capabilities, and governance frictions, but do not measure a payroll-weighted automatable share or five-year realized implementation in U.S. lower-middle-market agencies. The PROXY labels and directional bridges are honest, so this does not meet the rejection test.

The e base of 0.84 is an explicit estimate: the Census form establishes industry service categories but not firm-level repeat external revenue, independence, founder dependence, or transferability in the EBITDA band.

The s5 base maps a cross-industry owner-intention rate and broad marketing-services deal activity into qualifying five-year transfers. Neither source supplies a 541910 denominator or distinguishes all control-sale outcomes, but the bridge and range disclose this limitation.

The q range is informed by large public-company contract structures and market conditions, not direct evidence of five-year AI-benefit retention for smaller custom-research agencies. The source facts are accurate, but the numerical retention judgment remains weakly anchored.

The d5 range relies on real-output growth for broader NAICS 541900 and all-industry employment growth for one occupation. These are directionally useful but do not directly forecast six-digit outsourced research quantity.

The o range translates a vendor survey of expectations, self-service product capabilities, and professional governance guidance into an external-operator requirement. None directly measures whether oversight will remain with a lens firm rather than move to clients, a limitation the packet acknowledges.

sources_audited.note: The Census form identifies market research and public opinion polling as 541910 activities and lists the cited research methods, polling, and statistical sampling and survey-design services. It does not itself measure repeat revenue or transferability, a limitation disclosed in the e estimate.

O*NET lists reporting, consumer-data collection and analysis, survey and polling method design, forecasting, trend monitoring, and collaboration as occupation tasks. The cross-industry occupation-to-industry-payroll transformation is explicitly treated as a proxy.

MRII reports 426 global online interviews in February 2025, 62% team use, 53% literature-review use, 50% questionnaire-development use, and the cited perceived benefits and concerns. These are adoption and perception measures, not realized labor substitution, as disclosed.

The ESOMAR guidance directly addresses fitness for purpose, explainability, validation, bias, transparency, human oversight and stress testing, ethical compliance, and data governance. It is governance guidance rather than an implementation-rate measure.

SurveyMonkey documents conversational analysis, data segmentation and explanations, automatic thematic categorization of open text, and AI-assisted survey creation. The source demonstrates commercial capability but not heterogeneous deployment reliability.

Qualtrics reports that 71% of market researchers agreed synthetic responses would make up more than half of data collection within three years and gives the cited motivations. It measures expectations rather than realized substitution, which the packet discloses.

Gallup reports a fall 2024 survey and that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. The article also confirms the 1,264-owner analytic sample; intentions are not completed qualifying control transfers.

Houlihan Lokey reports 266 broad marketing-services deals in the first half of 2025, 8% above the first half of 2024, led by smaller transactions and strategic add-ons, and identifies market-research transactions. It supplies no eligible-firm denominator or 541910 transfer rate.

Forrester's 2025 Form 10-K describes annual or multi-year subscription products recognized over the contract term and fixed-fee consulting projects generally completed over short periods. It supports billing structure, not the numerical retention assumption.

Fitch describes Ipsos's competitive fragmented market, recurring contracts slightly above 30% of revenue, project-based visibility limits, repeated revenue from returning large clients, and expected U.S. bolt-ons. Ipsos is much larger than the target lens, as disclosed.

BLS projects 7% employment growth for market research analysts and marketing specialists from 2024 to 2034 and attributes growth to increasing use of data and market research. Occupation employment is not outsourced-service quantity.

BLS Table 2.11 reports 541900 real output of $200.2 billion in 2024 and $253.7 billion in 2034, in chained 2017 dollars, with a 2.4% compound annual growth rate. The cited industry is broader than 541910.

records.title: Marketing Research and Public Opinion Polling

caveats: a: BLS publishes the cited staffing pattern at NAICS 541200, which also includes CPA, bookkeeping, and payroll businesses.
a: O*NET task importance is not time share, wage share, or AI substitutability.
a: TaxCalcBench finds frontier models completed fewer than one-third of simplified federal returns correctly, so exposure should not be read as safe autonomous execution (S4).
rho: The surveys cover broader tax and accounting populations, including large firms and non-US respondents in the Thomson Reuters sample.
rho: Reported use or perceived productivity does not establish avoided hiring or sustained labor removal.
rho: Five-year implementation depends on integration with deterministic tax engines and secure client-data systems, not general-purpose model capability alone.
e: No source measures eligibility among the specific frozen LMM firm universe.
e: The frozen n estimate is margin-bridged from size-class data and may misclassify firms whose normalized EBITDA differs from the assumed industry margin.
e: An establishment can fit the NAICS definition yet fail buyer diligence or lack transferable goodwill.
s5: Stated plans are not realized transfers and the Gallup population spans all industries and business sizes.
s5: BizBuySell has no complete industry denominator and its reported accounting/tax practices often fall below the target EBITDA band.
s5: Retirement can lead to closure or client-book runoff rather than a qualifying control transfer.
q: H&R Block's brand, scale, refund products, and national marketing may overstate independent LMM pricing power.
q: The estimate concerns retention of implemented gross benefit, not revenue growth, demand, or implementation success.
q: Self-prepared e-file volume was growing faster than professionally prepared e-file volume at the March 2026 snapshot, increasing competitive sharing risk (S12).
d5: IRS projections are national filing counts and do not isolate paid preparation or NAICS 541213.
d5: The projection models extrapolate filing patterns and do not fully encode future tax-law simplification or major administrative redesign.
d5: Return count is an imperfect measure of constant-quality service quantity because complexity varies materially across returns.
o: The March 2026 data are an in-season snapshot and channel mix changes around deadlines and extensions.
o: IRS professional e-file statistics include CPA and other firms outside NAICS 541213.
o: An operator may remain legally or practically accountable while requiring far fewer labor hours; that labor effect belongs in a and rho, not o.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Annual or repeat outsourced tax-return engagements, generally priced per return and by form or complexity, with some add-on representation or advisory fees; revenue and staffing are highly concentrated in filing season.
lens.excluded_activities: CPA offices, bookkeeping, payroll, billing, captive tax departments, tax software sold without an accountable service operator, volunteer preparation, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms whose primary business is preparing and filing tax returns for external individual or small-business customers, including related return review and client explanation within the engagement.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 541200 occupation mix, not six-digit 541213, while O*NET measures the Tax Preparers occupation across employers. I used the broader mix to identify wage-bearing professional and clerical roles, mapped O*NET tasks to document intake, calculation, record review, research, drafting, client communication, and accountable judgment, then bounded the not-yet-automated wage share qualitatively. Broader-industry bookkeeping and payroll roles may bias exposure upward or downward relative to pure-play tax firms; failure to observe automation already embedded in tax software most likely biases the estimate upward.
caveats: BLS publishes the cited staffing pattern at NAICS 541200, which also includes CPA, bookkeeping, and payroll businesses.
O*NET task importance is not time share, wage share, or AI substitutability.
TaxCalcBench finds frontier models completed fewer than one-third of simplified federal returns correctly, so exposure should not be read as safe autonomous execution (S4).
a.change_evidence: A 541213-specific payroll-weighted staffing file joined to time-and-motion data by task, plus firm system logs separating already automated tax-software steps from human minutes, would materially change this range.
a.rationale: The broader industry employs substantial professional and clerical labor: accountants and auditors were 31.73% of employment, tax preparers 7.13%, bookkeeping clerks 8.88%, and several administrative categories added further workflow labor (S2). O*NET describes core tax-preparer work as computing tax, completing forms, finding deductions, reviewing records, verifying entries, researching rules, interviewing clients, and explaining law (S3). Intake, extraction, classification, first-pass calculation, research, drafting, and exception flagging are exposed; client fact-finding, atypical judgment, final review, and responsibility remain less substitutable. This supports a base exposed wage share modestly above one-half.

d5.bridge: IRS projects counts of returns processed, not a constant-price, constant-quality quantity index for services sold by 541213 firms. I use FY2026-to-FY2031 filing growth as the closest volume bridge, emphasizing individual returns because they dominate volumes while checking partnership and corporation returns. Counts may understate service demand if complexity per return rises and overstate it if data prefill or software reduces work content; channel displacement is reserved for o.
caveats: IRS projections are national filing counts and do not isolate paid preparation or NAICS 541213.
The projection models extrapolate filing patterns and do not fully encode future tax-law simplification or major administrative redesign.
Return count is an imperfect measure of constant-quality service quantity because complexity varies materially across returns.
d5.change_evidence: Build a return-type-weighted volume and complexity index from target-firm production data, IRS form/schedule counts, and realized revenue stripped of price changes, then update it with the next IRS Publication 6292 projection cycle.
d5.rationale: IRS projects total individual income-tax returns rising from 164.135 million in FY2026 to 168.570 million in FY2031, a ratio of about 1.03; partnership returns rise from 5.200 million to 5.630 million, while corporation returns rise from 8.259 million to 8.463 million (S11). Statutory filing obligations make underlying quantity resilient, but the wide range allows policy simplification, economic formation changes, and shifts in return complexity.

caveats: No source measures eligibility among the specific frozen LMM firm universe.
The frozen n estimate is margin-bridged from size-class data and may misclassify firms whose normalized EBITDA differs from the assumed industry margin.
An establishment can fit the NAICS definition yet fail buyer diligence or lack transferable goodwill.
e.change_evidence: Review the frozen 40-firm universe record by record for revenue concentration, owner dependence, franchise transfer restrictions, credential coverage, compliance history, normalized EBITDA quality, and buyer-control feasibility.
e.rationale: NAICS 541213 is already narrowly defined around non-CPA establishments providing tax-return preparation without bookkeeping, billing, or payroll, so most LMM firms in the frozen count should fit the outsourced-service lens (S1). Accounting and tax practices, including independent firms and occasional franchise units, are visibly saleable businesses (S8). The discount excludes businesses whose economics or customer book are inseparable from an owner, franchise arrangements without transferable control, poor-quality or noncompliant books, and other operational defects.
