n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $62,700 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541213); class <5: 15,428 firms, avg revenue/firm $162,364 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $25,410 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 2,632 firms, avg revenue/firm $421,091 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $65,901 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 915 firms, avg revenue/firm $786,051 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $123,017 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 436 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,867,025 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $292,189 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 40 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,812,650 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,535,680 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $208,711,769 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $32,663,392 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 40

o.bridge: IRS channel statistics measure current professionally prepared versus self-prepared e-filed individual returns, not the 2031 share of the total service quantity requiring a 541213 operator. I start from the roughly one-half professional channel, shade the base downward for continued commercial self-service and AI assistance, and retain upside for complexity and accountability. Excluding paper returns and business returns can bias the starting share either way; equating 'professionally e-filed' with this narrower NAICS likely biases it upward because CPA firms also prepare returns.
caveats: The March 2026 data are an in-season snapshot and channel mix changes around deadlines and extensions.
IRS professional e-file statistics include CPA and other firms outside NAICS 541213.
An operator may remain legally or practically accountable while requiring far fewer labor hours; that labor effect belongs in a and rho, not o.
o.change_evidence: Obtain annual IRS paid-preparer flags by return complexity and preparer type, matched to customer migration cohorts among independent firms and controlled trials of end-to-end AI self-preparation through 2031.
o.rationale: In the March 2026 filing-season snapshot, tax professionals e-filed 39.738 million returns and self-preparers 37.836 million; professional volume was down 1.0% year over year while self-prepared volume was up 1.9% (S12). GAO states that more than half of individual taxpayers used paid preparers in FY2024 and notes that paid preparers must use PTINs and remain subject to tax-code requirements, supporting durable accountable-operator demand even as software handles more steps (S14). Current AI limitations on even simplified tax calculations also argue against rapid elimination of review (S4).

caveats: H&R Block's brand, scale, refund products, and national marketing may overstate independent LMM pricing power.
The estimate concerns retention of implemented gross benefit, not revenue growth, demand, or implementation success.
Self-prepared e-file volume was growing faster than professionally prepared e-file volume at the March 2026 snapshot, increasing competitive sharing risk (S12).
q.change_evidence: Collect anonymized engagement letters, invoices, renewal cohorts, discount histories, churn by price change, and realized gross-margin bridges from 15-20 independent tax-preparation firms before and after workflow automation.
q.rationale: Per-return and form/complexity pricing weakens any automatic link between hours and customer price: H&R Block quotes an upfront base price and additional state or form-related charges (S10). Its fiscal 2025 US assisted revenue rose 6.1% as net average charge rose 5.1% and company-owned volume rose 1.0%, while total assisted volume including franchises fell 0.9%, evidence that at least a large operator can sustain price per return (S9). An acquirer can initially retain much of a labor saving, but renewals, low switching costs, DIY software, free options, and competing AI-enabled preparers should share part of the benefit over five years.

rho.bridge: The surveys measure current GenAI use, attitudes, and expected integration among tax/accounting professionals rather than the five-year implemented fraction of the exposed opportunity in LMM pure-play tax firms. I translate adoption momentum into workflow implementation, then discount for data security, integration, training, quality control, seasonal change capacity, and mandatory human review. Vendor-sponsored and technology-engaged respondents may bias implementation upward, while stand-alone-model benchmarks may bias it downward relative to governed tools embedded in tax software.
caveats: The surveys cover broader tax and accounting populations, including large firms and non-US respondents in the Thomson Reuters sample.
Reported use or perceived productivity does not establish avoided hiring or sustained labor removal.
Five-year implementation depends on integration with deterministic tax engines and secure client-data systems, not general-purpose model capability alone.
rho.change_evidence: Obtain workflow-level before/after studies from 20-30 independent 541213 firms showing automated minutes, exception rates, reviewer rework, data incidents, and production use by return type over two filing seasons.
rho.rationale: Enterprise GenAI adoption at tax firms rose from 8% in 2024 to 21% in 2025; 79% expected significant integration by 2027, and return preparation was already a cited use case for 63% of tax/accounting GenAI users (S5). A separate survey of 700 US accounting professionals found 46% used AI daily and 81% of adopters reported productivity gains (S6). Implementation will nevertheless lag exposure because current frontier models made frequent tax-table, calculation, and eligibility errors on a simplified benchmark (S4). A five-year base near three-fifths reflects fast adoption with retained review and control bottlenecks.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year plans across US employer-business owners, not completed qualifying transfers by 541213 LMM firms. I use the 22% employer intention rate as the center, raise uncertainty for the tax/accounting market's demonstrated liquidity and owner demographics, and exclude closures, gifts without control continuity, internal reorganizations, and failed processes. Intentions can overstate completions, while unplanned and sponsor-initiated sales can make them understate actual transfers.
caveats: Stated plans are not realized transfers and the Gallup population spans all industries and business sizes.
BizBuySell has no complete industry denominator and its reported accounting/tax practices often fall below the target EBITDA band.
Retirement can lead to closure or client-book runoff rather than a qualifying control transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Acquire five years of closed 541213 transactions and failed listings from BizBuySell, Axial, Accounting Practice Sales, Poe Group Advisors, and SBA 7(a) change-of-ownership loans, matched to an estimated LMM-firm denominator.
s5.rationale: Census data cited by Gallup show 52.3% of US employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older, and 22% of employer-business owners said they planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years (S7). BizBuySell reports actual accounting and tax practice sales, a 2025 median sale price of $500,000, and five-year reported sale multiples, confirming an active transfer channel even though its businesses are generally smaller than the lens (S8). The base therefore stays close to the broad employer intention rate rather than treating owner age as an automatic sale.

records.principal_driver: A large labor share sits in repeat document, calculation, research, and review workflows that can be compressed while customers continue buying a completed return.
records.principal_weakness: The technology that improves operator productivity can also shift simple returns to self-service and weaken long-run pricing retention.

a: Responsible-charge obligations constrain substitution unevenly across states, disciplines, and deliverables.

e: Eligibility varies substantially by discipline, end market, ownership structure, and state licensing regime.

s5: An ownership plan does not establish that a sale will occur or qualify under the lens.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External private- and public-sector customers purchase projects, task orders, master-service-agreement work, and repeat advisory or compliance support under lump-sum, cost-plus, time-and-materials, and hourly billing structures.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive engineering departments, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, firms outside the approximate $1-10M normalized EBITDA band, and businesses whose economics are primarily construction, product manufacturing, software licensing, architecture, surveying, or laboratory testing rather than engineering services.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market engineering-services firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced design, analysis, project delivery, technical documentation, inspection, testing coordination, and engineering advisory services to external customers.

a.bridge: The BLS occupation table measures employment and wages in six-digit NAICS 541330, while the ACEC survey measures leaders' views of task automation rather than a wage-weighted, not-yet-automated share. The range maps more exposure to drafting, project documentation, research, estimating, scheduling, and administrative tasks and less to field work, client judgment, and responsible-charge engineering, then weights that judgment toward the industry's engineering, management, business, computer, drafting, and administrative mix. ACEC members may be larger and more AI-aware than the lens, biasing exposure upward, while its current-task framing may omit capabilities that mature during the five-year horizon, biasing exposure downward.
caveats: OEWS excludes self-employed workers and reports employment rather than task hours.
The ACEC surveys do not isolate six-digit NAICS 541330 or the lower-middle-market band.
Responsible-charge obligations constrain substitution unevenly across states, disciplines, and deliverables.
a.change_evidence: A representative 541330 time-and-wage study that tags current workflow steps by occupation, current automation status, billability, and technical feasibility would materially replace this proxy; firm-level AI time studies covering design, drafting, project management, and back office would be especially useful.
a.rationale: BLS reports 1,134,700 industry jobs in May 2023: architecture and engineering occupations were 45.55% of employment, business and financial operations 13.21%, management 11.11%, computer and mathematical occupations 8.65%, and office and administrative support 7.18%. ACEC found that 68% of surveyed leaders thought AI could automate up to 29% of current tasks, with concrete use cases spanning cost estimation, scheduling, document review, contracts, reports, RFIs, quality control, and generative design. The base reflects meaningful task substitution and avoided hiring without treating whole engineering occupations as automatable.

d5.bridge: BLS publishes constant-dollar output projections for the adjacent four-digit architectural, engineering, and related services industry (541300), not exact six-digit 541330. Converting the projected 2024-to-2034 output ratio of 480.1/407.3 to a five-year ratio gives about 1.086, used as the base. The broader group includes architecture, surveying, and testing, so its growth can be above or below engineering services; the exact-industry employment projection of 4.2% over ten years is a lower-growth cross-check but is not a constant-quality demand measure.
caveats: The constant-dollar output series is for broader NAICS 541300, not exact 541330.
Backlog and business sentiment are nominal, self-reported, and sensitive to survey composition.
Demand differs sharply across transportation, water, power, industrial, commercial, defense, and residential end markets.
d5.change_evidence: Exact 541330 real output or revenue forecasts by end market, deflated with engineering-services price indexes and normalized for service quality, would materially change the range; a multi-year backlog and cancellation panel would tighten the near-term path.
d5.rationale: The BLS projection implies about 1.7% annual real output growth for the broader 541300 group and 4.2% ten-year employment growth for exact 541330. ACEC reported near-term nominal engineering and design revenue growth and, by late 2025, a median backlog of 11 months with 48% of surveyed firms carrying at least one year of backlog. The range allows a mild real contraction from funding, rates, tariffs, or sector cyclicality as well as stronger infrastructure, energy, manufacturing, and data-center demand.

caveats: No source directly measures the share of the injected LMM firm estimate that meets the recurring or repeat outsourced-service lens.
The injected firm count is itself an estimate based on size classes and a margin bridge, so the denominator may not align cleanly with normalized EBITDA.
Eligibility varies substantially by discipline, end market, ownership structure, and state licensing regime.
e.change_evidence: A stratified review of 541330 firms in Capital IQ, PitchBook, Grata, or similar private-company data, followed by broker and quality-of-earnings interviews on revenue mix, owner dependence, licensure, customer concentration, and contract assignability, would materially change this estimate.
e.rationale: A majority of firms in the dataset's LMM band should fit the lens because engineering is commonly sold as outsourced project or repeat technical work. The range excludes captive operations, businesses dominated by construction or products, one-person rainmaker practices without transferable delivery systems, and firms with customer, license-holder, or contract-assignment dependencies that make a control acquisition impractical. ACEC/FMI's ownership-transition evidence supports the existence of transferable firms but does not measure lens eligibility.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $172,759.1M / sectoral output (T30) $367,702.1M, year 2024

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $260,281 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541330); class <5: 27,901 firms, avg revenue/firm $412,809 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $64,605 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 6,267 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,523,903 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $238,491 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 4,702 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,089,183 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $483,457 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 4,840 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,897,160 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,548,906 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 1,297 firms, avg revenue/firm $42,835,429 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,703,745 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 685 firms, avg revenue/firm $252,830,867 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $39,568,031 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 6,137

caveats: Professional-engineering statutes and sealing requirements vary by state, discipline, asset type, and customer.
NSPE policy establishes accountability requirements but does not measure the quantity of work requiring an operator.
Software may complement licensed firms for longer than it substitutes for them, but customer self-service could expand faster than assumed in standardized scopes.
o.change_evidence: A representative buyer survey and lost-work analysis identifying engineering scopes brought in-house, eliminated, or purchased as software, combined with state-by-state data on sealed deliverables and AI-enabled project delivery, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Most year-five engineering-services quantity should still require an accountable operating firm because clients need multidisciplinary delivery, project coordination, insurance, quality systems, and, for regulated work, licensed professionals exercising responsible charge. NSPE states that engineering documents subject to professional-engineering practice should bear the seal of the licensee in responsible charge and that after-the-fact review is insufficient. Some basic analysis, drafting, visualization, document search, and monitoring can nevertheless be self-served or supplied directly by software, supporting a value below one.

q.bridge: ACEC's contracting study measures lump-sum adoption and stakeholder experience in transportation engineering, not the discounted benefit-retention share across all 541330 revenue. The estimate maps fixed or lump-sum work to stronger near-term retention and cost-plus or hourly work to more contractual pass-through, then discounts both for renewal repricing, customer sharing, and competition over five years. Transportation's procurement practices may understate fixed-fee prevalence in some private markets but may overstate the durability of public-sector pricing conventions.
caveats: The cited study is based on 14 client organizations and nine engineering firms in transportation.
Contract labels do not by themselves reveal scope changes, write-offs, utilization effects, or competitive repricing.
The mix of public infrastructure, industrial, energy, buildings, and other engineering end markets can shift retention materially.
q.change_evidence: Firm-level contract and invoice data segmented by fixed fee, lump sum, cost-plus, time-and-materials, and hourly work, paired with renewal pricing and realized margin changes after automation, would materially change this value.
q.rationale: Interviewed transportation clients reported lump-sum usage ranging from below 10% to above 80%. Lump-sum contracts can let firms retain labor efficiencies during the contract term, whereas cost-plus and hourly billing more directly pass fewer hours to customers. Repeat bidding, transparent labor schedules, scope expansion, and customer bargaining should share a substantial portion of implemented gains, leaving roughly half retained in the base case.

rho.bridge: ACEC measures current strategy, usage, and executive expectations across architecture, engineering, and construction firms, not the five-year implemented share of exposed opportunity in LMM 541330 firms. The estimate interprets current design/project-delivery adoption and strategic readiness as a starting point, then discounts for workflow integration, data governance, model reliability, liability, skills, and change management. Larger or more digitally mature ACEC respondents likely bias readiness upward relative to the lens; five years of product improvement and labor scarcity could make present adoption understate eventual implementation.
caveats: Strategy formation is not operational implementation and may overstate realized workflow change.
Survey responses may be affected by selection, optimism, and inconsistent definitions of AI use.
Integration with legacy design tools and professional-liability controls could slow smaller firms more than the source populations.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of 541330 firms reporting production workflow deployment, percentage of projects using AI, realized labor hours avoided, error and rework rates, and firm size would materially change the range.
rho.rationale: In early 2025, 63% of ACEC member firms had an AI strategy in place or under development, but AI use was much more prevalent in marketing and sales than in design and project delivery, where reported use was 36%. A later 2025 ACEC survey still described adoption as concentrated in pilots or limited use. Implementation is therefore likely to capture only part of the exposed opportunity within five years, despite strong incentives from scarce technical labor.

s5.bridge: ACEC's deal data cover US architecture, engineering, and environmental sellers of all sizes rather than eligible LMM 541330 firms, and its ownership survey covers a broad revenue range. Annual broader-market deal volume and a $4.0M median seller revenue indicate an active small-firm transfer market, while the estimate discounts transactions outside 541330, non-control deals, internal reorganizations, ineligible sellers, and repeated activity not representing a unique eligible firm. Broader-industry inclusion biases the crude incidence upward, while incomplete disclosure of private deals and unannounced internal transitions biases it downward.
caveats: The deal series is broader than NAICS 541330 and includes sellers outside the target EBITDA band.
The ownership survey had 186 respondents and does not report a five-year control-transfer probability.
An ownership plan does not establish that a sale will occur or qualify under the lens.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census matching announced and private 541330 control deals to unique eligible firms, with seller EBITDA, ownership type, internal-versus-external transfer, and transaction date, would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: The broader design and environmental market recorded 350 US deals by mid-September 2025 and was expected to reach at least 450 annual deals for a fifth consecutive year; the median seller had $4.0M of revenue. Separately, 62% of surveyed firm owners reported an ownership plan, but formal planning was much less common below 50 employees. These data support a material but minority five-year chance of a qualifying control transfer for an eligible firm.

records.principal_driver: A large technical labor base offers automatable supporting tasks while responsible-charge requirements preserve the need for an accountable engineering operator.
records.principal_weakness: Commercial retention is uncertain because hourly and cost-plus billing can convert labor savings directly into lower billable revenue before fixed-fee penetration or scope growth offsets it.

caveats: The a and rho bases are judgmental transformations of exact-industry employment structure and broad ACEC leader surveys, not a wage-weighted LMM task study or realized production-workflow panel; the PROXY labels and directional bridges disclose this appropriately.

S6 and S7 establish ownership-planning and broader A/E transaction activity but do not directly measure e or s5 for eligible 541330 firms; this is non-material to the base tier because F remains far above the weakest-link threshold across the registered range and the action is already MODEL_CONDITIONED.

The q base is not observed retention: it maps a small transportation interview study and contract-form logic into an industrywide five-year estimate, with no firm-level post-automation margin or repricing evidence.

The d5 base relies chiefly on constant-dollar output for broader 541300, while S10 and S11 are nominal or short-horizon broader-industry indicators; exact 541330 evidence is an employment-growth cross-check rather than real output.

The o estimate is directionally supported by professional-responsibility and sealing rules, but the cited policies do not quantify how much year-five demand requires an operating firm and applicability varies by state, discipline, deliverable, and customer.

sources_audited.note: The BLS exact-industry table supports the May 2023 total employment and cited occupational employment shares; it is employment and wage evidence, not direct task-hour exposure evidence.

The ACEC release supports the 68% up-to-29% task-automation response, 36% design/project-delivery use, 81% marketing/sales use, and the described application examples; the record correctly treats their use in a and rho as proxy evidence.

The primer supports the 63% strategy-or-development figure and qualitative use cases, implementation barriers, pilot guidance, governance, and liability concerns, but it does not measure five-year LMM implementation or the operator-required quantity share.
The NSPE ethics case supports direct control and personal supervision as responsible charge and states that post-preparation review without design involvement is insufficient.
The NSPE position supports the claim that professional-engineering documents should bear the seal of the licensed person in responsible charge subject to applicable state law.

The source supports the 186-owner sample, 62% ownership-plan prevalence, and 51% formal-plan prevalence below 50 employees, but it does not measure lens eligibility or a five-year qualifying control-transfer probability.

The article supports 350 tracked US design/environmental deals by mid-September 2025, expected fifth consecutive annual volume of at least 450, and $4.0M median seller revenue, but not unique eligible 541330 LMM control transfers over five years.

The transportation study supports the below-10% to above-80% lump-sum range, contract-form incentive discussion, and the 14-client/nine-firm interview design, but it does not directly observe an industrywide discounted benefit-retention share.

The BLS table supports 2024-2034 employment growth of 4.2% for exact 541330 and constant-dollar output of 407.3 to 480.1 billion chained 2017 dollars for broader 541300; the broader-industry bridge is explicit.

The ACEC assessment supports broader engineering-and-design revenue growth of 5.3% in 2024, a 2.3% 2025 forecast, and stated tailwinds and headwinds, but these are nominal broader-industry indicators rather than exact 541330 five-year real demand.

The 710-executive ACEC survey supports a median 11-month backlog, 48% with at least one year of work, and AI adoption concentrated in pilots or limited implementations, but it is broad, self-reported, and not a direct five-year primitive measure.

records.title: Engineering Services

caveats: a: The occupation distribution is published at NAICS 54169 rather than an independently verified six-digit cut.
a: Adjacent-sector AI surveys measure stated use and expectations, not wage-weighted task substitution in this industry.
a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts input uses nonmatching QCEW and SUSB vintages and may distort the economic importance of the exposed task share.
rho: The surveys cover adjacent professions, UK consulting, or the broader government-contracting market rather than the frozen US industry lens.
rho: Vendor-sponsored surveys may emphasize technology adoption and respondent expectations may not become realized labor savings.
e: No public source measures the eligible share of LMM firms directly.
e: The industry combines materially different specialties, and public data do not provide revenue weights across them.
e: The frozen LMM firm count is an estimate that bridges revenue-size classes through a broader-sector EBITDA margin and may misclassify firms near the band boundaries.
s5: Announced transaction data undercount small private transactions and do not isolate the six-digit industry.
s5: Deal counts cannot by themselves identify the share of eligible firms transferred.
s5: The frozen LMM firm-count denominator relies on size classes and a broader-sector margin bridge.
q: No public source provides a revenue-weighted contract-type mix or post-AI price-retention history for NAICS 541690.
q: Government-contracting evidence spans many industries and contract structures.
q: The result is highly sensitive to specialty, customer bargaining power, and the boundary between billable analysis and nonbillable overhead.
d5: The BLS projection is for broader NAICS 541600 and is not an observed forecast for the six-digit industry.
d5: Constant-quality demand is inferred from constant-dollar output and may still reflect service-mix or quality changes.
d5: Government-budget and commodity-sensitive specialties can diverge sharply from the broader consulting path.
o: No source directly measures future operator-required quantity for this industry.
o: Economic and desk-research practices face more self-service risk than field, safety, geological, or expert-accountability work.
o: The estimate assumes professional liability and customer demand for accountable judgment persist over the horizon.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business and government customers buy specialist analysis, compliance support, audits, studies, expert advice, and project deliverables through hourly, time-and-materials, fixed-fee, retainer, and government-contract arrangements.
lens.excluded_activities: Environmental consulting classified in NAICS 541620, captive internal units, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, and practices whose work is wholly episodic or inseparable from one individual expert.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced scientific and technical advice to external customers, including economic, energy, agricultural, biological, geological, workplace-safety, and public-safety consulting.

a.bridge: The occupation mix reported for NAICS 54169 is mapped to task evidence for management analysts and occupational-health-and-safety specialists, then informed by adoption evidence from adjacent professional-services populations. This likely overstates exposure for field-heavy geological, agricultural, and safety practices and may understate it for economic and desk-based analytical practices.
caveats: The occupation distribution is published at NAICS 54169 rather than an independently verified six-digit cut.
Adjacent-sector AI surveys measure stated use and expectations, not wage-weighted task substitution in this industry.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input uses nonmatching QCEW and SUSB vintages and may distort the economic importance of the exposed task share.
a.change_evidence: Acquire six-digit OEWS staffing and wage weights, then run a task-level time study across economic, safety, energy, agricultural, and geological consultancies that distinguishes already-automated work from current manual work.
a.rationale: The reported workforce is concentrated in business and financial operations, office support, computer and mathematical work, and management. Research, data synthesis, modeling, proposal preparation, documentation, and reporting are materially exposed, while inspection, sampling, interviewing, site observation, client persuasion, and accountable expert judgment remain less substitutable.

d5.bridge: BLS projected constant-dollar output for the broader Management, Scientific, and Technical Consulting Services industry is converted from its 2024 and 2034 endpoints to an implied five-year ratio using compound growth. Exact NAICS 541690 demand could grow more slowly if discretionary projects or federal procurement weaken, or faster in compliance, energy-transition, safety, and technical-risk niches.
caveats: The BLS projection is for broader NAICS 541600 and is not an observed forecast for the six-digit industry.
Constant-quality demand is inferred from constant-dollar output and may still reflect service-mix or quality changes.
Government-budget and commodity-sensitive specialties can diverge sharply from the broader consulting path.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain Census Service Annual Survey receipts and quantity indicators isolated to NAICS 541690, deflate by specialty-appropriate price indexes, and combine them with funded federal procurement pipelines and customer-industry capex or compliance forecasts.
d5.rationale: The broader consulting projection implies real output growth, while the service catalog includes durable needs tied to safety, regulation, resource assessment, public policy, energy, and scientific complexity. A downside near current demand allows for budget pressure, procurement delays, commodity cycles, and customers deferring discretionary studies.

caveats: No public source measures the eligible share of LMM firms directly.
The industry combines materially different specialties, and public data do not provide revenue weights across them.
The frozen LMM firm count is an estimate that bridges revenue-size classes through a broader-sector EBITDA margin and may misclassify firms near the band boundaries.
e.change_evidence: Obtain a revenue-weighted sample of LMM NAICS 541690 firms showing repeat-customer revenue, recurring or contracted revenue, owner-originated sales, customer concentration, project duration, and normalized EBITDA.
e.rationale: The classification covers externally sold advice across several specialties, and safety, security, energy, agricultural, and economic clients can generate repeat work. Eligibility is reduced by one-off studies, expert-witness assignments, project-led geology work, individual rainmaker dependence, and practices lacking transferable systems or recurring customer relationships.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541690 ($26,055,464,336) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541690 ($45,167,979,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $280,662 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541690); class <5: 21,931 firms, avg revenue/firm $392,629 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $61,446 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,982 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,624,294 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $254,202 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,074 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,555,412 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $556,422 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 892 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,179,598 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,436,607 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 247 firms, avg revenue/firm $38,382,737 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,006,898 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 162 firms, avg revenue/firm $73,152,086 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $11,448,302 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,139

caveats: No source directly measures future operator-required quantity for this industry.
Economic and desk-research practices face more self-service risk than field, safety, geological, or expert-accountability work.
The estimate assumes professional liability and customer demand for accountable judgment persist over the horizon.
o.change_evidence: Interview buyers and former buyers across each major specialty, then quantify current spend likely to move to self-service software, procurement consolidation, or fully automated products; validate against churn and scope-reduction data from firms deploying customer-facing AI.
o.rationale: Customers can self-serve some research, drafting, routine modeling, and standard reporting as AI tools improve. Most of the current basket still benefits from an accountable operator because it involves diagnosing an ambiguous client problem, inspecting workplaces or resources, collecting evidence, navigating regulation, defending conclusions, and sometimes supplying expert testimony.

caveats: No public source provides a revenue-weighted contract-type mix or post-AI price-retention history for NAICS 541690.
Government-contracting evidence spans many industries and contract structures.
The result is highly sensitive to specialty, customer bargaining power, and the boundary between billable analysis and nonbillable overhead.
q.change_evidence: Review contracts and invoice histories from a specialty-stratified firm sample to measure revenue by billing type, labor-hour pass-through clauses, realized gross-margin change after AI deployment, renewal repricing, win-rate changes, and customer givebacks.
q.rationale: Retention should be strongest in fixed-fee, retainer, and outcome-oriented engagements where faster production does not mechanically reduce invoices, and weaker in hourly, time-and-materials, and cost-plus work where fewer billable hours pass benefits to customers. Renewals, procurement, and competitor adoption are likely to share some gains with customers even when initial contracts are fixed price.

rho.bridge: Observed adoption, strategy, and expected productivity evidence from global legal, risk, compliance, tax, accounting, and audit professionals, UK architecture and engineering consultants, and US government contractors is translated into a five-year implementation share for scientific consulting. The bridge may overstate implementation where scientific validation, client confidentiality, field systems, and liability are more restrictive, but may understate it in economic consulting and standardized reporting workflows.
caveats: The surveys cover adjacent professions, UK consulting, or the broader government-contracting market rather than the frozen US industry lens.
Vendor-sponsored surveys may emphasize technology adoption and respondent expectations may not become realized labor savings.
rho.change_evidence: Survey US NAICS 541690 firms by specialty, firm size, workflow, production deployment, realized labor hours avoided, validation burden, and failed implementation, with results weighted by payroll.
rho.rationale: Current professional-services evidence shows active AI use in internal operations, product development, proposals, data analysis, and administration, but defined strategies and skills remain uneven. Five years allows workflow redesign and quality controls to mature, yet accuracy, security, validation, and change-management constraints prevent full realization of the exposed opportunity.

s5.bridge: Recent transaction volumes and trend commentary for US professional services, global specialty consulting, and US government services are used to infer the probability of a qualifying control transfer among eligible LMM firms. These sources include industries and deal sizes outside NAICS 541690, omit many undisclosed small deals, and provide transaction counts rather than a clean eligible-firm denominator, so the estimate is bounded widely.
caveats: Announced transaction data undercount small private transactions and do not isolate the six-digit industry.
Deal counts cannot by themselves identify the share of eligible firms transferred.
The frozen LMM firm-count denominator relies on size classes and a broader-sector margin bridge.
s5.change_evidence: Purchase PitchBook, GF Data, or CapIQ records filtered to US NAICS 541690 and the LMM EBITDA band, deduplicate add-ons and internal reorganizations, and divide qualifying control transfers by an independently verified eligible-firm panel; supplement with owner-age and succession interviews.
s5.rationale: Professional and specialty consulting show persistent strategic and private-equity acquisition activity, including compliance, energy, life-sciences, and public-sector niches. The weaker recent specialty-consulting and government-services cadence tempers the inference, while succession and fragmented small-firm ownership could create additional transfers not visible in announced-deal datasets.

records.principal_driver: A large share of desk-based analytical and documentation work can be augmented while clients continue to require specialist judgment and accountable delivery.
records.principal_weakness: Public data do not isolate specialty mix, eligible-firm prevalence, contract economics, or control transfers for the exact six-digit LMM population.

caveats: The a=0.38 base is a judgmental mapping from a commercially presented occupation mix and two illustrative O*NET occupations, not a six-digit, wage-weighted task calculation; the wide range and PROXY label are appropriate, but the base is not independently reproduced by the sources.

The rho=0.48 base translates vendor-sponsored surveys of adjacent global professions, UK A&E and consulting firms, and broad US government contractors into five-year implementation for 541690; the cited surveys support the direction and constraints, not the exact share.

The s5 sources establish an active but recently softer market in broad professional, specialty-consulting, and government-services populations, yet provide neither an exact-industry eligible-firm denominator nor a quantitative bridge to the 10%-32% five-year range; downside below the registered low remains plausible.

The q=0.50 estimate reaches the factor cap while no source measures the revenue-weighted billing-model mix or realized post-AI retention for 541690; S8 only confirms that relevant government-contract structures exist.
The d5 bridge is arithmetically consistent with BLS constant-dollar output endpoints for broad 541600, but the exact six-digit specialty mix can diverge materially, as the record itself discloses.

The packet marks the lens as non-heterogeneous even though its own narrative describes materially different economic, field-science, safety, energy, and other specialties without revenue weights; readers should treat results as an aggregate with substantial mix uncertainty.

The base PRIORITY tier rests on H=4.49, only modestly above the tier's L threshold of 4, so modestly lower a or rho judgments could move the base tier even though no audited source establishes a specific corrected value.

sources_audited.note: The Census page defines 541690 as scientific and technical advice other than environmental consulting, lists the cited illustrative specialties, and assigns environmental consulting to 541620.
The Census NAPCS list documents the cited economic, agricultural, geological, occupational-safety, public-safety, pharmaceutical, chemical, atomic-energy, and expert-witness service basket.

The page displays the cited product categories, occupation percentages, and federal-contract figures, but it does not expose enough methodology or dating to validate a six-digit, wage-weighted task-exposure measure.
O*NET documents management-analyst tasks spanning information gathering, data analysis, solution development, interviews, on-site observation, manuals, reports, recommendations, and training.
O*NET documents occupational-health-and-safety tasks including workplace inspection, incident investigation, sampling, compliance work, analysis, collaboration, and documentation.

The 2025 Thomson Reuters release reports a 2,275-person global survey across legal, risk, compliance, tax, accounting, audit, and trade, including strategy prevalence, expected time savings, perceived five-year impact, and reported returns; it is correctly disclosed as adjacent-profession evidence.

Deltek reports its January 2025 survey of 200 senior decision-makers at UK architecture, engineering, and consulting firms and the cited beliefs about efficiency, delivery, staffing, accuracy, bias, ethics, and implementation.

The government-contracting study supports current and intended AI use in proposals, operations, analysis, administration, and related workflows and discusses fixed-price and cost-plus contracting, but it does not measure NAICS 541690 implementation or post-AI price retention.

FOCUS reports 639 announced US professional-services transactions in 2025, flat consulting volume, and acquisition activity driven by private-equity-backed platforms, matching the cited broad-market claim.

Equiteq reports 166 specialty-consulting transactions in H2 2025, a marked sequential and year-over-year slowdown, selective buyer demand, and deals in regulation, compliance, energy, life sciences, and public-sector consulting.
KPMG reports the cited 2025 government-services deal slowdown, strategic deals, platform and bolt-on counts, and budget and procurement uncertainty.

The BLS table reports 2024 and 2034 employment and constant-dollar output for broader 541600; the output endpoints support the disclosed compound-growth bridge to an approximately 1.14 five-year ratio.

caveats: a: No source directly reports a wage-weighted exposure share for NAICS 541310.

a: Exposure is limited to current, not-yet-automated tasks; existing CAD/BIM productivity is excluded conceptually but cannot be measured precisely.
rho: Adoption status is not equivalent to implemented labor substitution or avoided hiring.
rho: The AIA studies include firms outside the frozen EBITDA band and may overweight firms active in the association.
rho: Tool capabilities and professional standards may change substantially during the five-year horizon.
e: The frozen n estimate already bridges firm size into an EBITDA band; e does not re-estimate that input.
e: The 84% architect-of-record statistic is billings-weighted, dated 2019, and not a firm eligibility rate.
e: Repeat-client behavior is not directly quantified in the fetched evidence.
s5: Surveyed likelihood is not observed transaction incidence and includes firms outside the LMM band.
s5: The small-firm transition-plan evidence is from 2020 and planning does not imply a control transfer.
s5: Architecture firms may transition internally over many years, which does not necessarily meet the qualifying-transfer definition.
q: Billing-method shares are from 2019, before the current generative-AI cycle.
q: Professional fee plus reimbursables does not reveal whether the professional component is fixed, hourly, or renegotiable.
q: The estimate does not include demand volume loss, which belongs in d5 and o, or implementation friction, which belongs in rho.
d5: Employment is an input proxy rather than a direct measure of constant-quality service quantity.
d5: The AIA forecast covers nonresidential construction only and extends just through 2027.
d5: Residential work, regional cycles, renovation, interest rates, and construction-cost inflation create substantial dispersion.
o: Licensing rules vary, and many jurisdictions exempt certain small residential, agricultural, or low-risk projects.
o: Title and practice regulation do not guarantee that the current firm model captures all required accountability.
o: The estimate covers year-five quantity after elimination or self-service, not task automation within retained projects.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project-based outsourced professional services for private and public owners, commonly billed by stipulated sum, professional fee plus reimbursables, hourly rates, or a percentage of construction cost; repeat demand comes from continuing owner relationships and successive projects rather than subscriptions.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal design units, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, design-build contractors classified in construction, landscape architecture, standalone engineering, and firms without transferable operating relationships or accountable architectural-service delivery.
lens.included_activities: US architectural-services firms in the $1-10M normalized EBITDA band that provide building planning, architectural design, documentation, specification, permitting, and construction-phase services to external owners and repeat or recurring clients.

a.bridge: AIA measures present AI use, task-level inefficiency, and common use cases among US architectural professionals, while BLS describes the architect task basket; neither measures wage-weighted task exposure. I map exposed documentation, specification research, takeoffs, visualization, proposals, communications, meeting records, and portions of design iteration onto the wage-bearing workflow, then exclude already-automated CAD/BIM work and retain human review, client judgment, coordination, site work, and sealing. Because survey enthusiasm and experimentation can overstate technically substitutable production work, the proxy is more likely to overstate than understate exposure.
caveats: No source directly reports a wage-weighted exposure share for NAICS 541310.
The AIA survey covers architectural professionals and firm leaders rather than a representative payroll-weighted sample of LMM firms.
Exposure is limited to current, not-yet-automated tasks; existing CAD/BIM productivity is excluded conceptually but cannot be measured precisely.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study from AIA, Deltek, or a large architecture-firm panel that reports labor hours and compensation by project phase and task, paired with controlled productivity trials for BIM documentation, specification, code review, and project administration, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: Architecture combines a large digital production and information-processing workload with substantial licensed judgment and coordination. AIA identifies cost estimation, takeoffs, product-library updates, and technical specifications as inefficient tasks, while current AI use clusters in chatbots, text analytics, transcription, image generation, and product research. A 40% base reflects material exposure across production and practice operations without treating whole architect roles as substitutable.

d5.bridge: BLS projects architect employment, and AIA forecasts nominal nonresidential construction spending, whereas d5 is constant-price, constant-quality service quantity for the full current architectural-service basket. I use the 4% 2024-34 architect employment projection as a slow-growth five-year anchor and temper it with the 2026-27 construction slowdown and divergent building-sector outlook. Employment can understate quantity when productivity rises, while nominal construction spending can overstate real service demand during cost inflation.
caveats: Employment is an input proxy rather than a direct measure of constant-quality service quantity.
The AIA forecast covers nonresidential construction only and extends just through 2027.
Residential work, regional cycles, renovation, interest rates, and construction-cost inflation create substantial dispersion.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year real forecast of architectural firm billings by residential and nonresidential building type, deflated by an architecture-services price index and including renovation, would materially change this range.
d5.rationale: BLS projects architect employment to grow 4% from 2024 to 2034 and says architects will remain needed for construction and renovation, including sustainable design. Near-term conditions are weak: AIA reports nonresidential spending fell 2% in 2025, forecasts a 0.3% decline in 2026 and 3.0% growth in 2027, with sharp sector divergence. The base therefore holds real service quantity approximately flat to slightly higher over five years, with a wide cyclical range.

caveats: The frozen n estimate already bridges firm size into an EBITDA band; e does not re-estimate that input.
The 84% architect-of-record statistic is billings-weighted, dated 2019, and not a firm eligibility rate.
Repeat-client behavior is not directly quantified in the fetched evidence.
e.change_evidence: A representative SUSB-linked survey of $1-10M EBITDA architecture firms reporting external-client revenue, repeat-client billings, principal concentration, architect-of-record status, and transferability after owner exit would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition centers external professional planning and design services, and AIA reports that 84% of 2019 billings were from projects where the firm served as architect of record. Most LMM practices should therefore provide a transferable outsourced service with repeat-client potential, though project-by-project revenue and principal-bound residential or design-boutique relationships disqualify a meaningful minority.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $25,388.4M / sectoral output (T30) $54,890.2M, year 2022

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $263,174 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541310); class <5: 13,996 firms, avg revenue/firm $377,518 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $59,082 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,719 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,387,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $217,144 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,928 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,115,513 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $487,578 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,361 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,632,600 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,664,002 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 228 firms, avg revenue/firm $44,196,899 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,916,815 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 62 firms, avg revenue/firm $111,625,710 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,469,424 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,589

o.bridge: NCARB observes universal jurisdictional licensing infrastructure and AIA states that the architect of record remains responsible for code compliance and design integrity regardless of AI involvement; neither measures the exact quantity share that still requires a lens-type operator. I translate regulated responsible control, sealing, client coordination, and construction-phase accountability into a high operator-required share, then deduct exempt project types, client self-service, commoditized early design, and software-only tasks. Regulation likely supports the upper side, but using title protection as a proxy may overstate required firm involvement for exempt or unsealed work.
caveats: Licensing rules vary, and many jurisdictions exempt certain small residential, agricultural, or low-risk projects.
Title and practice regulation do not guarantee that the current firm model captures all required accountability.
The estimate covers year-five quantity after elimination or self-service, not task automation within retained projects.
o.change_evidence: A jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction analysis of permit submissions and architecture-practice exemptions, combined with owner surveys on self-service and software-only procurement by project value and type, would materially move o.
o.rationale: All 55 US jurisdictions have architecture boards that issue licenses and regulate practice, and AIA's AI guidance says professional responsibility cannot be delegated and the architect of record remains responsible for code compliance and design integrity. Software can eliminate portions of ideation, drafting, research, and administration, but most permitted-building work should still require a licensed, accountable operator who coordinates and accepts responsibility for the deliverable.

q.bridge: AIA observes billing-method mix and perceived fee pressure, not the discounted five-year retention of an implemented gross benefit. I treat stipulated-sum and other pre-agreed professional-fee work as initially more retainable than hourly work, where fewer hours directly reduce invoices, then discount all methods for renewals, competitive bids, client negotiation, and gradual repricing. Fixed-fee project overrun risk can reduce realized retention, so the billing-method proxy can overstate q; differentiated quality and capacity expansion can offset that direction.
caveats: Billing-method shares are from 2019, before the current generative-AI cycle.
Professional fee plus reimbursables does not reveal whether the professional component is fixed, hourly, or renegotiable.
The estimate does not include demand volume loss, which belongs in d5 and o, or implementation friction, which belongs in rho.
q.change_evidence: Project-level contracts and invoices from a representative LMM firm cohort, linked to AI implementation costs, labor hours, realized margins, renewal pricing, win rates, and client-requested givebacks for five years, would materially change q.
q.rationale: In 2019, stipulated sums represented 37.7% of billings, professional fees plus reimbursables 31.6%, hourly rates 18.1%, and percentage-of-construction-cost work 10.0%. The mix gives operators meaningful near-term capture on fixed or pre-agreed fees, but 46% of firm leaders in 2024 said fee negotiation had become more challenging than four to five years earlier. A 56% discounted retention base reflects partial capture followed by client sharing and competition.

rho.bridge: AIA directly surveys firm-level adoption status, not the share of exposed labor opportunity implemented within five years. I translate 8% already implemented, 20% implementing, and 35% considering adoption into a five-year implementation share, discounting for the markedly lower adoption of smaller firms, workflow integration, data security, accuracy, training, and mandatory professional review. Stated consideration can exceed completed implementation, so the proxy is biased upward unless tool integration accelerates.
caveats: Adoption status is not equivalent to implemented labor substitution or avoided hiring.
The AIA studies include firms outside the frozen EBITDA band and may overweight firms active in the association.
Tool capabilities and professional standards may change substantially during the five-year horizon.
rho.change_evidence: Annual AIA Firm Survey microdata by employee count showing production hours saved, workflow penetration, failed deployments, and sustained headcount avoidance in firms under 50 employees would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: Only 8% of firm leaders reported integrated AI, but another 20% were implementing and 35% considering it, leaving a credible path to meaningful adoption over five years. Implementation should be strongest in low-risk administrative and research workflows and slower in construction documents and design decisions because 90% or more of respondents reported concerns about inaccuracy, unintended consequences, privacy, authenticity, and transparency.

s5.bridge: The AIA survey measures leaders' subjective likelihood of a merger or acquisition over the next 3-5 years, not realized qualifying control transfers among eligible LMM firms. I anchor below the 36% of respondents not classified as unlikely, recognizing that only 5% called a transaction very likely, and exclude non-control combinations, internal reorganizations, failed deals, and nontransferable practices. Expectations may overstate completed transfers, while unplanned retirements may make the proxy understate them.
caveats: Surveyed likelihood is not observed transaction incidence and includes firms outside the LMM band.
The small-firm transition-plan evidence is from 2020 and planning does not imply a control transfer.
Architecture firms may transition internally over many years, which does not necessarily meet the qualifying-transfer definition.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, or Axial deal counts matched to NAICS 541310 and firm revenue, plus AIA owner-age and completed succession microdata for 10-49-person practices, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: In the August 2025 AIA survey, 64% of firm leaders said acquisition or merger was not at all likely or not very likely for their firm, while 5% said very likely. Separately, only 15% of small firms had an ownership transition plan in the 2020 AIA Firm Survey, suggesting succession pressure but also execution risk. A 16% five-year probability allows for both third-party control transactions and transferable owner successions while excluding mere closures and internal reorganizations.

records.principal_driver: A labor-intensive digital production workflow creates broad assistive-AI use cases across documentation, specifications, research, visualization, and administration.
records.principal_weakness: Project cyclicality and competitive fee renegotiation can prevent technically achievable labor savings from becoming durable retained cash flow.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments planning and designing residential, institutional, leisure, commercial, and industrial buildings and structures using knowledge of design, construction, zoning, codes, and materials.

sources.supports: A statistically valid AIA survey reports 8% of firm leaders integrated AI, 20% implementing, 35% considering, 6% of individuals using regularly, 53% experimenting, common low-risk uses, inefficient task areas, and 90-94% concern levels across major risk categories.

sources.supports: Reports results from more than 1,200 firms, one-third of firms using AI day to day, and firm metrics spanning billings, practice, technology, and ownership-transition planning; the linked report summary also contextualizes architect-of-record billings.

caveats: a: OEWS is four-digit Legal Services and May 2023, not six-digit Offices of Lawyers or the LMM lens.
a: Clio reports potential task automation, not observed labor substitution, and its customer/survey population may be more technology-forward than the industry.

a: The frozen l input uses 2024 employee wages over 2022 receipts, is harmonized by a cross-code divisor, and omits proprietor and partner labor; this is material for law firms and may understate economic labor intensity.
rho: Adoption is not implementation depth, and usage surveys disagree sharply because they define AI and samples differently.
rho: Thomson Reuters respondents span multiple professional sectors and countries, and their 12-hour five-year expectation is a forecast, not observed savings.
rho: Implementation varies substantially by practice area, matter sensitivity, court rules, client requirements, and firm technology maturity.
e: No national dataset measures the share of LMM law firms with transferable client relationships or compliant ownership structures.
e: Ethics rules vary by jurisdiction; Arizona, Utah, and D.C. have exceptions or alternative regimes, while multistate practices face overlapping restrictions.

e: The frozen n=7193 is itself an ESTIMATE margin-bridged using a broad Business & Consumer Services EBITDA margin; law-firm margins and partner compensation conventions may move firms into or out of the band.
s5: Lawyer age is not owner age, and retirement is not equivalent to a control transfer.
s5: The public evidence does not provide an industry-wide denominator of eligible firms or verified completed deals.
s5: Sales to nonlawyers are generally constrained, and client choice can impair transferability even when a practice sale is permitted.
q: Clio's firms may not represent all LMM practices, and firm incidence of a fee type does not reveal its revenue share.
q: Benefit retention depends on the ability to refill freed capacity; volume loss is intentionally excluded from q.
q: ABA fee guidance is based on Model Rules and reasonableness; binding state rules and engagement terms vary.
d5: Employment is an imperfect demand proxy and the BLS projection is national, occupational, and ten-year rather than industry-specific and five-year.
d5: The current service basket is heterogeneous and cyclical: litigation, restructuring, transactions, real estate, and consumer practices can move in different directions.
d5: The ratio is intended to be constant-price and constant-quality, but no directly matching public forecast was found.
o: No source directly measures operator-required quantity for the current 541110 service basket.
o: Unauthorized-practice and paraprofessional rules vary by state and may liberalize over five years.
o: High-stakes and complex matters are more operator-dependent than routine consumer forms and information queries, so practice mix is decisive.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External individuals, businesses, insurers, and institutions buy matter-based or continuing legal services through hourly, flat, contingent, subscription, retainer, and blended fee arrangements.
lens.excluded_activities: In-house legal departments, captive insurance-defense units, nonprofit and government law offices, financing vehicles, dormant practices, and practices whose matters or client relationships are not reasonably transferable.
lens.included_activities: US law firms in the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band that provide recurring or repeat legal advice, representation, drafting, transactions, litigation, compliance, and related client service to external individuals or organizations.

a.bridge: May 2023 OEWS measures employment and mean wages for four-digit Legal Services, not six-digit Offices of Lawyers, while Clio estimates automatable task shares rather than wage-weighted, current, not-yet-automated opportunity. I wage-weight the dominant occupational groups implied by OEWS and temper Clio's 57% lawyer, 81% legal-secretary, and up-to-74% billable-task exposure for work already automated and for review, judgment, relationship, and courtroom tasks. Clio's technology-user sample and task methodology likely bias exposure upward; OEWS's inclusion of other legal services and exclusion of proprietors can bias the mix in either direction.
caveats: OEWS is four-digit Legal Services and May 2023, not six-digit Offices of Lawyers or the LMM lens.
Clio reports potential task automation, not observed labor substitution, and its customer/survey population may be more technology-forward than the industry.

The frozen l input uses 2024 employee wages over 2022 receipts, is harmonized by a cross-code divisor, and omits proprietor and partner labor; this is material for law firms and may understate economic labor intensity.
a.change_evidence: Obtain a task-level time study from LMM law firms that maps time and cash compensation by SOC occupation, practice area, and workflow, and separately records tasks already automated; Clio or Thomson Reuters billing-system telemetry linked to task codes would materially tighten the estimate.
a.rationale: Legal Services wages are concentrated in lawyers, with meaningful paralegal and administrative support. Research, document review, drafting, discovery, intake, scheduling, billing, and information gathering are exposed, but persuasion, client counseling, negotiation, fact judgment, and accountable sign-off remain substantially human. The range reflects both a large task ceiling and uncertainty over how much current tool use has already harvested it.

d5.bridge: BLS projects lawyer employment, not constant-price, constant-quality quantity of the current Offices of Lawyers service basket. I convert its 4% 2024-34 lawyer-employment growth to an approximate five-year midpoint ratio of 1.02 and widen the range for productivity, legal-cycle exposure, price competition, outsourcing, and substitution. Employment can understate demand if productivity rises, but it can overstate demand for the current basket if staffing persists while routine work migrates to software or lower-cost providers.
caveats: Employment is an imperfect demand proxy and the BLS projection is national, occupational, and ten-year rather than industry-specific and five-year.
The current service basket is heterogeneous and cyclical: litigation, restructuring, transactions, real estate, and consumer practices can move in different directions.
The ratio is intended to be constant-price and constant-quality, but no directly matching public forecast was found.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain BEA or Census real-output measures specific to 541110, deflate practice-area revenue with matched legal-service price indexes, and combine them with five-year client legal-spend forecasts and matter counts by litigation, transactional, regulatory, and consumer practice.
d5.rationale: BLS expects continued legal-service needs from individuals, businesses, and government and projects lawyer employment up 4% over ten years, while also warning that price competition, automation, and low-cost providers will affect routine work. On a five-year horizon, broadly stable to modestly growing real service quantity is more defensible than either rapid growth or collapse.

caveats: No national dataset measures the share of LMM law firms with transferable client relationships or compliant ownership structures.
Ethics rules vary by jurisdiction; Arizona, Utah, and D.C. have exceptions or alternative regimes, while multistate practices face overlapping restrictions.

The frozen n=7193 is itself an ESTIMATE margin-bridged using a broad Business & Consumer Services EBITDA margin; law-firm margins and partner compensation conventions may move firms into or out of the band.
e.change_evidence: Build a representative broker and state-bar diligence sample of LMM practices showing revenue recurrence, top-client concentration, matter duration, partner-originated revenue, client retention after partner departure, buyer licensing structure, and the fraction that can satisfy each governing jurisdiction's sale and ownership rules.
e.rationale: Most offices of lawyers serve external clients and many generate repeat matters or referrals, but eligibility is reduced by personal-goodwill dependence, client consent and conflicts, nonportable contingent or one-off books, and ownership restrictions. ABA Model Rule 1.17 permits a practice sale only under conditions including sale to lawyers or law firms and client notice; Model Rule 5.4 generally bars nonlawyer ownership, and an ABA review reported the traditional ban in 48 US jurisdictions. The estimate assumes a legally compliant lawyer-controlled buyer or structure rather than unrestricted investor ownership.
