caveats: No source directly measures operator-required quantity for the current 541110 service basket.
Unauthorized-practice and paraprofessional rules vary by state and may liberalize over five years.
High-stakes and complex matters are more operator-dependent than routine consumer forms and information queries, so practice mix is decisive.
o.change_evidence: Measure the share of matters and revenue by practice area that can legally and practically be completed without a lawyer, using state unauthorized-practice rules, court filing data, consumer legal-platform completion rates, client surveys, and longitudinal matter migration from firms to software or licensed paraprofessionals.
o.rationale: A large majority of year-five legal-service quantity should still require a responsible law-firm operator because representation, privileged advice, conflicts, confidentiality, client counseling, negotiation, and court-facing work require licensed judgment and supervision. ABA Formal Opinion 512 says generative AI may assist but cannot replace the judgment and experience needed to advise clients or carry out representations. The deduction recognizes consumer self-service, document automation, alternative legal providers, and software handling routine questions and forms.

q.bridge: Clio measures firm billing modes and reported revenue effects, not the discounted five-year share of a specific implemented gross benefit. I map exclusively hourly billing toward rapid pass-through, flat/contingent/subscription work toward greater initial retention, and mixed billing between them, then reduce retention for renewal repricing, competition, client sharing, and ethical fee limits. Firm-level billing incidence is not revenue-weighted and likely overstates retention where firms offer flat fees on only a small share of matters.
caveats: Clio's firms may not represent all LMM practices, and firm incidence of a fee type does not reveal its revenue share.
Benefit retention depends on the ability to refill freed capacity; volume loss is intentionally excluded from q.
ABA fee guidance is based on Model Rules and reasonableness; binding state rules and engagement terms vary.
q.change_evidence: Obtain firm-level revenue-weighted fee-mix data and matched matter cohorts before and after AI implementation, including hours, realized rates, flat-fee margins, matter volume, client concessions, renewal repricing, write-downs, and competitor pricing over at least three years.
q.rationale: Hourly billing remains a strong leakage channel: lawyers must bill actual time, and faster AI-assisted work can directly reduce invoices unless released capacity is sold. Flat, contingent, and subscription arrangements permit more capture, but even those fees must remain reasonable. Clio reports 41% of firms exclusively hourly and 59% using flat fees either exclusively or alongside hourly fees; among wide AI adopters, pricing responses include increases, reductions, and new fee types, supporting partial rather than full retention.

rho.bridge: ABA adoption surveys and Thomson Reuters expectations measure tool use and predicted hours saved across attorney firm sizes and, for Thomson Reuters, legal, tax, and risk professionals globally; they do not measure the fraction of exposed law-firm labor operationally implemented. I translate current small/midsize adoption, five-year time-saving expectations, and stated blockers into the share of the exposure in a that can survive workflow redesign, verification, confidentiality, supervision, and change management. Respondent optimism may bias upward, while rapid model improvement and falling tool costs may bias downward.
caveats: Adoption is not implementation depth, and usage surveys disagree sharply because they define AI and samples differently.
Thomson Reuters respondents span multiple professional sectors and countries, and their 12-hour five-year expectation is a forecast, not observed savings.
Implementation varies substantially by practice area, matter sensitivity, court rules, client requirements, and firm technology maturity.
rho.change_evidence: Survey the target firms annually for workflow-level deployment, percentage of matters using approved AI, review minutes per AI output, error and rework rates, data-security approvals, and realized FTE or avoided-hire effects; matched pre/post billing and payroll data would be decisive.
rho.rationale: Implementation should advance meaningfully within five years because legal research, drafting, review, discovery, and back-office tools already exist. It remains below the technical ceiling: the ABA found only 24.1% usage in 2-9 lawyer firms and 29.5% in 10-49 lawyer firms in 2024, alongside strong accuracy, reliability, and privacy concerns; professional rules require competent review and secure handling of client information.

s5.bridge: ABA/BLS age data describe all active lawyers, not equity owners of eligible LMM firms or completed control transfers: 13.1% were age 65+ and 17.9% were 55-64 in 2023. I use those cohorts as a succession-pressure proxy, then discount for lawyers who work past 65, firms that close, and internal reorganizations, while allowing for transfers driven by reasons other than retirement. Because owners are likely older than all lawyers, the age proxy tends to understate succession pressure; because many retirements do not create a qualifying transfer, it tends to overstate completed transfer probability.
caveats: Lawyer age is not owner age, and retirement is not equivalent to a control transfer.
The public evidence does not provide an industry-wide denominator of eligible firms or verified completed deals.
Sales to nonlawyers are generally constrained, and client choice can impair transferability even when a practice sale is permitted.
s5.change_evidence: Acquire five years of closed transaction data from specialist law-practice brokers, state-bar succession programs, and legal-sector M&A advisers, with seller age, firm revenue/EBITDA, internal versus external buyer, client retention, and failed or withdrawn processes.
s5.rationale: A meaningful owner cohort is near or beyond conventional retirement age, and law practices can legally be sold, but personal client relationships and regulatory requirements suppress conversion of succession need into qualifying control transfers. The base therefore sits near the observed 65+ lawyer share rather than treating all older lawyers as sellers.

records.principal_driver: High-cost research, drafting, review, discovery, and administrative workflows create meaningful implementable productivity potential.
records.principal_weakness: Hourly billing and lawyer-ownership/client-portability constraints can prevent technical productivity from becoming durable, transferable cash flow.

caveats: The stated no-deviation lens excludes practices whose matters or client relationships are not reasonably transferable even though transfer eligibility is separately estimated in e. This favorable pre-screening should be disclosed as a lens choice, but correcting it would not plausibly reduce the capped F factor enough to change the PRIORITY base tier or the VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The a bridge is directionally plausible but not numerically reproducible from the cited facts: Clio supplies task shares for lawyers and legal secretaries/administrative assistants, while the wage-weighted treatment of paralegals and the remaining occupations and the deduction for already-automated work remain reviewer-visible judgment. Its PROXY label and wide range are honest.

The s5 rationale describes the age evidence as applying to all active lawyers, while S5 is BLS/CPS occupational data and does not identify active-bar status, equity ownership, or eligible practices. The owner and transfer mismatches are otherwise explicitly disclosed, and F remains well above its tier threshold across the registered range.

S8 directly establishes the ownership-rule landscape but only indirectly informs o; no cited source measures the 0.80 operator-required quantity share. The primitive is appropriately labeled ESTIMATE and already keeps the action at VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS.

The frozen n estimate uses a broad cross-sector EBITDA margin and does not account cleanly for partner-compensation conventions in law firms. This can materially affect the count of firms in the LMM band, but the weakness is disclosed, n is labeled ESTIMATE, and the review contract holds dataset anchors fixed.

The principal driver and weakness are not tightly aligned with the scorecard: H is the weakest base factor, while the ownership-sensitive F factor is capped at 10 and the hourly-billing-sensitive C factor is 8.4. The narrative remains directionally supported, but readers should use the displayed factors rather than treat those labels as a ranking of score contributions.

sources_audited.note: The May 2023 BLS page is for four-digit NAICS 541100 and reports lawyers at 37.49%, paralegals and legal assistants at 23.04%, and office and administrative support at 26.25% of employment, with wage data.

Clio's October 2024 release reports that nearly three-quarters of hourly billable tasks are exposed, including 57% of lawyer tasks and 81% of legal-secretary and administrative-assistant tasks; it is potential automation rather than realized substitution, as disclosed.

The ABA TechReport identifies a late-2024 survey, reports 24.1% use for 2-9 lawyer firms and 29.5% for 10-49 lawyer firms, and quantifies accuracy, reliability, privacy, cost, and learning-time concerns.

The Thomson Reuters article describes a global survey of more than 2,200 legal, tax, and risk/compliance professionals, predicted savings of four hours in one year and 12 hours in five years, and nearly two-thirds emphasizing human oversight.
The ABA demographics page reproduces BLS/CPS occupational data for 2023: 17.9% of lawyers were 55-64, 13.1% were 65+, and median age was 46; it does not identify equity owners or completed transfers.
ABA Model Rule 1.17 permits practice sales subject to seller cessation, sale to lawyers or law firms, client notice and choice, and no fee increase by reason of the sale.
ABA Model Rule 5.4 restricts fee sharing and partnerships with nonlawyers, nonlawyer ownership, and outside direction of professional judgment.

The 2025 ABA magazine article supports the reported D.C., Arizona, and Utah alternatives and states that 48 other U.S. jurisdictions maintain the traditional ban. It is an advocacy article about ownership regimes and does not directly measure eligible-firm share or operator-required service quantity.

Clio's online report states that in 2024, 41% of firms billed exclusively hourly and 59% used flat fees exclusively or alongside hourly billing; it also reports varied pricing responses among wide AI adopters and consumer use of AI for legal problems.

BLS projects lawyer employment growth of 4% from 2024 to 2034, expects continuing legal demand, and identifies price competition plus automation or outsourcing of routine legal work. This is occupational employment evidence, not direct real-output measurement.
The 2026 BLS projections article identifies AI-assisted contract review, discovery, and research for paralegals and legal assistants and reports projected 2024-34 employment change of 0.2%.

ABA Formal Opinion 512 addresses competence, verification, confidentiality, supervision, professional judgment, actual-time hourly billing, and reasonableness of flat and contingent fees when using generative AI.

records.title: Offices of Lawyers

caveats: a: No source directly measures wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task exposure for six-digit NAICS 541850.
a: The occupation profile reports headcount shares rather than compensation shares and does not fully expose installation or maintenance occupations.
a: Advertising-industry AI evidence includes brands, agencies, publishers, and digital media rather than only display operators.
rho: General advertising adoption may run ahead of traditional local billboard operators.
rho: Declared use or intent does not establish sustained workflow integration or labor avoidance.
rho: Digital and static display operators may implement at very different rates.
e: Public sources do not measure eligibility in the frozen lower-middle-market population.
e: The industry combines display creation and placement with varying degrees of asset ownership, which affects transferability but not necessarily service eligibility.
s5: Gallup covers all employer industries rather than display advertising specifically.
s5: One large consolidator's acquisition count may not represent the probability for each eligible firm.
s5: Asset purchases do not always constitute qualifying firm control transfers.
q: Clear Channel and Lamar are much larger and more asset-intensive than many lower-middle-market firms.
q: The industry includes creative and operational services whose prices may track labor more closely.
q: Retention could be lower if self-service buying shifts bargaining power to advertisers or platforms.
d5: Industry revenue is not constant-price, constant-quality demand quantity.
d5: The 4.5% forecast was published in a 2024 industry report and ends in 2029 rather than 2031.
d5: Trade-association measures may not capture weaker segments or small-operator dispersion.
o: The estimate weights the asset-operating core more heavily than design-only services within the NAICS code.
o: Software could disintermediate local selling faster than expected even while a physical asset operator remains.
o: Human content approval is an industry best practice rather than a universal legal mandate.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Advertisers and agencies buy or lease access to location-specific billboard, transit, airport, street-furniture, retail, and other display inventory, commonly in campaign periods or network packages. Rates are driven primarily by location, audience, demand, competition, format, and occupancy; operators may also charge for design, printing, installation, and measurement.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive in-house advertising units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, solo nonemployers, general advertising agencies classified elsewhere, media-buying agencies, sign manufacturers whose primary activity is fabrication, and firms outside the lower-middle-market band.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat public-display advertising services to external customers, including campaign design, inventory sales, billboard and place-based display placement, digital-display management, printing, installation, and related measurement and account service.

a.bridge: A 2026 industry profile reports that management, business and financial, arts/design/media, sales, and office-support occupations together represent 89% of the workforce in Indoor and Outdoor Display Advertising. Advertising-industry AI use cases and buyer surveys are used to assign greater exposure to creative generation, prospecting, media planning, inventory matching, proposals, campaign setup, optimization, measurement, and administration, and lower exposure to relationship negotiation, physical installation, inspection, maintenance, and field operations. The source provides employment rather than wage weights and may overstate exposure where senior sales and management wages dominate.
caveats: No source directly measures wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task exposure for six-digit NAICS 541850.
The occupation profile reports headcount shares rather than compensation shares and does not fully expose installation or maintenance occupations.
Advertising-industry AI evidence includes brands, agencies, publishers, and digital media rather than only display operators.
a.change_evidence: A representative wage-weighted timesheet study of 541850 firms, segmented by sales, creative, campaign operations, administration, installation, maintenance, and management and adjusted for software already in production, would materially narrow the range.
a.rationale: The occupation mix is concentrated in cognitive, creative, commercial, and administrative work that AI can assist or partially substitute. Clear Channel is already piloting AI-enabled sales tools, while industry evidence shows AI use across planning, audience selection, forecasting, creative, and performance analysis. The opportunity is capped by high-value local selling, direct negotiations, brand judgment, and hands-on display operations.

d5.bridge: OAAA reported record U.S. OOH revenue of $9.46 billion in 2025, up 3.6%, with digital OOH up 10.5% and 36.3% of total revenue. Its 2024 facts report cited a 4.5% annual OOH ad-sales forecast for 2025 through 2029. These nominal revenue measures are converted into a lower five-year constant-price, constant-quality service-quantity estimate by discounting for media-rate inflation, digital mix shift, and the older forecast endpoint. This may still overstate real volume if pricing drives reported growth, or understate it if digital screens create materially more sellable campaign slots.
caveats: Industry revenue is not constant-price, constant-quality demand quantity.
The 4.5% forecast was published in a 2024 industry report and ends in 2029 rather than 2031.
Trade-association measures may not capture weaker segments or small-operator dispersion.
d5.change_evidence: A current U.S. five-year OOH forecast separating real impressions, occupancy, number of campaigns, rate inflation, static versus digital inventory, and local versus national spend would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: The medium has recorded sustained recent growth, with digital conversion, programmatic access, better measurement, and a broad local advertiser base expanding use. Demand is cyclical and correlated with GDP, and display operators compete with fast-growing digital channels, so a recession or weak attribution could produce a flat or declining five-year outcome despite the positive base case.

caveats: Public sources do not measure eligibility in the frozen lower-middle-market population.
The industry combines display creation and placement with varying degrees of asset ownership, which affects transferability but not necessarily service eligibility.
e.change_evidence: A stratified audit of the frozen firm universe using Business Register descriptions or commercial profiles, coding external customers, repeat revenue, asset operations, owner dependence, and actual primary activity, would replace this judgment.
e.rationale: NAICS 541850 is defined around creating, designing, or placing public display advertising for customers, so most employer firms in the lower-middle-market are external-service businesses within the lens. Deductions cover misclassified sign fabrication, owner-dependent operations without transferable systems, entities holding isolated sites without an operating service, and businesses without repeat customers or campaigns.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541850 ($2,510,459,678) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541850 ($8,196,645,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $382,092 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541850); class <5: 1,543 firms, avg revenue/firm $457,235 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $71,557 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 283 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,808,318 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $283,002 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 130 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,468,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $542,742 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 84 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,111,917 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,895,515 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $53,150,286 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,318,020 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 15 firms, avg revenue/firm $268,195,267 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $41,972,559 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 112

caveats: The estimate weights the asset-operating core more heavily than design-only services within the NAICS code.
Software could disintermediate local selling faster than expected even while a physical asset operator remains.
Human content approval is an industry best practice rather than a universal legal mandate.
o.change_evidence: Operator and advertiser cohort data quantifying self-service programmatic purchases, direct platform buying without local sales support, outsourced versus internal creative, and the share of revenue still requiring human content approval and physical operations would materially move the estimate.
o.rationale: Most year-five service quantity should still require an accountable display operator because someone must control or contract for permitted sites, maintain and inspect physical structures, schedule inventory, meet landlord and municipal obligations, and approve content. Programmatic and agentic tools can automate discovery, planning, buying, creative, and reporting, but they do not eliminate the permitted physical network. Some agency-like planning, creative, and low-touch sales demand can become self-service or shift entirely to software, preventing a near-one estimate.

q.bridge: Clear Channel's 2025 filing shows that most roadside advertising is sold in four-week periods and that rates depend on location, demand, competition, display size, occupancy, reach, and impressions rather than labor hours. This large-operator revenue model is used to infer above-average retention of implemented labor savings for lower-middle-market operators: media-space pricing does not mechanically fall when internal labor hours decline. The proxy may overstate retention for design, printing, installation, or agency-like firms and where programmatic price transparency accelerates competition.
caveats: Clear Channel and Lamar are much larger and more asset-intensive than many lower-middle-market firms.
The industry includes creative and operational services whose prices may track labor more closely.
Retention could be lower if self-service buying shifts bargaining power to advertisers or platforms.
q.change_evidence: Contract- and campaign-level data from a representative 541850 panel showing revenue by inventory rent versus labor-linked services, renewal pricing, discounts, commissions, platform fees, occupancy, and margins before and after automation would materially narrow the range.
q.rationale: Inventory-based and audience-based pricing gives an operator room to retain savings in sales preparation, creative versioning, scheduling, reporting, and administration. Over five years, advertisers, agencies, platforms, and competitors will still capture part of the gain through negotiation, lower service fees, faster turnaround expectations, and transparent programmatic markets, but the permitted location remains the scarce product.

rho.bridge: IAB's 2025 survey of more than 500 advertising buy- and sell-side experts found only 30% had fully integrated AI across the campaign lifecycle, while half of those not fully integrated expected to do so by 2026. A 2026 buyer survey shows strong intent for AI in analysis, creative optimization, planning, and inventory evaluation but lower intent for direct deal execution and negotiations. These broader advertising populations anchor five-year implementation in display operators; the estimate rises above current full integration as tools mature, then is discounted for smaller-firm resources, legacy inventory systems, data quality, content governance, and relationship-based sales. It may overstate small-operator integration but understate turnkey platform adoption.
caveats: General advertising adoption may run ahead of traditional local billboard operators.
Declared use or intent does not establish sustained workflow integration or labor avoidance.
Digital and static display operators may implement at very different rates.
rho.change_evidence: A 541850 operator survey measuring production deployment, percentage of workflows and campaigns touched, hours saved, system integration, human-review load, and results by firm size would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Five years is enough for standard tools to reach campaign planning, creative versioning, lead prioritization, yield support, reporting, and back-office work. Implementation should remain incomplete because nearly two-thirds of the 2025 IAB respondents cited data quality, protection, and tool fragmentation as leading barriers, and high-risk content and negotiations retain human control.

s5.bridge: Gallup's U.S. employer-business survey supplies a 22% five-year sell-or-transfer anchor. The base is adjusted upward because Lamar completed more than 50 outdoor-advertising asset acquisitions in 2025 and more than 20 in 2024, including customer contracts and site-location intangibles, demonstrating unusually active consolidator demand. The adjustment may overstate qualifying firm-control transfers because many reported transactions were asset packages rather than changes of control of eligible firms.
caveats: Gallup covers all employer industries rather than display advertising specifically.
One large consolidator's acquisition count may not represent the probability for each eligible firm.
Asset purchases do not always constitute qualifying firm control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A denominator-based five-year transaction census for U.S. 541850 firms in the frozen size band, separating control sales from isolated billboard purchases, internal reorganizations, closures, and non-control investments, would materially change this estimate.
s5.rationale: Scarce permitted sites, long-lived location rights, customer lists, and local scale create strategic acquisition value and a repeat buyer universe. These features support a transfer probability above the all-employer anchor, but some deals transfer only selected faces or leases, and local operators may remain family-owned or too owner-dependent for a qualifying sale.

records.principal_driver: Scarce permitted display inventory lets an operator retain AI savings from a workforce concentrated in sales, creative, analytical, and administrative work.
records.principal_weakness: Asset and lease quality, not labor efficiency, determines much of the business value, while programmatic self-service can erode the local sales moat.

caveats: The exact S1 page does not expose the cited 541850 definition. This weakens the documentary basis for e, but e is already an ESTIMATE and correcting its evidence state would not change the CONDITIONAL base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

S2 could not be reopened, leaving the claimed 89% occupation mix and product mix unverified. Downgrading the occupation-weight bridge for a from PROXY to ESTIMATE would not change the already MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action, and the audit does not establish a numeric correction that would change the base tier.

S5 supports four-week-to-one-year contract terms rather than the stronger statement that most roadside advertising is sold in four-week periods. The broader non-labor pricing evidence remains supported; weakening this q bridge does not change the base tier or action because C is capped at 10 in the base case and H is the weakest link.

The lens marks the industry non-heterogeneous even though the record itself distinguishes asset-operating display firms from design-heavy and agency-like firms. Large asset operators supply much of the evidence for q, s5, and o. This is disclosed repeatedly in primitive caveats, so it is not lens shopping, but target-level diligence must segment the two business models.

The d5 bridge converts nominal industry revenue growth into constant-price, constant-quality service quantity through an unstated discount. The packet honestly labels d5 a PROXY and discloses the limitation; plausible correction would lower D from its cap but is not shown to change the CONDITIONAL base tier or the action.

S7 measures owners' stated five-year plans rather than realized qualifying control transfers, while S8 counts many asset acquisitions. The s5 bridge discloses both limitations, and a correction is not shown to reduce F enough to change the base tier or action.

S11's task-level agentic-intent results are based on the 161 respondents aware of agentic AI buying, not the full 205-person sample. The directional use-case evidence remains valid, but the narrower conditional population should temper the a and rho bridges; no resulting correction is shown to change the base tier or action.

sources_audited.note: The cited Census URL opened, but the rendered page is a broad 2022 NAICS page/search for sector 54 and contains no 541850 definition. The exact cited page therefore does not support the stated six-digit definition.
The exact Pell Research URL failed to fetch on repeated reopening attempts, so the claimed January 2026 occupation shares and product-mix statements could not be verified.

The IAB page supports the survey population of more than 500 buy- and sell-side experts, 30% full integration, half of the remainder expecting integration by 2026, and nearly two-thirds citing data quality, protection, and tool fragmentation barriers.

The filing supports AI-enabled sales pilots, location/demand/competition/size/occupancy/reach/impression-based rates, site and permit obligations, and macroeconomic sensitivity. It says customer contracts typically range from four weeks to one year, not that most roadside advertising is sold in four-week periods.

OAAA explicitly recommends human approval for all digital display content and states that software and AI filters cannot fully replace contextual human judgment, especially for automated or programmatic content.

Gallup reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. This is an intention measure, not an observed transfer rate, as the packet's proxy treatment implicitly requires.

Lamar reports over 50 acquisitions for approximately $191.1 million in 2025, over 20 outdoor-advertising asset acquisitions in 2024, and acquired customer lists, contracts, and site-location intangibles. The filing also makes clear that many transactions are asset acquisitions rather than firm-control transfers.
OAAA reports 2025 U.S. OOH revenue of $9.46 billion, 3.6% annual growth, nineteen consecutive growth quarters, and DOOH at 36.3% of revenue with 10.5% growth.
The cited OAAA PDF reports local OOH at 65%, DOOH at 34%, historical annual OOH growth of 3.6%, and projected annual growth of 4.5% for 2025 through 2029.

The study supports stronger intent for performance analysis, creative optimization, planning, and inventory evaluation than for deal execution and negotiations. The overall survey has 205 U.S. buy-side respondents, but the specific agentic-use-case chart is limited to the 161 respondents aware of agentic AI ad buying/campaign execution.

sources.supports: Reports 2025 U.S. OOH revenue of $9.46 billion, 3.6% annual growth, nineteen consecutive growth quarters, and digital OOH at 36.3% of revenue with 10.5% growth.
sources.title: OAAA — Out of Home Advertising Revenue Reaches Record $9.46 Billion

sources.supports: Reports local OOH at 65% of sales, digital OOH at 34%, historical annual growth of 3.6%, and projected OOH ad-sales annual growth of 4.5% for 2025 through 2029.
sources.title: OAAA — 2024 OOH Facts and Figures

sources.supports: Shows strong buyer intent for agentic AI in performance analysis, creative optimization, planning, and inventory discovery, but lower intent for programmatic and direct deal execution and negotiations; based on 205 U.S. buy-side decision-makers.
sources.title: IAB — 2026 Outlook Study

records.title: Indoor and Outdoor Display Advertising

caveats: a: The OEWS mix is industry-specific but does not identify tasks within occupations.
a: The AI survey covers veterinary internal-medicine specialists and may not represent general, rural, large-animal or laboratory practices.
a: Potential labor savings are not the same as realized headcount reductions in capacity-constrained clinics.
rho: Current use of at least one AI tool does not measure depth, reliability or enterprise-wide implementation.
rho: The AVMA technology figures describe broad software readiness and may include practices outside the target EBITDA band.

s5: FTC evidence establishes acquisition activity but not an industry transfer rate and emphasizes specialty and emergency markets.
s5: Corporate tuck-ins can be acquisitions of clinics or assets rather than control transfers of independently counted firms.

q: Retention differs between routine primary care, emergency care, referral specialties, farm contracts, wellness plans and laboratories.
q: Using saved clinician time for more visits raises gross benefit but does not by itself establish retained unit economics.
d5: Employment projections are not direct forecasts of constant-quality service volume.
d5: BLS projections cover occupations across employers, although veterinary services employ 84% of veterinarians and 89% of veterinary technologists and technicians.
d5: Nominal pet spending can rise even if real service quantity is flat, and affordability can suppress visits.
o: The operator requirement varies by state scope-of-practice rules and by companion, farm, emergency, specialty and laboratory setting.
o: Remote monitoring and direct-to-consumer triage may reduce clinic touchpoints without eliminating clinical accountability.
o: The AI survey is attitudinal and specialist-heavy, not a direct displacement measure.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers are primarily pet owners, farms and other animal owners, with some referrals from other veterinary practices. Revenue is predominantly fee-for-service by examination, procedure, diagnostic test, treatment, hospitalization, and dispensed product; preventive, chronic-care, and follow-up needs create repeat demand, while emergency and surgical work is episodic.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal veterinary units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, research and development establishments, non-veterinary boarding or grooming, breeding and horse boarding, animal transportation, and retail-only pet supply activity. Incidental medicine or food sales inside an eligible veterinary practice remain included as ancillary revenue.
lens.included_activities: Veterinary clinics, animal hospitals, specialty and emergency practices, mobile and farm-animal practices, and veterinary diagnostic laboratories that serve external customers, generate recurring or repeat service revenue, and fall within the dataset-defined lower-middle-market EBITDA band.

a.bridge: BLS directly measures the industry's occupation and wage mix, not task-level AI substitutability. I map high exposure to reception, scheduling, billing, records and documentation; moderate exposure to diagnostic support, client communication and parts of management; and low exposure to examination, surgery, restraint, nursing and specimen handling. Wage weighting raises the contribution from veterinarians but their physical and accountable duties cap the estimate. The mapping may overstate substitution because some saved time becomes additional clinical capacity rather than avoided labor, while it may understate workflow redesign across administrative roles.
caveats: The OEWS mix is industry-specific but does not identify tasks within occupations.
The AI survey covers veterinary internal-medicine specialists and may not represent general, rural, large-animal or laboratory practices.
Potential labor savings are not the same as realized headcount reductions in capacity-constrained clinics.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study across general, emergency, specialty, large-animal and laboratory practices that reports wage-weighted task hours before and after deployed AI, separating avoided hiring from added visit capacity, would materially change this value.
a.rationale: The 2023 industry profile reports office and administrative support at 23.21% of employment, including receptionists at 13.26%, while veterinarians, veterinary technologists and technicians, and veterinary assistants account for most of the remaining core workforce [S3]. AI tools already used in specialty practice include scribes, image and laboratory interpretation, client communication, and generative documentation [S4]. However, veterinarians diagnose, prescribe and perform procedures under licensure, and technicians restrain animals, administer anesthesia, collect tests and prepare surgery [S8][S9]. A base of 0.29 therefore reflects substantial exposure in information work but limited exposure in hands-on clinical delivery.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment rather than constant-price, constant-quality service quantity. I convert the 10% ten-year veterinarian growth and 9% ten-year technician growth into an approximate five-year demand signal centered near 1.05, then widen the interval for productivity, labor scarcity, service-mix and affordability effects. Employment can overstate quantity growth if care becomes more labor-intensive, but it can understate demand when clinician shortages constrain observed employment.
caveats: Employment projections are not direct forecasts of constant-quality service volume.
BLS projections cover occupations across employers, although veterinary services employ 84% of veterinarians and 89% of veterinary technologists and technicians.
Nominal pet spending can rise even if real service quantity is flat, and affordability can suppress visits.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted AVMA or VetSuccess data on unique patients, visits, procedures and diagnostic tests per practice, together with pet-population cohorts and unmet-care surveys through 2031, would materially change this value.
d5.rationale: BLS projects veterinarian employment to grow 10% and veterinary technologist and technician employment to grow 9% through 2034, attributing growth to pet-related spending, expanding treatment options and a growing and aging pet population [S8][S9]. Because both major clinical occupations point to similar growth, the base assumes modest real service-quantity expansion rather than flat demand, while the low case allows affordability pressure or pet-population weakness to offset those drivers.

caveats: No source directly measures eligibility for the frozen acquisition lens.
The injected firm count is inferred from SUSB size classes using a generic 15.65% margin; veterinary practices can differ materially in margins and multi-site ownership structure.
NAICS 541940 mixes companion-animal, large-animal, specialty, emergency and diagnostic-laboratory models.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census or commercial database matching veterinary establishments to consolidated ownership, external-customer revenue, normalized EBITDA, recurring-client share and operating status would materially change this value.
e.rationale: The official industry scope centers on externally provided veterinary medicine, dentistry, surgery, testing, hospitals, clinics and laboratories [S1]. Its product list is dominated by examinations, preventive care, diagnosis, treatment, surgery, imaging and laboratory services that naturally support repeat relationships or recurring referral demand [S2]. Most operating firms in the target size band should therefore fit the service lens, with exclusions for captive units, unusual project-only laboratories, non-operating entities and firms whose normalized EBITDA or independence is misclassified.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $27,215.0M / sectoral output (T30) $60,564.7M, year 2022

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $134,841 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541940); class <5: 8,547 firms, avg revenue/firm $415,786 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $65,071 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 6,573 firms, avg revenue/firm $930,923 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $145,689 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 7,178 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,671,661 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $261,615 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 3,831 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,892,081 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $609,111 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 133 firms, avg revenue/firm $26,557,188 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,156,200 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 44 firms, avg revenue/firm $524,440,955 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $82,075,009 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 133

o.bridge: Occupational duties, licensing rules and clinician attitudes establish human accountability and physical-work requirements, not a measured year-five operator-required quantity share. I map examinations, prescribing, surgery, anesthesia, animal restraint, specimen collection and regulated diagnosis to operator-required service, while allowing some triage, monitoring, communication and interpretation to shift to customer self-service or software. This may overstate operator persistence in diagnostic laboratories, where automation is greater, and understate it where professional rules require veterinarian oversight.
caveats: The operator requirement varies by state scope-of-practice rules and by companion, farm, emergency, specialty and laboratory setting.
Remote monitoring and direct-to-consumer triage may reduce clinic touchpoints without eliminating clinical accountability.
The AI survey is attitudinal and specialist-heavy, not a direct displacement measure.
o.change_evidence: State-board rulings, insurer policies and audited deployment studies showing the share of consultations, prescriptions, diagnoses and laboratory outputs delivered without a veterinarian-led operator would materially change this value.
o.rationale: Veterinarians require a professional degree and state license and perform diagnosis, treatment, surgery, vaccination and prescribing [S8]. Technicians perform physical nursing, restraint, anesthesia, imaging, specimen collection and surgery preparation under veterinarian supervision [S9]. The specialist survey shows broad expectation that AI will become standard, but its reported tools are principally scribes, interpretation and decision support rather than substitutes for accountable clinical operators [S4]. Most year-five service quantity should therefore still require a veterinary practice or laboratory operator.

caveats: No direct study traces veterinary AI savings into prices, wages and margins over five years.
Retention differs between routine primary care, emergency care, referral specialties, farm contracts, wellness plans and laboratories.
Using saved clinician time for more visits raises gross benefit but does not by itself establish retained unit economics.
q.change_evidence: Matched practice-level data on AI-attributable labor savings, prices, visit volumes, wage changes and EBITDA over contract or customer-renewal cycles, segmented by local competition and corporate ownership, would materially change this value.
q.rationale: Veterinary practices generally charge customers for discrete examinations, procedures, tests, treatments, hospitalization and products rather than billing labor on a cost-plus contract [S2]. That structure allows an operator to retain much of an implemented labor benefit initially through capacity expansion, avoided hiring or margin improvement. Over five years, local competition, customer affordability, employee wage sharing, payer or wellness-plan repricing and lower price growth should transfer a meaningful share to customers and staff, producing a base retention estimate of 0.64.

rho.bridge: The specialist survey measures respondent use of any AI tool, attitudes and knowledge rather than the share of exposed labor opportunity implemented by a typical eligible firm. AVMA technology adoption measures digital infrastructure, not AI workflow completion. I use current AI use and practice-software penetration as starting readiness indicators, then discount for integration, validation, training, privacy and liability constraints. Specialist self-selection likely biases adoption upward, so the bridge is more likely to overstate than understate implementation for the full lens.
caveats: Current use of at least one AI tool does not measure depth, reliability or enterprise-wide implementation.
The AVMA technology figures describe broad software readiness and may include practices outside the target EBITDA band.
State professional rules, vendor interoperability and clinical liability may produce large variation among practices.
rho.change_evidence: A representative AVMA or AAHA longitudinal practice survey linking named AI workflows to staff hours, deployment duration, integration failures, override rates and realized avoided hires would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: Routine AI use was reported by 42% of ACVIM respondents and 33% of ECVIM-CA respondents; scribes were the most common tool, and 83% of nonusers expressed interest in learning integration [S4]. The AVMA reports widespread digital foundations, including 76.5% use of practice-management software and 64.1% use of electronic medical records among represented practices [S5]. These conditions support majority implementation within five years, but low self-reported AI knowledge and unresolved oversight, error, privacy and liability issues keep the base below full realization.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans across all U.S. employer-business owners, not completed qualifying control transfers among lower-middle-market veterinary firms. I anchor to its 22% intention rate, discount for plans that do not close or do not transfer control, and offset that discount with veterinary-specific evidence of strategic and private-equity acquisition activity. Intentions tend to overstate completions, while active veterinary consolidation and an older owner base may make the all-industry benchmark understate this industry's opportunity.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and measures intentions rather than completed transfers.
FTC evidence establishes acquisition activity but not an industry transfer rate and emphasizes specialty and emergency markets.
Corporate tuck-ins can be acquisitions of clinics or assets rather than control transfers of independently counted firms.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from VMG, Brakke, PitchBook or veterinary practice brokers that identifies unique target-band firms, control acquired, seller type, EBITDA and failed processes would materially change this value.
s5.rationale: Gallup found that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years and that 52.3% of employer businesses were owned by people age 55 or older [S6]. Veterinary deal flow is observable: the FTC's order concerning JAB's proposed $1.1 billion acquisition of SAGE describes existing general, specialty and emergency clinic chains and imposed conditions on future acquisitions [S7]. The base stays at the broad employer intention rate because not every intention closes and large platform transactions do not directly measure target-band turnover.

records.principal_driver: Durable, repeat animal-health demand delivered through digitally enabled practices creates room for AI-assisted capacity growth without removing the veterinarian-led operator.
records.principal_weakness: Most labor value sits in licensed or hands-on clinical work, so documentation and front-office automation may not translate into proportionate headcount reduction or retained margin.

a: PPAI technology surveys emphasize leading firms and may not represent LMM operators.
a: Labor mix varies with in-house warehousing, decoration, creative work, and independent sales representatives.

e: The supplied margin bridge may overstate LMM eligibility for product resellers with EBITDA margins below the broad business-services assumption.
e: Industry association market totals do not map cleanly to one NAICS code.

s5: Trade publications report visible transactions but not a complete denominator.
s5: A customer-portfolio purchase may not qualify if no transferable operation accompanies it.

d5: Nominal sales mix product cost, unit volume, premiumization, and service demand.
d5: Client budgets are sensitive to tariffs, economic conditions, employment, events, and difficulty proving marketing return.
o: Online sales through a distributor still require an operator and are not equivalent to elimination.
o: Self-service risk differs greatly between commodity reorders and complex branded programs.
o: Future supplier platforms may absorb more distributor coordination than current evidence shows.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business and institutional customers buy decorated merchandise and continuing program support. Providers primarily purchase products and branding from third parties and resell finished goods, while some also charge for technology, creative, loyalty, event, warehousing, fulfillment, and related services. Revenue ranges from repeat orders to multi-year, auto-renewing programs, often without minimum purchase guarantees.
lens.deviation_from_default: The official product list combines branded-merchandise coordination, public product demonstrations, lettering, window dressing, and other miscellaneous advertising services. Their labor, delivery, and pricing models are too different for one coherent screen, so the lens is narrowed to recurring branded-merchandise distributor and coordinator services for coherence rather than attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: One-person sales representatives without transferable operations; captive marketing units; shells; non-control financing vehicles; merchandise-demonstration staffing; sign and display lettering; window or mannequin dressing; welcoming services; manufacturers; printers; and full-service advertising agencies classified elsewhere.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market distributors and coordinators that repeatedly provide branded-merchandise and advertising-specialty programs to external customers, including product sourcing, decoration coordination, creative support, online stores, order management, fulfillment coordination, and account service.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations across the broader advertising sector and PPAI reports AI uses among leading promotional-products firms, not a wage-weighted task census of eligible LMM distributors. I map product search, quoting, artwork support, order entry, CRM updates, marketing content, routine customer communication, reporting, sourcing comparisons, and administrative coordination to exposure, while retaining relationship selling, complex program design, physical quality checks, kitting, warehousing, and exception handling as less exposed. The proxy may overstate exposure in fulfillment-heavy firms and understate it in digitally mature distributors.
caveats: The BLS occupation mix is for the broader advertising sector rather than this narrowed industry population.
PPAI technology surveys emphasize leading firms and may not represent LMM operators.
Labor mix varies with in-house warehousing, decoration, creative work, and independent sales representatives.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study across LMM distributors, joined to payroll and separating already automated e-commerce and ERP work, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: Sales, management, creative, and office work are prominent in advertising-sector employment, and the distributor workflow contains substantial digital information handling. Industry evidence identifies quoting, product recommendations, CRM prioritization, artwork, sourcing, reorders, customer service, and compliance documentation as practical AI opportunities. Existing e-commerce, ERP, and current AI use remove part of the gross opportunity, leaving a material but submajority share of current wage tasks exposed.

d5.bridge: PPAI measures nominal US distributor sales and channel mix, while Stran reports one operator's current demand and contract conditions. I use recent modest nominal growth, broad participation across firm sizes, online-channel gains, and continued corporate program use as evidence for a broadly stable service basket, then remove optimism because recent growth trailed inflation and advertising budgets, tariffs, employment, and events are cyclical. This may overstate constant-price demand if clients shift spend to digital media and understate it if premium branded merchandise and employee programs expand.
caveats: Trade-association sales estimates are broader than this NAICS and may include firms classified elsewhere.
Nominal sales mix product cost, unit volume, premiumization, and service demand.
Client budgets are sensitive to tariffs, economic conditions, employment, events, and difficulty proving marketing return.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-price, same-service sales panel from PPAI or ASI segmented by distributor size, client use case, order volume, and repeat program revenue would materially change this estimate.
d5.rationale: The market remained large and grew modestly in nominal terms despite severe cost and budget pressure, while online sales outpaced the broader channel. Physical merchandise continues to serve campaigns, events, recognition, recruiting, and loyalty programs, but it is often a discretionary secondary budget item. The base therefore implies near-flat to modest real growth rather than extrapolating nominal market expansion.

caveats: This is not an observed eligible-firm share.
The supplied firm count spans every miscellaneous service in the code rather than the narrowed lens.
The supplied margin bridge may overstate LMM eligibility for product resellers with EBITDA margins below the broad business-services assumption.
Industry association market totals do not map cleanly to one NAICS code.
e.change_evidence: A business-register match of firms in this NAICS to PPAI or ASI distributor rosters, with normalized EBITDA, external-customer revenue, repeat-order share, and operational headcount, would materially change this estimate.
e.rationale: Branded-merchandise coordination is an explicit product within the code and the trade market is large and fragmented, but the official scope also includes unrelated physical and staffing services. Some distributors are classified in wholesale, printing, or advertising-agency codes, and many tiny representatives lack transferable operations. No source located measures the share of supplied LMM firms satisfying the narrowed lens, so this is a bounded judgment.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541890 ($4,995,994,780) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541890 ($19,434,299,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

o.bridge: PPAI reports online-channel penetration and AI-suitable workflows, and Stran describes the services performed by a technology-enabled distributor. These sources do not measure the year-five share of demand still requiring an accountable LMM operator. I treat simple product discovery, quoting, artwork preparation, reorders, routine communication, and direct ordering as vulnerable to software or self-service, while retaining supplier selection, brand governance, complex customization, quality, landed-cost management, warehousing, fulfillment, compliance, and client accountability. The proxy may be high for simple commodity orders and low for complex enterprise programs.
caveats: Online sales through a distributor still require an operator and are not equivalent to elimination.
Self-service risk differs greatly between commodity reorders and complex branded programs.
Future supplier platforms may absorb more distributor coordination than current evidence shows.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement data measuring spend moved from distributors to direct suppliers, marketplaces, software-only platforms, and autonomous fulfillment, segmented by order complexity and program status, would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: E-commerce already handles a meaningful share of sales, and AI can remove friction across common order steps. Customers can also source some goods from general marketplaces or suppliers outside the traditional channel. However, physical products must still be selected, decorated, quality-checked, transported, and delivered against brand, deadline, inventory, and compliance requirements. Integrated programs therefore retain a substantial operator role even as transactional work migrates to software.

q.bridge: Stran discloses reseller transaction mechanics, observable order prices, program agreements, gross margins, and competitive pricing risks for a public distributor, while PPAI reports margin pressure across distributor sizes. Neither source measures the retained share of AI benefit for LMM firms. I apply the observed product-resale and service-fee model: internal SG&A savings can initially be retained, but transparent product markets, order rebids, customer allowances, and price competition share gains quickly. The proxy may overstate retention for commoditized orders and understate it for embedded programs with differentiated service.
caveats: A public consolidator may have different purchasing power and enterprise-client bargaining dynamics from LMM firms.
Gross-margin disclosure does not isolate retention of AI-created benefit.
Retention differs sharply between transactional merchandise orders and integrated program clients.
q.change_evidence: An anonymized panel of LMM distributor quotes, contract renewals, rebates, gross margins, service fees, and cost-to-serve before and after AI adoption would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: Most revenue is tied to merchandise with clearly identified selling prices, while program contracts add service and relationship value. AI savings in selling, artwork, support, and administration do not automatically reduce supplier product cost, giving an initial margin channel. Yet buyers compare products and prices, many purchases are order-specific, and competitors can reprice, so only part of the implemented gross benefit is likely to remain with the operator over time.

rho.bridge: PPAI surveys report current uses, ambitions, and barriers among leading suppliers and distributors, while Stran describes technology deployment at one public operator. Neither measures the five-year implemented share of exposed LMM labor. I translate evidence of current marketing, sales, CRM, customer-service, and operations use into implementation potential, discounted for weak data, system integration, privacy, expertise, and change constraints. The proxy may be high for small firms with fragmented systems and low for firms adopting integrated industry platforms.
caveats: Engagement with AI is not the same as production implementation or labor realization.
Survey respondents are disproportionately prominent industry firms.
Supplier catalog quality and disconnected legacy systems can constrain end-to-end automation.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor telemetry and audited before-and-after labor records from LMM distributors using integrated AI for quote-to-order, artwork, CRM, and support workflows would materially change this estimate.
rho.rationale: The use cases are operationally ordinary and can be added to existing CRM, e-commerce, artwork, and order-management systems. Industry firms are already using AI, yet reported workforce effects remain limited and internal expertise and systems integration are major barriers. Implementation should therefore be meaningful but incomplete, with human review preserved for brand accuracy, landed cost, product compliance, and customer commitments.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year transfer plans among US employer-business owners generally, while PPAI and ASI document promotional-products acquisitions without an eligible-firm denominator. I anchor the estimate near general employer-owner intent and move the base upward for visible consolidation, fragmentation, strategic buyers, and a public consolidator actively targeting small branded-merchandise firms. I discount for plans that do not close and for portfolio purchases that may not transfer a complete operating company. This may overstate qualifying controls if customer books are not durable and understate them if consolidation accelerates.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry intention evidence rather than closed promotional-products deals.
Trade publications report visible transactions but not a complete denominator.
A customer-portfolio purchase may not qualify if no transferable operation accompanies it.
Independent representative and franchise structures complicate the unit of control.
s5.change_evidence: A closed-deal database from Certified Marketing Consultants, ASI, PPAI, GF Data, or PitchBook matched to an eligible US LMM distributor denominator and coded for operating-company control would materially change this estimate.
s5.rationale: The industry has frequent strategic and private-equity-backed transactions, including small distributor acquisitions, and one public operator reports repeated asset acquisitions and continued target search. Customer portfolios are often the central transferable asset. However, many reported deals are portfolio asset purchases, and owner intentions are not completed control transfers, so the estimate remains moderate rather than treating deal headlines as a probability.

records.principal_driver: Information-heavy quote-to-order and account workflows offer practical AI leverage while complex physical branded programs still need an accountable coordinator.
records.principal_weakness: Transparent product pricing and uncertain target eligibility can erode both benefit retention and the usable firm pool.

caveats: a: BLS occupation data are from May 2023 and omit the self-employed.
a: AAPOR focuses on survey research, which is important but not coextensive with social-science and humanities R&D.
a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts input has a vintage mismatch and therefore may not represent the same operating year as the occupation evidence.
