a: O*NET task importance does not measure time, wage weight, technical feasibility, or current automation.
a: The estimate concerns current not-yet-automated work; the available adoption survey does not quantify how much work is already automated.
rho: Regular tool use is not equivalent to production-grade implementation or realized labor avoidance.
rho: The interior-design survey is small, global, and associated with a 3D-design platform.
rho: Autodesk's AECO population is much broader than independent US LMM interior design firms and overrepresents Autodesk users.
e: No source measures eligibility under the frozen recurring-or-repeat outsourced LMM lens.
e: The injected firm-count bridge uses a 15.65% broad-sector margin and may place some procurement-heavy or owner-compensation-heavy firms in the wrong EBITDA band.
e: NAICS 541410 includes aesthetic-only decorating consultants, increasing business-model variation within the code.
s5: Gallup covers all US employer businesses rather than interior design or the LMM band.
s5: Survey intentions combine sales with other transfers and do not establish completed qualifying control transactions.
s5: Founder dependence may reduce sale completion or require long earn-outs.
q: The pricing survey is from 2019, UK-based, and mostly residential, not US LMM commercial practices.
q: Pricing-model shares do not directly measure five-year benefit retention.
q: Procurement markups and pass-through purchases may make receipts an imperfect measure of service economics.
d5: The BLS output forecast is for NAICS 541400, not 541410.
d5: Occupation employment is not service quantity and includes interior designers working outside specialized design firms.
d5: The estimate holds quality and the current service basket constant and therefore excludes new AI-enabled offerings.
o: No source directly measures the future operator-required share of interior design demand.
o: Aesthetic-only residential and decorating work is substantially more exposed to self-service than regulated commercial, healthcare, or institutional work.
o: Rapid improvement in spatially accurate generative design, automated code checking, and direct-to-consumer procurement could push the value below the range.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External property owners, developers, operators, institutions, and homeowners buy project-based services. Revenue may be fixed fee, hourly, a percentage of project cost, procurement markup, or a hybrid; repeatability is strongest with commercial, hospitality, institutional, developer, and multi-site clients.
lens.excluded_activities: Architectural and engineering firms outside NAICS 541410; furniture retailers and manufacturers; construction and installation contractors; captive corporate design teams; shells, non-control financing vehicles, solo practitioners below the LMM band, and firms limited to nonrepeat one-off engagements.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market interior design and interior decorating firms providing repeat outsourced planning, design, documentation, specification, procurement coordination, and project-administration services for commercial, hospitality, healthcare, institutional, and residential clients.

a.bridge: BLS measures the occupation mix for four-digit NAICS 541400, not six-digit 541410, and O*NET describes tasks for interior designers rather than all firm employees. I mapped higher exposure to CAD drafting, early rendering, materials research, estimating, routine documentation, and office support, and lower exposure to client discovery, code responsibility, field inspection, purchasing, and multidisciplinary coordination. The broader industry includes graphic and industrial design, so its mix may understate 541410's interior-designer concentration but overstate purely digital-production exposure.
caveats: The BLS industry occupation table is four-digit NAICS 541400 and includes graphic, industrial, and other specialized design services.
O*NET task importance does not measure time, wage weight, technical feasibility, or current automation.
The estimate concerns current not-yet-automated work; the available adoption survey does not quantify how much work is already automated.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit 541410 staffing table linked to time-and-motion data by workflow, or payroll-level job and wage data from a representative sample of US interior design firms, would materially change the wage-weighted estimate.
a.rationale: Specialized design services employ a large design workforce plus meaningful office support, and interior-design work includes several document- and image-intensive tasks that generative and drafting tools can accelerate. Much of the workflow nonetheless depends on physical-site facts, safety and accessibility rules, client judgment, vendor execution, and accountable coordination, keeping the exposed share below a majority at base.

d5.bridge: BLS projects constant-dollar output for four-digit specialized design services to grow 2.0% annually from 2024 to 2034, implying about 1.10 over five years, while interior-designer employment grows 3% over the decade and BLS cites renovation, code, and accessibility demand. I center below the broad output implication to account for the slower occupation outlook and cyclical exposure of renovation and fit-out work. The broader industry may grow differently from interior design, and output growth may include quality or service-mix changes, creating two-sided error.
caveats: The BLS output forecast is for NAICS 541400, not 541410.
Occupation employment is not service quantity and includes interior designers working outside specialized design firms.
The estimate holds quality and the current service basket constant and therefore excludes new AI-enabled offerings.
d5.change_evidence: A six-digit BLS or Census real-output forecast for 541410, supplemented by inflation-adjusted ASID project billings and backlog by end market, would materially change the year-five ratio.
d5.rationale: Renovation needs and continuing code and accessibility requirements support modest real demand growth, while commercial fit-out, housing, hospitality, and discretionary residential projects remain sensitive to construction cycles, interest rates, and client capital budgets. A roughly flat downside and modest-growth base are therefore defensible for the current service basket.

caveats: No source measures eligibility under the frozen recurring-or-repeat outsourced LMM lens.
The injected firm-count bridge uses a 15.65% broad-sector margin and may place some procurement-heavy or owner-compensation-heavy firms in the wrong EBITDA band.
NAICS 541410 includes aesthetic-only decorating consultants, increasing business-model variation within the code.
e.change_evidence: A six-digit establishment census or transaction database segmented by revenue source, customer type, repeat-client share, independence, and normalized EBITDA would directly establish the eligible share.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is inherently an external professional service, and firms large enough to reach the LMM EBITDA band are more likely than sole decorators to serve repeat commercial, hospitality, institutional, developer, or affluent-residential referral channels. Ineligible firms remain where revenue is mostly one-off residential decoration, product resale, construction, captive work, or owner-personalized engagements that are not repeat outsourced services.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: No source directly measures the future operator-required share of interior design demand.
Aesthetic-only residential and decorating work is substantially more exposed to self-service than regulated commercial, healthcare, or institutional work.
Rapid improvement in spatially accurate generative design, automated code checking, and direct-to-consumer procurement could push the value below the range.
o.change_evidence: Customer cohort data from major design platforms and US interior design firms showing five-year substitution, project unbundling, and client migration from full-service firms to self-service AI by project type would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Self-service software and AI can eliminate or unbundle low-complexity mood boards, decorative concepts, product discovery, and early visualization. Most LMM-firm demand should still require an accountable operator because the current service includes client discovery, site-specific measurement, construction documents, code and accessibility judgment, procurement, multidisciplinary coordination, installation oversight, and post-completion verification.

q.bridge: The British Institute of Interior Design measured UK interior designers' charging methods, not US LMM benefit retention: 51% used combinations, 21% fixed fees, 18% hourly rates, and 6% project-cost percentages. I map fixed and percentage-based fees to higher initial retention, hourly billing to lower retention because fewer billable hours can reduce revenue, and hybrid arrangements to the middle, then discount all models for competitive repricing and customer sharing over five years. UK residential-heavy respondents may understate fixed-fee prevalence in US commercial work and make the direction of bias uncertain.
caveats: The pricing survey is from 2019, UK-based, and mostly residential, not US LMM commercial practices.
Pricing-model shares do not directly measure five-year benefit retention.
Procurement markups and pass-through purchases may make receipts an imperfect measure of service economics.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of US 541410 contracts and project P&Ls showing fee model, procurement markup, renewal repricing, realized hours, and gross margin before and after AI deployment would materially move retention.
q.rationale: A mixed pricing market permits meaningful capture where firms deliver a defined scope for a fixed fee or earn procurement and project-cost-linked revenue. Capture is weaker in hourly phases because productivity can shrink billable hours, and renewals and competitive bids should share part of the benefit with customers. The result is moderate retention rather than either full pass-through or full capture.

rho.bridge: A 2025 Mattoboard survey reported regular AI use among 328 interior-design professionals in more than 70 countries, while Autodesk surveyed the broader AECO sector. I translate tool use and sector sentiment into the share of the remaining exposed opportunity operationalized within five years, discounting for experimentation, workflow integration, quality control, client acceptance, and the survey's non-US and vendor-selected population. The 82% user rate likely overstates mature implementation, while current use concentrated in early ideation likely leaves room for deeper workflow capture.
caveats: Regular tool use is not equivalent to production-grade implementation or realized labor avoidance.
The interior-design survey is small, global, and associated with a 3D-design platform.
Autodesk's AECO population is much broader than independent US LMM interior design firms and overrepresents Autodesk users.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal US ASID or firm-software survey reporting production hours, rework rates, and headcount avoided by workflow before and after AI deployment would materially move this value.
rho.rationale: AI is already used for concept sketches, mood boards, layouts, early renderings, sourcing, bookkeeping, and deadline management, which lowers behavioral adoption barriers. Five-year implementation should spread across these bounded workflows, but construction documents, code compliance, material accuracy, intellectual-property concerns, and integration with CAD/BIM and project controls constrain conversion of exposure into dependable labor avoidance.

s5.bridge: Gallup found that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. I use that as an upper anchor and discount gifts, internal transfers, unexecuted intentions, and firms whose client relationships or creative identity are too owner-dependent for a qualifying control sale. LMM scale and employees improve transferability relative to nonemployers, but the creative-services dependence on principals may make the industry less transferable than the all-employer average.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses rather than interior design or the LMM band.
Survey intentions combine sales with other transfers and do not establish completed qualifying control transactions.
Founder dependence may reduce sale completion or require long earn-outs.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, BizBuySell, or broker data isolating US interior-design control transactions and failed sale processes by eligible firm count over five years would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Employer firms have materially more transferable operations than nonemployers, and owner succession creates a plausible five-year supply of control opportunities. However, a stated plan is not a completed transaction, and interior design practices can depend heavily on founder taste, reputation, and client relationships, so the base qualifying-transfer probability is below the survey's 22% plan rate.

records.principal_driver: Document-, image-, research-, and coordination-heavy workflows offer substantial bounded AI leverage while regulated, site-specific delivery preserves the operator role.
records.principal_weakness: Founder dependence and mixed hourly or pass-through economics can limit both firm transferability and retained productivity benefit.

caveats: a: The occupational sources include government and construction-sector inspectors outside the private-industry lens.
a: Task importance ratings do not measure hours or compensation weights.
a: Current inspection software adoption is not measured, so the not-yet-automated condition is judged rather than observed.
rho: The strongest implementation examples concern code officials and permitting rather than buyer-paid private inspections.
rho: The ICC sources are product and partnership materials, not independent adoption studies.
rho: Legal responsibility and insurer acceptance may vary materially by state and inspection type.
e: The supplied firm count is an estimate bridged from establishment-size data and an industry margin, not a verified buyer universe.
e: The NAICS definition excludes government code-enforcement establishments, but firm-level miscoding and mixed operations remain possible.
e: Eligibility of owner-led inspection practices depends on whether licenses, referral relationships, and quality controls survive a change of control.
s5: The Gallup sample spans all employer industries and is not restricted to the LMM band.
s5: Survey intentions may not be realized and include transfers that would not qualify as an outside control event.
s5: No direct industry deal-rate series was found.
q: No representative billing-mix or pass-through study for NAICS 541350 was found.
q: Residential one-off work and negotiated portfolio contracts may retain benefits differently.
q: The estimate excludes volume effects, which belong in demand and operator-need assumptions.
d5: Occupational employment is not service demand and includes government inspectors outside the lens.
d5: Monthly housing indicators are noisy and cover only part of the industry's demand basket.
d5: The base does not assume that a cyclical housing recovery will occur on a specific schedule.
o: Requirements differ by state, customer, and inspection specialty.
o: Remote inspection can preserve an accountable operator while changing travel and field labor, so it should not automatically be counted as service elimination.
o: Rapid improvement in sensor integration or accepted self-capture would lower operator need.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include homebuyers and sellers, property owners and managers, lenders, insurers, developers, and public or institutional clients. Residential work is generally sold as a fixed inspection fee adjusted for property size and add-on tests; larger commercial and programmatic work may use project fees, unit-price schedules, or recurring service contracts. Repeat business often comes through referral channels and portfolio or construction relationships even when each property produces a separate report.
lens.excluded_activities: Government code-enforcement departments, captive internal inspection units, pest and termite inspection, hazardous-material consulting, real-estate appraisal, engineering-led design services, testing laboratories, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Private lower-middle-market firms whose primary service is outsourced inspection of residential or commercial buildings, including home inspections, property-condition inspections, and repeat inspection programs for external customers.

a.bridge: O*NET and BLS describe the broader construction-and-building-inspector occupation rather than the wage-weighted task mix of private NAICS 541350 firms. I mapped plan review, code research, report drafting, photograph organization, scheduling, records, and routine customer communication to current AI exposure, while treating site access, measurement, equipment use, and physical defect judgment as largely unexposed. Task importance is not time or wage share, so it can overstate highly important desk tasks; omission of dispatch, sales, and administrative occupations can understate firmwide exposure.
caveats: The occupational sources include government and construction-sector inspectors outside the private-industry lens.
Task importance ratings do not measure hours or compensation weights.
Current inspection software adoption is not measured, so the not-yet-automated condition is judged rather than observed.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study from multi-inspector home and commercial inspection firms, split by field inspection, travel, report production, sales, scheduling, and administration, combined with payroll by role and observed AI-assisted cycle-time changes.
a.rationale: The work contains a substantial document and information layer that AI can assist or partially substitute, but BLS says inspectors spend most of their time examining worksites and O*NET assigns core importance to physical inspection, monitoring, measuring, and test-equipment use. The base therefore places exposed labor below half of current wage-weighted work.

caveats: Occupational employment is not service demand and includes government inspectors outside the lens.
Monthly housing indicators are noisy and cover only part of the industry's demand basket.
The base does not assume that a cyclical housing recovery will occur on a specific schedule.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast that combines existing-home transactions, housing starts and completions, commercial construction, renovation permits, insurer inspection mandates, and actual inspection counts from major scheduling platforms, separated by residential and commercial service lines.
d5.rationale: Inspection demand is tied to property transactions, construction, renovation, lender and insurer requirements, and ongoing safety concerns. BLS projects nearly flat inspector employment while explicitly noting that safety and construction quality support demand and remote inspection restrains staffing. Current Census housing data are volatile, with weaker new-home sales alongside a recent rebound in starts, so the base assumes roughly stable constant-quality service volume and the range preserves substantial cycle risk.

caveats: The supplied firm count is an estimate bridged from establishment-size data and an industry margin, not a verified buyer universe.
The NAICS definition excludes government code-enforcement establishments, but firm-level miscoding and mixed operations remain possible.
Eligibility of owner-led inspection practices depends on whether licenses, referral relationships, and quality controls survive a change of control.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the supplied 40 LMM candidates using websites, service mix, customer concentration, ownership, license portability, recurring-contract share, and normalized EBITDA quality.
e.rationale: The Census definition is centered on external building-condition inspection and reporting, which fits the default outsourced-service lens. Most private firms in the supplied LMM count should therefore qualify. The discount covers mixed-service firms, customer concentration, captive-like arrangements, weak recurring or referral channels, and firms whose owner dependence or licensing structure makes control ownership impractical.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541350 ($1,864,147,476) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541350 ($3,995,784,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $146,226 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541350); class <5: 6,523 firms, avg revenue/firm $193,991 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $30,360 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 612 firms, avg revenue/firm $827,992 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $129,581 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 305 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,798,961 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $281,537 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 143 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,816,294 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $910,250 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 23 firms, avg revenue/firm $18,547,304 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,902,653 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 17 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,508,765 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,835,622 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 40

o.bridge: BLS and O*NET measure occupational duties, work setting, and licensing rather than the year-five share of industry quantity requiring a lens operator. I treat physical site examination, access, measurement, equipment use, signed judgment, and regulated accountability as operator-required; I treat remote triage, automated plan checks, and limited self-service as potential quantity removal. Because occupational task evidence does not reveal customer willingness to self-serve, the bridge may overstate operator need if computer vision and remote capture mature quickly, but government-focused remote-inspection evidence may also overstate elimination for private condition reports.
caveats: Requirements differ by state, customer, and inspection specialty.
Remote inspection can preserve an accountable operator while changing travel and field labor, so it should not automatically be counted as service elimination.
Rapid improvement in sensor integration or accepted self-capture would lower operator need.
o.change_evidence: State-by-state regulatory analysis plus insurer, lender, real-estate buyer, and municipal acceptance data for unattended computer-vision inspections, together with the share of paid inspections replaced by self-capture or software-only reports.
o.rationale: Most service quantity should still require an accountable inspection firm because the product depends on physical access, measurements, trade knowledge, defect judgment, and often licensure or certification. AI can reduce labor per report and automate narrow checks, but current tools emphasize assistance, source verification, and faster review rather than autonomous acceptance of property condition and liability.

caveats: No representative billing-mix or pass-through study for NAICS 541350 was found.
Residential one-off work and negotiated portfolio contracts may retain benefits differently.
The estimate excludes volume effects, which belong in demand and operator-need assumptions.
q.change_evidence: Contract and invoice samples from residential, commercial, lender, insurer, and public-program customers showing fixed-fee, hourly, cost-plus, and unit-price revenue shares, repricing cadence, discount history, and margin changes after workflow automation.
q.rationale: A building inspection produces a defined condition report, and residential customers typically pay the inspection cost, with price varying by property size and add-on tests. Output-based fees let an operator retain much of a labor saving during a contract period. Local price comparison, realtor referral competition, renewal negotiation on portfolio or public work, and pressure to spend savings on faster turnaround or stronger quality should share part of the benefit over five years.

rho.bridge: ICC product evidence and an AI plan-checking pilot in 11 cities show technical feasibility in code research and compliance review, not five-year implementation by private building-inspection firms. I translate demonstrated tools into the share of exposed workflows likely to reach routine production, then discount for integration, local-code variation, verification, training, liability, and change management. Vendor and partner claims likely bias the proxy upward, while their focus on permitting may omit easier private-firm reporting and scheduling uses and bias it downward.
caveats: The strongest implementation examples concern code officials and permitting rather than buyer-paid private inspections.
The ICC sources are product and partnership materials, not independent adoption studies.
Legal responsibility and insurer acceptance may vary materially by state and inspection type.
rho.change_evidence: Production data from inspection-management vendors or a cohort of private firms showing active usage, error and rework rates, report cycle time, labor hours per inspection, and retained staffing changes after at least one year.
rho.rationale: Purpose-built code tools already provide source-linked answers, and the ICC collaboration demonstrates AI-assisted plan checking in real jurisdictions. Implementation should be meaningful in research, drafting, and quality control, but mandatory verification, local code variation, fragmented systems, and professional liability prevent full conversion of exposed work.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sell, transfer, or public-offering plans among U.S. employer-business owners across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers in building inspection. I use the 22 percent employer intention rate as an upper anchor, reduce it for gifts, internal transfers, abandoned plans, and businesses that are too owner-dependent to transfer, then allow some upward offset because LMM firms are larger and more operationally transferable than the typical surveyed employer. The intention-to-close gap makes the proxy more likely to overstate completed market transactions than understate them.
caveats: The Gallup sample spans all employer industries and is not restricted to the LMM band.
Survey intentions may not be realized and include transfers that would not qualify as an outside control event.
No direct industry deal-rate series was found.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed NAICS 541350 transaction records from Axial, GF Data, PitchBook, BizBuySell, SBA change-of-ownership loans, and industry brokers, with a verified denominator of eligible private firms and separation of internal succession from outside control sales.
s5.rationale: Employer-business owners are more transfer-oriented than nonemployers, and owner aging supplies a credible succession catalyst. Still, a stated plan is not a completed third-party control transaction, and personal reputation, licensure, and referral dependence can make inspection firms harder to transfer.

records.principal_driver: A repeatable report-and-code workflow sits beside indispensable field inspection, creating room for AI-enabled throughput without requiring customers to abandon the trusted operator.
records.principal_weakness: The available-firm denominator and five-year control-transfer rate are modeled from broad proxies, while housing cyclicality and owner-dependent licenses or referral relationships can sharply reduce realizable opportunity.

sources.supports: BLS describes field and office duties, licensing, physical requirements, work settings, the 2024 employment base, nearly flat projected employment, replacement openings, remote-inspection pressure, and safety-supported demand.
sources.title: Construction and Building Inspectors, Occupational Outlook Handbook

sources.supports: The 2026 occupation profile details the importance of plan review, approval, physical inspection, site monitoring, measurement, recordkeeping, customer communication, and test-equipment tasks.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Construction and Building Inspectors

sources.supports: The official NAICS definition centers 541350 on building-condition evaluation and reporting for transaction participants, includes home inspection and inspection bureaus, and identifies excluded pest, hazardous-material, appraisal, and government-enforcement activities.
sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual

sources.supports: ICC describes an expert-trained AI tool for code research and plan-review questions, direct links for source verification, state and model-code coverage, and the continuing need to check the official code text.
sources.title: Digital Codes Premium AI Navigator

sources.supports: The July 2025 release describes AI-powered compliance checking, a pilot with 11 U.S. cities, and reported improvements in plan-review speed and consistency, while remaining partnership evidence rather than an independent controlled study.
sources.title: International Code Council Collaborates with Archistar to Modernize Permitting and Accelerate Housing Development

sources.supports: Gallup reports that 22 percent of employer-business owners planned a sale, transfer, or public offering within five years and documents older ownership, transfer intentions, and survey methods from fall 2024.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: NAR describes the building systems examined, buyer responsibility for inspection cost, price variation with property size and extra tests, and the role of the inspector's report in a home transaction.
sources.title: Consumer Guide: Home Inspections

sources.supports: The June 2026 Census and HUD release reports weaker new single-family home sales than the prior month and prior year, illustrating current transaction cyclicality and statistical uncertainty.
sources.title: Monthly New Residential Sales, May 2026

records.title: Building Inspection Services

caveats: a: The commercial occupation compilation says it uses US government data but displays graphic and interior designer shares even though those services are formally excluded from NAICS 541490, indicating classification or reporting noise.
a: Occupation shares are headcount rather than wages or task time, so wage weighting is judgmental.

a: The supplied compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized by a cross-industry median; specialty mix and merchandise contamination could make it unrepresentative of the narrowed service lens.
a: Creative-professional surveys include disciplines and firm sizes outside this industry.
rho: Adobe is a creative-software vendor, and its respondents are likely more digitally engaged than the full target population.
rho: Reported time savings are self-assessed and do not establish net labor savings after prompt development, comparison, revision, provenance checks, and client review.
rho: Fashion workflow evidence comes from a large retailer's proprietary system and may not transfer economically or operationally to LMM outsourced studios.
rho: Implementation will vary widely across digital concept work, technical product design, lighting, jewelry, costume, and physical prototype activities.
e: The supplied firm count is an ESTIMATE produced from size-class receipts and an EBITDA-margin bridge, not an observed list of firms.
e: No public source measures repeat-client or B2B revenue share for NAICS 541490 firms in the target EBITDA band.
e: The broad residual code and displayed neighboring-service revenue create material classification risk.
s5: Gallup covers all US employer businesses and measures intentions rather than closed transactions.
s5: The survey does not isolate professional design firms or the target EBITDA band.
s5: Internal minority buy-ins, talent acquisitions without durable operations, and owner deaths without transfer are excluded from the primitive.
q: The pricing evidence pools several agency types and reports the primary model per firm, not revenue share by contract type.

q: Client self-service belongs principally in operator-required demand, but it also increases competitive price pressure on retained projects; the estimate avoids counting eliminated volume as retained-benefit erosion.
q: Licensing, royalty, and merchandise economics are not quantified and vary sharply by specialty.
d5: Fashion designers are only one occupation and many work in-house outside NAICS 541490.
d5: The revenue series is nominal, taxable-employer only, ends in 2022, and does not isolate the narrowed repeat-B2B lens.
d5: Creative content demand is adjacent to, but not the same as, fashion, textile, jewelry, footwear, lighting, costume, and other product-oriented design demand.
d5: End-market cyclicality and specialty mix make one industry-wide quantity ratio unusually uncertain.
o: No source directly measures revenue-weighted software substitution for the narrowed NAICS 541490 lens.
o: Operator need differs sharply between a simple concept image and a manufacturable shoe, textile repeat, jewelry line, lighting plan, or costume package.
o: Copyrightability is only one form of commercial protection and does not by itself require an outside design firm.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project, collection, season, campaign, or product-line design services sold mainly to brands, retailers, manufacturers, entertainment producers, developers, and institutions. Revenue may be fixed per project or deliverable, hourly, retainer-based, productized or subscription-based, and sometimes supplemented by change orders, licensing, royalties, or merchandise resale.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 541490 is a residual category spanning fashion, textile, footwear, jewelry, lighting, costume, float, and other design businesses whose customers, production processes, and economics differ materially. To preserve a coherent screen without selecting a favored specialty, the lens keeps repeat B2B outsourced design studios across all listed specialties and excludes goods-led, captive, and one-off consumer models.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive in-house design teams, shells, non-control financing vehicles, firms outside the approximate $1-10M normalized EBITDA band, independent theatrical artists classified in NAICS 711510, design fields assigned to other NAICS codes, merchandise- or manufacturing-led businesses, and predominantly one-off bespoke consumer practices without repeat outsourced service relationships.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market studios whose primary business is repeat outsourced design work for external business or institutional customers across the NAICS 541490 specialties, including fashion, clothing, footwear, textile, jewelry, lighting, costume, and other residual professional design services. Included firms manage briefs, research, concepts, design development, technical specifications or production packages, revisions, and client delivery.

a.bridge: The industry occupation source reports headcount, not wage-weighted task time: arts/design/media occupations are 47% of workers, office and administrative support 15%, management 7%, business and financial operations 6%, sales 7%, and production 7%. I mapped these groups to documented fashion-design and broader creative tasks, then applied broad relative-pay judgment to approximate wage weights. Exposed work includes trend and reference research, brainstorming, first-pass variants, sketches, mood boards, routine specifications, image finishing, proposals, administration, and sales support; less-exposed work includes client interpretation, taste and selection, material evaluation, physical prototypes, supplier interaction, and production oversight. This may overstate remaining opportunity because current creative professionals already use AI on many projects, and may understate it if emerging CAD and technical-package tools reach production reliability.
caveats: The commercial occupation compilation says it uses US government data but displays graphic and interior designer shares even though those services are formally excluded from NAICS 541490, indicating classification or reporting noise.
Occupation shares are headcount rather than wages or task time, so wage weighting is judgmental.

The supplied compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized by a cross-industry median; specialty mix and merchandise contamination could make it unrepresentative of the narrowed service lens.
Creative-professional surveys include disciplines and firm sizes outside this industry.
a.change_evidence: Obtain anonymized time-and-payroll data from at least 30 LMM studios across fashion, textile, footwear, jewelry, lighting, and costume design. Map six months of hours by role and task, identify existing automation, and have practitioners test current AI tools on the remaining tasks with quality, revision, and approval outcomes.
a.rationale: Design and support labor dominate the reported occupation mix, and much of the workflow is digital. Adobe's task research finds high AI enablement in brainstorming and ideation and substantial use in post-production, while a fashion implementation shows research, mood boards, concepts, and a tech pack can be accelerated dramatically. Yet creative labor is shifting toward selection, direction, client negotiation, provenance, and accountability, and fashion work still requires fabrics, prototypes, team collaboration, supplier knowledge, and final production oversight. The resulting wage-weighted exposure is material but below a majority in the central case after excluding work already automated.

d5.bridge: BLS projects fashion-designer employment rather than constant-price, constant-quality demand for the narrowed NAICS 541490 service basket. Its 2% ten-year projection translates to about 1% over five years under a constant-growth assumption and anchors the base at 1.01. Census Service Annual Survey nominal taxable-employer revenue rose from $2.051 billion in 2019 to $2.890 billion in 2022, but that history is only directional context because it includes inflation and mix changes. Adobe's broader creative research reports rising hiring and content demand, which may overstate product-design demand, while fashion employment may understate other specialties such as lighting or entertainment design.
caveats: Fashion designers are only one occupation and many work in-house outside NAICS 541490.
The revenue series is nominal, taxable-employer only, ends in 2022, and does not isolate the narrowed repeat-B2B lens.
Creative content demand is adjacent to, but not the same as, fashion, textile, jewelry, footwear, lighting, costume, and other product-oriented design demand.
End-market cyclicality and specialty mix make one industry-wide quantity ratio unusually uncertain.
d5.change_evidence: Build an inflation-adjusted five-year revenue and backlog panel for eligible firms by specialty and end market, using Census SAS/QSS, Bureau of Economic Analysis price indexes, fashion collection calendars, retail product-launch data, entertainment production starts, construction lighting-project starts, and direct firm bookings.
d5.rationale: The central case is essentially stable real service quantity. BLS cites a constant flow of new designs, social commerce, direct-to-consumer retail, and sustainable-material demand, but projects only modest fashion-designer employment growth. New collections, product refreshes, entertainment productions, and brand differentiation sustain work, while discretionary consumer categories, retail cycles, offshoring, in-house teams, and AI self-service create downside. The wide range reflects the residual code's disparate end markets.

caveats: The supplied firm count is an ESTIMATE produced from size-class receipts and an EBITDA-margin bridge, not an observed list of firms.
No public source measures repeat-client or B2B revenue share for NAICS 541490 firms in the target EBITDA band.
The broad residual code and displayed neighboring-service revenue create material classification risk.
e.change_evidence: Create a named census of the 82 dataset-estimated LMM-band firms from Data Axle, D&B Hoovers, PitchBook, and state registrations. Review websites, invoices, and customer concentration to classify revenue by external design services, repeat clients, merchandise, licensing, and one-off consumer commissions, and test owner dependence through management interviews.
e.rationale: The official definition establishes a professional-service category, which supports external outsourced work, but the catch-all spans materially different specialties. The commercial product list also shows merchandise resale, other receipts, and services formally associated with neighboring design codes, signaling mixed or noisy classification. The narrowed lens includes studios with repeat B2B engagements and operational teams; it excludes goods-led brands, captive design, bespoke consumer practices, and founder-only creative work. A central estimate modestly above half reflects the likely service orientation of employer firms while recognizing that recurring relationships and clean classification are not observed.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541490 ($2,027,827,545) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541490 ($3,671,109,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $308,134 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541490); class <5: 2,871 firms, avg revenue/firm $385,018 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $60,255 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 302 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,501,623 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $235,004 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 131 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,384,664 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $529,700 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 57 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,240,281 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,915,604 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 16 firms, avg revenue/firm $43,541,688 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,814,274 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $30,497,444 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,772,850 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 82

caveats: No source directly measures revenue-weighted software substitution for the narrowed NAICS 541490 lens.
Operator need differs sharply between a simple concept image and a manufacturable shoe, textile repeat, jewelry line, lighting plan, or costume package.
Copyrightability is only one form of commercial protection and does not by itself require an outside design firm.
o.change_evidence: Survey brand, retailer, manufacturer, entertainment, and institutional buyers about five-year insourcing and AI self-service plans by deliverable. Match buyer responses to actual RFP volumes, outsourced-design spend, vendor counts, and project cancellations, and audit which final assets require provenance, rights clearance, physical sampling, or supplier-ready specifications.
o.rationale: AI makes first-pass concepts and finishing easier, and Adobe's research reports clients already self-producing some lower-stakes work. Unlike licensed professional services, this category has little structural requirement that a specialized studio remain between software and the customer. Even so, repeat B2B work often requires brief interpretation, distinctive taste, material and manufacturing knowledge, prototype judgment, supplier coordination, revisions, commercial provenance, and accountability for final output. Copyright guidance also favors meaningful human creative control. The central estimate therefore assumes substantial self-service and software substitution at the low end but continued operator demand for differentiated, production-ready, and commercially defensible work.

q.bridge: The pricing survey covers 273 US marketing, branding, design, creative, digital, and advertising agencies rather than only 541490 firms. Primary models were productized or subscription packages for 28.2%, hourly billing for 28.2%, project-by-project pricing for 25.3%, and fixed monthly retainers for 9.9%. I map fixed project, subscription, and retainer work to stronger initial retention and hourly work to immediate pass-through, then reduce five-year retention for renewal repricing, client demands for faster delivery, self-service of lower-stakes output, and competition from AI-enabled entrants. Agency data may overstate recurring packages for product and fashion studios, while founder differentiation and licensing economics could increase retention in some specialties.
caveats: The pricing evidence pools several agency types and reports the primary model per firm, not revenue share by contract type.

Client self-service belongs principally in operator-required demand, but it also increases competitive price pressure on retained projects; the estimate avoids counting eliminated volume as retained-benefit erosion.
Licensing, royalty, and merchandise economics are not quantified and vary sharply by specialty.
q.change_evidence: Collect five years of contracts, invoices, proposal win rates, renewal prices, project hours, write-offs, licensing income, and change orders from at least 20 eligible studios. Compare realized price and margin for matched deliverables before and after AI deployment, segmented by fixed, hourly, retainer, subscription, royalty, and value-based terms.
q.rationale: Fixed deliverables, productized services, and retainers let studios retain near-term labor savings, while hourly contracts transmit fewer hours directly to customers. Over time, compressed deadlines, renegotiation, and clients' ability to generate acceptable low-stakes work themselves should share much of the benefit. Differentiated taste, trusted direction, commercial safety, and the ability to deliver more seasonal work preserve a meaningful operator share, but durable full capture is unlikely in an unlicensed and contestable service market.

rho.bridge: Adobe surveys measure use and perceived productivity among broader creative professionals rather than the five-year implemented share of exposed labor in LMM 541490 studios. More than 400 creative professionals reported using AI on over 40% of projects on average; 94% said they produced content faster, a majority said at least 50% faster, and average reported time saved was 17 hours weekly. A separate task study found AI concentrated in ideation and finishing while collaboration and physical work remained lightly used. I translate strong current deployment into a high-but-not-complete five-year implementation rate, discounting for self-selection, vendor sponsorship, intellectual-property risk, brand continuity, specialty-specific CAD, physical prototyping, and workflow change. The bridge likely overstates small-studio implementation if Adobe users are unusually AI-active, but could understate implementation as tools become embedded in standard design software.
caveats: Adobe is a creative-software vendor, and its respondents are likely more digitally engaged than the full target population.
Reported time savings are self-assessed and do not establish net labor savings after prompt development, comparison, revision, provenance checks, and client review.
Fashion workflow evidence comes from a large retailer's proprietary system and may not transfer economically or operationally to LMM outsourced studios.
Implementation will vary widely across digital concept work, technical product design, lighting, jewelry, costume, and physical prototype activities.
rho.change_evidence: Track a panel of target studios for two years using software telemetry, project accounting, and staffing records. For each deployed AI workflow, measure hours avoided, additional iterations, rework, rights review, client rejection, freelancer displacement, and whether capacity gains reduced hiring or only increased output.
rho.rationale: Implementation is already beyond experimentation for many digital creatives and is likely to spread through common software. Fashion-specific evidence demonstrates that trend research, mood boards, concepts, and technical packs can be integrated end to end. Constraints remain substantial: Adobe respondents reported concern about brand continuity and commercial safety, clients restrict AI in final commercial output, human contribution matters for copyright protection, and material or physical deliverables require review. Most exposed work should become assisted, but only part should become dependable labor substitution or avoided hiring.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures plans among US business owners, not completed qualifying transfers among eligible specialized-design studios. In its 2024 survey, 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while owner-personal businesses were specifically cited as difficult to continue after retirement. I reduce the central estimate below 22% for noncompletion, gifts or reorganizations that may not transfer control, and the unusually high dependence of design firms on founder taste and client relationships. This may understate transfer likelihood for the LMM employer subset, which is larger and more operationally independent than the artists and nonemployers highlighted by Gallup.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses and measures intentions rather than closed transactions.
The survey does not isolate professional design firms or the target EBITDA band.
Internal minority buy-ins, talent acquisitions without durable operations, and owner deaths without transfer are excluded from the primitive.
s5.change_evidence: Compile completed control transactions for NAICS 541490 and named fashion, textile, jewelry, lighting, and costume studios from PitchBook, GF Data, DealStats, Axial, BizBuySell, and trade press. Interview at least 30 eligible owners about age, exit horizon, ownership percentage, successor, client portability, and whether the firm can operate without the founder.
s5.rationale: The target firms are employers at meaningful scale and therefore more transferable than solo creative practices, but the asset being sold often depends on a founder's creative direction, reputation, and relationships. Broad succession intentions create a credible pipeline, while failures to plan, client portability, key-person risk, and the absence of observed 541490 deal data limit confidence.

records.principal_driver: A predominantly digital design-and-support workforce exposes research, ideation, variation, finishing, documentation, and administration to rapidly improving creative AI.
records.principal_weakness: The unlicensed, heterogeneous service basket faces customer self-service, rapid repricing, and founder-dependent transferability precisely where AI makes generic creative output abundant.

sources.supports: Defines the residual professional-design category and lists costume, fashion, float, fur, jewelry, shoe, and textile design, while excluding architecture, landscape architecture, engineering, interior, industrial, graphic, computer-system, and independent theatrical design.
sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau NAICS: 541490 Other Specialized Design Services

sources.supports: Displayed occupation mix includes arts/design/media 47%, office and administrative support 15%, management 7%, business and financial operations 6%, sales 7%, and production 7%; displayed product lines include clothing design, other design, merchandise resale, other receipts, and neighboring design services.
sources.title: Other Specialized Design Services - Market Size, Financial Statistics, Industry Trends

sources.supports: Documents fashion-design tasks including trend research, CAD, material selection, prototypes, presentations, marketing, and production oversight; reports 20% self-employment and projects 2% employment growth from 2024 to 2034, citing social commerce, direct-to-consumer sales, and sustainable materials.
sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Fashion Designers

sources.supports: Defines fashion designers and documents tasks such as sketches and specifications, examining and modifying sample garments, client and management discussions, material and production-technique selection, and arranging showings.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Fashion Designers (27-1022.00)

sources.supports: Survey of 2,002 US creative professionals, developers, and freelancers: 90% believed generative AI could save time and money and create new ideas; 56% believed it could harm creators; large majorities sought attribution, labeling, and protections against unconsented training and infringement.
sources.title: Adobe AI and the Creative Frontier Study

sources.supports: Survey of more than 400 creative professionals and more than 400 marketers: creatives used AI on over 40% of projects on average; 94% said they produced faster, a majority said at least 50% faster, and average reported savings were 17 hours weekly; brand continuity and commercial safety remained major concerns.
sources.title: Creatives Say AI Is Helping Them Meet Growing Demand for Content

sources.supports: Task research across creative professionals finds 81.7% of US creative pros rated brainstorming and ideation highly AI-enabled; AI concentrates at ideation and finishing while selection, direction, client communication, and accountability expand; clients self-produce some lower-stakes work, restrict final AI use, and still demand high-stakes output with provenance.
sources.title: Adobe Research: How AI Is Redistributing Creative Work

sources.supports: Describes a fashion workflow in which AI condenses research and design from weeks to minutes, generates mood boards and a technical pack, and shortens concept-to-shelf timing by about 18 weeks while designers and merchants refine ideas and apply experience.
sources.title: Walmart: Trend-to-Product Uses GenAI in Fashion Design

sources.supports: Fall 2024 survey found 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years; it also identifies personal-labor dependence, including artist businesses, as a barrier to continuation and distinguishes employer from nonemployer transferability.
sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: April 2025 survey of 273 US marketing, branding, design, creative, digital, and advertising agency leaders: 28.2% primarily used productized or subscription packages, 28.2% hourly, 25.3% project pricing, and 9.9% fixed monthly retainers; 45% adjusted prices at renewals.
sources.title: Agencies Split on Pricing Models

sources.supports: US Census Service Annual Survey nominal revenue series for taxable employer firms, including $2.051 billion in 2019, $1.829 billion in 2020, $2.256 billion in 2021, and $2.890 billion in 2022; used only as historical directional context.
sources.title: FRED Table Data: Total Revenue for Other Specialized Design Services, Taxable Employer Firms

sources.supports: Copyright Office conclusion that generative-AI output is copyrightable only where a human determines sufficient expressive elements; human creative arrangement or modification may qualify, while prompts alone do not, and AI assistance does not bar copyrightability.
sources.title: U.S. Copyright Office Releases Part 2 of Artificial Intelligence Report

records.title: Other Specialized Design Services

caveats: a: OEWS is available only for NAICS 541800 and includes media representatives, creative agencies, public relations, and other businesses unlike media buyers.
a: IAB evidence describes organizations across the buy and sell sides, not wage-weighted tasks in LMM media buying agencies.

rho: Ad-platform automation can shift work rather than eliminate it by increasing campaign variants and measurement demands.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Advertisers and agencies engage media buyers through recurring retainers, labor-based fees, fixed or output-based fees, commissions or percentage-of-spend arrangements, and occasional performance incentives; client media spend may flow through agency receipts.
lens.excluded_activities: Media sales representatives, publishers and media owners, creative-only advertising agencies, software-only adtech vendors, captive in-house buying teams, shell firms, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market agencies providing repeat outsourced media planning and buying, price and placement negotiation, campaign trafficking, performance monitoring, research, verification, and post-campaign analysis for advertisers or other agencies.

a.bridge: Source population: BLS May 2023 employees in broader NAICS 541800 Advertising, Public Relations, and Related Services, combined with IAB evidence about AI functions across agencies, brands, and publishers. Transformation: emphasize the wage-bearing media-buyer work in management, market research, project management, computer/data, and administration and assign high exposure to audience analysis, plan generation, partner selection, forecasting, trafficking, optimization, reporting, and verification, but lower exposure to client strategy, negotiation, brand-safety judgment, and relationship ownership. Direction of error: broad 541800 includes creative and advertising-sales jobs that are not representative of 541830, while IAB use cases may overstate substitutability by describing technical capability rather than current task quality.
caveats: OEWS is available only for NAICS 541800 and includes media representatives, creative agencies, public relations, and other businesses unlike media buyers.
IAB evidence describes organizations across the buy and sell sides, not wage-weighted tasks in LMM media buying agencies.

The frozen labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and may be materially distorted because receipts can include resold or pass-through media spend; it is also harmonized by a cross-industry median adjustment.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task diary from 541830 LMM agencies separating already-programmatic execution from still-manual planning, trafficking, optimization, analytics, reporting, negotiation, client service, and governance would materially narrow the range.
a.rationale: In broader 541800, management is 16.77% of employment, business and financial operations 16.65%, computer and mathematical work 5.18%, arts/design/media 24.48%, sales 18.41%, and office support 11.22%; market research analysts alone are 8.15%. IAB says AI can build media plans, generate audience segments, select partners, forecast performance, and support attribution, directly covering a large portion of media-buying workflow. Existing programmatic automation means already-automated auction execution is excluded from the remaining opportunity.

d5.bridge: Source population: US digital advertising revenue and surveyed media-buyer spending plans, plus national employment projections for advertising and marketing managers, rather than constant-price demand for 541830's current service basket. Transformation: translate strong nominal digital and programmatic growth into a lower five-year real service-demand ratio after removing price effects, cyclical event effects, and channel mix, while recognizing growing cross-platform complexity. Direction of error: rapid digital, creator, commerce, and video expansion could raise buying-service demand more than assumed, while direct platform buying, consolidation, recessions, and movement away from traditional channels could lower demand for the current basket.
caveats: Digital ad revenue is publisher/platform revenue, not agency service quantity.
One-year nominal growth and buyer plans are cyclical and do not directly measure five-year constant-price demand.
Political and sports events, recession, privacy rules, and platform changes can materially alter spending.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted 541830 net service revenue, agency managed-spend histories, client retention and pitch volumes, and channel-level five-year forecasts from IAB, Magna, and WARC would directly test demand for the current basket.
d5.rationale: IAB reports US digital ad revenue rose 13.9% to $294.6 billion in 2025 and programmatic revenue rose 20.5% to $162.4 billion. Buyers projected 9.5% ad-spend growth for 2026, or 7.1%–7.8% excluding major events. BLS projects overall advertising, promotions, and marketing managers to grow 6% over 2024–34, but advertising and promotions managers alone to decline 2%, supporting growth in media activity with meaningful structural displacement risk.

caveats: No source measures the share of 541830 firms satisfying every LMM lens condition.

The frozen firm count uses a generic 15.65% margin bridge; media pass-through accounting and commission structures can make receipts a poor proxy for agency economic revenue and therefore distort size-band membership.
e.change_evidence: A stratified review of 541830 LMM firms covering net revenue versus gross billings, repeat-client share, contract duration and assignability, customer concentration, founder billability, platform dependence, and second-layer management would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: Recurring outsourced client service is central to media buying, and the Census product definition includes planning, placement negotiation, trafficking, monitoring, and post-campaign analysis. Eligibility is reduced for agencies that primarily resell media with limited service differentiation, rely on a founder's client relationships, have severe customer concentration, lack assignable contracts, perform mostly one-off campaigns, or function as software wrappers.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541830 ($2,332,376,359) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541830 ($9,013,654,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $435,127 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541830); class <5: 406 firms, avg revenue/firm $751,810 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $117,658 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 90 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,826,767 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $598,889 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 65 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,081,677 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,264,782 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 56 firms, avg revenue/firm $21,907,357 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,428,501 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $94,779,679 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $14,833,020 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $219,892,111 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $34,413,115 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 121

o.bridge: Source population: IAB agencies, brands, publishers, and media buyers across company sizes, plus Census descriptions of current media-buying services, not a measured year-five operator-required share for LMM 541830. Transformation: classify routine plan assembly, audience segmentation, bidding, trafficking, monitoring, optimization, and reporting as increasingly platform- or agent-supplied, while retaining external operator need for objectives, cross-platform allocation, negotiation, measurement design, brand safety, privacy, exception handling, and accountable client representation. Direction of error: interoperable agentic systems and brand insourcing could eliminate more agency quantity, while platform fragmentation, opaque measurement, governance, and bargaining complexity could preserve more.
caveats: Current AI adoption and stated buyer priorities do not directly measure eliminated agency service quantity.
The operator/software boundary is fluid because agencies can embed proprietary technology and sell managed outcomes.
Large platforms may both automate execution and increase demand for independent verification and cross-platform accountability.
o.change_evidence: Buyer interviews and campaign-level loss data identifying work moved in-house or to self-service platforms, together with audited benchmarks of autonomous agents on cross-channel planning, spend control, brand safety, incrementality, and exception handling, would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Programmatic advertising already automates a large volume of buying, and IAB describes agentic systems moving planning, activation, and optimization toward autonomous execution. It also reports that half of agencies fear brands bringing AI capabilities in-house. Yet Census defines the service to include price negotiation, traffic management, performance monitoring, verification, and post-campaign analysis, while IAB identifies data quality, protection, fragmentation, transparency, and measurement as unresolved constraints that preserve accountable operator work.

q.bridge: Source population: 101 ANA client-side marketers representing 336 agency relationships across advertising services, not specifically LMM 541830 clients. Transformation: infer lower retention where labor-based fees rebase with staffing and hours, higher retention where fixed/output, commission, retainer, or performance structures decouple fees from effort, and discount all models for renewal competition, client procurement, transparency demands, and insourcing. Direction of error: media-buying commissions and percent-of-spend fees may retain more benefit than the broad agency sample, while platform price transparency and measurable automation may force faster pass-through.
caveats: ANA's 2022 sample is small, includes large marketers, and is not media-buying-only.
Compensation method incidence does not reveal revenue shares, contract duration, or realized pass-through.
The five-year discounted retention rate depends on future client procurement and competitor adoption that are not observed.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level 541830 data on net agency revenue by labor fee, fixed fee, retainer, commission, percentage of spend, and performance fee—linked to renewal repricing, staffing changes, and client churn after AI deployment—would materially move this estimate.
q.rationale: ANA found labor-based fees remained dominant, 82% of marketers used fee-based compensation in at least one agreement, and smaller advertisers used labor-based agreements more often at 76%. Fixed/output fees are growing and are negotiated without regard to labor time, allowing better initial retention, but 51% planned to change full-service compensation agreements. IAB also found half of brands worry about agency AI transparency and half of agencies worry brands will bring AI capabilities in-house, intensifying sharing and repricing pressure.

rho.bridge: Source population: more than 500 advertising experts from agencies, brands, and publishers in IAB's 2025 study, plus 205 media buyers in its 2026 outlook, rather than 541830 LMM agencies. Transformation: use observed integration and near-term adoption intent to estimate the five-year implemented share of exposed agency labor, then discount for data quality, privacy, fragmented tools, client approvals, model errors, governance, and workflow redesign. Direction of error: media buying is more digital and automation-ready than many agency services, raising implementation, while small agencies may have weaker proprietary data and integration resources than the surveyed leaders, lowering it.
caveats: Full campaign-lifecycle integration is not the same as implementing a given share of labor substitution.
IAB respondents skew toward digitally engaged industry participants and may be more advanced than LMM agencies.
Ad-platform automation can shift work rather than eliminate it by increasing campaign variants and measurement demands.
rho.change_evidence: A 541830-specific panel measuring AI-enabled hours saved, avoided hiring, campaigns run per employee, error and override rates, data integration, and client approval constraints by workflow would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: IAB found only 30% of agencies, brands, and publishers had fully integrated AI across the campaign lifecycle in 2025, but half of those not fully integrated expected to do so by 2026. Nearly two-thirds cited data quality, data protection, and tool fragmentation as leading barriers. By 2026, five of buyers' top six focus areas involved AI and two-thirds focused on agentic buying, supporting substantial five-year implementation without assuming complete autonomous operation.

s5.bridge: Source population: US employer-business owners across industries in Gallup's fall 2024 survey and middle-market marketing-services transactions tracked by Capstone, not eligible 541830 firms. Transformation: begin with the 22% of employer owners planning a five-year sale or transfer, discount for nonexecution, internal gifts, owner-dependent practices, and deals outside the lens, then allow some upward pressure from active marketing-services consolidation. Direction of error: technology-enabled recurring media agencies may attract strategic and sponsor buyers more readily than typical small businesses, raising transfers, but client concentration and platform dependence may prevent contemplated deals, lowering them.
caveats: Gallup measures intentions across all employer industries, not completed media-agency transfers.
Capstone covers broad marketing services and middle-to-large transactions without an at-risk firm denominator.
Agency client relationships can be nontransferable even when the legal entity is sold.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year 541830 transaction cohort from PitchBook, Capital IQ, COMvergence, Axial, broker records, and state filings—matched to the eligible LMM denominator and separating add-ons, internal successions, and closures—would materially improve this probability.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. Capstone reports marketing-services M&A volume increased each year since 2023, averaging 4.7% annual growth through 2025, with 2026 year-to-date dealmaking up 7.5%; acquirers prioritized recurring revenue, low concentration, and technology-enabled offerings. Qualifying completed control transfers remain a subset of intentions and broad sector transactions.

records.principal_driver: Remaining manual campaign planning, optimization, analytics, and reporting are directly exposed to AI in a large and growing digital advertising market.
records.principal_weakness: The same AI and platform capabilities that create labor efficiency also let clients self-serve and reprice agency work, while accounting for media pass-through obscures true firm size and labor intensity.
